title: A が いちばん～ (A ga ichiban～)
formation: Noun + が + いちばん + Adjective/Verb
shortExplanationEn: Express the superlative degree; 'the most', 'the best', 'the least'.
longExplanationEn: The A が いちばん～ grammar point is used to express a superlative degree or the highest rank. It can be translated as 'the most', 'the best', or 'the least' in English. The formation is used with various adjectives and verbs, and can also be used in comparison with other items or people.

examples.ja: この中で、寿司が一番好きです。
examples.romaji: Kono naka de, sushi ga ichiban suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: Of all these, I like sushi the most.

examples.ja: 彼女はクラスで一番速く泳げます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kurasu de ichiban hayaku oyogemasu.
examples.meaningEn: She can swim the fastest in the class.

examples.ja: この町では、この公園が一番きれいです。
examples.romaji: Kono machi de wa, kono kouen ga ichiban kirei desu.
examples.meaningEn: In this town, this park is the most beautiful.

examples.ja: 飲み物の中では、紅茶が一番好きです。
examples.romaji: Nomimono no naka de wa, koucha ga ichiban suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: Among all drinks, I like tea the most.

title: A。けれども、～B。(A. Keredomo,~ B.)
formation: Statement A + けれども、+ Statement B
shortExplanationEn: Used to express a contrast between two statements. It can be translated as 'but' or 'however'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point A。けれども、～B。 is used to express a contrast or contradiction between statement A and statement B. It can be translated as 'but' or 'however' in English. けれども is a conjunction that links two clauses to show there is a conflicting or opposing idea between them.

examples.ja: 今日は晴れている。けれども、寒いです。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa harete iru. Keredo mo, samui desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is sunny today. However, it is cold.

examples.ja: 彼女はうるさい。けれども、面白いです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa urusai. Keredo mo, omoshiroi desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is noisy. But, she is interesting.

examples.ja: その映画は長かった。けれども、退屈じゃなかった。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga wa nagakatta. Keredo mo, taikutsu janakatta.
examples.meaningEn: The movie was long. But, it wasn't boring.

examples.ja: 彼は運動が得意だ。けれども、勉強は苦手だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa undou ga tokui da. Keredo mo, benkyou wa nigate da.
examples.meaningEn: He is good at sports. However, he is not good at studying.

title: A。しかし、～B。 (A. Shikashi, ~B.)
formation: Sentence A + しかし + Sentence B.
shortExplanationEn: Expresses contrast or contradiction between two clauses; 'A, but B', 'A, however B'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point A。しかし、～B。 is used to show contrast or contradiction between two clauses. It can be translated as 'A, but B' or 'A, however B' in English. しかし is placed between two sentences to indicate a contrast between them.

examples.ja: 今日は忙しい。しかし、時間があるなら、コーヒーを飲もう。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa isogashii. Shikashi, jikan ga aru nara, koohii o nomou.
examples.meaningEn: I'm busy today. However, if I have time, let's have coffee.

examples.ja: その映画は面白かった。しかし、長すぎました。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga wa omoshirokatta. Shikashi, nagasugimashita.
examples.meaningEn: The movie was interesting. But, it was too long.

examples.ja: 彼は疲れていた。しかし、宿題を終わらせた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tsukareteita. Shikashi, shukudai o owaraseta.
examples.meaningEn: He was tired. However, he finished his homework.

examples.ja: 部屋は狭いです。しかし、便利な場所にあります。
examples.romaji: Heya wa semai desu. Shikashi, benri na basho ni arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The room is small. But, it is in a convenient location.

title: A。じゃ、～B。(A. Ja, ~B.)
formation: A: Sentence, B: Sentence (A and B can be any sentence type)
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a decision or suggestion; 'well then', 'in that case', 'so'.
longExplanationEn: The A。じゃ、～B。grammar point is used to express a decision, suggestion, or course of action based on a given situation or information. It can be translated as 'well then', 'in that case', or 'so' in English. This grammar works with any form of sentence, whether it's a verb, noun, い-adjective, or な-adjective in both parts (A and B).

examples.ja: 映画は時間がない。じゃ、また今度行こう。
examples.romaji: Eiga wa jikan ga nai. Ja, mata kondo ikou.
examples.meaningEn: There's no time for a movie. Well then, let's go another time.

examples.ja: お腹がすいた。じゃ、レストランに行こう。
examples.romaji: Onaka ga suita. Ja, resutoran ni ikou.
examples.meaningEn: I'm hungry. In that case, let's go to a restaurant.

examples.ja: 電車が遅れた。じゃ、バスで行こう。
examples.romaji: Densha ga okureta. Ja, basu de ikou.
examples.meaningEn: The train is late. So, let's go by bus.

examples.ja: このシャツは高い。じゃ、別のを買おう。
examples.romaji: Kono shatsu wa takai. Ja, betsu no o kau.
examples.meaningEn: This shirt is expensive. Well then, let's buy a different one.

title: A。それじゃ、～B。(A. Soreja,~B.)
formation: Sentence A + 。それじゃ、 + Sentence B
shortExplanationEn: Connect two sentences; 'A, so/in that case, B'
longExplanationEn: The A。それじゃ、～B。grammar point is used to connect two sentences or ideas. It translates to 'A, so/in that case, B' in English. This expression is used when the first sentence (A) creates a situation or context and the second sentence (B) indicates the consequence or response to that situation.

examples.ja: 今日は休みです。それじゃ、映画を観に行きましょう。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa yasumi desu. Soreja, eiga wo mi ni ikimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Today is a day off. In that case, let's go watch a movie.

examples.ja: 電気が切れた。それじゃ、ろうそくを使いましょう。
examples.romaji: Denki ga kireta. Soreja, rousoku wo tsukaimashou.
examples.meaningEn: The electricity went out. In that case, let's use candles.

examples.ja: レストランが混んでいます。それじゃ、別の場所で食べましょう。
examples.romaji: Resutoran ga kondeimasu. Soreja, betsu no basho de tabemashou.
examples.meaningEn: The restaurant is crowded. In that case, let's eat somewhere else.

examples.ja: 明日は雨が降るらしい。それじゃ、ピクニックは中止しましょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa ame ga furu rashii. Soreja, pikunikku wa chuushi shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: It seems it will rain tomorrow. In that case, let's cancel the picnic.

title: A。それでは、～B。(A. Soredewa,~B.)
formation: A (Statement) + 。それでは、 + B (Follow-up action or statement)
shortExplanationEn: Express a change in situation; 'then', 'in that case', 'so'.
longExplanationEn: The A。それでは、～B。grammar point is used to express a change in situation or transition from one action to another. It can be translated as 'then', 'in that case', or 'so' in English. The formation consists of two sentences or clauses, with それでは connecting them and reflecting a change based on the previous statement (A).

examples.ja: 今日は休みだ。それでは、映画を見に行こう。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa yasumi da. Sore dewa, eiga wo mi ni ikou.
examples.meaningEn: Today is a day off. In that case, let's go see a movie.

examples.ja: このパンはもう古いです。それでは、捨てましょう。
examples.romaji: Kono pan wa mou furui desu. Sore dewa, sutemashou.
examples.meaningEn: This bread is already old. Then, let's throw it away.

examples.ja: 雨が降ってきた。それでは、室内で遊ぼう。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futtekita. Sore dewa, shitsunai de asobou.
examples.meaningEn: It started raining. In that case, let's play indoors.

examples.ja: お腹が空いた。それでは、何か食べに行きましょう。
examples.romaji: Onaka ga suita. Sore dewa, nani ka tabe ni ikimashou.
examples.meaningEn: I'm hungry. So, let's go eat something.

title: A。 では、～B。 (A. Dewa, ~B)
formation: Noun A + では、 + Subject + Predicate B
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a contrast or comparison between A and B; 'As for A, ~B'.
longExplanationEn: The A では、～B grammar point is used to indicate a contrast or comparison between A and B. It is used to express that 'As for A, ~ B' or 'Regarding A, ~ B'. This grammar structure is often used for contrasting or comparing different subjects/topics.

examples.ja: 果物では、リンゴが一番好きです。
examples.romaji: Kudamono dewa, ringo ga ichiban suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for fruits, apples are my favorite.

examples.ja: スポーツでは、サッカーが一番人気です。
examples.romaji: Supōtsu dewa, sakkā ga ichiban ninki desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for sports, soccer is the most popular.

examples.ja: 今日の天気では、雨が降りそうです。
examples.romaji: Kyou no tenki dewa, ame ga furisou desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for today's weather, it looks like it will rain.

examples.ja: 料理では、和食が一番得意です。
examples.romaji: Ryouri dewa, washoku ga ichiban tokui desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for cooking, I am best at Japanese cuisine.

title: A。でも、～B。(A. Demo, ~B)
formation: Clause A + でも + Clause B
shortExplanationEn: Used to express contrast between two statements; 'A, but B', 'A, however B'.
longExplanationEn: The A。でも、～B。grammar point is used to express contrast or an unexpected continuation from statement A to statement B. It can be translated as 'A, but B' or 'A, however B' in English. The formation simply places でも at the beginning of the second sentence to show contrast with the first sentence.

examples.ja: 昨日は寒かった。でも、今日は暖かいです。
examples.romaji: Kinou wa samukatta. Demo, kyou wa atatakai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Yesterday was cold. But today it's warm.

examples.ja: 彼は忙しい。でも、友達と遊ぶ時間がある。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isogashii. Demo, tomodachi to asobu jikan ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: He is busy. However, he still has time to hang out with friends.

examples.ja: 試験に合格した。でも、まだ勉強を続けています。
examples.romaji: Shiken ni goukaku shita. Demo, mada benkyou wo tsuzuketeimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I passed the exam. But I'm still continuing to study.

examples.ja: 彼女は有名だ。でも、とても謙虚です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa yuumei da. Demo, totemo kenkyo desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is famous. However, she's very humble.

title: Aと Bと どちら～ (A to B to dochira~)
formation: Aと Bと どちら + Verb/Adjective/Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to ask which of two alternatives is preferred or to compare two things
longExplanationEn: The grammar pattern Aと Bと どちら～ is used to compare two alternatives A and B, and ask which one is preferred, better, or more suitable. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, or nouns.

examples.ja: りんごとみかんとどちらが好きですか。
examples.romaji: Ringo to mikan to dochira ga suki desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which do you like, apples or oranges?

examples.ja: 電車とバスとどちらで行くべきですか。
examples.romaji: Densha to basu to dochira de iku beki desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Should I go by train or bus?

examples.ja: 赤いワンピースと青いワンピースとどちらが似合うと思いますか。
examples.romaji: Akai wanpiisu to aoi wanpiisu to dochira ga niau to omoimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which do you think suits me better, the red dress or the blue dress?

examples.ja: このカメラとあのカメラとどちらのほうが使いやすいですか。
examples.romaji: Kono kamera to ano kamera to dochira no hou ga tsukaiyasui desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which one is easier to use, this camera or that camera?

title: AとBと どっち〜 (A to B to docchi〜)
formation: AとBと どっち + Verb, AかBか どっち + Verb
shortExplanationEn: Asking which one; 'which', 'A or B'.
longExplanationEn: The  AとBと どっち〜 grammar point is used to ask which one to choose between two options, A and B. It can be translated as 'which', 'A or B' in English. This is often used in casual speech and can be shortened to  AかBか どっち〜.

examples.ja: コーヒーと紅茶とどっちが好き？
examples.romaji: Koohii to koucha to docchi ga suki?
examples.meaningEn: Which do you like, coffee or tea?

examples.ja: 映画と音楽とどっちを楽しむ？
examples.romaji: Eiga to ongaku to docchi o tanoshimu?
examples.meaningEn: Which do you enjoy, movies or music?

examples.ja: 赤いシャツと青いシャツとどっちを買う？
examples.romaji: Akai shatsu to aoi shatsu to docchi o kau?
examples.meaningEn: Which one will you buy, the red shirt or the blue shirt?

examples.ja: 日本語と韓国語とどっちを勉強したい？
examples.romaji: Nihongo to kankokugo to docchi o benkyou shitai?
examples.meaningEn: Which do you want to study, Japanese or Korean?

title: A は B が〜 (A wa B ga〜)
formation: A は (Topic) + B が (Characteristic/Feature) + Description/Action
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that there is something unique or different about A and describes what is special about it.
longExplanationEn: The A は B が〜 grammar point is used to emphasize the uniqueness or difference of 'A' by describing a characteristic or feature 'B' that is specific to 'A'. This structure is used to convey that there is something notable or special about 'A' when compared to others.

examples.ja: 彼女は髪が長いです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kami ga nagai desu.
examples.meaningEn: What's special about her is that she has long hair.

examples.ja: このレストランは雰囲気が良いです。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa fun'iki ga ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: What's great about this restaurant is the atmosphere.

examples.ja: 私は朝ごはんが一番好きです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa asagohan ga ichiban suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: What I like the most is breakfast.

examples.ja: 彼は英語が得意です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eigo ga tokui desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is particularly good at English.

title: A は B より～ (A wa B yori～)
formation: A は B より + Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Express a comparison between A and B; 'A is more/less than B'.
longExplanationEn: The A は B より grammar point is used to express a comparison between two items or subjects (A and B). It can be translated as 'A is more/less than B' in English. This structure is followed by an adjective that describes the comparison.

examples.ja: このジュースはあのジュースより甘いです。
examples.romaji: Kono juusu wa ano juusu yori amai desu.
examples.meaningEn: This juice is sweeter than that juice.

examples.ja: 夏は冬より暑いです。
examples.romaji: Natsu wa fuyu yori atsui desu.
examples.meaningEn: Summer is hotter than winter.

examples.ja: 彼女は私より速く走ります。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa watashi yori hayaku hashirimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She runs faster than me.

examples.ja: スーパーはコンビニより安いです。
examples.romaji: Suupaa wa konbini yori yasui desu.
examples.meaningEn: The supermarket is cheaper than the convenience store.

title: A より B のほうが～ (A yori B no hou ga ～)
formation: A + より + B + のほうが + Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Used to compare two items; 'B is more ～ than A'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point A より B のほうが～ is used to compare two items or situations and indicate that one is more (adjective) than the other. It can be translated as 'B is more ～ than A' in English. It is used with both い-adjectives and な-adjectives.

examples.ja: ピザよりハンバーガーのほうが美味しい。
examples.romaji: Piza yori hanbaagaa no hou ga oishii.
examples.meaningEn: Hamburgers are more delicious than pizza.

examples.ja: 夏より冬のほうが寒い。
examples.romaji: Natsu yori fuyu no hou ga samui.
examples.meaningEn: Winter is colder than summer.

examples.ja: ここよりそこのほうが静かです。
examples.romaji: Koko yori soko no hou ga shizuka desu.
examples.meaningEn: There is quieter than here.

examples.ja: 車より電車のほうが速い。
examples.romaji: Kuruma yori densha no hou ga hayai.
examples.meaningEn: Trains are faster than cars.

title: い-Adjective く + Verb (i-Adjective + ku + Verb)
formation: い-Adjective (remove い) + く + Verb
shortExplanationEn: Used to connect an い-adjective to a verb, modifying the verb (adverbial usage).
longExplanationEn: The い-Adjective + く + Verb grammar point transforms an い-adjective into its adverbial form, describing how the verb is performed. In English, this often corresponds to '-ly' adverbs (e.g., 'quickly', 'slowly').

examples.ja: 彼は速く走りました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa hayaku hashirimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He ran quickly.

examples.ja: この料理は美味しく食べました。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa oishiku tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I enjoyed eating this dish.

examples.ja: 彼女は優しく話します。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa yasashiku hanashimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She speaks kindly.

examples.ja: 母は忙しく仕事をしています。
examples.romaji: Haha wa isogashiku shigoto o shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: My mother is working busily.

title: い-Adjective く します (i-Adjective ku shimasu)
formation: い-Adjective (remove い) + くします
shortExplanationEn: Use an い-adjective to express 'to make' or 'to become'.
longExplanationEn: By adding くします to an い-adjective, you express making something (or causing something to become) the quality of that adjective. In English, this often corresponds to phrases like 'make something more ADJECTIVE.'

examples.ja: 部屋を明るくします。
examples.romaji: Heya o akaruku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make the room brighter.

examples.ja: 友達の気分を楽しくします。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi no kibun o tanoshiku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make my friend’s mood more pleasant.

examples.ja: 仕事が簡単になるように、やり方を早くします。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga kantan ni naru you ni, yarikata o hayaku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make the way of doing things faster so that the work becomes easier.

examples.ja: 食べ物を美味しくします。
examples.romaji: Tabemono o oishiku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make the food delicious.

title: い-Adjective: Negative Polite Form
formation: い-Adjective (remove い) + くないです
shortExplanationEn: Change an い-adjective into a polite negative form with ～くないです (or ～くありません).
longExplanationEn: To make an い-adjective negative and polite, remove the final い and add くないです. An alternative more formal style is くありません. For example, 美味しい → 美味しくないです / 美味しくありません。

examples.ja: このピザは美味しくないです。
examples.romaji: Kono piza wa oishiku nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: This pizza is not delicious.

examples.ja: この部屋は広くないです。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa hiroku nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: This room is not spacious.

examples.ja: 彼の意見は面白くないです。
examples.romaji: Kare no iken wa omoshiroku nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: His opinion is not interesting.

examples.ja: その映画は楽しくありません。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga wa tanoshiku arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: That movie is not enjoyable.

title: い-Adjective て (i-Adjective + te~)
formation: い-Adjective (remove い) + くて + い-Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Used to connect two or more い-adjectives to describe something.
longExplanationEn: The い-Adjective て form is used to connect two or more い-adjectives when listing attributes of something or someone. To form it, replace the final い with くて. For example, かわいい → かわいくて, おいしい → おいしくて.

examples.ja: このリンゴは甘くて美味しいです。
examples.romaji: Kono ringo wa amakute oishii desu.
examples.meaningEn: This apple is sweet and delicious.

examples.ja: 彼女は優しくてかわいいです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa yasashikute kawaii desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is kind and cute.

examples.ja: 映画は面白くて感動的でした。
examples.romaji: Eiga wa omoshirokute kandouteki deshita.
examples.meaningEn: The movie was interesting and moving.

examples.ja: この部屋は広くて明るいです。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa hirokute akarui desu.
examples.meaningEn: This room is spacious and bright.

title: な-Adjective に + Verb
formation: な-Adjective (remove な) + に + Verb
shortExplanationEn: Turn a な-adjective into an adverb by adding に.
longExplanationEn: To make a な-adjective modify a verb, remove the な and add に. This form describes how an action is performed. For instance, 静か（な） → 静かに, 親切（な） → 親切に.

examples.ja: 彼は静かに話す。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shizuka ni hanasu.
examples.meaningEn: He speaks quietly.

examples.ja: 部屋をきれいに掃除した。
examples.romaji: Heya o kirei ni souji shita.
examples.meaningEn: I cleaned the room neatly.

examples.ja: 彼女は元気に働いています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa genki ni hataraite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She works energetically.

examples.ja: 先生は親切に説明してくれる。
examples.romaji: Sensei wa shinsetsu ni setsumei shite kureru.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher explains kindly.

title: な-Adjective で～ (na-Adjective de~)
formation: な-Adjective + で
shortExplanationEn: Usage of で to connect or add information with a な-adjective.
longExplanationEn: Use な-adjective + で to connect it to another quality, action, or description. It can combine a な-adjective with another adjective, verb, or clause.

examples.ja: 彼は綺麗で優しい女性が好きです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kirei de yasashii josei ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: He likes women who are beautiful and kind.

examples.ja: この料理は健康的で、元気な気分になります。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa kenkouteki de, genki na kibun ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: This dish is healthy and makes you feel energetic.

examples.ja: 彼は静かで落ち着いた環境で仕事をしたい。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shizuka de ochitsuita kankyou de shigoto o shitai.
examples.meaningEn: He wants to work in a quiet and calm environment.

examples.ja: この部屋は清潔で気持ちがいいです。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa seiketsu de kimochi ga ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: This room is clean and feels good.

title: な-Adjective に します (na-Adjective ni shimasu)
formation: な-Adjective + に + します
shortExplanationEn: Expressing a decision or preference with a な-adjective.
longExplanationEn: The な-adjective に します pattern is used to express a decision or choice based on a な-adjective. It often translates as 'I’ll make it ...,' 'I will choose ...,' or 'I’ve decided on ...'.

examples.ja: 静かにします。
examples.romaji: Shizuka ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will be quiet.

examples.ja: 部屋をきれいにします。
examples.romaji: Heya o kirei ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make the room clean.

examples.ja: 明日の会議は簡単にします。
examples.romaji: Ashita no kaigi wa kantan ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make tomorrow’s meeting simple.

examples.ja: 健康的な食事にします。
examples.romaji: Kenkou-teki na shokuji ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will choose a healthy meal.

title: な-Adjective に なります (na-Adjective ni narimasu)
formation: な-Adjective (remove な) + に + なります
shortExplanationEn: Express a change or transformation into a state described by the な-adjective.
longExplanationEn: The な-Adjective + に + なります grammar point is used to express a change or transformation into the state described by the な-adjective. It translates to 'become' or 'turn into' along with the meaning of the な-adjective.

examples.ja: この部屋は静かになります。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa shizuka ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: This room becomes quiet.

examples.ja: 彼女はきれいになります。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kirei ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She becomes beautiful.

examples.ja: 彼は元気になります。
examples.romaji: Kare wa genki ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He becomes energetic.

examples.ja: 町はにぎやかになります。
examples.romaji: Machi wa nigiyaka ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The town becomes lively.

title: Noun か Noun か～ (Noun ka Noun ka～)
formation: Noun1 + か + Noun2 + か～
shortExplanationEn: Expresses choice or alternatives; 'either', 'or'.
longExplanationEn: The Noun か Noun か～ grammar point is used to present two or more alternatives for a choice. It can be translated to 'either', 'or' in English. It is formed by placing the particle か in between two nouns or noun phrases.

examples.ja: 今日、映画を見るか、散歩するかどちらにしますか？
examples.romaji: Kyou, eiga wo miru ka, sanpo suru ka dochira ni shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Will we watch a movie or go for a walk today?

examples.ja: コーヒーか紅茶か、一つだけ選んでください。
examples.romaji: Koohii ka koucha ka, hitotsu dake erande kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please choose only one, either coffee or tea.

examples.ja: 電車かバスを使って、学校に行くつもりです。
examples.romaji: Densha ka basu o tsukatte, gakkou ni iku tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to go to school using either the train or the bus.

examples.ja: 週末は山に登るか海に行くか、まだ決めていません。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu wa yama ni noboru ka umi ni iku ka, mada kimete imasen.
examples.meaningEn: I haven't decided whether to climb the mountain or go to the beach this weekend.

title: Noun が できます (Noun ga dekimasu)
formation: Noun + が + できます
shortExplanationEn: Express the ability to do something.
longExplanationEn: The Noun が できます grammar point is used to express the ability to do something. In this grammar pattern, the noun is the activity that the speaker or subject can do. It translates as 'can do' or 'able to do' in English.

examples.ja: 英語ができます。
examples.romaji: Eigo ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I can speak English.

examples.ja: 料理ができます。
examples.romaji: Ryouri ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I can cook.

examples.ja: 彼は泳ぎができます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa oyogi ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can swim.

examples.ja: 彼女はピアノができます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa piano ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She can play the piano.

title: Noun がほしいです (〜ga hoshii desu)
formation: Noun + が + ほしいです
shortExplanationEn: Express a desire or wanting something; 'I want', 'I would like'.
longExplanationEn: The がほしいです grammar point is used to express a desire or the act of wanting something in polite form. It can be translated as 'I want' or 'I would like' in English. It is used with a noun and the particle が.

examples.ja: 新しい車がほしいです。
examples.romaji: Atarashii kuruma ga hoshii desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want a new car.

examples.ja: コーヒーがほしいです。
examples.romaji: Koohii ga hoshii desu.
examples.meaningEn: I would like some coffee.

examples.ja: 彼女は花がほしいです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa hana ga hoshii desu.
examples.meaningEn: She wants flowers.

examples.ja: 友達がほしいです。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga hoshii desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want a friend.

title: Noun から Noun まで (Noun kara Noun made)
formation: Noun1 + から + Noun2 + まで
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a range; 'from...to', 'between...and'.
longExplanationEn: The から...まで grammar point is used to express a range between two nouns, indicating the starting point and ending point of an action, event, or period of time. It can be translated as 'from...to' or 'between...and' in English.

examples.ja: 朝9時から午後5時まで働きます。
examples.romaji: Asa ku-ji kara gogo go-ji made hatarakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I work from 9 AM to 5 PM.

examples.ja: 東京から大阪まで電車で行く。
examples.romaji: Toukyou kara Osaka made densha de iku.
examples.meaningEn: I will go by train from Tokyo to Osaka.

examples.ja: 地下鉄は午前6時から深夜まで運行しています。
examples.romaji: Chikatetsu wa gozen roku-ji kara shin'ya made unkou shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: The subway runs from 6 AM until midnight.

examples.ja: 月曜日から金曜日まで学校に通います。
examples.romaji: Getsuyoubi kara Kinyoubi made gakkou ni kayoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I attend school from Monday to Friday.

title: Noun くらい～ (Noun kurai～)
formation: Noun + くらい
shortExplanationEn: Express the extent or degree of something; 'about', 'approximately', 'at least'.
longExplanationEn: The くらい grammar point is used with a noun to express the extent or degree of something, meaning 'about', 'approximately', or 'at least' in English. It helps to provide a rough estimate or minimum standard to the noun it is attached to.

examples.ja: この本は三百ページくらいあります。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa sanbyaku peeji kurai arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: This book has about 300 pages.

examples.ja: 彼の家まで歩いて20分くらいかかります。
examples.romaji: Kare no ie made aruite nijuppun kurai kakarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: It takes about 20 minutes to walk to his house.

examples.ja: 明日は暑くて、30度くらいになるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa atsukute, sanjuu-do kurai ni naru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: Tomorrow will be hot, about 30 degrees.

examples.ja: この料理を作るのに、材料は500円くらい必要です。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri o tsukuru no ni, zairyou wa gohyaku-en kurai hitsuyou desu.
examples.meaningEn: You need about 500 yen worth of ingredients to make this dish.

title: Noun ぐらい～ (Noun gurai～)
formation: Noun + ぐらい
shortExplanationEn: Express an approximate amount or degree; 'about', 'approximately'.
longExplanationEn: The ぐらい grammar point is used with nouns to express an approximate amount or degree. It is often translated as 'about', 'approximately', or 'around' in English. This can be used for time, quantity, or other measurements.

examples.ja: 一日中勉強したので、１０時間ぐらい勉強しました。
examples.romaji: Ichinichijuu benkyou shita node, juu-jikan gurai benkyou shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I studied all day, so I studied for about 10 hours.

examples.ja: この本は１００ページぐらいある。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa hyaku peeji gurai aru.
examples.meaningEn: This book has about 100 pages.

examples.ja: 彼は果物を５個ぐらい食べました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kudamono o go-ko gurai tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: He ate about 5 pieces of fruit.

examples.ja: 駅から家まで歩いて２０分ぐらいかかります。
examples.romaji: Eki kara ie made aruite nijuppun gurai kakarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: It takes about 20 minutes to walk from the station to my house.

title: Noun ごろ～ (Noun + goro～)
formation: Noun + ごろ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'around' or 'about' a certain time or age.
longExplanationEn: The ごろ grammar point is used to indicate 'around' or 'about' a certain time or age. It is commonly used with time-related expressions and age-related expressions to give an approximate time or age.

examples.ja: 昼ごろに電話してください。
examples.romaji: Hiru goro ni denwa shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please call me around noon.

examples.ja: 彼は10歳ごろに日本に来ました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa juu-sai goro ni Nihon ni kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He came to Japan around the age of 10.

examples.ja: 午後３時ごろに会いましょう。
examples.romaji: Gogo san-ji goro ni aimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's meet around 3 PM.

examples.ja: 昨日の夜8時ごろに帰りました。
examples.romaji: Kinou no yoru hachi-ji goro ni kaerimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I returned home last night around 8 o'clock.

title: Noun だけ〜 (〜dake)
formation: Noun + だけ, Verb-casual + だけ, い-Adjective + だけ, な-Adjective-な + だけ
shortExplanationEn: Indicates 'only', 'just', or 'merely'.
longExplanationEn: The Noun だけ grammar point is used to emphasize the idea of 'only', 'just', or 'merely' in a sentence. It can be attached to nouns, verbs, い-adjectives, and な-adjectives to show this emphasis. This grammar point is commonly used in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.ja: コーヒーだけ飲んで、帰りました。
examples.romaji: Koohii dake nonde, kaerimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I only drank coffee and went home.

examples.ja: この本は二十ページだけ読んだ。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa nijuu peeji dake yonda.
examples.meaningEn: I only read twenty pages of this book.

examples.ja: 彼女はとても速いですが、走るだけでなく、考えるのも速いです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa totemo hayai desu ga, hashiru dake denaku, kangaeru no mo hayai desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is very fast, not only at running but also at thinking.

examples.ja: この部屋は寝るだけの場所です。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa neru dake no basho desu.
examples.meaningEn: This room is a place only for sleeping.

title: Nounで～ (Noun de ~)
formation: Noun + で
shortExplanationEn: Indicate a place or means for an action or situation; 'at, in, on', 'using', or 'by means of'.
longExplanationEn: The で grammar point is used to indicate a location, method, or tool for doing an action or for being in a situation. It can be translated as 'at, in, on', 'using', or 'by means of' in English. Nounで is formed by adding で after a noun.

examples.ja: 図書館で勉強します。
examples.romaji: Toshokan de benkyou shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I study at the library.

examples.ja: バスで学校に行きます。
examples.romaji: Basu de gakkou ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I go to school by bus.

examples.ja: 鉛筆で書きました。
examples.romaji: Enpitsu de kakimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I wrote it using a pencil.

examples.ja: 英語で話しましょう。
examples.romaji: Eigo de hanashimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's talk in English.

title: Noun と～ (Noun to～)
formation: Noun1 + と + Noun2
shortExplanationEn: Express 'with', 'and' or 'together with' when connecting nouns.
longExplanationEn: The Noun と grammar point is used to express a connection between two or more nouns, indicating 'with', 'and', or 'together with' in English. It functions as a particle that is used to simply list multiple items or express companionship.

examples.ja: リンゴとバナナを買いました。
examples.romaji: Ringo to banana wo kaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I bought apples and bananas.

examples.ja: 友達と映画を見に行きます。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to eiga wo mi ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to watch a movie with my friend.

examples.ja: 父と母と一緒に旅行しました。
examples.romaji: Chichi to haha to issho ni ryokou shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I traveled together with my father and mother.

examples.ja: 彼と彼女は結婚しました。
examples.romaji: Kare to kanojo wa kekkon shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He and she got married.

title: Noun に～ (Noun ni～)
formation: Noun + に
shortExplanationEn: Express a specific direction, target or purpose; 'to', 'toward', 'for'.
longExplanationEn: The 'Noun に' grammar point is used to express a specific direction, target, or purpose in relation to a noun. It can be translated as 'to', 'toward', or 'for' in English. The に particle is added directly after the noun which indicates direction, target or purpose.

examples.ja: 彼女にプレゼントをあげました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni purezento wo agemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I gave a present to her.

examples.ja: 日本に行きます。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am going to Japan.

examples.ja: 6時に集合しましょう。
examples.romaji: Roku-ji ni shuugou shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's meet at 6 o'clock.

examples.ja: 友達に手紙を書きます。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni tegami wo kakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will write a letter to my friend.

title: Noun に 帰ります (Noun ni kaerimasu)
formation: Noun + に + 帰ります
shortExplanationEn: Express going back or returning to a place: 'to return to', 'to go back to'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point 'Noun に 帰ります' is used to express physically going back or returning to a specific place. It can be translated as 'to go back to' or 'to return to' in English. The particle に indicates the destination of the return.

examples.ja: 夏休みが終わったら、学校に帰ります。
examples.romaji: Natsuyasumi ga owattara, gakkou ni kaerimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When summer vacation ends, I'll go back to school.

examples.ja: 買い物を終えたら、家に帰ります。
examples.romaji: Kaimono o oetara, ie ni kaerimasu.
examples.meaningEn: After finishing shopping, I will go back home.

examples.ja: 今週末、実家に帰ります。
examples.romaji: Konshuumatsu, jikka ni kaerimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I'm going back to my parents' home this weekend.

examples.ja: 友達と旅行に行ったあと、東京に帰ります。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to ryokou ni itta ato, Toukyou ni kaerimasu.
examples.meaningEn: After traveling with my friend, I'll return to Tokyo.

title: Noun に します (Noun ni shimasu)
formation: Noun + に します
shortExplanationEn: Express a choice or decision; 'choose', 'decide on'.
longExplanationEn: The に します grammar point is used to express making a choice or decision. It can be translated as 'choose' or 'decide on' in English. It is often used in situations where someone has to make a selection or decide on something, such as ordering food at a restaurant or deciding on a vacation destination.

examples.ja: 今日の昼ご飯は寿司にします。
examples.romaji: Kyou no hirugohan wa sushi ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I'll choose sushi for today's lunch.

examples.ja: この靴を買うか迷っていますが、赤い色の方にします。
examples.romaji: Kono kutsu wo kau ka mayotteimasu ga, akai iro no hou ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I was hesitating about which shoes to buy, but I'll go with the red ones.

examples.ja: 休みの日に何をするか決めた。映画館に行くことにします。
examples.romaji: Yasumi no hi ni nani wo suru ka kimeta. Eigakan ni iku koto ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I've decided what to do on my day off. I'll go to the movie theater.

examples.ja: フランス語とスペイン語のどちらを勉強するか悩んだが、フランス語にします。
examples.romaji: Furansugo to Supeingo no dochira wo benkyou suru ka nayanda ga, Furansugo ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I was torn between studying French and Spanish, but I decided on French.

title: Noun に なります (Noun ni narimasu)
formation: Noun + に + なります
shortExplanationEn: Expresses change or becoming something; 'to become', 'to turn into', 'to be'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Noun に なります is used to express a change or transformation into something else, or to indicate that something 'becomes', 'turns into', or simply 'is' something. It is commonly used with nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は医者になります。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isha ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He will become a doctor.

examples.ja: この茶色の葉が赤になります。
examples.romaji: Kono chairo no ha ga aka ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: These brown leaves will turn red.

examples.ja: 料理が冷めたら、美味しくなくなります。
examples.romaji: Ryouri ga sametara, oishikunaku narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When the dish cools down, it becomes less delicious.

examples.ja: 来年、東京オリンピックになります。
examples.romaji: Rainen, Toukyou Orinpikku ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Next year, it will be the Tokyo Olympics.

title: Noun に 来ます (Noun ni kimasu)
formation: Noun + に + 来ます (kimasu)
shortExplanationEn: Used to express coming to a place.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Noun に 来ます is used to indicate that someone or something comes to a specific place. The noun before に indicates the destination or place, while the verb 来ます expresses the action of coming. (Similarly, 'Noun に 行きます' means 'go to [Noun],' and 'Noun に 帰ります' means 'return to [Noun].')

examples.ja: 友達が私の家に来ます。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga watashi no ie ni kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: My friend will come to my house.

examples.ja: 今晩、父が駅に迎えに来ます。
examples.romaji: Konban, chichi ga eki ni mukae ni kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Tonight, my father will come to pick me up at the station.

examples.ja: 明日、先生が学校に来ます。
examples.romaji: Ashita, sensei ga gakkou ni kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Tomorrow, the teacher will come to school.

examples.ja: 有名な歌手がこの街に来ます。
examples.romaji: Yuumei na kashu ga kono machi ni kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: A famous singer will come to this town.

title: Noun に 行きます (Noun ni ikimasu)
formation: Noun + に + 行きます
shortExplanationEn: Used to express going to a location.
longExplanationEn: The Noun に 行きます grammar point is used to express going to a specific location. This structure requires a noun (indicating the destination) followed by に and the verb 行きます, which means 'to go'.

examples.ja: 学校に行きます。
examples.romaji: Gakkou ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to school.

examples.ja: 明日、スーパーに行きます。
examples.romaji: Ashita, suupaa ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the supermarket tomorrow.

examples.ja: 週末に映画館に行きます。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu ni eigakan ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the movie theater on the weekend.

examples.ja: 夏休みに海に行きます。
examples.romaji: Natsu yasumi ni umi ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the sea during the summer vacation.

title: Noun の あとで (Noun no atode)
formation: Noun + の + あとで
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the order of events; 'after', 'later than'.
longExplanationEn: The の あとで grammar point is used to express the order of events, specifying that an action occurs after or later than the noun mentioned. It translates to 'after' or 'later than' in English. This structure is simply formed by attaching の あとで to the noun.

examples.ja: 朝ご飯のあとで、歯を磨きます。
examples.romaji: Asagohan no atode, ha wo migakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: After breakfast, I brush my teeth.

examples.ja: 映画のあとで、レストランで夕食を食べました。
examples.romaji: Eiga no atode, resutoran de yuushoku o tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: After the movie, we ate dinner at a restaurant.

examples.ja: 会議のあとで、上司と話し合います。
examples.romaji: Kaigi no atode, joushi to hanashiaimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will discuss with my boss after the meeting.

examples.ja: 晩ご飯のあとで、散歩に行きました。
examples.romaji: Bangohan no atode, sanpo ni ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: After dinner, I went for a walk.

title: Noun の 前に (Noun no mae ni)
formation: Noun + の + 前に
shortExplanationEn: Used to describe a location or position 'in front of' a noun.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Noun の 前に is used to describe a location or position 'in front of' a noun. It is formed by adding の 前に after the noun it refers to. (Note that 'Noun の 前に' can also mean 'before [Noun]' in time contexts, but these examples focus on physical location.)

examples.ja: 駅の前にコンビニがあります。
examples.romaji: Eki no mae ni konbini ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a convenience store in front of the station.

examples.ja: 家の前に車を止めてください。
examples.romaji: Ie no mae ni kuruma wo tomete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please park the car in front of the house.

examples.ja: 私の前に彼が立っていました。
examples.romaji: Watashi no mae ni kare ga tatte imashita.
examples.meaningEn: He was standing in front of me.

examples.ja: ホテルの前にタクシーが来た。
examples.romaji: Hoteru no mae ni takushii ga kita.
examples.meaningEn: A taxi came in front of the hotel.

title: Noun は～ (Noun wa〜)
formation: Noun + は
shortExplanationEn: Used to mark the topic of a sentence; 'as for', 'speaking of'.
longExplanationEn: The は (wa) particle is used to indicate the topic of a sentence. It is usually translated as 'as for', 'speaking of', or it simply marks what the sentence is about. This is different from が (ga), which marks the subject of a verb.

examples.ja: 私は日本の料理が好きです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa Nihon no ryouri ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for me, I like Japanese cuisine.

examples.ja: 彼は英語が上手です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eigo ga jouzu desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for him, he is good at English.

examples.ja: この本は面白いです。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa omoshiroi desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for this book, it's interesting.

examples.ja: 今日は暑いですね。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa atsui desu ne.
examples.meaningEn: Speaking of today, it's hot, isn't it?

title: Noun も〜 (Noun mo~)
formation: Noun + も
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that the action or state is the same for multiple items or people; 'also', 'as well'.
longExplanationEn: The も grammar point is used after a noun to indicate that the action or state is the same for multiple items or people. It means 'also', 'as well', or 'too' in English. も often replaces は or が in the sentence to show that the same statement applies to this additional noun.

examples.ja: 私も日本語を勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Watashi mo nihongo wo benkyou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am also studying Japanese.

examples.ja: 彼もケーキが好きです。
examples.romaji: Kare mo keeki ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: He also likes cake.

examples.ja: 東京も大阪も観光スポットがたくさんあります。
examples.romaji: Toukyou mo Oosaka mo kankou supotto ga takusan arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There are many tourist spots in both Tokyo and Osaka.

examples.ja: この映画もあの映画も面白かった。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga mo ano eiga mo omoshirokatta.
examples.meaningEn: Both this movie and that movie were interesting.

title: Noun や Noun など～ (Noun ya Noun nado)
formation: Noun1 + や + Noun2 + など
shortExplanationEn: Lists items or examples but not exhaustively; 'such as', 'like', 'and so on'.
longExplanationEn: The や...など grammar point is used to list a few items or examples that belong to a similar category or context, but it is not exhaustive. It can be translated as 'such as', 'like', or 'and so on' in English. This grammar pattern is used with nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は野菜や果物などを食べるのが好きです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa yasai ya kudamono nado o taberu no ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: He likes to eat things like vegetables and fruits.

examples.ja: 日本の都市や東京、大阪などに行きたいです。
examples.romaji: Nihon no toshi ya Toukyou, Oosaka nado ni ikitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to go to Japanese cities such as Tokyo, Osaka, and so on.

examples.ja: 彼女は猫や犬など動物が好きです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa neko ya inu nado doubutsu ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: She likes animals like cats, dogs, and so on.

examples.ja: この本屋では漫画や小説などたくさんの本が売られています。
examples.romaji: Kono honya de wa manga ya shousetsu nado takusan no hon ga urarete imasu.
examples.meaningEn: In this bookstore, many books such as manga and novels are sold.

title: Noun を〜 (Noun wo〜)
formation: Noun + を + Verb
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the direct object of a verb.
longExplanationEn: The を (wo) particle is used to mark the direct object of a verb in a sentence. It shows the noun that the action (verb) is performed on. The particle を is usually pronounced as 'o' in spoken Japanese.

examples.ja: 私は本を読みます。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa hon wo yomimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I read a book.

examples.ja: 彼はケーキを食べました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa keeki wo tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: He ate a cake.

examples.ja: 母はコーヒーを飲むのが好きです。
examples.romaji: Haha wa koohii wo nomu no ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: My mother likes to drink coffee.

examples.ja: 友達が私のペンを使いました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga watashi no pen wo tsukaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: My friend used my pen.

title: Verb た あとで (ta ato de)
formation: Verb-た form + あとで
shortExplanationEn: Indicates an action that takes place after another action.
longExplanationEn: The たあとで grammar point is used to indicate that one action takes place after another action is completed. It means 'after' or 'after doing' in English. The formation is created with the た form (past tense) of a verb, followed by あとで (ato de).

examples.ja: 宿題をしたあとで、テレビを見ました。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo shita ato de, terebi wo mimashita.
examples.meaningEn: After doing my homework, I watched TV.

examples.ja: 朝ごはんを食べたあとで、歯を磨きます。
examples.romaji: Asagohan wo tabeta ato de, ha wo migakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: After eating breakfast, I brush my teeth.

examples.ja: 仕事が終わったあとで、ジムに行く予定です。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga owatta ato de, jimu ni iku yotei desu.
examples.meaningEn: After finishing work, I plan to go to the gym.

examples.ja: 映画を見たあとで、カフェでコーヒーを飲みました。
examples.romaji: Eiga wo mita ato de, kafe de koohii wo nomimashita.
examples.meaningEn: After watching the movie, I drank coffee at a cafe.

title: Verb たいです (taidesu)
formation: る-Verb: Remove る and add たいです, う-Verb: Replace the final verb character with the ～い form and add たいです, Exception: する becomes したいです
shortExplanationEn: Express desire or want to do something; 'I want to', 'I would like to'.
longExplanationEn: The たいです grammar point is used to express the speaker's desire or want to perform a certain action. It can be translated as 'I want to' or 'I would like to' in English. The formation differs depending on whether the verb is a る-verb or an う-verb.

examples.ja: 私は寿司を食べたいです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa sushi wo tabetai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to eat sushi.

examples.ja: 彼は日本に行きたいです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa Nihon ni ikitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: He wants to go to Japan.

examples.ja: 彼女は英語を勉強したいです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa eigo wo benkyou shitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: She wants to study English.

examples.ja: 子供たちは遊びたいです。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi wa asobitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: The children want to play.

title: Verb て～ (Verb + te～)
formation: Verb (Group 1) - Replace last syllable with 〜て, Verb (Group 2) - Replace 'る' with 〜て, Verb (Group 3) - する becomes して, くる becomes きて
shortExplanationEn: A verb conjugation that connects actions or states.
longExplanationEn: The ～て form of a verb is used to connect actions or states in a sequence, implying that the first action is completed before the second one starts. The ～て form is frequently used in casual speech, and it can also be used in polite speech when combined with certain auxiliary verbs like ください (kudasai) or いる (iru).

examples.ja: コーヒーを飲んで、出かけます。
examples.romaji: Koohii o nonde, dekakemasu.
examples.meaningEn: After drinking coffee, I will go out.

examples.ja: 買い物をして、家に帰りましょう。
examples.romaji: Kaimono o shite, ie ni kaerimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's go shopping and then go home.

examples.ja: 宿題をして、寝ます。
examples.romaji: Shukudai o shite, nemasu.
examples.meaningEn: After doing my homework, I will sleep.

examples.ja: 友達を待って、一緒に映画を見に行きます。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi o matte, issho ni eiga o mini ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will wait for my friend and go watch a movie together.

title: Verb て います (Verb te imasu)
formation: Verb て-form + います
shortExplanationEn: Express ongoing action or state; 'is doing', 'is doing (continuous)', 'has been doing'.
longExplanationEn: The て います grammar point is used to express an action that is ongoing, continuous, or has been happening. It is formed by conjugating the verb in its て-form and adding います. It can be translated as 'is doing', 'is doing (continuous)', or 'has been doing' in English.

examples.ja: 彼女は今ピアノを弾いています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ima piano wo hiite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is playing the piano now.

examples.ja: 私たちは日本語を勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa nihongo wo benkyou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: We are studying Japanese.

examples.ja: 彼は映画を見ています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eiga wo miteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is watching a movie.

examples.ja: 入っていますので、お待ちください。
examples.romaji: Haitteimasu node, omachi kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: I am in, so please wait.

title: Verb てから～ (〜te kara)
formation: Verb-て形 + から
shortExplanationEn: Express a sequence of actions or events; 'after', 'since', 'once'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てから grammar point is used to indicate the sequence of actions or events, meaning that one action or event occurs after another. It can be translated as 'after', 'since', or 'once' in English. The て-form indicates the first action and から shows the sequence.

examples.ja: 宿題をしてから、テレビを見ました。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo shite kara, terebi wo mimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I watched TV after doing my homework.

examples.ja: 食べてから、歯を磨きましょう。
examples.romaji: Tabete kara, ha wo migakimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's brush our teeth after eating.

examples.ja: 仕事が終わってから、友達と飲みに行きます。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga owatte kara, tomodachi to nomi ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go drinking with my friends after work is done.

examples.ja: 学校が終わってから、図書館で勉強します。
examples.romaji: Gakkou ga owatte kara, toshokan de benkyou shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will study in the library after school is over.

title: Verb て ください (Verb-te kudasai)
formation: Verb-て form + ください
shortExplanationEn: A request or command; 'please do'.
longExplanationEn: The て ください grammar point is used to make a request or command someone to do something politely. The verb is conjugated into its て-form and followed by ください, which means 'please'. This structure is used when asking someone to do something with a polite and respectful tone.

examples.ja: ドアを開けてください。
examples.romaji: Doa wo akete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please open the door.

examples.ja: 電話番号を教えてください。
examples.romaji: Denwa bangou wo oshiete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please tell me your phone number.

examples.ja: ゆっくり話してください。
examples.romaji: Yukkuri hanashite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please speak slowly.

examples.ja: コーヒーを一つ持ってきてください。
examples.romaji: Koohii wo hitotsu motte kite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please bring me a coffee.

title: Verb ないで ください (〜naide kudasai)
formation: Verb-ない-form + で ください
shortExplanationEn: Used to make a polite request for someone not to do something; 'please don't'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Verb ないで ください is used to make a polite request for someone not to do a certain action. In English, it can be translated as 'please don't'. To form this expression, take the verb in its negative form (ない-form) and add で ください.

examples.ja: タバコを吸わないでください。
examples.romaji: Tabako wo suwanaide kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please don't smoke.

examples.ja: ここで走らないでください。
examples.romaji: Koko de hashiranaide kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please don't run here.

examples.ja: 大きな声で話さないでください。
examples.romaji: Ookina koe de hanasanaide kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please don't speak loudly.

examples.ja: 写真を撮らないでください。
examples.romaji: Shashin wo toranaide kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please don't take photos.

title: Noun に 戻ります (Noun ni modorimasu)
formation: Noun + に + 戻ります
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the action of returning to a previous state, place, or condition.
longExplanationEn: The Noun に 戻ります grammar point is used to express going back or reverting to a previous state, place, or condition. 戻る (modoru) literally means 'to go back' or 'to revert'. This is often used after a change has occurred, indicating that something is being undone or returned to its original form.

examples.ja: 冷蔵庫に入れると、溶けたアイスクリームは固体に戻ります。
examples.romaji: Reizouko ni ireru to, toketa aisukuriimu wa kotai ni modorimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When put in the refrigerator, the melted ice cream returns to its solid state.

examples.ja: 休暇が終わったら、仕事に戻ります。
examples.romaji: Kyuuka ga owattara, shigoto ni modorimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When the vacation ends, I will return to work.

examples.ja: リモートワークが終わった後、元のオフィスに戻ります。
examples.romaji: Rimooto waaku ga owatta ato, moto no ofisu ni modorimasu.
examples.meaningEn: After remote work is over, we will return to our original office.

examples.ja: 外国で勉強した後、日本に帰ります。
examples.romaji: Gaikoku de benkyou shita ato, Nihon ni kaerimasu.
examples.meaningEn: After studying abroad, I will return to Japan.

title: Verb に 来ます (Verb ni kimasu)
formation: Verb (ます-stem) + に + 来ます
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the purpose of coming to a place; 'come to (do something)'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Verb に きます is used to express the purpose of coming to a place to perform an action. It can be translated as 'come to (do something)' in English. To form this expression, use the verb's ます-stem (i.e., remove ます) followed by に きます.

examples.ja: 友達に会いに来ました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni ai ni kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I came to meet my friend.

examples.ja: 映画を見に来ました。
examples.romaji: Eiga wo mi ni kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I came to watch a movie.

examples.ja: 先生に質問しに来ました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni shitsumon shi ni kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I came to ask the teacher a question.

examples.ja: 買い物をしに来ました。
examples.romaji: Kaimono wo shi ni kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I came to go shopping.

title: Verb に 行きます (Verb ni ikimasu)
formation: Verb (ます-stem) + に + 行きます
shortExplanationEn: To go (somewhere) in order to do an action.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Verb に 行きます is used when expressing that someone is going to a certain place with the purpose of doing a specific action. Form it by taking the verb's ます-stem (for suru-verbs, remove する and add し) and then adding に 行きます.

examples.ja: 公園に遊びに行きます。
examples.romaji: Kouen ni asobi ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the park to play.

examples.ja: 図書館に勉強しに行きます。
examples.romaji: Toshokan ni benkyou shi ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the library to study.

examples.ja: スーパーに買い物に行きます。
examples.romaji: Suupaa ni kaimono ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the supermarket to do some shopping.

examples.ja: 映画館に映画を見に行きます。
examples.romaji: Eigakan ni eiga wo mi ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the movie theater to watch a movie.

title: Verb ましょう (mashou)
formation: Verb (ます-stem) + ましょう
shortExplanationEn: Making a suggestion or invitation; 'let's' or 'shall we'.
longExplanationEn: The ましょう grammar point is used to suggest or invite someone to do something in a polite and friendly way. It can be translated as 'let's' or 'shall we' in English. This grammar point is formed by attaching ましょう to the stem of the verb (i.e. remove ます).

examples.ja: 一緒に映画を見ましょう。
examples.romaji: Issho ni eiga wo mimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's watch a movie together.

examples.ja: 散歩しましょう。
examples.romaji: Sanpo shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's take a walk.

examples.ja: 今晩、食事に行きましょう。
examples.romaji: Konban, shokuji ni ikimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's go out for dinner tonight.

examples.ja: 明日勉強しましょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita benkyou shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's study tomorrow.

title: Verb ましょうか。 (〜mashou ka.)
formation: Verb (ます-stem) + ましょうか
shortExplanationEn: Used to make a polite suggestion or offer help; 'shall we', 'should we', 'do you want me to'.
longExplanationEn: The ましょうか grammar point is used to make a polite suggestion, propose an action, or offer help. It can be translated as 'shall we', 'should we', or 'do you want me to' in English. This formation is used with verbs in their ます-form, replacing ます with ましょう and adding か.

examples.ja: 一緒に映画を見に行きましょうか。
examples.romaji: Issho ni eiga wo mi ni ikimashou ka.
examples.meaningEn: Shall we go see a movie together?

examples.ja: コーヒーをもう一杯飲みましょうか。
examples.romaji: Koohii wo mou ippai nomimashou ka.
examples.meaningEn: Should we have another cup of coffee?

examples.ja: あなたの荷物を持ちましょうか。
examples.romaji: Anata no nimotsu wo mochimashou ka.
examples.meaningEn: Do you want me to carry your luggage?

examples.ja: 窓を開けましょうか。
examples.romaji: Mado wo akemashou ka.
examples.meaningEn: Shall I open the window?

title: Verb ませんか。 (Verb-masenka)
formation: Verb (ます-form) + ませんか
shortExplanationEn: An invitation or suggestion; 'won't you', 'why don't you', 'shall we'.
longExplanationEn: The ませんか grammar point is used for making invitations or suggestions in a polite manner. The verb is conjugated to its ます form and replaced with ませんか. This can be translated as 'won't you', 'why don't you', or 'shall we' in English.

examples.ja: 一緒に映画を見ませんか。
examples.romaji: Issho ni eiga wo mimasenka.
examples.meaningEn: Won't you watch a movie together with me?

examples.ja: 明日のパーティーに参加しませんか。
examples.romaji: Ashita no paatii ni sanka shimasenka.
examples.meaningEn: Why don't you join tomorrow's party?

examples.ja: 週末にピクニックに行きませんか。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu ni pikunikku ni ikimasenka.
examples.meaningEn: Shall we go for a picnic on the weekend?

examples.ja: 一緒に勉強しませんか。
examples.romaji: Issho ni benkyou shimasenka.
examples.meaningEn: How about studying together?

title: Verb ること～ (〜ru koto)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + こと
shortExplanationEn: Express an action or event as a noun; 'the act of', 'doing'.
longExplanationEn: The ること grammar point is used to nominalize or turn a verb into a noun, which represents the action or event itself as a concept. It can be translated as 'the act of' or simply 'doing' in English. This formation is used with verbs in the dictionary (ru-)form.

examples.ja: 泳ぐことが大好きです。
examples.romaji: Oyogu koto ga daisuki desu.
examples.meaningEn: I love swimming.

examples.ja: 日本語を勉強することは楽しいです。
examples.romaji: Nihongo wo benkyou suru koto wa tanoshii desu.
examples.meaningEn: Studying Japanese is fun.

examples.ja: 友達と話すことがストレスを軽減します。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to hanasu koto ga sutoresu wo keigen shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Talking with friends reduces stress.

examples.ja: 彼は歌うことが得意です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa utau koto ga tokui desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is good at singing.

title: Verb る こと が できます (ru koto ga dekimasu)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + ことができます
shortExplanationEn: Express the potential form, or the ability to do something.
longExplanationEn: The 〜ることができます grammar point is used to express that someone is capable of doing something or that something is possible. It is essentially the potential form of the verb. The formation is made by changing the verb to a noun phrase with こと and then adding ができます.

examples.ja: この問題を解くことができます。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai o toku koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I can solve this problem.

examples.ja: 彼は英語を話すことができます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eigo o hanasu koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can speak English.

examples.ja: 私は高い所に登ることができません。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa takai tokoro ni noboru koto ga dekimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I can't climb to high places.

examples.ja: 彼女はピアノを弾くことができます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa piano o hiku koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She can play the piano.

title: Verb る こと ができる (ru koto ga dekiru)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + ことができる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the potential form; 'can', 'be able to', 'is possible'.
longExplanationEn: The る こと ができる grammar point is used to express the potential form of a verb, meaning the ability or possibility to do something. It can be translated as 'can', 'be able to', or 'is possible' in English. This grammar is often used with verbs ending in る, but can also apply to other verbs once they are in the dictionary form.

examples.ja: この問題を解くことができる？
examples.romaji: Kono mondai o toku koto ga dekiru?
examples.meaningEn: Can you solve this problem?

examples.ja: 彼は泳ぐことができます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa oyogu koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can swim.

examples.ja: 日本に行くことができて、とても嬉しいです。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni iku koto ga dekite, totemo ureshii desu.
examples.meaningEn: I'm very happy that I can go to Japan.

examples.ja: 彼女はピアノを弾くことができます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa piano o hiku koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She can play the piano.

title: Verb る の～ (Verb + ru + no~)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + の (casual)
shortExplanationEn: Used to express the purpose or reason in casual speech, or nominalize the verb with の.
longExplanationEn: The る の～ grammar point is a casual spoken pattern that can nominalize a verb or explain a purpose/reason by adding の. It's often seen in everyday conversation, and can be used for nuance such as giving an explanation. It's similar to ること, but is more colloquial or explanatory in tone.

examples.ja: 買い物をするために、銀行でお金をおろすの。
examples.romaji: Kaimono o suru tame ni, ginkou de okane o orosu no.
examples.meaningEn: I'm withdrawing money at the bank so I can go shopping.

examples.ja: 友達にプレゼントをあげるので、ケーキを焼くの。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni purezento o ageru node, keeki o yaku no.
examples.meaningEn: I'm baking a cake because I'm giving my friend a present.

examples.ja: ダイエットを始めるので、ジムに入会するの。
examples.romaji: Daietto o hajimeru node, jimu ni nyuukai suru no.
examples.meaningEn: I’m joining a gym since I'm starting a diet.

examples.ja: 軽い食事がいいので、サラダを作るの。
examples.romaji: Karui shokuji ga ii node, sarada o tsukuru no.
examples.meaningEn: I'm making a salad because I want a light meal.

title: Verb る 前に (ru mae ni)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + 前に
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'before' in a sense of an action must be done before another action or event.
longExplanationEn: The る前に (ru mae ni) grammar point is used to indicate that an action (in the dictionary form) should be completed before another action occurs or before a specific event happens.

examples.ja: 出かける前に、部屋を掃除してください。
examples.romaji: Dekakeru mae ni, heya o souji shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please clean the room before leaving.

examples.ja: 寝る前に、歯を磨きます。
examples.romaji: Neru mae ni, ha o migakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I brush my teeth before going to bed.

examples.ja: 雨が降る前に、洗濯物を取り込むこと。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu mae ni, sentakumono o torikomu koto.
examples.meaningEn: Take in the laundry before it rains.

examples.ja: 会議が始まる前に、資料を準備しましょう。
examples.romaji: Kaigi ga hajimaru mae ni, shiryou o junbi shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's prepare the documents before the meeting starts.

title: あまり～ありません (amari ~ arimasen)
formation: あまり + Verb-ないform + ありません / あまり + い-Adjective-く ありません / あまり + な-Adjective・Noun じゃありません
shortExplanationEn: Express 'not very', 'not much', or 'not really' when describing a negative state or situation.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point あまり～ありません is used to describe a negative state or condition and indicate that it's not very, not much, or not really. It is commonly used with verbs in the negative form and can be used with い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼はあまり疲れていません。
examples.romaji: Kare wa amari tsukareteimasen.
examples.meaningEn: He is not really tired.

examples.ja: 彼女はあまり早くないですよ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa amari hayakunai desu yo.
examples.meaningEn: She is not very fast.

examples.ja: 私はあまり勉強しないです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa amari benkyou shinai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I don't study very much.

examples.ja: あまりお金がありません。
examples.romaji: Amari okane ga arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I don't have much money.

title: あまり～ないです (amari ~ nai desu)
formation: あまり + Verb-ないform + です / あまり + い-Adjective-くない + です / あまり + な-Adjective-じゃない + です
shortExplanationEn: Express 'not really' or 'not very'; used to soften a negative statement.
longExplanationEn: The あまり～ないです grammar point is used to express that something is 'not really' or 'not very' to a certain extent. It is often used to soften a negative statement. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, and な-adjectives in their negative forms.

examples.ja: あまり食べないです。
examples.romaji: Amari tabenai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I don't really eat much.

examples.ja: この映画はあまり面白くないです。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa amari omoshirokunai desu.
examples.meaningEn: This movie is not really interesting.

examples.ja: 彼はあまり親切じゃないです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa amari shinsetsu ja nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is not very kind.

examples.ja: 私はあまり日本語が上手じゃないです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa amari nihongo ga jouzu ja nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I am not very good at Japanese.

title: ～あります (〜arimasu)
formation: Object + が + あります
shortExplanationEn: Indicate possession or existence of inanimate objects; 'there is', 'there are'.
longExplanationEn: The あります grammar point is used to indicate the possession or existence of inanimate objects. It can be translated as 'there is' or 'there are' in English. It is different from います, which is used for people and most living creatures. あります is commonly used with particle が to mark the object.

examples.ja: 教室に机があります。
examples.romaji: Kyoushitsu ni tsukue ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a desk in the classroom.

examples.ja: この公園にたくさんの花があります。
examples.romaji: Kono kouen ni takusan no hana ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There are many flowers in this park.

examples.ja: 駅の近くにコンビニがあります。
examples.romaji: Eki no chikaku ni konbini ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a convenience store near the station.

examples.ja: 家の中に古いテレビがあります。
examples.romaji: Ie no naka ni furui terebi ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is an old TV inside the house.

title: ～いかがですか。 (〜ikaga desu ka.)
formation: Noun + は + いかがですか / (Verb-て form + は) いかがですか
shortExplanationEn: A polite way to ask someone's thoughts, opinions, or preferences; 'how about', 'how is'.
longExplanationEn: The ～いかがですか grammar point is used to politely ask someone's thoughts, opinions, or preferences. It can be translated as 'how about' or 'how is' in English. The formation is quite straightforward when used with verbs or nouns.

examples.ja: お茶はいかがですか？
examples.romaji: Ocha wa ikaga desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How about some tea?

examples.ja: このドレスはいかがですか？
examples.romaji: Kono doresu wa ikaga desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How about this dress?

examples.ja: ケーキを食べてはいかがですか？
examples.romaji: Keeki wo tabete wa ikaga desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How about eating some cake?

examples.ja: 映画を見てはいかがですか？
examples.romaji: Eiga wo mite wa ikaga desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How about watching a movie?

title: いくつ～ (ikutsu~)
formation: いくつ + Noun (optional)
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'how many' or ask for the number of something.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point いくつ is used when asking or expressing 'how many' of something there are, or to indicate an unspecified number. In English, it can be translated as 'how many' or 'several'.

examples.ja: リンゴはいくつありますか？
examples.romaji: Ringo wa ikutsu arimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How many apples are there?

examples.ja: いくつかの問題を解決しなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Ikutsu ka no mondai o kaiketsu shinakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I have to solve several problems.

examples.ja: あなたはいくつの言語を話せますか？
examples.romaji: Anata wa ikutsu no gengo o hanasemasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How many languages can you speak?

examples.ja: パーティーにはいくつかの飲み物があります。
examples.romaji: Paatii ni wa ikutsu ka no nomimono ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There are several drinks at the party.

title: いつか～ (itsuka～)
formation: いつか + sentence
shortExplanationEn: Indicates an unspecified point in time; 'someday', 'one day', 'at some point'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point いつか is used to indicate an unspecified point in time, meaning 'someday', 'one day', or 'at some point' in the future or the past. It can be used in sentences to express hopes, dreams, or possibilities.

examples.ja: いつか日本に行きたいです。
examples.romaji: Itsuka Nihon ni ikitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to go to Japan someday.

examples.ja: 彼はいつか有名な作家になるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kare wa itsuka yuumei na sakka ni naru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: He will become a famous writer someday.

examples.ja: いつかあの山を登りたいと思っています。
examples.romaji: Itsuka ano yama wo noboritai to omotte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think I want to climb that mountain someday.

examples.ja: 彼女はいつか夢を叶えることができると信じています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa itsuka yume wo kanaeru koto ga dekiru to shinjiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I believe she can achieve her dream someday.

title: いつでも～ (itsudemo～)
formation: いつでも + (Verb)
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'anytime', 'always', or 'whenever'.
longExplanationEn: The いつでも grammar point is used to express that something occurs, can occur, or is allowed to occur anytime, always, or whenever. It emphasizes the lack of restrictions related to time for a certain action or event.

examples.ja: いつでも遊びに来てください。
examples.romaji: Itsudemo asobi ni kite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please come to play anytime.

examples.ja: 彼女はいつでも笑顔です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa itsudemo egao desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is always smiling.

examples.ja: いつでも電話で連絡できます。
examples.romaji: Itsudemo denwa de renraku dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: You can contact me by phone anytime.

examples.ja: この店はいつでも混んでいます。
examples.romaji: Kono mise wa itsudemo konde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: This store is always crowded.

title: いつも～ (itsumo～)
formation: いつも + Verb / Adjective / Noun
shortExplanationEn: Expressing 'always' or 'usually' in a sentence.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point いつも (itsumo) is used to express the meaning of 'always' or 'usually' when describing a habitual action or situation. It is often used with the present tense or past tense to show that something is done on a regular basis or has been happening consistently.

examples.ja: 彼はいつも遅刻します。
examples.romaji: Kare wa itsumo chikoku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is always late.

examples.ja: 私はいつも優しい犬が好きです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa itsumo yasashii inu ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: I always like gentle dogs.

examples.ja: 彼女はいつも静かな場所で勉強します。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa itsumo shizuka na basho de benkyou shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She always studies in a quiet place.

examples.ja: いつも朝ご飯を食べます。
examples.romaji: Itsumo asagohan wo tabemasu.
examples.meaningEn: I always eat breakfast.

title: ～て います (～te imasu)
formation: Verb て-form + います
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the present continuous or ongoing action; 'to be doing', 'currently doing'.
longExplanationEn: The ～て います grammar pattern is used to express that an action is currently happening or ongoing. It can be translated as 'to be doing' or 'currently doing' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is a る-verb, う-verb, or an irregular verb, but all use the verb’s て-form + います.

examples.ja: 今、音楽を聞いています。
examples.romaji: Ima, ongaku wo kiite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am listening to music right now.

examples.ja: 父は新聞を読んでいます。
examples.romaji: Chichi wa shinbun wo yonde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: My father is reading a newspaper.

examples.ja: 彼女は英語を勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa eigo wo benkyou shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is studying English.

examples.ja: 子供たちは公園で遊んでいます。
examples.romaji: Kodomotachi wa kouen de asonde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: The children are playing in the park.

title: ～が (〜ga)
formation: Noun + が / い-Adjective + が / な-Adjective + が / Verb + が
shortExplanationEn: Particle indicating the subject of a sentence or expressing 'but'.
longExplanationEn: The ～が grammar point is a particle that is used to indicate the subject of a sentence or to express a contrast similar to 'but' in English. It has various functions depending on the context.

examples.ja: 私が学生です。
examples.romaji: Watashi ga gakusei desu.
examples.meaningEn: I am a student.

examples.ja: このりんごが美味しい。
examples.romaji: Kono ringo ga oishii.
examples.meaningEn: This apple is delicious.

examples.ja: 彼は頭がいいが、性格が悪い。
examples.romaji: Kare wa atama ga ii ga, seikaku ga warui.
examples.meaningEn: He is smart but has a bad personality.

examples.ja: 走りたいが、足が痛い。
examples.romaji: Hashiritai ga, ashi ga itai.
examples.meaningEn: I want to run, but my leg hurts.

title: ～が、～ (〜ga, 〜)
formation: Sentence 1 + が、+ Sentence 2
shortExplanationEn: Express a contrast between two sentences; 'but', 'however'.
longExplanationEn: The ～が、～ grammar point is used to express a contrast or contradiction between two sentences or clauses. It can be translated as 'but' or 'however' in English. This grammar point is slightly more formal than けど and is used to show a contrast in the situation.

examples.ja: デザートは美味しいが、カロリーが高い。
examples.romaji: Dezaato wa oishii ga, karorii ga takai.
examples.meaningEn: The dessert is delicious, but it is high in calories.

examples.ja: 彼は頭がいいが、少し怠け者だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa atama ga ii ga, sukoshi namakemono da.
examples.meaningEn: He is smart, but a bit lazy.

examples.ja: 会社は利益を上げたが、従業員の給与は上がらなかった。
examples.romaji: Kaisha wa rieki wo ageta ga, juugyouin no kyuuyo wa agaranakatta.
examples.meaningEn: The company made a profit, however, the employees' salaries did not increase.

examples.ja: 外国語を勉強することが楽しいが、時々難しい。
examples.romaji: Gaikokugo wo benkyou suru koto ga tanoshii ga, tokidoki muzukashii.
examples.meaningEn: Studying foreign languages is fun, but sometimes difficult.

title: ～から、～ (〜kara、～)
formation: Verb-casual + から / い-Adjective + から / な-Adjective + だから / Noun + だから
shortExplanationEn: Expresses reason or cause; 'because', 'since', 'so'.
longExplanationEn: The ～から grammar point is used to indicate the reason or cause for something. It can be translated as 'because', 'since', or 'so' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 明日は試験があるから、今晩勉強しなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa shiken ga aru kara, konban benkyou shinakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Because I have an exam tomorrow, I must study tonight.

examples.ja: この部屋は狭いから、もっと大きい部屋を探しています。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa semai kara, motto ookii heya wo sagashiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Since this room is small, I am looking for a bigger one.

examples.ja: 彼は友達だから、手伝ってくれるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tomodachi dakara, tetsudatte kureru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: Since he is my friend, he will probably help me.

examples.ja: 雨が降ったから、外に出られません。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futta kara, soto ni deraremasen.
examples.meaningEn: Because it rained, I can't go outside.

title: ～からです (〜kara desu)
formation: Verb-casual + からです / い-Adjective + からです / な-Adjective + だからです / Noun + だからです
shortExplanationEn: Express the reason or cause; 'because', 'since', 'due to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～からです grammar point is used to state the reason or cause of something, often as a short concluding statement. It can be translated as 'because', 'since', or 'due to' in English. The formation can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, or nouns, but be careful with な-adjectives and nouns: typically, it's [Noun/な-adjective] + だからです.

examples.ja: 寒いからです。コートを着ています。
examples.romaji: Samui kara desu. Kooto wo kiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: It’s because it’s cold. That’s why I’m wearing a coat.

examples.ja: この店は安いからです。よく来ます。
examples.romaji: Kono mise wa yasui kara desu. Yoku kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: It’s because this store is cheap. That’s why I come often.

examples.ja: 彼は親切だからです。みんなに好かれています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shinsetsu dakara desu. Minna ni suka-rete imasu.
examples.meaningEn: It’s because he’s kind. Everyone likes him.

examples.ja: 彼女は学生だからです。図書館で勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa gakusei dakara desu. Toshokan de benkyou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: It’s because she’s a student. She studies at the library.

title: ～から もらいます (〜kara moraimasu)
formation: Noun + から + (Verb-て form) + もらいます
shortExplanationEn: To receive something or get someone to do something.
longExplanationEn: The ～からもらいます grammar point is used to express receiving something from someone or getting someone to do something for you. In English, it can be translated as 'to receive from' or 'to get someone to do something'.

examples.ja: 友達からプレゼントをもらいました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi kara purezento wo moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I received a present from my friend.

examples.ja: 先生に宿題を渡してもらいました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni shukudai wo watashite moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I had the teacher hand me the homework.

examples.ja: お母さんに料理を作ってもらいました。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ni ryouri wo tsukutte moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I got my mom to cook for me.

examples.ja: 兄に車を貸してもらいました。
examples.romaji: Ani ni kuruma wo kashite moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I got my older brother to lend me his car.

title: ～が 私に くれます (〜ga watashi ni kuremasu)
formation: Person + が + (Thing or Action) + 私に + くれます
shortExplanationEn: Used to express that someone gives a gift or favor to 'me'; 'someone gives me'.
longExplanationEn: The ～が私にくれます grammar point is used to express that someone gives 'me' something or does something for me. It can be translated as 'someone gives me' or 'someone does something for me' in English. Typically, the giver is marked by が, and the receiver is 私に (to me).

examples.ja: 彼がプレゼントを私にくれました。
examples.romaji: Kare ga purezento wo watashi ni kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: He gave me a present.

examples.ja: 友達が宿題を手伝って私にくれました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga shukudai wo tetsudatte watashi ni kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: My friend helped me with my homework.

examples.ja: お母さんがお弁当を作って私にくれました。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ga obentou wo tsukutte watashi ni kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: My mom made me a lunch box.

examples.ja: 先生がアドバイスを私にくれました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga adobaisu wo watashi ni kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher gave me advice.

title: ～けど、～ (〜kedo、～)
formation: Verb-casual + けど / い-adjective + けど / な-adjective + だけど / Noun + だけど
shortExplanationEn: Used to connect two clauses; 'but', 'however', 'although'
longExplanationEn: The ～けど grammar point is used to connect two clauses, often to show a contrast or to introduce a different point of view. It can be translated as 'but', 'however', or 'although' in English. It's more casual than けれど or が.

examples.ja: 授業に出たけど、まだ宿題をしないといけない。
examples.romaji: Jugyou ni deta kedo, mada shukudai wo shinai to ikenai.
examples.meaningEn: I attended the class, but I still have to do my homework.

examples.ja: 昨日寒かったけど、今日は暖かいです。
examples.romaji: Kinou samukatta kedo, kyou wa atatakai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Yesterday was cold, but today is warm.

examples.ja: 彼は親切だけど、ちょっと怖い。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shinsetsu da kedo, chotto kowai.
examples.meaningEn: He is kind, but a little scary.

examples.ja: これはおいしそうだけど、高すぎる。
examples.romaji: Kore wa oishisou da kedo, takasugiru.
examples.meaningEn: This looks delicious, but it is too expensive.

title: ～けれど、～ (〜keredo、～)
formation: Verb-casual + けれど / い-Adjective + けれど / な-Adjective + だけれど / Noun + だけれど
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a contrast or contradiction; 'but', 'however', 'even though'.
longExplanationEn: The ～けれど grammar point is used to express a contrast or contradiction between two statements. It can be translated as 'but', 'however', or 'even though' in English. This grammar point is slightly more formal than けど.

examples.ja: 今日は寒いけれど、散歩に行きます。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa samui keredo, sanpo ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even though it's cold today, I'll go for a walk.

examples.ja: 彼は親切けれど、少し怖いです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shinsetsu keredo, sukoshi kowai desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is kind, but a little scary.

examples.ja: この部屋は狭いけれど、明るいです。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa semai keredo, akarui desu.
examples.meaningEn: This room is small, but it's bright.

examples.ja: 彼女は病気だけれど、毎日働いています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa byouki dakeredo, mainichi hataraite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is sick, but she works every day.

title: こちら～ (kochira～)
formation: こちら / こちら + の + Person/Thing
shortExplanationEn: Refers to a person, place or thing; 'this', 'this one', 'this way', 'here'.
longExplanationEn: The こちら grammar point is used to refer to a person, place, or thing that is closer to the speaker, or to show politeness when referring to someone. It can mean 'this', 'this one', 'this way', or 'here'.

examples.ja: こちらは田中さんです。
examples.romaji: Kochira wa Tanaka-san desu.
examples.meaningEn: This is Mr./Ms. Tanaka.

examples.ja: こちらのレストランは美味しいです。
examples.romaji: Kochira no resutoran wa oishii desu.
examples.meaningEn: This restaurant is delicious.

examples.ja: こちらへどうぞ。
examples.romaji: Kochira e douzo.
examples.meaningEn: Please come this way.

examples.ja: こちらの方がいいですか、それともあちらの方がいいですか？
examples.romaji: Kochira no hou ga ii desu ka, sore to mo achira no hou ga ii desu ka?
