examples.meaningEn: Is this one better, or is that one better?

title: さっき～ (sakki～)
formation: さっき + verb / さっき + phrase
shortExplanationEn: Refers to a short time ago or just now.
longExplanationEn: The word さっき (sakki) is an adverb used to express that something happened or was done a short time ago or just now. It can be combined with verbs or other phrases to convey that meaning.

examples.ja: さっき電話がありました。
examples.romaji: Sakki denwa ga arimashita.
examples.meaningEn: There was a phone call just now.

examples.ja: さっき部屋を掃除しました。
examples.romaji: Sakki heya wo souji shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I cleaned the room a short while ago.

examples.ja: さっき彼に会いました。
examples.romaji: Sakki kare ni aimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I met him just now.

examples.ja: さっきのニュースを見ましたか？
examples.romaji: Sakki no nyuusu wo mimashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: Did you watch the news a short time ago?

title: すぐに～ (sugu ni～)
formation: すぐに + Verb/Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Immediately or right away; used to express promptness or quickness.
longExplanationEn: The すぐに～ grammar point is used to express an action happening immediately or right away. It often indicates promptness or quickness and can be used with both verbs and adjectives.

examples.ja: 友達が来たら、すぐに電話をかけてください。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga kitara, sugu ni denwa wo kakete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: When my friend arrives, please call me right away.

examples.ja: そのニュースを聞いたら、すぐに会社に行きました。
examples.romaji: Sono nyuusu wo kiitara, sugu ni kaisha ni ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Upon hearing that news, I went to the company immediately.

examples.ja: 彼女はすぐに泣き出した。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa sugu ni nakidashita.
examples.meaningEn: She started crying right away.

examples.ja: 教室に入ると、先生はすぐに授業を始めた。
examples.romaji: Kyoushitsu ni hairu to, sensei wa sugu ni jugyou wo hajimeta.
examples.meaningEn: As soon as I entered the classroom, the teacher started the lesson immediately.

title: ぜんぜん～ (zenzen～)
formation: ぜんぜん + [Negative verb/adjective]
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'not at all' or 'completely' in negative sentences.
longExplanationEn: The ぜんぜん grammar point is used to emphasize a negative statement and can be translated as 'not at all' or 'completely (in negative sense)'. It is commonly used with negative verbs or negative forms of adjectives, placed before the negative part.

examples.ja: 彼はぜんぜん話さない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa zenzen hanasanai.
examples.meaningEn: He doesn't talk at all.

examples.ja: この部屋はぜんぜん暗くない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa zenzen kurakunai.
examples.meaningEn: This room is not dark at all.

examples.ja: 彼女はぜんぜん疲れていない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa zenzen tsukarete inai.
examples.meaningEn: She is not tired at all.

examples.ja: その映画はぜんぜん面白くなかった。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga wa zenzen omoshirokunakatta.
examples.meaningEn: That movie was not interesting at all.

title: そして、～ (soshite、～)
formation: Sentence 1 + そして + Sentence 2
shortExplanationEn: Used to connect two sentences; 'and then', 'and'.
longExplanationEn: The そして grammar point is used to connect two sentences or ideas, expressing a sequence of events or actions. It is commonly translated to 'and then' or simply 'and' in English.

examples.ja: 朝起きて、そして宿題をしました。
examples.romaji: Asa okite, soshite shukudai wo shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I woke up in the morning and then did my homework.

examples.ja: コーヒーを飲んで、そして新聞を読みました。
examples.romaji: Koohii wo nonde, soshite shinbun wo yomimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I drank coffee and then read the newspaper.

examples.ja: 家を出発しました。そして、駅へ行きました。
examples.romaji: Ie wo shuppatsu shimashita. Soshite, eki e ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I left my house and then went to the station.

examples.ja: 彼はゲームをした。そして、テレビを見ました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa geemu wo shita. Soshite, terebi wo mimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He played games and then watched TV.

title: そちら～ (sochira～)
formation: そちら [alone] / そちら + の + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a direction or person in moderate distance; 'that way', 'that person'.
longExplanationEn: The そちら grammar point is used to refer to a direction or a person that is moderate distance from both the speaker and listener, often closer to the listener. It can be translated as 'that way', 'that person', or 'that side'. This is distinct from あちら, used for things far from both speaker and listener.

examples.ja: そちらのレストランはどうですか？
examples.romaji: Sochira no resutoran wa dou desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How is that restaurant over there?

examples.ja: そちらの方、お名前を教えていただけますか？
examples.romaji: Sochira no kata, onamae o oshiete itadakemasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Could you tell me the name of that person over there?

examples.ja: そちらに行く途中で駅があります。
examples.romaji: Sochira ni iku tochuu de eki ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a station on the way to that direction.

examples.ja: そちらのかばん、私のと似ていますね。
examples.romaji: Sochira no kaban, watashi no to niteimasu ne.
examples.meaningEn: That bag over there looks similar to mine.

title: それから、～ (sorekara、～)
formation: Clause 1 + それから + Clause 2
shortExplanationEn: Meaning 'after that', 'and then' or 'then', indicating sequence of events.
longExplanationEn: The それから grammar point is used to describe the sequence of events or actions, indicating that one action or event takes place after another. It can be translated as 'after that', 'and then', or 'then' in English. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, and nouns but should be placed between two clauses.

examples.ja: コーヒーを飲んで、それから家を出ました。
examples.romaji: Koohii o nonde, sorekara ie o demashita.
examples.meaningEn: I drank coffee, and then I left the house.

examples.ja: 買い物をして、それから映画を見に行きました。
examples.romaji: Kaimono o shite, sorekara eiga o mini ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I went shopping, and then I went to watch a movie.

examples.ja: 彼女は勉強をして、それから寝ました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa benkyou o shite, sorekara nemashita.
examples.meaningEn: She studied, and then she went to sleep.

examples.ja: パーティーに行って、それから友達と話しました。
examples.romaji: Paatii ni itte, sorekara tomodachi to hanashimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I went to the party, and then I talked with my friends.

title: だいたい〜 (daitai〜)
formation: だいたい + Number/Amount/Percentage
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'roughly', 'approximately', or 'almost'.
longExplanationEn: The だいたい〜 (daitai〜) grammar point is used to express a rough estimate or generalization, meaning 'roughly', 'approximately', or 'almost' in English. It can be used before numbers, amounts, or percentages to show that the value is not exact.

examples.ja: だいたい１００人がパーティーに参加しました。
examples.romaji: Daitai hyaku-nin ga paatii ni sanka shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Approximately 100 people attended the party.

examples.ja: 彼女はだいたい２時間でその仕事を終えます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa daitai ni-jikan de sono shigoto o oemasu.
examples.meaningEn: She finishes that work in roughly two hours.

examples.ja: だいたい８０％の生徒が試験に合格しました。
examples.romaji: Daitai hachijuu-paasento no seito ga shiken ni goukaku shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: About 80% of the students passed the exam.

examples.ja: 今日はだいたい３０度の暑さです。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa daitai sanjuu-do no atsusa desu.
examples.meaningEn: Today is almost 30 degrees hot.

title: たいてい～ (taitei～)
formation: たいてい + Verb/い-Adjective/な-Adjective/Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used for expressing 'usually', 'mostly', or 'generally'.
longExplanationEn: The たいてい grammar point is used to describe a situation that occurs frequently or under normal circumstances. It can be translated as 'usually', 'mostly', or 'generally' in English. It is often used with verbs, but can also be used with adjectives and nouns.

examples.ja: たいてい朝８時に起きます。
examples.romaji: Taitei asa hachi-ji ni okimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I usually wake up at 8 o'clock in the morning.

examples.ja: 彼はたいてい遅刻します。
examples.romaji: Kare wa taitei chikoku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is usually late.

examples.ja: この店の商品はたいてい安いです。
examples.romaji: Kono mise no shouhin wa taitei yasui desu.
examples.meaningEn: The items at this store are usually cheap.

examples.ja: たいていの人はその映画が好きです。
examples.romaji: Taitei no hito wa sono eiga ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: Most people like that movie.

title: だから、～ (dakara、～)
formation: Noun + だから / な-Adjective + だから
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a reason or cause; 'because', 'so', 'therefore'.
longExplanationEn: The だから grammar point is used to connect two sentences and express a reason or cause for the second sentence. It is typically used with nouns or な-adjectives, and can be translated as 'because', 'so', or 'therefore' in English. For verbs/い-adjectives, the form is usually ～から (not だから).

examples.ja: 雨だから、傘を持って行きましょう。
examples.romaji: Ame dakara, kasa wo motte ikimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Because it's raining, let's bring an umbrella.

examples.ja: このケーキは有名だから、みんなが食べたいです。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa yuumei dakara, minna ga tabetai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Because this cake is famous, everyone wants to eat it.

examples.ja: 彼は病気だから、学校に行けません。
examples.romaji: Kare wa byouki dakara, gakkou ni ikemasen.
examples.meaningEn: Because he is sick, he cannot go to school.

examples.ja: 彼女は学生だから、このレストランで割引があります。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa gakusei dakara, kono resutoran de waribiki ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Because she is a student, there is a discount at this restaurant.

title: ～たり、～たり します (〜tari, 〜tari shimasu)
formation: Verb-casual-past + たり + Verb-casual-past + たり + します
shortExplanationEn: Used to list multiple actions or states; 'do things like', 'and'.
longExplanationEn: The ～たり、～たり します grammar point is used to list multiple actions or states without fully enumerating them. It can imply that there are more items in the list and can be translated as 'do things like', 'and', 'among other things', or 'and so on' in English. It is formed by attaching たり to the casual past tense of the verb, followed by します.

examples.ja: 休日には、映画を見たり、友達と遊んだりします。
examples.romaji: Kyuujitsu ni wa, eiga wo mitari, tomodachi to asondari shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: On holidays, I do things like watch movies and hang out with friends.

examples.ja: 彼は歌ったり、踊ったり、ピアノを弾いたりします。
examples.romaji: Kare wa utattari, odottari, piano wo hikitari shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He sings, dances, plays the piano, among other things.

examples.ja: このレストランでは、寿司を食べたり、刺身を食べたりします。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran de wa, sushi wo tabetari, sashimi wo tabetari shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: At this restaurant, you can eat sushi, sashimi, and so on.

examples.ja: 彼女は毎朝、ジョギングしたり、ヨガをしたりします。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa maiasa, joggingu shitari, yoga wo shitari shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Every morning, she does things like jogging and yoga.

title: だれ～ (dare～)
formation: だれ + appropriate particle (が, は or を)
shortExplanationEn: Asking 'who' in a sentence, used to inquire about someone's identity or to clarify who is being referred to.
longExplanationEn: The だれ〜 grammar point is used to ask about the identity of someone in a sentence. It can be translated as 'who' or 'whom' in English. It is often used with the particle が, は, or を, but can also be combined with other particles depending on the context.

examples.ja: だれがその本を買いましたか。
examples.romaji: Dare ga sono hon o kaimashita ka.
examples.meaningEn: Who bought that book?

examples.ja: だれを待っていますか。
examples.romaji: Dare o matteimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: Whom are you waiting for?

examples.ja: だれがそのリンゴを食べましたか。
examples.romaji: Dare ga sono ringo o tabemashita ka.
examples.meaningEn: Who ate that apple?

examples.ja: だれが鍵を持っていますか。
examples.romaji: Dare ga kagi o motteimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: Who has the key?

title: だれか〜 (dareka〜)
formation: だれか + Verb, だれか + Adjective, だれか + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Indicates 'someone' or something done or possessed by 'someone'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point だれか〜 is used to suggest that something is done by, possessed by, or related to 'someone', usually when the actual person is unidentified or not important. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, or nouns.

examples.ja: だれかがドアをノックしました。
examples.romaji: Dareka ga doa wo nokku shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Someone knocked on the door.

examples.ja: だれかが私の傘を盗みました。
examples.romaji: Dareka ga watashi no kasa wo nusumimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Someone stole my umbrella.

examples.ja: だれか助けてください。
examples.romaji: Dareka tasukete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Someone, please help.

examples.ja: だれかのせいで、電気がきれました。
examples.romaji: Dareka no sei de, denki ga kiremashita.
examples.meaningEn: Because of someone, the electricity went out.

title: だれでも～ (dare demo～)
formation: だれでも + Verb/Adjective/Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'anyone' or 'everyone'; 'no matter who'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point だれでも is used to emphasize that something applies to everyone or anyone, regardless of who they are. It can be translated as 'anyone', 'everyone', or 'no matter who' in English. It is used in sentences to show that a certain situation, characteristic, or condition applies to all people.

examples.ja: だれでもこのゲームができます。
examples.romaji: Dare demo kono geemu ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Anyone can play this game.

examples.ja: だれでも疲れることがあります。
examples.romaji: Dare demo tsukareru koto ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Everyone gets tired sometimes.

examples.ja: だれでも無料でこのイベントに参加できます。
examples.romaji: Dare demo muryou de kono ibento ni sanka dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Anyone can participate in this event for free.

examples.ja: だれでもこの町の観光スポットを楽しむことができます。
examples.romaji: Dare demo kono machi no kankou supotto wo tanoshimu koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: No matter who you are, you can enjoy the sightseeing spots in this town.

title: だれも～ないです (dare mo ~ nai desu)
formation: だれも + Verb-negative form (ないです), だれも + い-Adjective-negative form (ないです), だれも + な-Adjective-negative form (じゃないです)
shortExplanationEn: Express 'nobody' or 'no one' in a negative sentence.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point だれも～ないです is used to express the idea of 'nobody' or 'no one' in a negative sentence. だれも is followed by the negative form of a verb or adjective, often using the polite ないです form.

examples.ja: だれも知らないです。
examples.romaji: Daremo shiranai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Nobody knows.

examples.ja: だれも来ないです。
examples.romaji: Daremo konai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Nobody is coming.

examples.ja: 彼女はだれも好きじゃないです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa daremo suki ja nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: She doesn't like anyone.

examples.ja: ここにはだれもいないです。
examples.romaji: Koko ni wa daremo inai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There is nobody here.

title: だれも～ません (daremo ~masen)
formation: だれも + Verb-negative (ません)
shortExplanationEn: Express 'nobody' or 'no one' in negative sentences.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point だれも is used in combination with a negative form of the verb, usually ません, to express the idea of 'nobody' or 'no one' doing something. It is used with questions or statements and can be translated as 'nobody' or 'no one' in English.

examples.ja: だれも秘密を知りません。
examples.romaji: Daremo himitsu wo shirimasen.
examples.meaningEn: No one knows the secret.

examples.ja: だれもその質問に答えませんでした。
examples.romaji: Daremo sono shitsumon ni kotaemasen deshita.
examples.meaningEn: Nobody answered that question.

examples.ja: だれも彼を助けてあげません。
examples.romaji: Daremo kare wo tasukete agemasen.
examples.meaningEn: Nobody will help him.

examples.ja: だれもその映画を見たがりません。
examples.romaji: Daremo sono eiga wo mitagarimasen.
examples.meaningEn: No one wants to watch that movie.

title: ～どう しますか。 (～dou shimasu ka.)
formation: Situation + どうしますか
shortExplanationEn: Asking for someone's opinion or decision; 'what will you do?', 'how will you handle it?'.
longExplanationEn: The ～どうしますか grammar point is used to ask for someone's opinion, decision, or course of action in a specific situation. It can be translated as 'what will you do?' or 'how will you handle it?' in English. This grammar point is formed by attaching どうしますか to the end of a sentence.

examples.ja: 雨が降っているので、どうしますか？
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte iru node, dou shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: It's raining, so what will you do?

examples.ja: この問題について、どうしますか？
examples.romaji: Kono mondai ni tsuite, dou shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What are you going to do about this issue?

examples.ja: 彼が来なかったら、どうしますか？
examples.romaji: Kare ga konakattara, dou shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: If he doesn't come, what will you do?

examples.ja: 進路を決める時、どうしますか？
examples.romaji: Shinro wo kimeru toki, dou shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What will you do when choosing your career path?

title: ～どうですか。 (〜dou desu ka.)
formation: Noun + は/が + どうですか / Verb-casual + の + どうですか / い-Adjective + どうですか / な-Adjective + な + どうですか
shortExplanationEn: Asking for opinions or suggestions; 'how about', 'what do you think of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～どうですか grammar point is used to ask for someone's opinion or suggestion on something. It can be translated as 'how about' or 'what do you think of' in English. This expression is neither casual nor super-formal, and can be used in various situations.

examples.ja: この映画はどうですか？
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa dou desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How about this movie? / What do you think of this movie?

examples.ja: 晩ご飯に寿司を食べるのはどうですか？
examples.romaji: Bangohan ni sushi wo taberu no wa dou desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How about eating sushi for dinner?

examples.ja: この部屋は広いと思いますが、どうですか？
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa hiroi to omoimasu ga, dou desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: I think this room is spacious, what do you think?

examples.ja: 新しい会社の要件が厳しいですね。鈴木さん、転職するならどうですか？
examples.romaji: Atarashii kaisha no youken ga kibishii desu ne. Suzuki-san, tenshoku suru nara dou desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: The new company's requirements are strict, aren't they? Suzuki-san, how about changing jobs if that's the case?

title: どうやって～ (douyatte～)
formation: どうやって + Verb-casual
shortExplanationEn: Ask 'how' something is done or how to do something.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point どうやって is used to ask how something is done or how to do something, and is translated as 'how' in English. It is often used in combination with verbs to inquire about the method or process someone goes through to complete a task or action.

examples.ja: どうやってお寿司を作りますか？
examples.romaji: Douyatte o-sushi wo tsukurimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How do you make sushi?

examples.ja: どうやって日本語を勉強しましたか？
examples.romaji: Douyatte nihongo wo benkyou shimashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: How did you study Japanese?

examples.ja: どうやってこの問題を解決しますか？
examples.romaji: Douyatte kono mondai wo kaiketsu shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How do you solve this problem?

examples.ja: どうやって駅に行きますか？
examples.romaji: Douyatte eki ni ikimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How do you go to the station?

title: ～どう 言いますか。 (〜dou iimasu ka.)
formation: Word/Phrase + は [language]でどう言いますか / Word/Phrase + をどう言いますか
shortExplanationEn: Asking for the way to say something; 'how do you say'.
longExplanationEn: The ～どう言いますか grammar point is used to ask someone how to express something in a particular language or the appropriate way to say it. It can be translated as 'how do you say' in English.

examples.ja: 「ありがとう」は英語でどう言いますか。
examples.romaji: 「Arigatou」wa eigo de dou iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How do you say 'thank you' in English?

examples.ja: この言葉を日本語でどう言いますか。
examples.romaji: Kono kotoba wo nihongo de dou iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How do you say this word in Japanese?

examples.ja: 「すみません」はフランス語でどう言いますか。
examples.romaji: 「Sumimasen」wa furansugo de dou iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How do you say 'excuse me' in French?

examples.ja: その表現をスペイン語でどう言いますか。
examples.romaji: Sono hyougen wo supeingo de dou iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How do you say that expression in Spanish?

title: ～とき (〜toki)
formation: Verb-casual + とき / い-Adjective + とき / な-Adjective + なとき / Noun + のとき
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the time when something happens, 'when', 'at the time'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とき grammar point is used to express the time when something happens or happened. It can be translated as 'when' or 'at the time' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 公園で遊ぶとき、帽子をかぶりましょう。
examples.romaji: Kouen de asobu toki, boushi wo kaburimashou.
examples.meaningEn: When playing in the park, let's wear a hat.

examples.ja: このりんごが赤いとき、食べごろです。
examples.romaji: Kono ringo ga akai toki, tabegoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: This apple is ready to eat when it is red.

examples.ja: 私が勉強するとき、家族は静かにしてくれます。
examples.romaji: Watashi ga benkyou suru toki, kazoku wa shizuka ni shite kuremasu.
examples.meaningEn: When I am studying, my family keeps quiet for me.

examples.ja: 子供のとき、よくおばあちゃんの家に遊びに行きました。
examples.romaji: Kodomo no toki, yoku obaachan no ie ni asobi ni ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: When I was a child, I often went to play at my grandmother's house.

title: ときどき～ (tokidoki～)
formation: ときどき + Verb/Adjective/Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to indicate the frequency of an event; 'sometimes', 'occasionally', 'now and then'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point ときどき is an adverb used to describe an event or action that occurs occasionally or at irregular intervals. It can be translated as 'sometimes', 'occasionally', or 'now and then' in English. It is often used to indicate that the speaker cannot predict the exact time when a certain event occurs.

examples.ja: 彼はときどきケーキを作ります。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tokidoki keeki o tsukurimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He sometimes makes cakes.

examples.ja: ときどき雨が降るので、傘を持って行ったほうがいいです。
examples.romaji: Tokidoki ame ga furu node, kasa o motte itta hou ga ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: It occasionally rains, so it's better to bring an umbrella.

examples.ja: 私はときどきこの店で買い物をします。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa tokidoki kono mise de kaimono o shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I shop at this store now and then.

examples.ja: 彼女はときどき遅刻します。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa tokidoki chikoku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is sometimes late.

title: どこ～ (doko～)
formation: どこ + question particle
shortExplanationEn: Express 'where', used with question words for asking about locations.
longExplanationEn: The どこ～ grammar point is used to express 'where', primarily for asking about locations or places. It is often combined with question words such as どこへ (doko e), どこで (doko de), and どこに (doko ni) to ask about destinations, locations of actions, and existence, respectively.

examples.ja: 駅はどこですか？
examples.romaji: Eki wa doko desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Where is the station?

examples.ja: どこで映画を見ましたか？
examples.romaji: Doko de eiga wo mimashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: Where did you watch the movie?

examples.ja: どこに住んでいますか？
examples.romaji: Doko ni sunde imasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Where do you live?

examples.ja: どこへ行きたいですか？
examples.romaji: Doko e ikitai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Where do you want to go?

title: どこか～ (dokoka～)
formation: どこか + (Particle, if needed) + Verb/Noun/Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'somewhere', used when the specific location is unknown or uncertain.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point どこか is used when the speaker wants to express that something is happening or located 'somewhere', but the exact location is either unknown or uncertain. It can be used by itself or combined with other words.

examples.ja: どこかで財布をなくしました。
examples.romaji: Dokoka de saifu wo nakushimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I lost my wallet somewhere.

examples.ja: 今日はどこかに行きたいです。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa dokoka ni ikitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to go somewhere today.

examples.ja: どこか安いレストランがありますか？
examples.romaji: Dokoka yasui resutoran ga arimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Is there a cheap restaurant somewhere?

examples.ja: どこか静かな場所で勉強したい。
examples.romaji: Dokoka shizuka na basho de benkyou shitai.
examples.meaningEn: I want to study in a quiet place somewhere.

title: どこでも～ (dokodemo～)
formation: Simply use the word どこでも in the sentence as an adverb to indicate 'anywhere' or 'everywhere'.
shortExplanationEn: Express 'anywhere' or 'everywhere' in Japanese.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point どこでも is used to express 'anywhere' or 'everywhere' in Japanese. It is usually used in sentences to indicate that an action or situation could happen or exist in any location.

examples.ja: どこでも好きな場所で勉強できます。
examples.romaji: Dokodemo sukina basho de benkyou dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: You can study anywhere you like.

examples.ja: このノートパソコンは便利なので、どこでもインターネットが使えます。
examples.romaji: Kono nootopasokon wa benri nano de, dokodemo intaanetto ga tsukaemasu.
examples.meaningEn: This laptop is convenient, so you can use the internet anywhere.

examples.ja: 彼はどこでも寝ることができます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa dokodemo neru koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can sleep anywhere.

examples.ja: 彼女はどこでも歌うのが大好きです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa dokodemo utau no ga daisuki desu.
examples.meaningEn: She loves singing anywhere.

title: どこにも + Verb + ないです (doko ni mo + Verb + nai desu)
formation: どこにも + Verb in ない-form + です
shortExplanationEn: Express that an action cannot be performed anywhere or something cannot be found anywhere.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point どこにも + Verb + ないです is used to express that an action cannot be performed anywhere or something cannot be found anywhere. It is used with a negative verb conjugation (ない-form) and highlights the unavailability or impossibility of finding or doing something in any location.

examples.ja: どこにも傘が売ってないです。
examples.romaji: Doko ni mo kasa ga uttenai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Umbrellas are not sold anywhere.

examples.ja: 彼はどこにも見つけられないです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa doko ni mo mitsukerarenai desu.
examples.meaningEn: He cannot be found anywhere.

examples.ja: どこにも勉強できる場所がないです。
examples.romaji: Doko ni mo benkyou dekiru basho ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There is no place to study anywhere.

examples.ja: どこにも美味しいビーガン料理がないです。
examples.romaji: Doko ni mo oishii bi-gan ryouri ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There are no delicious vegan dishes anywhere.

title: どこにも + Verb + ません (doko ni mo + Verb + masen)
formation: どこにも + Verb-negative
shortExplanationEn: Express 'not anywhere' or 'nowhere' with a negative verb.
longExplanationEn: The どこにも～ません grammar point is used to express that something doesn't exist, appear, or happen anywhere. It means 'not anywhere' or 'nowhere' when used with a negative verb. The formation is quite simple as you just need to add どこにも before the negative verb.

examples.ja: どこにもあの猫がいません。
examples.romaji: Doko ni mo ano neko ga imasen.
examples.meaningEn: The cat is not anywhere.

examples.ja: どこにもお金を置いていません。
examples.romaji: Doko ni mo okane wo oiteimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I didn't put the money anywhere.

examples.ja: この町にはどこにも美味しいラーメン屋がありません。
examples.romaji: Kono machi ni wa doko ni mo oishii raamenya ga arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: There are no delicious ramen shops anywhere in this town.

examples.ja: どこにも彼女の名前が書いていません。
examples.romaji: Doko ni mo kanojo no namae ga kaiteimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Her name is not written anywhere.

title: どこへも Verb ないです (doko e mo + Verb + nai desu)
formation: どこへも + Verb in negative form (ないです)
shortExplanationEn: Express that an action doesn't take place anywhere; 'nowhere', 'not anywhere'.
longExplanationEn: The どこへも + Verb ないです grammar point is used to express a situation where an action doesn't occur or isn't applicable anywhere. It emphasizes a strong sense of not performing the action anywhere. Keep in mind that only negative verbs can follow 'どこへも' in this construction.

examples.ja: 今週末どこへも行かないです。
examples.romaji: Konshuumatsu doko e mo ikanai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I'm not going anywhere this weekend.

examples.ja: 彼女はどこへも買い物に行きませんでした。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa doko e mo kaimono ni ikimasen deshita.
examples.meaningEn: She didn't go shopping anywhere.

examples.ja: スーパーはどこへも売らないです。
examples.romaji: Suupaa wa doko e mo uranai desu.
examples.meaningEn: It's not sold at any supermarket.

examples.ja: 私はどこへも行く予定がないです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa doko e mo iku yotei ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I have no plans to go anywhere.

title: どこへも Verb ません (doko e mo + Verb + masen)
formation: どこへも + Verb-negative
shortExplanationEn: Nowhere; Not to any place
longExplanationEn: The grammar point どこへも is used to express the idea of 'nowhere' or 'not to any place'. It is combined with the negative form of a verb to indicate that the action does not happen to any place or destination.

examples.ja: 今日はどこへも行きません。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa doko e mo ikimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I won't go anywhere today.

examples.ja: 彼はどこへも走りませんでした。
examples.romaji: Kare wa doko e mo hashirimasen deshita.
examples.meaningEn: He didn't run anywhere.

examples.ja: どこへも買い物に行きませんでした。
examples.romaji: Doko e mo kaimono ni ikimasen deshita.
examples.meaningEn: I didn't go shopping anywhere.

examples.ja: 彼女はどこへも連れて行かせません。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa doko e mo tsurete ikasemasen.
examples.meaningEn: She won't take me anywhere.

title: どこも Verb ないです (dokomo + Verb + naidesu)
formation: どこも + Verb-negative
shortExplanationEn: Express that something cannot be found anywhere or in any place; 'nowhere', 'in no place'.
longExplanationEn: The どこも～ないです grammar point is used to express that something cannot be found anywhere or in any place. It can be translated as 'nowhere' or 'in no place' in English. It is used in combination with a negative verb (ないです) to indicate the absence of something.

examples.ja: どこもうちわがないです。
examples.romaji: Doko mo uchiwa ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I can't find fans anywhere.

examples.ja: この町ではどこも美味しいラーメンがないです。
examples.romaji: Kono machi de wa doko mo oishii raamen ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There are no delicious ramen shops anywhere in this town.

examples.ja: どこも駐車場がないです。
examples.romaji: Doko mo chuushajou ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There is no parking anywhere.

examples.ja: どこも席がないです。
examples.romaji: Doko mo seki ga nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There are no seats available anywhere.

title: どこも Verb ません (dokomo + Verb + masen)
formation: どこも + Verb-ます form + ません
shortExplanationEn: Express that 'nowhere' meets the verb action; 'nowhere does/can'.
longExplanationEn: The どこも + Verb ません grammar point is used to express that nowhere does or can fulfill the verb action. It is a negative statement and can be translated as 'nowhere (+ verb)' in English. Note that the first example below uses an adjective rather than a verb, which still conveys 'nowhere is ...'.

examples.ja: どこも静かじゃありません。
examples.romaji: Doko mo shizuka ja arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Nowhere is quiet.

examples.ja: どこもその本を売っていません。
examples.romaji: Doko mo sono hon wo utte imasen.
examples.meaningEn: Nowhere sells that book.

examples.ja: この地域では、どこもインターネットに接続できません。
examples.romaji: Kono chiiki de wa, doko mo intaanetto ni setsuzoku dekimasen.
examples.meaningEn: In this area, nowhere can connect to the internet.

examples.ja: どこも彼を知りません。
examples.romaji: Doko mo kare wo shirimasen.
examples.meaningEn: No place knows him.

title: どちら～ (dochira～)
formation: どちら + が/を + verb, どちら + が/を + adjective, どちら + が/を + noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to ask about two choices or options.
longExplanationEn: The どちら grammar point is used to ask about two choices or options. It translates to 'which of the two' or simply 'which' in English. It is commonly used with question words like どちらが or どちらを and can be combined with verbs, adjectives, and nouns to ask about preferences or to make comparisons.

examples.ja: この二つのリンゴ、どちらが美味しいですか？
examples.romaji: Kono futatsu no ringo, dochira ga oishii desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which of these two apples is delicious?

examples.ja: どちらのドレスを着たいですか？
examples.romaji: Dochira no doresu wo kitai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which dress would you like to wear?

examples.ja: この二つの町、どちらに行きたいですか？
examples.romaji: Kono futatsu no machi, dochira ni ikitai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which of these two towns do you want to go to?

examples.ja: どちらの映画が面白いと思いますか？
examples.romaji: Dochira no eiga ga omoshiroi to omoimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which movie do you think is interesting?

title: どなた～ (donata～)
formation: どなた
shortExplanationEn: A polite way to ask 'who', used for asking about someone's identity or when seeking information about an unknown person.
longExplanationEn: The word どなた is a polite version of the question word だれ (who). It is used when asking about someone's identity or when seeking information about an unknown person in a polite or formal context. It can be used in questions and sometimes with も to express 'no matter who'.

examples.ja: すみません、どなたが山田さんですか？
examples.romaji: Sumimasen, donata ga Yamada-san desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Excuse me, who is Mr. Yamada?

examples.ja: どなたも知っている有名な俳優です。
examples.romaji: Donata mo shitte iru yuumei na haiyuu desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is a famous actor whom everyone knows.

examples.ja: 明日のパーティーにはどなたが来ますか？
examples.romaji: Ashita no paatii ni wa donata ga kimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Who is coming to the party tomorrow?

examples.ja: どなたか手伝っていただけますか？
examples.romaji: Donata ka tetsudatte itadakemasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Could someone please help me?

title: どの Noun (dono Noun)
formation: どの + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'which'; used to ask about specific items within a group.
longExplanationEn: The どの Noun grammar point is used to ask about specific items or objects within a group. It is the combination of the question word ど (which) and the particle の to connect to a noun. In English, it can be translated as 'which' depending on the context.

examples.ja: どの本が一番面白いですか。
examples.romaji: Dono hon ga ichiban omoshiroi desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which book is the most interesting?

examples.ja: どの服を着たいですか。
examples.romaji: Dono fuku wo kitai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which clothes do you want to wear?

examples.ja: どの色が好きですか。
examples.romaji: Dono iro ga suki desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which color do you like?

examples.ja: どのレストランに行きたいですか。
examples.romaji: Dono resutoran ni ikitai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Which restaurant do you want to go to?

title: どれでも～ (dore demo～)
formation: どれ + でも
shortExplanationEn: Indicates 'any' or 'whichever' when referring to multiple items.
longExplanationEn: The どれでも grammar point is used to express that any option or element among multiple choices is acceptable. It can be translated as 'any' or 'whichever' in English. It is often used in questions or suggestions and is commonly seen with でも, but it can also be used with だれでも, いつでも, どこでも, etc.

examples.ja: どれでもいいですか？
examples.romaji: Dore demo ii desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Is any choice okay?

examples.ja: どれでも選んでください。
examples.romaji: Dore demo erande kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please choose whichever you like.

examples.ja: どれでもおいしいです。
examples.romaji: Dore demo oishii desu.
examples.meaningEn: All of them are delicious.

examples.ja: この中でどれでも好きなものを持って行ってください。
examples.romaji: Kono naka de dore demo suki na mono wo motte itte kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please take whichever you like from these.

title: どんな Noun (donna)
formation: どんな + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to ask or describe 'what kind' or 'what type' of something.
longExplanationEn: The どんな (donna) grammar point is used to ask or describe 'what kind' or 'what type' of something. It is usually followed by a noun. It is similar to the English expression 'What kind of' or 'What type of' when asking about a specific characteristic or attribute.

examples.ja: どんな音楽が好きですか？
examples.romaji: Donna ongaku ga suki desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What kind of music do you like?

examples.ja: 彼女はどんな映画が好きだと思う？
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa donna eiga ga suki da to omou?
examples.meaningEn: What kind of movies do you think she likes?

examples.ja: どんな本が一番面白いですか？
examples.romaji: Donna hon ga ichiban omoshiroi desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What kind of books are the most interesting?

examples.ja: どんな料理が得意ですか？
examples.romaji: Donna ryouri ga tokui desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What kind of dishes are you good at making?

title: なに～ (nani~)
formation: Verb + なに, い-Adjective + なに, な-Adjective + なに, Noun + なに
shortExplanationEn: Asking about 'what' or 'which' in Japanese
longExplanationEn: The なに～ grammar point is used to ask questions about 'what' or 'which' in Japanese. It can be combined with various parts of speech to create different questions. The formation differs depending on whether it's used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: これはなにですか。
examples.romaji: Kore wa nani desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What is this?

examples.ja: あの人はなにをしているのですか？
examples.romaji: Ano hito wa nani wo shite iru no desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What is that person doing?

examples.ja: なにが好きですか？
examples.romaji: Nani ga suki desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What do you like?

examples.ja: 今日の天気はなにですか？
examples.romaji: Kyou no tenki wa nani desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What is today's weather?

title: なにか～ (nanika～)
formation: なにか + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Indicates 'something' or 'some kind of'.
longExplanationEn: The なにか grammar point is used to express the idea of 'something' or 'some kind of' in Japanese. It is often used when the speaker is unsure or not specific about the details of the noun being referred to. なにか is used before the noun it modifies.

examples.ja: なにか飲み物を持ってきてください。
examples.romaji: Nanika nomimono wo motte kite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please bring some kind of drink.

examples.ja: なにか質問があれば、遠慮なく聞いてください。
examples.romaji: Nanika shitsumon ga areba, enryo naku kiite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: If you have any questions, please feel free to ask.

examples.ja: 彼女はなにかプレゼントを持っている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa nanika purezento wo motte iru.
examples.meaningEn: She has some kind of present.

examples.ja: なにか助けが必要なら、言ってください。
examples.romaji: Nanika tasuke ga hitsuyou nara, itte kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: If you need any help, please let me know.

title: なにも～ないです (nani mo~nai desu)
formation: なにも + Verb-negative
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'not any' or 'nothing' in a negative sentence.
longExplanationEn: The なにも～ないです grammar point is used in negative sentences to express the idea of 'not any', 'not at all', 'nothing', or 'none'. It is commonly used with verbs in the negative form.

examples.ja: 彼はなにも言わなかったです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nani mo iwanakatta desu.
examples.meaningEn: He didn't say anything.

examples.ja: 私はなにも食べないです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa nani mo tabenai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I don't eat anything.

examples.ja: 彼女はなにも持っていないです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa nani mo motte inai desu.
examples.meaningEn: She doesn't have anything.

examples.ja: この箱にはなにも入っていないです。
examples.romaji: Kono hako ni wa nani mo haitte inai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There is nothing in this box.

title: なにも～ません (nanimo~masen)
formation: なにも + Verb-ます form + ません
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'not anything', 'nothing'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point なにも～ません is used to express the idea of 'not anything' or 'nothing' in negative sentences. It is placed before the verb in its negative form (～ません).

examples.ja: 昨日はなにも食べませんでした。
examples.romaji: Kinou wa nanimo tabemasen deshita.
examples.meaningEn: I didn't eat anything yesterday.

examples.ja: 彼はなにも話しません。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nanimo hanashimasen.
examples.meaningEn: He doesn't say anything.

examples.ja: この部屋ではなにも見えません。
examples.romaji: Kono heya de wa nanimo miemasen.
examples.meaningEn: I cannot see anything in this room.

examples.ja: 彼女はなにも買いませんでした。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa nanimo kaimasen deshita.
examples.meaningEn: She didn't buy anything.

title: なん～ (nan~)
formation: なん + Counter / なん + Verb / なん + Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Used to form interrogative questions and emphasize the question word for 'what', 'how'.
longExplanationEn: The なん～ grammar point is used to form interrogative questions and emphasize the question word for 'what' or 'how'. It is an abbreviation of '何' meaning 'what'. Its usage can vary depending on whether it is used with a counter, verb, or adjective.

examples.ja: あなたはなん歳ですか？
examples.romaji: Anata wa nan-sai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: How old are you?

examples.ja: これはなんですか？
examples.romaji: Kore wa nan desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What is this?

examples.ja: 彼はなんで来ましたか？
examples.romaji: Kare wa nan de kimashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: Why did he come?

examples.ja: あのレストランはなんと言う名前ですか？
examples.romaji: Ano resutoran wa nan to iu namae desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: What is the name of that restaurant?

title: なんで～ (nande～)
formation: なんで + [phrase or sentence]
shortExplanationEn: Asking the reason or cause; 'why', 'how come'.
longExplanationEn: The なんで grammar point is used to ask the reason or cause behind something. It can be translated as 'why' or 'how come' in English. It is a casual way of asking for an explanation, and it can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: なんで遅れたの？
examples.romaji: Nande okureta no?
examples.meaningEn: Why were you late?

examples.ja: なんでこの部屋はこんなに汚いの？
examples.romaji: Nande kono heya wa konna ni kitanai no?
examples.meaningEn: Why is this room so dirty?

examples.ja: なんで彼女は怒っているの？
examples.romaji: Nande kanojo wa okotte iru no?
examples.meaningEn: Why is she angry?

examples.ja: なんで今日は休みなの？
examples.romaji: Nande kyou wa yasumi na no?
examples.meaningEn: Why is today a holiday?

title: なんでも～ (nandemo～)
formation: なんでも + Verb / なんでも + Adjective / なんでも + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Means 'any', 'anything', 'anyone'.
longExplanationEn: The なんでも grammar point is used to express the concept of 'any', 'anything', or 'anyone' in Japanese. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, and nouns. It is often used in the meaning of 'no matter what' or 'regardless of'.

examples.ja: なんでも食べられるから、何を頼んでも大丈夫です。
examples.romaji: Nandemo taberareru kara, nani wo tanondemo daijoubu desu.
examples.meaningEn: I can eat anything, so it's okay to order whatever you want.

examples.ja: なんでもよければ、この本をおすすめします。
examples.romaji: Nandemo yokereba, kono hon wo osusume shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: If you're okay with anything, I recommend this book.

examples.ja: なんでもできるスタッフを探しています。
examples.romaji: Nandemo dekiru sutaffu wo sagashiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: We are looking for a staff member who can do anything.

examples.ja: この食堂では、なんでも美味しくて安い料理が出されます。
examples.romaji: Kono shokudou de wa, nandemo oishikute yasui ryouri ga dasaremasu.
examples.meaningEn: In this cafeteria, any dish served is delicious and inexpensive.

title: ～なんと 言いますか。 (〜nan to iimasu ka.)
formation: Noun + は/が + なんと言いますか
shortExplanationEn: Asking how something is called or referred to; 'how do you say'
longExplanationEn: The ～なんと言いますか grammar point is used to ask how something is called, expressed, or referred to in a polite manner. It can be translated as 'how do you say' or 'what do you call' in English. The formation is used with a noun or a topic where you would like to know the proper expression or name for something.

examples.ja: この花はなんと言いますか。
examples.romaji: Kono hana wa nan to iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How do you say this flower?

examples.ja: この料理はなんと言いますか。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa nan to iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: What do you call this dish?

examples.ja: 日本でこれはなんと言いますか。
examples.romaji: Nihon de kore wa nan to iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How do you say this in Japanese?

examples.ja: その映画の名前はなんと言いますか。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga no namae wa nan to iimasu ka.
examples.meaningEn: What is the name of that movie?

title: ～に あげます (〜 ni agemasu)
formation: Receiver + に + Object + を + あげます
shortExplanationEn: Used to express giving something to someone; 'give', 'offer'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に あげます grammar point is used when the speaker offers or gives something to someone. It emphasizes that the action is performed for the benefit of the receiver. The formation involves using the particle 'に' after the person receiving the item, followed by the verb あげます.

examples.ja: 私は友達に本をあげました。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa tomodachi ni hon wo agemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I gave a book to my friend.

examples.ja: 彼にプレゼントをあげましょう。
examples.romaji: Kare ni purezento wo agemashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's give him a present.

examples.ja: 先生に花をあげます。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni hana wo agemasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will give flowers to the teacher.

examples.ja: お母さんにチョコレートをあげたいです。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ni chokoreeto wo agetai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to give chocolate to my mom.

title: ～に もらいます (〜ni moraimasu)
formation: Giver + に + Verb-te form + もらいます / Giver + に + Object + を + もらいます
shortExplanationEn: To receive something or some action from someone; 'to get', 'to receive'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に もらいます grammar point is used to express receiving something or some action from someone. It can be translated as 'to get' or 'to receive' in English. The formation involves placing に after the giver and もらいます to show the act of receiving.

examples.ja: 友達にプレゼントをもらいました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni purezento wo moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I received a gift from my friend.

examples.ja: 先生にアドバイスをもらいました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni adobaisu wo moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I got advice from my teacher.

examples.ja: おばあさんに手紙を書いてもらいました。
examples.romaji: Obaasan ni tegami wo kaite moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I had my grandmother write a letter for me.

examples.ja: 弟に宿題を手伝ってもらいました。
examples.romaji: Otouto ni shukudai wo tetsudatte moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I got help from my younger brother with my homework.

title: ～の (〜no)
formation: Noun1 + の + Noun2, (Adjective/Clause) + の (nominalized), etc.
shortExplanationEn: Express possession, apposition, or description using 'of' or ''s'.
longExplanationEn: The ～の grammar point is used to express possession, apposition, or description. It functions like 'of' or an apostrophe + 's' (’s) in English. It can be used with nouns, adjectives, and verbs to show relationships between words.

examples.ja: これは私の鞄です。
examples.romaji: Kore wa watashi no kaban desu.
examples.meaningEn: This is my bag.

examples.ja: 彼女は赤いバッグを持っています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa akai baggu o motte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She has a red bag.

examples.ja: 彼はきれいな部屋があります。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kirei na heya ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He has a clean room.

examples.ja: 東京の天気は暑いです。
examples.romaji: Toukyou no tenki wa atsui desu.
examples.meaningEn: The weather in Tokyo is hot.

title: ～はたいへんです (〜wa taihen desu)
formation: Noun + はたいへんです
shortExplanationEn: Express that something is difficult or tough.
longExplanationEn: The ～はたいへんです grammar point is used to express that something is difficult or tough to do, handle, or face. It can be translated as 'is difficult' or 'is tough' in English. The formation is used with a noun followed by はたいへんです.

examples.ja: 漢字を覚えるのはたいへんです。
examples.romaji: Kanji o oboeru no wa taihen desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is difficult to remember kanji.

examples.ja: この仕事はたいへんです。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto wa taihen desu.
examples.meaningEn: This job is tough.

examples.ja: 日本の駅のことを理解するのはたいへんです。
examples.romaji: Nihon no eki no koto o rikai suru no wa taihen desu.
examples.meaningEn: Understanding Japanese train stations is difficult.

examples.ja: 彼女は病気の時、生活はたいへんです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa byouki no toki, seikatsu wa taihen desu.
examples.meaningEn: When she is sick, life is tough.

title: ほとんど〜 (hotondo〜)
formation: ほとんど + Verb / ほとんど + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'almost', 'nearly', or 'most'.
longExplanationEn: The ほとんど〜 grammar point is used to express that something is 'almost', 'nearly', or 'most'. It can appear in both negative contexts (hardly/barely) or positive contexts (almost all). It is typically followed by a verb or used with a noun to describe the quantity or extent of something.

examples.ja: 彼女はほとんど英語を話せません。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa hotondo eigo o hanasemasen.
examples.meaningEn: She can hardly speak English.

examples.ja: ほとんどの人がパーティーに出席しました。
examples.romaji: Hotondo no hito ga paatii ni shusseki shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Most people attended the party.

examples.ja: 私たちはほとんど勉強しないでテストに合格しました。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa hotondo benkyou shinaide tesuto ni goukaku shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: We passed the test with hardly any studying.

examples.ja: この本はほとんど読み終わりました。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa hotondo yomiowarimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I have almost finished reading this book.

title: まあまあ～ (maa maa～)
formation: Used as an adverb or short expression with no special grammar structure.
shortExplanationEn: Expresses something as 'so-so', 'not bad', or 'average'.
longExplanationEn: The expression まあまあ is used to describe something that is neither particularly good nor bad, but rather average or 'so-so'. It is a neutral term and can be used in various contexts when discussing quality, feelings, or situations.

examples.ja: 彼の料理の腕前はまあまあです。
examples.romaji: Kare no ryouri no udemae wa maa maa desu.
examples.meaningEn: His cooking skills are so-so.

examples.ja: 今日の天気はまあまあですね。
examples.romaji: Kyou no tenki wa maa maa desu ne.
examples.meaningEn: The weather today is not bad, huh?

examples.ja: あの映画はまあまあ面白かったです。
examples.romaji: Ano eiga wa maa maa omoshirokatta desu.
examples.meaningEn: That movie was somewhat interesting.

examples.ja: 彼女の歌はまあまあ上手だと思います。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no uta wa maa maa jouzu da to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think her singing is not bad.

title: まだ〜 (mada〜)
formation: まだ + Verb (non-past / negative / te-form), まだ + い-adjective, まだ + な-adjective + じゃない/ではない, まだ + Noun + じゃない/ではない
shortExplanationEn: Indicates something has not yet happened or is still happening; 'still', 'not yet'.
longExplanationEn: The まだ〜 grammar point is used to express that something has not yet occurred or is still in progress. It can be translated as 'still' or 'not yet' in English. Depending on the context or if expressing the negative, まだ can be used with different combinations of verb, adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 昼ご飯はまだ食べていません。
examples.romaji: Hirugohan wa mada tabete imasen.
examples.meaningEn: I haven't eaten lunch yet.

examples.ja: 山田さんはまだ来ていません。
examples.romaji: Yamada-san wa mada kite imasen.
examples.meaningEn: Yamada-san has not arrived yet.

examples.ja: この部屋はまだ暑いです。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa mada atsui desu.
examples.meaningEn: This room is still hot.

examples.ja: 彼はまだ学生じゃないです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mada gakusei ja nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is not a student yet.

title: まだ〜ないです (mada 〜 nai desu)
formation: まだ + Verb-negative + です
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something has not happened or been done yet.
longExplanationEn: The まだ〜ないです grammar point is used to indicate that a certain action or event has not yet happened or been completed. It is used with the negative form of a verb (casual negative + です) and can be translated as 'not yet', 'still', or 'haven't' in English.

examples.ja: まだ宿題をしていないです。
examples.romaji: Mada shukudai o shite inai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I haven't done my homework yet.

examples.ja: 彼はまだ来ないです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mada konai desu.
examples.meaningEn: He hasn't come yet.

examples.ja: 映画はまだ始まっていないです。
examples.romaji: Eiga wa mada hajimatte inai desu.
examples.meaningEn: The movie hasn't started yet.

examples.ja: 私たちはまだ夕食を食べていないです。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa mada yuushoku o tabete inai desu.
examples.meaningEn: We haven't eaten dinner yet.

title: まだ～ません (mada ~masen)
formation: まだ + Verb-ます form (negative)
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something has not happened or been done yet (polite form).
longExplanationEn: The まだ～ません grammar point is used to express that something has not happened or been done yet in a polite register. It is used with the negative ます-form of a verb (～ません). まだ is placed before the verb phrase.

examples.ja: 昼ご飯をまだ食べていません。
examples.romaji: Hirugohan o mada tabete imasen.
examples.meaningEn: I have not eaten lunch yet.

examples.ja: 宿題がまだ終わっていません。
examples.romaji: Shukudai ga mada owatte imasen.
examples.meaningEn: I have not finished my homework yet.

examples.ja: 東京にはまだ行ったことがありません。
examples.romaji: Toukyou ni wa mada itta koto ga arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I have not been to Tokyo yet.

examples.ja: 彼はまだ来ていません。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mada kite imasen.
examples.meaningEn: He hasn't come yet.

title: もう～ (mou～)
formation: もう + Verb / もう + Adjective / もう + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'already', 'anymore', or 'soon'.
longExplanationEn: The もう grammar point is used to express the idea of 'already', 'anymore', or 'soon' in English. It indicates that something has already happened, won't happen anymore, or will happen soon. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, or nouns depending on context.

examples.ja: もう昼ごはんを食べました。
examples.romaji: Mou hirugohan o tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I already ate lunch.

examples.ja: もうここでは働かない。
examples.romaji: Mou koko de wa hatarakanai.
examples.meaningEn: I won't work here anymore.

examples.ja: もうすぐ夏休みですね。
examples.romaji: Mou sugu natsuyasumi desu ne.
examples.meaningEn: Summer vacation is coming soon, isn't it?

examples.ja: もう十分です。
examples.romaji: Mou juubun desu.
examples.meaningEn: It's already enough.

title: もうすぐ〜 (mou sugu~)
formation: もうすぐ + Verb (~ます form or 〜る form)
shortExplanationEn: Express an action or event happening soon: 'soon', 'in a short while', 'almost'.
longExplanationEn: The もうすぐ grammar point is used to express that an action or event is going to happen soon or is almost happening. It can be translated as 'soon', 'in a short while', or 'almost' in English. もうすぐ is usually followed by a verb or a noun indicating an event.

examples.ja: もうすぐ春が来ます。
examples.romaji: Mou sugu haru ga kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Spring is coming soon.

examples.ja: もうすぐ彼女の誕生日です。
examples.romaji: Mou sugu kanojo no tanjoubi desu.
examples.meaningEn: Her birthday is coming up soon.

examples.ja: もうすぐ試験が終わる。
examples.romaji: Mou sugu shiken ga owaru.
examples.meaningEn: The exam will be over soon.

examples.ja: もうすぐ雨が止むでしょう。
examples.romaji: Mou sugu ame ga yamu deshou.
examples.meaningEn: The rain will likely stop soon.

title: もっと〜 (motto〜)
formation: もっと + Verb / い-Adjective / な-Adjective
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'more' or 'even more' when describing a quantity, frequency, or degree.
longExplanationEn: The もっと〜 grammar point is used to emphasize the desire or need for something to be 'more' or 'even more' in terms of quantity, frequency, or degree. It is often translated as 'more' or 'even more' in English. もっと can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, or な-adjectives.

examples.ja: もっと勉強しなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Motto benkyou shinakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I have to study more.

examples.ja: このかばんをもっと大きいサイズに変えたいです。
examples.romaji: Kono kaban o motto ookii saizu ni kaetai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to change this bag to a larger size.

examples.ja: もっと静かな場所で働きたいです。
examples.romaji: Motto shizuka na basho de hatarakitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to work in a quieter place.

examples.ja: 彼女はもっと上手になりたいと思っています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa motto jouzu ni naritai to omotte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She wants to become even better.

title: よく～ (yoku ~)
formation: よく + Verb-casual
shortExplanationEn: Often used to express 'doing something well' or 'often'.
longExplanationEn: The よく～ grammar point is used to express that someone does something well, frequently, or often. It is often placed before the verb and can also mean 'well done' or 'good job' when praising someone's action.

examples.ja: 彼はよく勉強します。
examples.romaji: Kare wa yoku benkyou shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He studies often.

examples.ja: 彼女はよく料理を作ります。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa yoku ryouri o tsukurimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She often cooks.

examples.ja: 昔の友達とよく遊んでいました。
examples.romaji: Mukashi no tomodachi to yoku asonde imashita.
examples.meaningEn: I used to play a lot with my old friends.

examples.ja: よくできました！
examples.romaji: Yoku dekimashita!
examples.meaningEn: Well done!

title: ～（場所）に～があります (〜basho ni 〜 ga arimasu)
formation: Place + に + Object/Thing + が + あります
shortExplanationEn: Express the existence or presence of something in a place.
longExplanationEn: The ～（場所）に～があります grammar point is used to express the existence or presence of something in a specific location. In this structure, the place is followed by 'に', then the object, plus が, and finally あります (to exist / there is).

examples.ja: 図書館にたくさんの本があります。
examples.romaji: Toshokan ni takusan no hon ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There are many books in the library.

examples.ja: 公園に池があります。
examples.romaji: Kouen ni ike ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a pond in the park.

examples.ja: 駅の前にコンビニがあります。
examples.romaji: Eki no mae ni konbini ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a convenience store in front of the station.

examples.ja: 山の上に神社があります。
examples.romaji: Yama no ue ni jinja ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There is a shrine on top of the mountain.

title: A とか B とか
formation: Noun + とか + Noun + とか
shortExplanationEn: Used to express multiple examples or possibilities; 'such as', 'like', 'for example'.
longExplanationEn: The とか grammar point is used to list multiple examples or possibilities. It can be translated as 'such as', 'like', or 'for example' in English. とか is used between the listed items and expresses that the list is not exhaustive.

examples.ja: 週末は映画とか買い物とか楽しいことをしたいです。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu wa eiga toka kaimono toka tanoshii koto o shitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to do fun things like watching movies and shopping on the weekend.

examples.ja: 彼女は英語とかフランス語とかいろいろな言葉を話せます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa eigo toka furansugo toka iroirona kotoba o hanasemasu.
examples.meaningEn: She can speak various languages, like English and French.

examples.ja: この店は寿司とか刺身とか日本料理を提供しています。
examples.romaji: Kono mise wa sushi toka sashimi toka nihon ryouri o teikyou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: This restaurant offers Japanese dishes such as sushi and sashimi.

examples.ja: $1BOXとか$5BOXとか、異なる価格のランチBOXがあります。
examples.romaji: Ichi-doru BOX toka go-doru BOX toka, kotonaru kakaku no ranchi BOX ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: There are lunch boxes with different prices, like $1 boxes and $5 boxes.

title: A は B ほど～ありません (A wa B hodo ～ arimasen)
formation: A は B ほど Verb/adjective ありません
shortExplanationEn: Express that A is not as much as B; 'not as... as', 'not... to the extent of'.
longExplanationEn: The は B ほど～ありません grammar point is used to express that A is not as much as B or not to the same extent as B, emphasizing the difference. It can be translated as 'not as... as' or 'not... to the extent of' in English.

examples.ja: 今日は昨日ほど寒くありません。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa kinou hodo samuku arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Today is not as cold as yesterday.

examples.ja: 彼は私ほど上手に英語を話せません。
examples.romaji: Kare wa watashi hodo jouzu ni eigo o hanasemasen.
examples.meaningEn: He cannot speak English as well as I can.

examples.ja: このレストランはあのレストランほど高くありません。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa ano resutoran hodo takaku arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: This restaurant is not as expensive as that restaurant.

examples.ja: 彼女は彼ほど忙しくありません。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kare hodo isogashiku arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: She is not as busy as him.

title: A は B ほど～ない (A wa B hodo ~ nai)
formation: A は B ほど + い-Adjective stem (without い) + くない, な-Adjective + じゃない, Verb-negative potential form
shortExplanationEn: Expressing that A is not as ~ as B; 'not as...as'
longExplanationEn: The A は B ほど～ない grammar point is used to express that A is not as ~ as B in some aspect. It can be translated as 'not as...as' in English. This structure is commonly used with い-adjectives (without い) and な-adjective. For verbs, you can use the negative potential form.

examples.ja: 山田さんは鈴木さんほど背が高くない。
examples.romaji: Yamada-san wa Suzuki-san hodo se ga takakunai.
examples.meaningEn: Yamada is not as tall as Suzuki.

examples.ja: このリンゴはあのリンゴほど甘くない。
examples.romaji: Kono ringo wa ano ringo hodo amakunai.
examples.meaningEn: This apple is not as sweet as that apple.

examples.ja: 彼は私ほど勉強ができない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa watashi hodo benkyou ga dekinai.
examples.meaningEn: He can't study as well as I can.

examples.ja: このレストランはあのレストランほど静かじゃない。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa ano resutoran hodo shizuka janai.
examples.meaningEn: This restaurant is not as quiet as that restaurant.

title: A より B のほうが〜 (A yori B no hou ga 〜)
formation: A + より + B + のほうが + Adjective/Verb
shortExplanationEn: Comparing two things; 'B is more ~ than A'.
longExplanationEn: The より and のほうが grammar pattern is used to compare two things by means of an adjective or verb. In this structure, item A is compared to item B, suggesting that item B is more ~ than item A.

examples.ja: 田中さんより鈴木さんのほうが背が高いです。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san yori Suzuki-san no hou ga se ga takai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Suzuki is taller than Tanaka.

examples.ja: リンゴよりバナナのほうが甘いです。
examples.romaji: Ringo yori banana no hou ga amai desu.
examples.meaningEn: Bananas are sweeter than apples.

examples.ja: 冬より夏のほうが暑いです。
examples.romaji: Fuyu yori natsu no hou ga atsui desu.
examples.meaningEn: Summer is hotter than winter.

examples.ja: 東京より京都のほうが歴史があると思います。
examples.romaji: Toukyou yori Kyouto no hou ga rekishi ga aru to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think Kyoto has more history than Tokyo.

title: い-Adjective く する/なる (i-Adjective kusuru/naru)
formation: い-Adjective (remove い) + く + する/なる
shortExplanationEn: Express a change happening or making something change.
longExplanationEn: The い-Adjective く する/なる grammar point is used to indicate a change happening (naru) or intentionally making something change (suru). When using this grammatical structure, the い-adjective is changed to its く form, and then is followed by either する (to make the change) or なる (the change happens).

examples.ja: 部屋を明るくします。
examples.romaji: Heya o akaruku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make the room brighter.

examples.ja: 彼の顔は赤くなりました。
examples.romaji: Kare no kao wa akaku narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: His face turned red.

examples.ja: この料理を辛くしてください。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri o karaku shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please make this dish spicy.

examples.ja: 雨が降ると、道が滑りやすくなります。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu to, michi ga suberiyasuku narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When it rains, the road becomes slippery.

title: な-adjective に する/なる
formation: な-Adjective + に + する/なる
shortExplanationEn: Used to indicate change or decision; 'to become' or 'to make' with na-adjectives.
longExplanationEn: The な-adjective に する/なる grammar point is used to express a change or a decision related to a na-adjective. The なる (naru) form indicates a change that occurs by itself, while the する (suru) form indicates a conscious decision made by someone.

examples.ja: 部屋をきれいにする。
examples.romaji: Heya o kirei ni suru.
examples.meaningEn: To make the room clean.

examples.ja: 彼は元気になりました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa genki ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He became energetic.

examples.ja: 毎日静かにすることが大切です。
examples.romaji: Mainichi shizuka ni suru koto ga taisetsu desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is important to make it quiet every day.

examples.ja: 日本語の勉強が楽しくなるように努力します。
examples.romaji: Nihongo no benkyou ga tanoshiku naru you ni doryoku shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will try to make studying Japanese become enjoyable.

title: Noun しか～ない (Noun shika~nai)
formation: Noun + しか + Negative Verb
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'only', 'nothing but', or 'no other'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Noun しか～ない is used to emphasize the small quantity or exclusiveness of something. It can be translated as 'only', 'nothing but', or 'no other' in English. It is used with a negative verb to convey the meaning.

examples.ja: 私はリンゴしか食べない。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa ringo shika tabenai.
examples.meaningEn: I eat only apples.

examples.ja: 電車で行くしかないです。
examples.romaji: Densha de iku shika nai desu.
examples.meaningEn: There is no other way but to go by train.

examples.ja: 彼は一冊の本しか持っていない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isatsu no hon shika motte inai.
examples.meaningEn: He has only one book.

examples.ja: この店にはビールしかない。
examples.romaji: Kono mise ni wa biiru shika nai.
examples.meaningEn: There is nothing but beer in this store.

title: Noun に する (Noun ni suru)
formation: Noun + に + する
shortExplanationEn: Used to indicate a choice or decision, 'to decide on' or 'to choose'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point 'Noun に する' is used to express making a choice or decision, which can be translated as 'to decide on' or 'to choose' in English. It is often used when deciding between different options or making a decision in various situations, such as choosing a menu item, deciding on a plan, or selecting a gift.

examples.ja: メニューからピザにしました。
examples.romaji: Menyu kara piza ni shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I decided on pizza from the menu.

examples.ja: 誕生日プレゼントに時計にするつもりです。
examples.romaji: Tanjoubi purezento ni tokei ni suru tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to choose a watch as a birthday present.

examples.ja: 今日の午後は買い物にすることにしました。
examples.romaji: Kyou no gogo wa kaimono ni suru koto ni shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I decided to go shopping this afternoon.

examples.ja: 海外旅行の目的地として、日本にする。
examples.romaji: Kaigai ryokou no mokutekichi toshite, Nihon ni suru.
examples.meaningEn: I will choose Japan as the destination for my overseas trip.

title: Noun に なる (Noun ni naru)
formation: Noun + に + なる
shortExplanationEn: To become; to change into; to turn into.
longExplanationEn: The 'Noun に なる' grammar point is used to show a change or a transformation from one state or form into another. In English, it can be translated as 'to become', 'to change into', or 'to turn into'. It is often used with nouns that indicate profession, status, or condition.

examples.ja: 彼は医者になりました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isha ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He became a doctor.

examples.ja: 子供の頃、私は先生になりたかったです。
examples.romaji: Kodomo no koro, watashi wa sensei ni naritakatta desu.
examples.meaningEn: When I was a child, I wanted to become a teacher.

examples.ja: 彼女は緊張して顔が赤くなりました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kinchou shite kao ga akaku narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: She got nervous, and her face turned red.

examples.ja: 春になると桜が咲きます。
examples.romaji: Haru ni naru to sakura ga sakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When it becomes spring, cherry blossoms bloom.

title: のために (no tame ni)
formation: Noun + のために
shortExplanationEn: Used to express purpose or reason; 'for', 'for the sake of', 'in order to'.
longExplanationEn: The のために grammar point is used to express the purpose or reason for doing something, or for the benefit of someone or something. It can be translated as 'for', 'for the sake of', or 'in order to' in English. The formation is straightforward, simply place のために after a noun.

examples.ja: 勉強のために、図書館で静かな場所を探しています。
examples.romaji: Benkyou no tame ni, toshokan de shizukana basho o sagashiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: In order to study, I am looking for a quiet place in the library.

examples.ja: 彼は家族のために、一生懸命働いています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kazoku no tame ni, isshoukenmei hataraiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He works hard for the sake of his family.

examples.ja: 健康のために、毎日運動をしています。
examples.romaji: Kenkou no tame ni, mainichi undou o shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I exercise every day for the sake of my health.

examples.ja: 世界平和のために、みんなで協力しましょう。
examples.romaji: Sekai heiwa no tame ni, minna de kyouryoku shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's all cooperate for the sake of world peace.

title: Noun の 間に (〜no aida ni)
formation: Noun + の間に
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'while' or 'during' a specific period or time.
longExplanationEn: The Noun の 間に grammar point is used to indicate a specific period or time during which an action takes place. It is often translated as 'while' or 'during' in English. This grammar point requires a noun to represent the time or period.

examples.ja: 昼休みの間に銀行に行きます。
examples.romaji: Hiruyasumi no aida ni ginkou ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to the bank during lunch break.

examples.ja: 夏休みの間に海外旅行に行く予定です。
examples.romaji: Natsuyasumi no aida ni kaigai ryokou ni iku yotei desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to go on an overseas trip during the summer vacation.

examples.ja: 子供たちが学校の間に買い物に行きました。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi ga gakkou no aida ni kaimono ni ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I went shopping while the kids were at school.

examples.ja: 母の日にお母さんにプレゼントを送りました。
examples.romaji: Haha no hi ni okaasan ni purezento o okurimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I sent a gift to my mother on Mother’s Day.

title: Noun ばかり (〜bakari)
formation: Noun + ばかり, Verb-casual + ばかり, い-Adjective + ばかり, な-Adjective + だ ばかり
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'only', 'nothing but', 'always', or 'just'.
longExplanationEn: The ばかり grammar point is used to express that something is only, nothing but, just, or always. It can be attached to nouns, verbs, or adjectives, and has various meanings based on the context.

examples.ja: 彼はお金の話ばかりする。
examples.romaji: Kare wa okane no hanashi bakari suru.
examples.meaningEn: He talks about nothing but money.

examples.ja: 彼女は勉強ばかりしている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa benkyou bakari shiteiru.
examples.meaningEn: She is always studying.

examples.ja: デパートには高い商品ばかりだ。
examples.romaji: Depaato ni wa takai shouhin bakari da.
examples.meaningEn: There are only expensive items in the department store.

examples.ja: この部屋は狭いばかりだ。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa semai bakari da.
examples.meaningEn: This room is nothing but small.

title: Noun を あげる (Noun wo ageru)
formation: Noun + を + あげる
shortExplanationEn: To give something to someone; 'give', 'offer', 'present'.
longExplanationEn: The を あげる grammar point is used to express giving something to someone. It can be translated as 'give', 'offer', or 'present' in English. This construction is commonly used with nouns to indicate giving or offering a tangible item, action, or favor to someone else.

examples.ja: プレゼントをあげる。
examples.romaji: Purezento wo ageru.
examples.meaningEn: I give a present.

examples.ja: 友達に花をあげた。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni hana wo ageta.
examples.meaningEn: I gave flowers to my friend.

examples.ja: 父に時計をあげます。
examples.romaji: Chichi ni tokei wo agemasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will give a watch to my father.

examples.ja: 彼女に手紙をあげたい。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni tegami wo agetai.
examples.meaningEn: I want to give her a letter.

title: Nounをいただく (Noun wo itadaku)
formation: Noun + を + いただく
shortExplanationEn: To receive or accept something politely; 'to receive', 'to get', 'to accept'.
longExplanationEn: The Nounをいただく grammar point is used to express the action of receiving or accepting something in a polite or humble manner. The verb 'いただく' is a more respectful version of the verb 'もらう' (to receive), so using 'いただく' shows humility and respect towards the person who is giving you something.

examples.ja: プレゼントをいただきありがとうございます。
examples.romaji: Purezento wo itadaki arigatou gozaimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Thank you for the gift that I received.

examples.ja: 先生からアドバイスをいただきました。
examples.romaji: Sensei kara adobaisu wo itadakimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I received advice from my teacher.

examples.ja: このコーヒーをいただいていいですか？
examples.romaji: Kono koohii wo itadaite ii desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: May I have this coffee?

examples.ja: 会社の上司に昇進のお知らせをいただきました。
examples.romaji: Kaisha no joushi ni shoushin no oshirase wo itadakimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I received a promotion notification from my boss at the company.

title: Nounをくださる
formation: Noun + を + くださる
shortExplanationEn: Used to express someone doing something as a favor or for the benefit of the speaker.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point 'Nounをくださる' is a polite expression used to say that someone is giving or doing something for the benefit of the speaker. It is often used when referring to people who have a higher social status or when expressing gratitude for their actions.

examples.ja: 先生が資料をくださった。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga shiryou wo kudasatta.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher gave me the materials.

examples.ja: お母さんがおやつをくださる。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ga oyatsu wo kudasaru.
examples.meaningEn: My mother gives me snacks.

examples.ja: 上司がアドバイスをくださいました。
examples.romaji: Joushi ga adobaisu wo kudasaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: My boss gave me advice.

examples.ja: 友達がプレゼントをくださった。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga purezento wo kudasatta.
examples.meaningEn: My friend gave me a present.

title: Noun を くれる (Noun wo kureru)
formation: Giver + が + Receiver + に + Noun + を + くれる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that someone gives a thing to the speaker or someone close to the speaker.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Noun を くれる is used to express when someone (other than the speaker) gives something to the speaker or someone close to the speaker. The giver is the subject, and the receiver is the speaker or someone close to the speaker. くれる indicates that the action of giving 'favors' the speaker or someone close to the speaker.

examples.ja: 友達が私にプレゼントをくれました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga watashi ni purezento wo kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: My friend gave me a present.

examples.ja: 彼が私に花をくれた。
examples.romaji: Kare ga watashi ni hana wo kureta.
examples.meaningEn: He gave me flowers.

examples.ja: 先生が私に本をくれました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga watashi ni hon wo kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher gave me a book.

examples.ja: お母さんが弟にお菓子をくれました。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ga otouto ni okashi wo kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: Mom gave my little brother some sweets.

title: Noun を さしあげる (Noun wo sashiageru)
formation: Noun + を + さしあげる
shortExplanationEn: A polite way of expressing to give something.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Noun を さしあげる is used to say that you are giving something to someone in a polite way. When offering something to a person of higher status, this expression is used. It is used in conjunction with a noun and replaces the regular verb あげる (ageru) to give.

examples.ja: 先生に本をさしあげます。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni hon wo sashiagemasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will give a book to the teacher.

examples.ja: お母さんに花をさしあげたいです。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ni hana wo sashiagetai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to give flowers to my mother.

examples.ja: お客様にお茶をさしあげましょう。
examples.romaji: Okyakusama ni ocha wo sashiagemashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's offer tea to the guest.

examples.ja: 先輩にプレゼントをさしあげたら、喜んでいました。
examples.romaji: Senpai ni purezento wo sashiagetara, yorokonde imashita.
examples.meaningEn: When I gave a present to my senior, they were delighted.

title: Noun もらう (Noun wo morau)
formation: Noun + を + もらう
shortExplanationEn: To receive or get something from someone; 'to receive', 'to get'.
longExplanationEn: The もらう (morau) grammar point is used to express the action of receiving or getting something from someone. It usually emphasizes that the action was done for you or you were the beneficiary of the action. The formation requires a noun followed by the particle を (wo) before 'もらう'.

examples.ja: 友達からプレゼントをもらいました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi kara purezento wo moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I received a gift from my friend.

examples.ja: 先生にアドバイスをもらいました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni adobaisu wo moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I got advice from my teacher.

examples.ja: お父さんからお小遣いをもらう。
examples.romaji: Otousan kara okozukai wo morau.
examples.meaningEn: To receive an allowance from one's father.

examples.ja: 彼女に手紙をもらった。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni tegami wo moratta.
examples.meaningEn: I got a letter from her.

title: Noun + 中 (Noun + ちゅう)
formation: Noun + 中 (ちゅう)
shortExplanationEn: Express 'in the middle of', 'during', or 'in the process of'.
longExplanationEn: The 中 (ちゅう) grammar point is used to express that something is in the middle of, during, or in the process of happening. It is attached to a noun to convey this meaning (e.g., 勉強中, 仕事中, 会議中). It is also sometimes read as じゅう depending on the word (e.g., 授業中).

examples.ja: 彼は仕事中です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shigotochuu desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is in the middle of work.

examples.ja: 試験中、携帯電話を使ってはいけません。
examples.romaji: Shiken chuu, keitai denwa wo tsukatte wa ikemasen.
examples.meaningEn: During the exam, you must not use your mobile phone.

examples.ja: 映画を観ている最中に、彼は泣き始めました。
examples.romaji: Eiga o mite iru saichuu ni, kare wa naki hajimemashita.
examples.meaningEn: In the middle of watching the movie, he started to cry.

examples.ja: 彼女は今勉強中です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ima benkyouchuu desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is currently studying.

title: Verb させられる (Verb-saserareru)
formation: Group 1 Verb: Replace ～う with ～わせられる, Group 2 Verb: Replace ～る with ～させられる, Group 3 Verb: する → させられる, くる → こさせられる
shortExplanationEn: Express the passive causative form; 'to be made to', 'to be forced to'.
longExplanationEn: The Verb させられる grammar point is the passive causative form of the verb. It is used to describe a situation where someone or something causes or forces another person to do something. This construction of passive causative is useful to show that the action was not done voluntarily by the person.

examples.ja: 子供たちは先生に宿題をやらせられました。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi wa sensei ni shukudai wo yaraseraremashita.
examples.meaningEn: The children were made to do their homework by the teacher.

examples.ja: 彼は会社にもっと働かせられた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kaisha ni motto hatarakaserareta.
examples.meaningEn: He was forced to work more by the company.

examples.ja: 私は友達にその機密を話させられました。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa tomodachi ni sono kimitsu o hanasaseraremashita.
examples.meaningEn: I was made to reveal that secret by my friend.

examples.ja: 怪我をした選手は、コーチに休ませられました。
examples.romaji: Kega wo shita senshu wa, kouchi ni yasumaseraremashita.
examples.meaningEn: The injured player was forced to rest by the coach.

title: Verb させる (Verb-saseru)
formation: Group 1 Verbs: Change the last hiragana to ～せる, Group 2 Verbs: Remove ～る and add ～させる, Group 3 Verbs: する → させる, くる → こさせる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'to make someone do something', 'to let someone do something', or 'to cause someone to do something'.
longExplanationEn: The ～させる verb form is used to describe causing or allowing someone to do something. It can be translated as 'to make someone do something', 'to let someone do something', or 'to cause someone to do something' in English. The formation of the ～させる form depends on the verb it is attached to.

examples.ja: 先生が生徒に宿題をさせました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga seito ni shukudai o sasemashita.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher made the students do homework.

examples.ja: 母が私に部屋を掃除させました。
examples.romaji: Haha ga watashi ni heya o souji sasemashita.
examples.meaningEn: My mother made me clean my room.

examples.ja: 彼女にピアノを弾かせてあげました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni piano o hikasete agemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I let her play the piano.

examples.ja: 上司は彼にそのプログラムを作らせました。
examples.romaji: Joushi wa kare ni sono puroguramu o tsukurasemashita.
examples.meaningEn: The boss made him create the program.

title: Verb た ことがある (Verb ta koto ga aru)
formation: Verb-た-form + ことがある
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the experience of having done something in the past.
longExplanationEn: The ～たことがある grammar point is used to express the experience of having done an action in the past. It indicates that the speaker has had the experience of doing something at least once before. It is formed by adding ことがある to the た-form of the verb.

examples.ja: 日本に行ったことがあります。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni itta koto ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I have been to Japan before.

examples.ja: 寿司を食べたことがありますか？
examples.romaji: Sushi wo tabeta koto ga arimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Have you ever eaten sushi before?
