examples.ja: 彼はマラソンを走ったことがあります。
examples.romaji: Kare wa marason wo hashitta koto ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He has experienced running a marathon.

examples.ja: 私はスカイダイビングをしたことがありません。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa sukaidaibingu wo shita koto ga arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I have never experienced skydiving.

title: Verb た ときに (Verb た ときに)
formation: Verb-た + ときに
shortExplanationEn: Express an action or event that occurred at a specific time in the past; 'when', 'at the time'.
longExplanationEn: The たときに grammar point is used to express an action that happened at a specific time in the past, usually with a focus on a specific event or moment. It can be translated as 'when' or 'at the time' in English. This grammar point requires a verb in its た-form followed by ときに to indicate the time at which the action occurred.

examples.ja: 映画を見たときに、泣きました。
examples.romaji: Eiga wo mita toki ni, nakimashita.
examples.meaningEn: When I watched the movie, I cried.

examples.ja: 彼に初めて会ったときに、遅刻していました。
examples.romaji: Kare ni hajimete atta toki ni, chikoku shite imashita.
examples.meaningEn: When I first met him, he was late.

examples.ja: 日本へ行ったときに、たくさんの写真を撮りました。
examples.romaji: Nihon e itta toki ni, takusan no shashin wo torimashita.
examples.meaningEn: When I went to Japan, I took a lot of photos.

examples.ja: 友達が家に来たときに、ゲームをしました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga ie ni kita toki ni, geemu wo shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: When my friend came to my house, we played games.

title: Verb た ところ (Verb ta tokoro)
formation: Verb (た form) + ところ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the situation right after an action has taken place.
longExplanationEn: The たところ grammar point is used to describe a situation that occurs right after an action has taken place. It is often translated as 'just now', 'right after', or 'when'. The formation is relatively simple as it only requires the verb in its た form (past tense) followed by ところ.

examples.ja: ドアを開けたところ、猫が飛び出してきた。
examples.romaji: Doa wo aketa tokoro, neko ga tobidashite kita.
examples.meaningEn: When I opened the door, a cat came jumping out.

examples.ja: 電話を切ったところ、また鳴った。
examples.romaji: Denwa wo kitta tokoro, mata natta.
examples.meaningEn: Right after I hung up the phone, it rang again.

examples.ja: 宿題を終わらせたところで、友達から連絡が来た。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo owaraseta tokoro de, tomodachi kara renraku ga kita.
examples.meaningEn: Just as I finished my homework, I got a message from my friend.

examples.ja: 買い物をして帰ったところ、財布を失くしたことに気づいた。
examples.romaji: Kaimono wo shite kaetta tokoro, saifu wo nakushita koto ni kizuita.
examples.meaningEn: After shopping and returning home, I realized I had lost my wallet.

title: Verb たほうがいい (〜ta hou ga ii)
formation: Verb (past tense) + ほうがいい
shortExplanationEn: Used to give advice or make a suggestion to someone - 'it's better if...'.
longExplanationEn: The たほうがいい grammar point is used to give advice or make a suggestion to someone. It can be translated as 'it's better if...', 'should', or 'it's a good idea to'. It is formed by conjugating the verb into the past tense (た-form) and adding ほうがいい.

examples.ja: 早く寝たほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Hayaku neta hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: It's better if you go to bed early.

examples.ja: もっと勉強したほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Motto benkyou shita hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: You should study more.

examples.ja: 病気の時は、薬を飲んだほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Byouki no toki wa, kusuri wo nonda hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: It's a good idea to take medicine when you're sick.

examples.ja: コーヒーを飲まないほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Koohii wo nomanai hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: It's better if you don't drink coffee.

title: Verb ために (tame ni)
formation:
1) Verb (dictionary form) + ために (purpose)
2) い-Adjective + ために (reason)
3) な-Adjective + な + ために (reason)
4) Noun + の + ために (reason)
shortExplanationEn: Used to describe the purpose or reason for an action; 'for the sake of', 'in order to', 'because of'.
longExplanationEn: The ために (tame ni) grammar point is used to express either the purpose of doing something (in which case it translates to 'for the sake of' or 'in order to') or the cause/reason for a situation (in which case it translates to 'because of'). Typically, we use the dictionary form of the verb (e.g., 合格する) for the 'in order to' meaning. For an adjective or noun, attach ために appropriately (い-Adjective + ために, な-Adjective + な + ために, Noun + の + ために).

examples.ja: 試験に合格するために、毎日勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Shiken ni goukaku suru tame ni, mainichi benkyou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I study every day in order to pass the exam.

examples.ja: 健康のために、毎日運動しなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Kenkou no tame ni, mainichi undou shinakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: For the sake of health, I must exercise daily.

examples.ja: 仕事が忙しいために、友達と会う時間がありません。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga isogashii tame ni, tomodachi to au jikan ga arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Because my work is busy, I don't have time to meet friends.

examples.ja: 雨が降ったために、試合が中止されました。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futta tame ni, shiai ga chuushi saremashita.
examples.meaningEn: The game was canceled because of the rain.

title: Verb つもり (〜tsumori)
formation: Verb-casual + つもり
shortExplanationEn: Express the intention, plan, or determination to do something; 'I intend to', 'I plan to', 'I'm determined to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～つもり grammar point is used to express the intention, plan, or determination to do something. It can be translated as 'I intend to', 'I plan to', or 'I'm determined to' in English. It is used with verbs in the casual form.

examples.ja: 明日、友達に会うつもりです。
examples.romaji: Ashita, tomodachi ni au tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to meet my friend tomorrow.

examples.ja: 今夜、晩ご飯を作るつもりです。
examples.romaji: Konya, bangohan o tsukuru tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I intend to cook dinner tonight.

examples.ja: 来年、留学するつもりです。
examples.romaji: Rainen, ryuugaku suru tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to study abroad next year.

examples.ja: この週末、山に登るつもりです。
examples.romaji: Kono shuumatsu, yama ni noboru tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I'm determined to climb the mountain this weekend.

title: Verb てあげる (Verb te ageru)
formation: Verb-て form + あげる
shortExplanationEn: Used to express doing something for someone; 'do something for someone'.
longExplanationEn: The てあげる grammar point is used to express that the speaker or someone other than the listener is doing something for someone else. It shows that the action is being done as a favor or out of kindness. This grammar point is used with verbs in their て-form, followed by あげる.

examples.ja: 友達が疲れていたから、荷物を持ってあげた。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga tsukareteita kara, nimotsu o motte ageta.
examples.meaningEn: Since my friend was tired, I carried their luggage for them.

examples.ja: 弟は宿題がわからなかったので、手伝ってあげた。
examples.romaji: Otouto wa shukudai ga wakaranakatta node, tetsudatte ageta.
examples.meaningEn: My younger brother couldn't understand the homework, so I helped him.

examples.ja: 彼女が遅れそうだったから、駅まで迎えに行ってあげた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga okuresou datta kara, eki made mukae ni itte ageta.
examples.meaningEn: She was running late, so I went to pick her up at the station.

examples.ja: 犬はのどが渇いたそうだったので、水を飲ませてあげた。
examples.romaji: Inu wa nodo ga kawaita sou datta node, mizu o nomasete ageta.
examples.meaningEn: The dog seemed thirsty, so I gave it water to drink.

title: Verb て ある (Verb-te aru)
formation: Verb-て form + ある
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that an action has been done and its result is still in effect.
longExplanationEn: The ～てある grammar point is used to express that an action has been done and its result remains in effect. It indicates that the action was intentionally performed and its effect continues. The formation is done by conjugating the verb into its て-form and then adding ある.

examples.ja: 窓が開けてある。
examples.romaji: Mado ga akete aru.
examples.meaningEn: The window has been opened (and remains open).

examples.ja: 宿題がもう終わってある。
examples.romaji: Shukudai ga mou owatte aru.
examples.meaningEn: The homework has already been done (and remains that way).

examples.ja: 洗濯物が全部干してある。
examples.romaji: Sentakumono ga zenbu hoshite aru.
examples.meaningEn: The laundry has been hung up to dry (and remains that way).

examples.ja: この部屋はきれいに掃除してある。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa kirei ni souji shite aru.
examples.meaningEn: This room has been cleaned (and remains clean).

title: Verb て いく (Verb-te iku)
formation: Verb in て-form + いく
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an action that continues on into the future or a change that progresses over time.
longExplanationEn: The て いく grammar point is used to indicate that an action will continue or a change will progress into the future. It can be attached to the て-form of a verb and is often translated as 'to keep', 'to continue', or 'to go on' in English.

examples.ja: このまま練習を続けていくと、上達するでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono mama renshuu o tsuzukete iku to, joutatsu suru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If you keep practicing like this, you will improve.

examples.ja: 彼は成長していくうちに、自立心が強くなった。
examples.romaji: Kare wa seichou shite iku uchi ni, jiritsushin ga tsuyoku natta.
examples.meaningEn: As he grew up, his independence became stronger.

examples.ja: この会社はこれからも技術革新を行っていくでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono kaisha wa korekara mo gijutsu kakushin o okonatte iku deshou.
examples.meaningEn: This company will continue to innovate in technology in the future.

examples.ja: 子供たちはどんどん学んでいく。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi wa dondon manande iku.
examples.meaningEn: Children will keep learning more and more.

title: Verb ていただきたい (te itadakitai)
formation: Verb-て form + いただきたい
shortExplanationEn: Express a polite request or desire that someone does something for you.
longExplanationEn: The ていただきたい grammar point is used to express a polite request or a desire for someone to do something for you. It is formed by attaching いただきたい to the て-form of the verb. It is more polite than the て-form + ほしい construction.

examples.ja: 資料をコピーしていただきたいです。
examples.romaji: Shiryou wo kopii shite itadakitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I would like you to make copies of the documents.

examples.ja: この仕事を手伝っていただきたいのですが。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto wo tetsudatte itadakitai no desu ga.
examples.meaningEn: I would like to ask for your assistance with this work.

examples.ja: もしよろしければ、指導していただきたいです。
examples.romaji: Moshi yoroshikereba, shidou shite itadakitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: If it is not too much trouble, I would like you to guide me.

examples.ja: 駅まで送っていただきたいんですが。
examples.romaji: Eki made okutte itadakitain desu ga.
examples.meaningEn: I was wondering if you could give me a ride to the station.

title: Verb ていただく (〜te itadaku)
formation: Verb-て form + いただく
shortExplanationEn: Used to express receiving someone's favor or help; 'to kindly do'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ていただく grammar point is used to express that someone has kindly done a favor or provided help. It is a more polite and humble way of saying that you received someone's help. The formation is created by attaching the て-form of the verb + いただく.

examples.ja: メールを送っていただいて、ありがとうございます。
examples.romaji: Meeru o okutte itadaite, arigatou gozaimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Thank you for kindly sending the email.

examples.ja: 荷物を運んでいただいて、助かりました。
examples.romaji: Nimotsu o hakonde itadaite, tasukarimashita.
examples.meaningEn: You really helped me by carrying the luggage.

examples.ja: 資料をチェックしていただきたいです。
examples.romaji: Shiryou o chekku shite itadakitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I would like you to kindly check the documents.

examples.ja: 先生にアドバイスをしていただきました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni adobaisu o shite itadakimashita.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher kindly gave me advice.

title: Verb て いただけませんか (Verb te itadakemasen ka)
formation: Verb-て form + いただけませんか
shortExplanationEn: A polite request; 'Could you please...', 'Would you mind...'
longExplanationEn: The て いただけませんか grammar point is used to make a polite request to someone. It is a more polite form of て くれませんか, and can be translated as 'Could you please...' or 'Would you mind...' in English. To use it, change the verb to its て-form and add いただけませんか.

examples.ja: この手紙を投函していただけませんか。
examples.romaji: Kono tegami wo toukan shite itadakemasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Could you please mail this letter for me?

examples.ja: この荷物を持っていただけませんか。
examples.romaji: Kono nimotsu wo motte itadakemasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Could you please carry this luggage for me?

examples.ja: 隣の部屋にいる人に静かにしていただけませんか。
examples.romaji: Tonari no heya ni iru hito ni shizuka ni shite itadakemasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Could you please ask the person next door to be quiet?

examples.ja: 説明書を読んでいただけませんか。
examples.romaji: Setsumeisho wo yonde itadakemasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Could you please read the instruction manual?

title: Verb て いる (Verb-te iru)
formation: Verb (て-form) + いる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an ongoing action or a state resulting from a past action.
longExplanationEn: The ている form is used to express either an ongoing action or a state resulting from a past action. It is formed by conjugating the verb into its て-form and then adding いる. In English, this can be translated as verb-ing or 'have verb-ed'. Its usage depends on the type of verb: action verbs or state verbs.

examples.ja: 今、本を読んでいます。
examples.romaji: Ima, hon wo yonde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am reading a book now.

examples.ja: 彼は英語を勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eigo wo benkyou shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is studying English.

examples.ja: ケーキが冷蔵庫に入っています。
examples.romaji: Keeki ga reizouko ni haitte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: The cake is in the refrigerator.

examples.ja: 部屋がきれいになっています。
examples.romaji: Heya ga kirei ni natte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: The room has become clean.

title: Verb て いる ところ (Verb te iru tokoro)
formation: Verb-て form + いる ところ
shortExplanationEn: Describes an action that is currently happening; 'in the middle of', 'in the process of'.
longExplanationEn: The て いる ところ grammar point is used to express an action that is currently happening or taking place. It can be translated as 'in the middle of' or 'in the process of' in English. It is formed by using the て-form of a verb followed by いる and ところ.

examples.ja: 彼は宿題をしているところです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shukudai wo shite iru tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is in the middle of doing his homework.

examples.ja: 私たちは映画を見ているところでした。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa eiga wo mite iru tokoro deshita.
examples.meaningEn: We were in the process of watching a movie.

examples.ja: 子供たちは公園で遊んでいるところです。
examples.romaji: Kodomotachi wa kouen de asonde iru tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: The kids are in the middle of playing in the park.

examples.ja: 彼女はケーキを作っているところで、すぐ来ます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa keeki wo tsukutte iru tokoro de, sugu kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is in the process of making a cake and will come soon.

title: Verb ている間に (te iru aida ni)
formation: Verb-て form + いる + 間に
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'while' or 'during the time' something is happening.
longExplanationEn: The ～ている間に grammar point is used to express that an action or event occurs during the time another action or event is happening. It is often translated to 'while' or 'during the time' in English. It is formed by combining the て-form of a verb with いる and adding 間に.

examples.ja: 映画を見ている間に、彼は寝てしまった。
examples.romaji: Eiga o mite iru aida ni, kare wa nete shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: While watching the movie, he fell asleep.

examples.ja: 子供が遊んでいる間に、私は料理を作ります。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ga asonde iru aida ni, watashi wa ryouri o tsukurimasu.
examples.meaningEn: While the children are playing, I'll cook.

examples.ja: 会議が行われている間に、雨が降り始めた。
examples.romaji: Kaigi ga okonawarete iru aida ni, ame ga furihajimeta.
examples.meaningEn: While the meeting was being held, it started raining.

examples.ja: 彼が仕事をしている間に、私たちは買い物に行く。
examples.romaji: Kare ga shigoto o shite iru aida ni, watashitachi wa kaimono ni iku.
examples.meaningEn: While he is working, we'll go shopping.

title: Verb ておく (〜te oku)
formation: Verb-て form + おく
shortExplanationEn: Perform an action in preparation for a future situation; 'to do something in advance'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ておく grammar point is used when you want to express performing an action in preparation for a future situation or to do something in advance. It is attached to the te-form of a verb and expresses the idea of doing something beforehand, as a preparatory action.

examples.ja: 駅で切符を買っておこう。
examples.romaji: Eki de kippu wo katte okou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's buy the tickets in advance at the station.

examples.ja: 明日早く起きるために、今晩早く寝ておきます。
examples.romaji: Ashita hayaku okiru tame ni, konban hayaku nete okimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go to bed early tonight to wake up early tomorrow.

examples.ja: 試験前に勉強しておかないと、失敗するかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Shiken mae ni benkyou shite okanai to, shippai suru kamo shirenai.
examples.meaningEn: If I don't study in advance before the exam, I might fail.

examples.ja: 彼が到着する前に、部屋をきれいに掃除しておきましょう。
examples.romaji: Kare ga touchaku suru mae ni, heya wo kirei ni souji shite okimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's clean the room before he arrives.

title: Verb て くださいませんか (Verb-te kudasaimasen ka)
formation: Verb in て-form + くださいませんか
shortExplanationEn: Polite request for asking someone for a favor.
longExplanationEn: The て くださいませんか grammar point is a polite way of asking someone for a favor or requesting them to do something. It is more formal than the simple て form followed by ください, and it is commonly used in more formal settings or when speaking to someone who is not close.

examples.ja: コーヒーを飲んでくださいませんか。
examples.romaji: Koohii o nonde kudasaimasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Would you please drink some coffee?

examples.ja: ドアを閉めてくださいませんか。
examples.romaji: Doa o shimete kudasaimasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Would you please close the door?

examples.ja: 明日お昼に会ってくださいませんか。
examples.romaji: Ashita ohiru ni atte kudasaimasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Would you please meet me at lunch tomorrow?

examples.ja: その資料を送ってくださいませんか。
examples.romaji: Sono shiryou o okutte kudasaimasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Would you please send me that document?

title: Verb て くださる (Verb-te kudasaru)
formation: Verb て-form + くださる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses gratitude for receiving someone's favor; 'to kindly do'.
longExplanationEn: The て くださる grammar point is used to express gratitude for receiving a favor or action from someone else. It is an honorific way to say that someone kindly does something for the speaker or the listener. The verb is conjugated to its ～て form and followed by くださる.

examples.ja: 先生が手伝ってくださる。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga tetsudatte kudasaru.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher kindly helps.

examples.ja: お母さんが美味しい料理を作ってくださる。
examples.romaji: Okaasan ga oishii ryouri wo tsukutte kudasaru.
examples.meaningEn: My mother kindly cooks delicious meals.

examples.ja: 友達が私を駅まで送ってくださる。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga watashi wo eki made okutte kudasaru.
examples.meaningEn: My friend kindly gives me a ride to the station.

examples.ja: 部長がすぐに質問に答えてくださる。
examples.romaji: Buchou ga sugu ni shitsumon ni kotaete kudasaru.
examples.meaningEn: The manager kindly answers the questions immediately.

title: Verb て くる (Verb te kuru)
formation: Verb て-form + くる
shortExplanationEn: An action that starts in the past and continues to the present or future; 'come to', 'begin to', 'become'.
longExplanationEn: The て くる grammar point is used to express an action that starts in the past and continues to the present or future. It can also show a change in state or condition. The formation involves attaching the て-form of the verb followed by くる.

examples.ja: 最近、日本語が分かってくるようになりました。
examples.romaji: Saikin, nihongo ga wakatte kuru you ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Recently, I've come to understand Japanese.

examples.ja: 寒くなってきたので、コートを着ましょう。
examples.romaji: Samuku natte kita node, kooto wo kimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Since it's gotten cold, let's put on a coat.

examples.ja: 雨がやんできました。
examples.romaji: Ame ga yande kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: The rain has stopped (and it's cleared up).

examples.ja: 彼がだんだん怖くなってきた。
examples.romaji: Kare ga dandan kowaku natte kita.
examples.meaningEn: He has become progressively scarier.

title: Verb てくれませんか (〜te kuremasen ka)
formation: Verb-て form + くれませんか
shortExplanationEn: A polite way to request someone to do something for you; 'Could you please', 'Would you please', 'Won't you'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てくれませんか grammar point is used to ask someone politely to do something for you. It is formed by using the て-form of a verb followed by くれませんか. It translates to 'Could you please', 'Would you please', or 'Won't you' in English.

examples.ja: ドアを開けてくれませんか。
examples.romaji: Doa wo akete kuremasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Could you please open the door?

examples.ja: これを見てくれませんか。
examples.romaji: Kore wo mite kuremasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Would you please take a look at this?

examples.ja: 手伝ってくれませんか。
examples.romaji: Tetsudatte kuremasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Won't you help me?

examples.ja: 明日、迎えに来てくれませんか。
examples.romaji: Ashita, mukae ni kite kuremasen ka.
examples.meaningEn: Could you please come to pick me up tomorrow?

title: Verb て くれる (Verb-te kureru)
formation: Verb-te form + くれる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that someone does something for the speaker (or another person in speaker's group); 'to do something for me/us'.
longExplanationEn: The て くれる grammar point is used to express when someone does something for, or gives something to the speaker (or someone in the speaker's group). The action is usually done willingly or as a favor by the person performing the action. It is usually translated into English as 'to do something for me' or 'to give something to me'.

examples.ja: 友達が宿題を手伝ってくれました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga shukudai wo tetsudatte kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: My friend helped me with my homework.

examples.ja: 弟が部屋を掃除してくれた。
examples.romaji: Otouto ga heya wo souji shite kureta.
examples.meaningEn: My younger brother cleaned the room for me.

examples.ja: 先生が質問に答えてくれます。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga shitsumon ni kotaete kuremasu.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher answers my questions for me.

examples.ja: 彼女が手紙を書いてくれる。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga tegami wo kaite kureru.
examples.meaningEn: She writes a letter for me.

title: Verb て + さしあげる (Verb TE sashiageru)
formation: Verb-てform + さしあげる
shortExplanationEn: A polite way to express giving or doing something for someone.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point 'Verb て + さしあげる' is used to express the action of giving or doing something for someone in a polite manner. It is mainly used when the speaker is offering or doing something for someone of higher social status or showing respect towards the person. To form this expression, you need to conjugate the verb into its て-form and attach さしあげる.

examples.ja: お茶を入れてさしあげます。
examples.romaji: Ocha wo irete sashiagemasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will prepare and serve tea (for you).

examples.ja: メッセージを送ってさしあげました。
examples.romaji: Messeiji wo okutte sashiagemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I have sent the message (for you).

examples.ja: 荷物を持ってさしあげましょうか。
examples.romaji: Nimotsu wo motte sashiagemashou ka.
examples.meaningEn: Shall I carry the luggage (for you)?

examples.ja: お電話をかけてさしあげます。
examples.romaji: Odenwa wo kakete sashiagemasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make a phone call (for you).

title: Verb てしまう (〜te shimau)
formation: Verb-te form + しまう
shortExplanationEn: Expresses unintentional actions or actions with regrettable outcomes; 'to end up doing', 'to accidentally do'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てしまう grammar point is used to indicate unintentional actions or actions with regrettable outcomes. It can often be translated as 'to end up doing' or 'to accidentally do' in English. The formation requires adding しまう to the て-form of a verb.

examples.ja: ケーキを全部食べてしまった。
examples.romaji: Keeki wo zenbu tabete shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: I ended up eating the whole cake.

examples.ja: 宿題を忘れてしまいました。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo wasurete shimaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I accidentally forgot my homework.

examples.ja: 彼に秘密を言ってしまった。
examples.romaji: Kare ni himitsu wo itte shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: I accidentally told him the secret.

examples.ja: 値札を見ずに高い服を買ってしまった。
examples.romaji: Nefuda wo mizu ni takai fuku wo katte shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: I ended up buying expensive clothes without looking at the price tag.

title: Verb て ほしい (Verb-te hoshii)
formation: Verb-て form + ほしい
shortExplanationEn: Express a desire for someone else to do something; 'I want (someone) to'.
longExplanationEn: The て ほしい grammar point is used to express the speaker's desire for someone else to do an action. It is formed by combining the て-form of a verb with ほしい. The subject is usually followed by the particle に, but it can be dropped in casual speech.

examples.ja: 子供にもっと勉強してほしい。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ni motto benkyou shite hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want my child to study more.

examples.ja: 彼にもっと早く来てほしい。
examples.romaji: Kare ni motto hayaku kite hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want him to come earlier.

examples.ja: 友達にその本を読んでほしい。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni sono hon o yonde hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want my friend to read that book.

examples.ja: 先生に質問に答えてほしい。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni shitsumon ni kotaete hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want the teacher to answer the question.

title: Verb てみる (〜te miru)
formation: Verb-て form + みる
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'try to' or 'attempt to do'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てみる grammar point is used with verbs to express 'trying' or 'attempting' to do something. It emphasizes the idea of giving something a go or trying it out, often to see what the results or outcome will be.

examples.ja: この料理を作ってみます。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri o tsukutte mimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will try to make this dish.

examples.ja: その服を着てみてください。
examples.romaji: Sono fuku o kite mite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please try on those clothes.

examples.ja: 新しいレストランに行ってみよう。
examples.romaji: Atarashii resutoran ni itte miyou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's try going to the new restaurant.

examples.ja: 映画を見てみたいです。
examples.romaji: Eiga o mite mitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to try watching the movie.

title: Verb てもらいたい (～te moraitai)
formation: Verb-て-form + もらいたい
shortExplanationEn: Express a desire to receive a favor or service from someone else.
longExplanationEn: The ～てもらいたい grammar point is used to express one's desire to receive a favor, service, or action from someone else. It is formed by attaching もらいたい to the て-form of verbs. This structure emphasizes the help or favor the speaker wants from someone else.

examples.ja: この荷物を運んでもらいたいです。
examples.romaji: Kono nimotsu o hakonde moraitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want you to carry this luggage for me.

examples.ja: 彼にそのニュースを教えてもらいたい。
examples.romaji: Kare ni sono nyuusu o oshiete moraitai.
examples.meaningEn: I want him to tell me the news.

examples.ja: 友達に日本語を教えてもらいたい。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni Nihongo o oshiete moraitai.
examples.meaningEn: I want my friend to teach me Japanese.

examples.ja: スタッフにチェックインの方法を説明してもらいたい。
examples.romaji: Sutaffu ni chekkuin no houhou o setsumei shite moraitai.
examples.meaningEn: I want the staff to explain the check-in process to me.

title: Verb て もらう (Verb-te morau)
formation: Verb-て form + もらう
shortExplanationEn: Express receiving a favor or action from someone.
longExplanationEn: The Verb て もらう grammar point is used to express that the speaker or a third person receives a favor or action from someone else. It describes an action performed by someone for the benefit of the speaker or a third person, implying gratitude or request from the beneficiary.

examples.ja: 友達に宿題を手伝ってもらった。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni shukudai o tetsudatte moratta.
examples.meaningEn: I had my friend help me with my homework.

examples.ja: 母に部屋を掃除してもらいました。
examples.romaji: Haha ni heya o souji shite moraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I had my mother clean my room.

examples.ja: 彼に荷物を持ってもらいたい。
examples.romaji: Kare ni nimotsu o motte moraitai.
examples.meaningEn: I want him to carry my luggage for me.

examples.ja: 先生に質問に答えてもらえますか？
examples.romaji: Sensei ni shitsumon ni kotaete moraemasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Can I have the teacher answer my question?

title: Verb てもらえませんか (～te moraemasen ka)
formation: Verb-て form + もらえませんか
shortExplanationEn: A polite way to ask for a favor or request someone to do something.
longExplanationEn: The ～てもらえませんか grammar point is a polite and indirect way of asking someone to do something for you or asking for a favor. It is formed by conjugating the verb into the て-form and adding もらえませんか. This form is used in conversations to show politeness and humility.

examples.ja: この手紙を見てもらえませんか。
examples.romaji: Kono tegami o mite moraemasen ka?
examples.meaningEn: Could you take a look at this letter for me?

examples.ja: 荷物を持ってもらえませんか。
examples.romaji: Nimotsu o motte moraemasen ka?
examples.meaningEn: Could you carry this luggage for me?

examples.ja: 部屋を掃除してもらえませんか。
examples.romaji: Heya o souji shite moraemasen ka?
examples.meaningEn: Could you clean the room for me?

examples.ja: 明日のミーティングに参加してもらえませんか。
examples.romaji: Ashita no miitingu ni sanka shite moraemasen ka?
examples.meaningEn: Could you attend tomorrow's meeting for me?

title: Verb ない ことがある (Verb-nai koto ga aru)
formation: Verb-negative form + ことがある
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something doesn't always happen or there are occasions when it doesn't happen.
longExplanationEn: The ないことがある grammar point is used to indicate that there are instances or times when something doesn't happen or isn't done. It is formed by combining the negative form of a verb (ない) with ことがある.

examples.ja: 彼は会議に出席しないことがある。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kaigi ni shusseki shinai koto ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: There are times when he doesn't attend meetings.

examples.ja: この電車は定刻に来ないことがある。
examples.romaji: Kono densha wa teikoku ni konai koto ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: There are occasions when this train doesn't come on time.

examples.ja: 私は朝食を食べないことがある。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa choushoku o tabenai koto ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: There are times when I don't eat breakfast.

examples.ja: 彼女は電話に出ないことがある。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa denwa ni denai koto ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: There are occasions when she doesn't answer the phone.

title: Verb ない + ことにする (Verb nai koto ni suru)
formation: Verb-ない form + ことにする
shortExplanationEn: To decide not to do something.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Verb ない + ことにする is used to express the decision not to do something. This structure shows that the speaker has made a conscious choice to not take the action expressed in the verb.

examples.ja: 明日のパーティーに行かないことにする。
examples.romaji: Ashita no paatii ni ikanai koto ni suru.
examples.meaningEn: I've decided not to go to tomorrow's party.

examples.ja: そのケーキを食べないことにします。
examples.romaji: Sono keeki wo tabenai koto ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I've decided not to eat that cake.

examples.ja: 今日はジョギングをしないことにする。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa jogingu wo shinai koto ni suru.
examples.meaningEn: I've decided not to go jogging today.

examples.ja: 彼と会わないことにしました。
examples.romaji: Kare to awanai koto ni shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I've decided not to meet him.

title: Verb ない ことになる (Verb nai koto ni naru)
formation: Verb-negative (ない) + ことになる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a result; 'end up not', 'it will become that', 'it would mean that'.
longExplanationEn: The Verb ない ことになる grammar point is used to express a result or consequence. It translates to 'end up not', 'it will become that', or 'it would mean that' in English. It is formed by using the negative form (ない) of a verb followed by ことになる.

examples.ja: 今日彼が来ないことになると、私たちの予定はキャンセルされます。
examples.romaji: Kyou kare ga konai koto ni naru to, watashitachi no yotei wa kyanseru saremasu.
examples.meaningEn: If he doesn't come today, our plans will be canceled.

examples.ja: このプロジェクトが進まないことになると、収益が減るでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono purojekuto ga susumanai koto ni naru to, shuueki ga heru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If this project doesn't progress, it would mean that revenue will decrease.

examples.ja: 彼女は試験に合格しないことになると、卒業が遅れます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa shiken ni goukaku shinai koto ni naru to, sotsugyou ga okuremasu.
examples.meaningEn: If she ends up not passing the exam, her graduation will be delayed.

examples.ja: 雨が降ると、ピクニックが行われないことになるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu to, pikunikku ga okonawarenai koto ni naru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If it rains, it will become that the picnic doesn't take place.

title: Verb ないほうがいい (Verb nai hou ga ii)
formation: Verb-negative form + ほうがいい
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the idea that it is better not to do something.
longExplanationEn: The ないほうがいい grammar point is used to suggest that it's better not to do a certain action. It is formed by taking the negative form of the verb and adding ほうがいい.

examples.ja: この薬は飲まないほうがいいです。
examples.romaji: Kono kusuri wa nomanai hou ga ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: It's better not to take this medicine.

examples.ja: 急いでいるときは、走らないほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Isoide iru toki wa, hashiranai hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: When you're in a hurry, it's better not to run.

examples.ja: 彼に秘密を知られたくなければ、話さないほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Kare ni himitsu o shiraretakunakereba, hanasanai hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: If you don't want him to know your secret, it's better not to tell him.

examples.ja: 雨の日には、外出しないほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Ame no hi ni wa, gaishutsu shinai hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: It's better not to go out on a rainy day.

title: Verb ながら (〜nagara)
formation: Verb-masu stem + ながら
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'while' or 'as' in the sense of doing two things simultaneously.
longExplanationEn: The ～ながら grammar point is used to indicate that two actions are happening simultaneously or that one action is happening while another is taking place. This can be translated to 'while' or 'as' in English. It is primarily used with verbs.

examples.ja: テレビを見ながら宿題をしました。
examples.romaji: Terebi wo minagara shukudai wo shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I did my homework while watching television.

examples.ja: 音楽を聞きながら料理をしました。
examples.romaji: Ongaku wo kikinagara ryouri wo shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I cooked while listening to music.

examples.ja: 歩きながらスマホを見るのは危険です。
examples.romaji: Arukinagara sumaho wo miru no wa kiken desu.
examples.meaningEn: Looking at your smartphone while walking is dangerous.

examples.ja: 彼は話しながら笑っていました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa hanashi nagara waratte imashita.
examples.meaningEn: He was laughing while talking.

title: Verb なさい (〜nasai)
formation: Verb-stem + なさい
shortExplanationEn: A command form used to give orders or instructions; 'Please do', 'Do this'.
longExplanationEn: The なさい grammar point is used to give orders or instructions, often used by teachers, parents, or other authority figures when addressing someone younger or of lower status. It is formed by attaching なさい to the stem of a verb. This form sounds polite, but can be considered strong and direct.

examples.ja: 部屋を掃除しなさい。
examples.romaji: Heya wo souji shinasai.
examples.meaningEn: Clean your room.

examples.ja: 勉強しなさい。
examples.romaji: Benkyou shinasai.
examples.meaningEn: Study.

examples.ja: 早く起きなさい。
examples.romaji: Hayaku okinasai.
examples.meaningEn: Wake up early.

examples.ja: 宿題をやりなさい。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo yarinasai.
examples.meaningEn: Do your homework.

title: Verb にくい (〜nikui)
formation: Verb-stem + にくい
shortExplanationEn: Express difficulty or complexity; 'hard to', 'difficult to', 'not easily'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にくい grammar point is used to express that an action is difficult or relatively hard to do. It is used by attaching にくい to the stem of a verb. It can be translated as 'hard to', 'difficult to', or 'not easily' in English.

examples.ja: この字は読みにくいです。
examples.romaji: Kono ji wa yomi nikui desu.
examples.meaningEn: This character is hard to read.

examples.ja: このドアは開けにくい。
examples.romaji: Kono doa wa ake nikui.
examples.meaningEn: This door is difficult to open.

examples.ja: 彼の字は読みにくい。
examples.romaji: Kare no ji wa yomi nikui.
examples.meaningEn: His handwriting is hard to read.

examples.ja: この料理は作りにくい。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa tsukuri nikui.
examples.meaningEn: This dish is difficult to make.

title: Verb やすい (〜yasui)
formation: Verb-stem + やすい
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that something is easy to do; 'easy to', 'easy for'.
longExplanationEn: The やすい grammar point is used to express that something is easy to do or easy for someone. It is attached to the verb stem and can be translated as 'easy to' or 'easy for' in English. This formation is used with verbs only.

examples.ja: この本は読みやすいです。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa yomiyasui desu.
examples.meaningEn: This book is easy to read.

examples.ja: このシャツは着やすい。
examples.romaji: Kono shatsu wa kiyasui.
examples.meaningEn: This shirt is easy to wear.

examples.ja: 彼は話しやすい人です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa hanashiyasui hito desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is an easy person to talk to.

examples.ja: この料理は作りやすい。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa tsukuriyasui.
examples.meaningEn: This dish is easy to make.

title: Verb ようと思う (Verb-you to omou)
formation: Verb-volitional + と思う
shortExplanationEn: Express intention or plan to do something; 'I think I will', 'I plan to', 'I intend to'.
longExplanationEn: The ようと思う grammar point is used to express the intention or plan to do something in the future. It can be translated as 'I think I will', 'I plan to', or 'I intend to' in English. The formation involves using the volitional form of the verb followed by と思う.

examples.ja: 明日、映画を見ようと思います。
examples.romaji: Ashita, eiga o miyou to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think I will watch a movie tomorrow.

examples.ja: 週末に友達と遊ぼうと思っています。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu ni tomodachi to asobou to omotteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to hang out with friends on the weekend.

examples.ja: 勉強をもっと頑張ろうと思う。
examples.romaji: Benkyou o motto ganbarou to omou.
examples.meaningEn: I intend to study harder.

examples.ja: 来年は海外旅行に行こうと思っている。
examples.romaji: Rainen wa kaigai ryokou ni ikou to omotteiru.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to go on an overseas trip next year.

title: Verb ように (〜you ni)
formation: Examples: Noun + のように / Verb-casual + ように / い-adjective + ように
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'like', 'as if', 'in order to', or 'so that'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ように grammar point is used to express different meanings depending on the context: 'like', 'as if', 'in order to', or 'so that'. Formation patterns can include: Noun + のように, Verb dictionary/negative form + ように, い-adjective + ように, etc.

examples.ja: 彼女は鳥のように軽やかに踊る。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa tori no you ni karoyaka ni odoru.
examples.meaningEn: She dances lightly like a bird.

examples.ja: 覚えたことを忘れないように、毎日復習しましょう。
examples.romaji: Oboeta koto wo wasurenai you ni, mainichi fukushuu shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: In order not to forget what you've learned, let's review every day.

examples.ja: 歌手になるように、毎日歌の練習をしています。
examples.romaji: Kashu ni naru you ni, mainichi uta no renshuu wo shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: In order to become a singer, I practice singing every day.

examples.ja: 道を渡る時、車が来ないように確認してください。
examples.romaji: Michi wo wataru toki, kuruma ga konai you ni kakunin shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: When crossing the road, please make sure no cars are coming.

title: Verb ようにする (Verb ~you ni suru)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + ようにする
shortExplanationEn: Make an effort to; try to; see to it that; ensure that.
longExplanationEn: The ようにする grammar point is used to express that someone is making an effort, trying, or seeing to it that something happens. It can be translated as 'make sure to', 'try to', or 'ensure that' in English. The formation is simple, involving a verb in the dictionary form followed by ようにする.

examples.ja: 毎日運動するようにしています。
examples.romaji: Mainichi undou suru you ni shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I try to exercise every day.

examples.ja: 早く寝るようにしてください。
examples.romaji: Hayaku neru you ni shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please try to go to bed early.

examples.ja: 約束を守るようにします。
examples.romaji: Yakusoku o mamoru you ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will make sure to keep my promise.

examples.ja: 彼に会わないようにする。
examples.romaji: Kare ni awanai you ni suru.
examples.meaningEn: I will try not to see him.

title: Verb ようになる (〜you ni naru)
formation: Verb-dictionary/potential form + ようになる
shortExplanationEn: Express a change in ability, situation or state; 'become able to', 'come to', 'begin to'.
longExplanationEn: The ようになる grammar implies a change or development involving new abilities, situations or states. It can be translated into English as 'become able to', 'come to' or 'begin to'. This grammar is often used when talking about a skill that has been acquired or a change in habit or situation over time.

examples.ja: 日本語が話せるようになりました。
examples.romaji: Nihongo ga hanaseru you ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I have become able to speak Japanese.

examples.ja: 最近、早く寝るようになった。
examples.romaji: Saikin, hayaku neru you ni natta.
examples.meaningEn: Recently, I have come to go to bed early.

examples.ja: 彼はピアノを弾けるようになりました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa piano wo hikeru you ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He has become able to play the piano.

examples.ja: この仕事を始めてから、毎日忙しいと感じるようになりました。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto o hajimete kara, mainichi isogashii to kanjiru you ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Since I started this job, I've begun to feel busy every day.

title: Verb ように言う (Verb-you ni iu)
formation: Verb-casual + ように言う
shortExplanationEn: Tell someone to do something; 'tell to do', 'instruct to do'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point Verb ように言う is used when someone tells or instructs another person to do something. It can be translated as 'tell to do' or 'instruct to do' in English. The formation is created by adding ように言う to the casual form of a verb.

examples.ja: 先生は私たちに宿題をやるように言いました。
examples.romaji: Sensei wa watashitachi ni shukudai o yaru you ni iimashita.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher told us to do our homework.

examples.ja: 母は弟に部屋を片付けるように言いました。
examples.romaji: Haha wa otouto ni heya wo katazukeru you ni iimashita.
examples.meaningEn: My mother told my younger brother to clean his room.

examples.ja: 上司は私に報告書を提出するように言いました。
examples.romaji: Joushi wa watashi ni houkokusho wo teishutsu suru you ni iimashita.
examples.meaningEn: My boss told me to submit the report.

examples.ja: 友達が私に早く来るように言いました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga watashi ni hayaku kuru you ni iimashita.
examples.meaningEn: My friend told me to come early.

title: Verb られる (〜rareru)
formation: う-verb (passive): Replace the final う with われる / る-verb (passive & potential): Replace る with られる / Irregular: する → される, くる → こられる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses passive voice or potential form for verbs.
longExplanationEn: The られる grammar point can be used to express passive voice or potential form for verbs. Passive voice indicates that the action is being done to the subject, while potential form expresses the ability or possibility to do an action. The formation of られる depends on the type of verb (う, る, or irregular verbs).

examples.ja: 彼は先生に褒められました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa sensei ni homeraremashita.
examples.meaningEn: He was praised by the teacher.

examples.ja: 彼は友達に笑われました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tomodachi ni warawaremashita.
examples.meaningEn: He was laughed at by his friends.

examples.ja: 映画は観られましたか？
examples.romaji: Eiga wa miraremashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: Were you able to watch the movie?

examples.ja: 彼女はリーダーに選ばれました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa riidaa ni erabaremashita.
examples.meaningEn: She was chosen as the leader.

title: Verb る ことがある (〜ru koto ga aru)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + ことがある
shortExplanationEn: Used to express that something happens sometimes or has happened before.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point る ことがある is used to express that something occurs sometimes or has occurred in the past. It indicates that the action is not a regular or constant event, but rather something that takes place occasionally or has happened at least once.

examples.ja: 彼は遅刻することがあります。
examples.romaji: Kare wa chikoku suru koto ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is sometimes late.

examples.ja: 私は寝坊することがあります。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa nebou suru koto ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I sometimes oversleep.

examples.ja: 彼女は歌を歌うことがあります。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa uta o utau koto ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She sometimes sings songs.

examples.ja: 子供の頃、よく山に登ることがありました。
examples.romaji: Kodomo no koro, yoku yama ni noboru koto ga arimashita.
examples.meaningEn: When I was a child, I sometimes climbed mountains.

title: Verb ることができる (〜ru koto ga dekiru)
formation: Verb-る + ことができる
shortExplanationEn: Express ability or possibility of doing an action; 'can', 'be able to', 'is possible'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ることができる grammar point is used to express the ability or possibility of doing an action in Japanese. It can be translated as 'can', 'be able to', or 'is possible' in English. The formation is made by adding the phrase 'ことができる' to the verb in its conjugated る-form.

examples.ja: 私は日本語を話すことができます。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa nihongo wo hanasu koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I can speak Japanese.

examples.ja: 彼はギターを弾くことができます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa gitaa wo hiku koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can play the guitar.

examples.ja: この問題を解決することができますか？
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wo kaiketsu suru koto ga dekimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Can you solve this problem?

examples.ja: 彼女は犬を泳がせることができます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa inu wo oyogaseru koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She can make the dog swim.

title: Verb ることにする (〜ru koto ni suru)
formation: Verb-る + ことにする
shortExplanationEn: Decide to do something, make a decision.
longExplanationEn: The ～ることにする grammar point is used to express that someone has made a decision to do something or has decided on a specific course of action. It is formed by attaching ことにする to the verb in its る form.

examples.ja: 来週、ジムに行くことにしました。
examples.romaji: Raishuu, jimu ni iku koto ni shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I decided to go to the gym next week.

examples.ja: この靴を買うことにしました。
examples.romaji: Kono kutsu wo kau koto ni shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I decided to buy these shoes.

examples.ja: 毎日、散歩をすることにしました。
examples.romaji: Mainichi, sanpo wo suru koto ni shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I decided to take a walk every day.

examples.ja: 彼に電話をかけることにします。
examples.romaji: Kare ni denwa wo kakeru koto ni shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I’ve decided to call him.

title: Verb る ことになる (〜ru koto ni naru)
formation: Verb-る + ことになる
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that something has been decided or arranged; 'it has been decided that', 'it turns out that'.
longExplanationEn: The る ことになる grammar point is used to express that something has been decided or arranged. It often indicates a decision made by someone else or due to circumstances. It can be translated as 'it has been decided that' or 'it turns out that' in English. The formation requires a verb in る (dictionary) form.

examples.ja: 今日は会議がなくなることになりました。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa kaigi ga nakunaru koto ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: It has been decided that there will be no meeting today.

examples.ja: 来週、友達と旅行することになった。
examples.romaji: Raishuu, tomodachi to ryokou suru koto ni natta.
examples.meaningEn: It has been arranged that I will travel with my friends next week.

examples.ja: 彼女が今度のプロジェクトのリーダーになることになりました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga kondo no purojekuto no riida ni naru koto ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: It has been decided that she will be the leader of the next project.

examples.ja: 急に仕事が入ったので、休みを取ることになった。
examples.romaji: Kyuu ni shigoto ga haitta node, yasumi wo toru koto ni natta.
examples.meaningEn: Due to sudden work, it has been decided that I will take a day off.

title: Verb るときに (〜ru toki ni)
formation: Verb-る + ときに
shortExplanationEn: Describes the time when a certain action takes place; 'when', 'at the time'.
longExplanationEn: The ～るときに grammar point is used to indicate the time when a certain action or event happens. It can be translated as 'when' or 'at the time' in English. The formation involves attaching ときに to the verb in its dictionary (る) form.

examples.ja: 映画を見るときに、ポップコーンを食べます。
examples.romaji: Eiga wo miru toki ni, poppukoon wo tabemasu.
examples.meaningEn: When I watch a movie, I eat popcorn.

examples.ja: 寝るときに、部屋の電気を消します。
examples.romaji: Neru toki ni, heya no denki wo keshimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When I go to bed, I turn off the room lights.

examples.ja: 運動するときに、水分補給が大切です。
examples.romaji: Undou suru toki ni, suibun hokyuu ga taisetsu desu.
examples.meaningEn: When exercising, it's important to stay hydrated.

examples.ja: 旅行するときに、カメラを持っていくことが好きです。
examples.romaji: Ryokou suru toki ni, kamera wo motteiku koto ga suki desu.
examples.meaningEn: When I travel, I like to bring a camera.

title: Verb る ところ (Verb-ru tokoro)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + ところ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the state or moment when an action is about to happen or is happening.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point 'Verb る ところ' is used to express that one is just about to do something, or is in the process of doing it. It can be translated as 'just about to' or 'in the middle of' in English, depending on context.

examples.ja: 彼は食べるところです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa taberu tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is in the process of eating.

examples.ja: 私は出かけるところです。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa dekakeru tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: I am just about to leave.

examples.ja: 彼女は泣くところでした。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa naku tokoro deshita.
examples.meaningEn: She was on the verge of crying.

examples.ja: 私たちは勉強するところです。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa benkyou suru tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: We are in the middle of studying.

title: Verb 出す (~dasu)
formation: Verb stem + 出す (~dasu)
shortExplanationEn: Express the action of taking something out, starting an action, or emphasizing a verb.
longExplanationEn: The verb 出す (~dasu) is used to express the action of taking something out, starting an action, or emphasizing the verb it is attached to. To form this grammar point, simply attach 出す (~dasu) to the stem of the verb.

examples.ja: 本を棚から出してください。
examples.romaji: Hon o tana kara dashite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please take the book out from the shelf.

examples.ja: 彼は部屋から急いで出ました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa heya kara isoide demashita.
examples.meaningEn: He rushed out of the room.

examples.ja: 授業が始まる前に宿題を出してください。
examples.romaji: Jugyou ga hajimaru mae ni shukudai wo dashite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Submit your homework before class starts.

examples.ja: 彼女は歌い出しました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa utaidashimashita.
examples.meaningEn: She began to sing.

title: Verb 方 (〜hou)
formation: Verb-stem + 方 (read as かた)
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the way or method of doing something; often read as '～かた' ('how to', 'the way of').
longExplanationEn: The ～方 grammar point is used to express the way or method of doing something, similar to 'how to' or 'the way of' in English. It can be attached to the stem of a verb to form a noun that indicates the method of doing the action. In practice, ～方 is usually read as '～かた' (e.g., 作り方＝つくりかた).

examples.ja: 誰かがケーキの作り方を知っていますか？
examples.romaji: Dareka ga keeki no tsukurikata o shitteimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Does anyone know how to make a cake?

examples.ja: 彼女は英語の話し方を学んでいます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa eigo no hanashikata o manandeimasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is learning how to speak English.

examples.ja: このパソコンの使い方を教えてください。
examples.romaji: Kono pasokon no tsukaikata o oshiete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please teach me how to use this computer.

examples.ja: 数学の問題の解き方を覚えるのが難しいです。
examples.romaji: Suugaku no mondai no tokikata o oboeru no ga muzukashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is difficult to remember the way to solve math problems.

title: Verb 終わる (〜owaru)
formation: 終わる (owaru) is a godan (u-verb)
shortExplanationEn: Verbal action is completed; 'to finish', 'to end'.
longExplanationEn: The verb 終わる (owaru) is used to express that an action has been completed or something has come to an end. Despite ending in る, 終わる is a godan (u-verb), so it does not follow the typical ichidan (る-verb) conjugation. It is translated as 'to finish', 'to end' or 'to be over' in English.

examples.ja: 映画が終わったら、家に帰ります。
examples.romaji: Eiga ga owattara, ie ni kaerimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When the movie finishes, I'll go home.

examples.ja: 仕事が終わるまで、待ってください。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga owaru made, matte kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please wait until the work is finished.

examples.ja: パーティーはもうすぐ終わります。
examples.romaji: Paatii wa mou sugu owarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The party will be over soon.

examples.ja: 試験が終わったら、友達と遊びに行く予定です。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga owattara, tomodachi to asobi ni iku yotei desu.
examples.meaningEn: Once the exam is over, I plan to go out with my friends.

title: Verb + 続ける (つづける, tsuzukeru)
formation: Verb-masu-stem + 続ける
shortExplanationEn: To continue doing or to keep doing an action.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point 続ける (tsuzukeru) is attached to the masu-stem form of verbs, and it indicates the ongoing continuity of an action. It can be translated as 'to continue doing' or 'to keep doing' in English.

examples.ja: 彼は毎日ジムで運動を続けています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mainichi jimu de undou o tsuzuketeimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He continues to exercise at the gym every day.

examples.ja: 彼女は勉強を続けるために図書館に行きました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa benkyou o tsuzukeru tame ni toshokan ni ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: She went to the library to continue studying.

examples.ja: 私たちは雨にも関わらず散歩を続けました。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa ame ni mo kakawarazu sanpo o tsuzukemashita.
examples.meaningEn: We continued walking despite the rain.

examples.ja: 彼は仕事を続けることに決めた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shigoto o tsuzukeru koto ni kimeta.
examples.meaningEn: He decided to continue working.

title: ～かしら (〜kashira)
formation: Verb-casual + かしら, い-Adjective + かしら, な-Adjective + かしら, Noun + かしら
shortExplanationEn: Indicates uncertainty or doubt; 'I wonder', 'I'm not sure'.
longExplanationEn: The ～かしら grammar point is used to express uncertainty or doubt about a situation. It can be translated as 'I wonder' or 'I'm not sure' in English. It is primarily used by females in casual conversations but can be used by males in some contexts as well. ～かしら is used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 今日は雨が降るかしら？
examples.romaji: Kyou wa ame ga furu kashira?
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if it will rain today?

examples.ja: このケーキは美味しいかしら？
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa oishii kashira?
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if this cake is delicious?

examples.ja: 彼は病気かしら？
examples.romaji: Kare wa byouki kashira?
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if he is sick?

examples.ja: 彼女は学生かしら？
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa gakusei kashira?
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if she is a student?

title: ～かどうか (〜ka dou ka)
formation: Verb-casual + かどうか, い-Adjective + かどうか, な-Adjective + (だ)かどうか, Noun + (だ)かどうか
shortExplanationEn: Used to express 'whether' or 'if' in indirect questions or statements.
longExplanationEn: The ～かどうか grammar point is used to express 'whether' or 'if' in indirect questions or statements. It is often used to express uncertainty or to inquire about information in a more polite form. ～かどうか can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼が来るかどうか知りません。
examples.romaji: Kare ga kuru ka dou ka shirimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I don't know if he will come or not.

examples.ja: この本が面白いかどうか教えてください。
examples.romaji: Kono hon ga omoshiroi ka dou ka oshiete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please tell me if this book is interesting or not.

examples.ja: 彼女が綺麗だかどうかは分かりません。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga kirei da ka dou ka wa wakarimasen.
examples.meaningEn: I don't know whether she is beautiful or not.

examples.ja: 彼が先生だかどうか覚えていません。
examples.romaji: Kare ga sensei da ka dou ka oboete imasen.
examples.meaningEn: I don't remember if he is a teacher or not.

title: ～かなあ (〜kanaa)
formation: Verb-casual + かなあ, い-Adjective + かなあ, (な-Adjective/Noun) + (だ) + かなあ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses uncertainty or wonder; 'I wonder', 'I'm not sure'.
longExplanationEn: The ～かなあ grammar point is used to express a feeling of uncertainty or wonder about a situation or statement. It can be translated as 'I wonder' or 'I'm not sure' in English. In casual spoken Japanese, when using nouns or な-adjectives before かなあ, the copula だ is often omitted or replaced with の (e.g., 学生かなあ or 学生なのかなあ). This grammar point should not be used when asking questions directly to someone, as it may come off as too direct or impolite.

examples.ja: 明日雨が降るかなあ。
examples.romaji: Ashita ame ga furu kanaa.
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if it will rain tomorrow.

examples.ja: このケーキは美味しいかなあ。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa oishii kanaa.
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if this cake is delicious.

examples.ja: 彼は病気かなあ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa byouki kanaa.
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if he is sick.

examples.ja: 彼女は学生かなあ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa gakusei kanaa.
examples.meaningEn: I wonder if she is a student.

title: ～かもしれない (〜kamoshirenai)
formation: Verb-casual + かもしれない, い-Adjective + かもしれない, な-Adjective + だかもしれない, Noun + だかもしれない
shortExplanationEn: Express uncertainty; 'maybe', 'might', 'possibly'.
longExplanationEn: The ～かもしれない grammar point is used to express uncertainty or the possibility of something occurring. It can be translated as 'maybe', 'might', or 'possibly' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun. In everyday speech, speakers often omit だ before かもしれない for nouns and な-adjectives.

examples.ja: 明日雨が降るかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Ashita ame ga furu kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: It might rain tomorrow.

examples.ja: このケーキは美味しいかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa oishii kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: This cake might be delicious.

examples.ja: 彼が病気かもしれない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga byouki kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: He might be sick.

examples.ja: 彼女は学生かもしれない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa gakusei kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: She might be a student.

title: ～から (〜kara)
formation: Verb-casual + から, い-Adjective + から, な-Adjective + だから, Noun + だから
shortExplanationEn: Express a reason or cause; 'because', 'since', 'due to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～から grammar point is used to express a reason, cause, or the origin of something. It can be translated as 'because', 'since', or 'due to' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 雨が降るから、傘を持って行ってください。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu kara, kasa wo motte itte kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Because it's raining, please bring an umbrella.

examples.ja: このケーキは美味しいから、みんな食べるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa oishii kara, minna taberu deshou.
examples.meaningEn: Because this cake is delicious, everyone will probably eat it.

examples.ja: 彼は病気だから、学校を休みます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa byouki dakara, gakkou wo yasumimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He will be absent from school because he is sick.

examples.ja: 今日は日曜日だから、学校がありません。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa nichiyoubi dakara, gakkou ga arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: There is no school today because it is Sunday.

title: ～けれど (〜keredo)
formation: Verb-casual + けれど, い-Adjective + けれど, な-Adjective + だけれど, Noun + だけれど
shortExplanationEn: Indicate a soft contrast or contradiction; 'but', 'however'.
longExplanationEn: The ～けれど grammar point is used to express a soft contrast or contradiction between two statements. It can be translated as 'but' or 'however' in English. It is relatively informal and can be replaced by more formal expressions like けれども or が. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 今日は忙しいけれど、明日はもっと時間がある。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa isogashii keredo, ashita wa motto jikan ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: I'm busy today, but I have more time tomorrow.

examples.ja: このレストランは高いけれど、料理が美味しい。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa takai keredo, ryouri ga oishii.
examples.meaningEn: This restaurant is expensive, but the food is delicious.

examples.ja: 彼は若いけれど、とても経験豊富だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa wakai keredo, totemo keiken houfu da.
examples.meaningEn: He is young, but he is very experienced.

examples.ja: 彼女は病気だけれど、明るく元気だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa byouki da keredo, akaruku genki da.
examples.meaningEn: She is sick, but she is cheerful and energetic.

title: ～させてください (〜sasete kudasai)
formation: Verb-causative form + ください
shortExplanationEn: Express a polite request to allow or permit someone to do something.
longExplanationEn: The ～させてください grammar point is used to make a polite request to allow or permit someone to do something. It can be translated as 'please let me' or 'please allow me to' in English. It is formed by taking the causative form of a verb and adding ください.

examples.ja: もう少し考えさせてください。
examples.romaji: Mou sukoshi kangaesasete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please let me think a little more.

examples.ja: 写真を撮らせてください。
examples.romaji: Shashin o torasete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please let me take a photo.

examples.ja: もうちょっと練習させてください。
examples.romaji: Mou chotto renshuu sasete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please let me practice a little more.

examples.ja: ここで待たせてください。
examples.romaji: Koko de matasete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please let me wait here.

title: ～し、～し、～ (〜shi, 〜shi, 〜)
formation: Verb-casual + し, い-Adjective + し, な-Adjective + だし, Noun + だし
shortExplanationEn: Express multiple reasons or characteristics; 'and', 'because', 'since'.
longExplanationEn: The ～し、～し、～ grammar point is used to connect multiple reasons or characteristics that support a conclusion or statement. It can be translated as 'and', 'because', or 'since' in English. This grammar can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は若いし、元気だし、フレンドリーだし、みんなと仲良くなれると思います。
examples.romaji: Kare wa wakai shi, genki da shi, furendorī da shi, minna to nakayoku nareru to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is young, energetic, friendly, and I think he can get along with everyone.

examples.ja: このレストランは料理が美味しいし、雰囲気がいいし、価格も手頃だからお勧めです。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa ryouri ga oishii shi, fun'iki ga ii shi, kakaku mo tegoro dakara osusume desu.
examples.meaningEn: I recommend this restaurant because the food is delicious, the atmosphere is nice, and the prices are reasonable.

examples.ja: 彼女は勉強ができるし、スポーツも得意だし、性格もいい。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa benkyou ga dekiru shi, supōtsu mo tokui da shi, seikaku mo ii.
examples.meaningEn: She is good at studying, good at sports, and has a good personality.

examples.ja: 今日は寒いし、雨も降っているし、家でゆっくり休んだ方がいいでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa samui shi, ame mo futte iru shi, ie de yukkuri yasunda hou ga ii deshou.
examples.meaningEn: It's cold today, and it's raining, so it's better to relax at home.

title: ～すぎる (〜sugiru)
formation: Verb-stem + すぎる, い-Adjective (remove い) + すぎる, な-Adjective + すぎる
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is excessive or overdone; 'too much', 'too', 'over'.
longExplanationEn: The ～すぎる grammar point is used to express that something is excessive or beyond a certain level. It can be translated as 'too much', 'too', or 'over' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, or な-adjective.

examples.ja: この部屋は暑すぎる。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa atsui sugiru.
examples.meaningEn: This room is too hot.

examples.ja: 彼は早く来すぎた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa hayaku kisugita.
examples.meaningEn: He came too early.

examples.ja: この箱は重すぎて持ち上げられない。
examples.romaji: Kono hako wa omosugite mochiagerarenai.
examples.meaningEn: This box is too heavy to lift.

examples.ja: 彼女は忙しくて遊びすぎはしない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa isogashikute asobisugi wa shinai.
examples.meaningEn: She's too busy to play too much.

title: ～ずつ (〜zutsu)
formation: Noun + ずつ
shortExplanationEn: Indicates equal distribution; 'each', 'per', 'apiece'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ずつ grammar point is used to express an equal distribution of something, whether it is an action, an amount, or an item. It is translated as 'each', 'per', or 'apiece' in English.

examples.ja: りんごを2つずつ配ります。
examples.romaji: Ringo wo futatsu zutsu kubarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will distribute 2 apples each.

examples.ja: 私たちは週に一度ずつ掃除をします。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi wa shuu ni ichido zutsu souji o shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: We each clean once per week.

examples.ja: みんなは同じ距離を一歩ずつ歩いた。
examples.romaji: Minna wa onaji kyori o ippo zutsu aruita.
examples.meaningEn: Everyone walked the same distance, one step at a time.

examples.ja: 彼女は子供たちに一枚ずつプレゼントをくれました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kodomotachi ni ichimai zutsu purezento o kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: She gave one present to each child.

title: ～そうだ (〜sou da)
formation: Verb-ますstem + そうだ, い-Adjective (without い) + そうだ, な-Adjective + そうだ
shortExplanationEn: Express the appearance, seeming, or feeling of something; 'looks like', 'seems', 'appears'.
longExplanationEn: The ～そうだ grammar point is used to describe the appearance or feeling of something based on what is observed or inferred. It can be translated as 'looks like', 'seems', or 'appears' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, or な-adjective.

examples.ja: その花はとてもきれいそうだ。
examples.romaji: Sono hana wa totemo kirei sou da.
examples.meaningEn: That flower looks very beautiful.

examples.ja: 彼は疲れそうだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tsukare sou da.
examples.meaningEn: He looks tired.

examples.ja: この本は面白そうだ。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa omoshiro sou da.
examples.meaningEn: This book looks interesting.

examples.ja: 雨が降りそうだ。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furi sou da.
examples.meaningEn: It looks like it's going to rain.

title: そんな (sonna) + Noun
formation: そんな + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'that kind/type of'; referring to something near the listener or previously mentioned.
longExplanationEn: The word 'そんな' is used before a noun to indicate 'that kind of' or 'that type of', typically referring to something near the listener or previously mentioned in the conversation. It specifies the noun based on context or shared understanding between the speaker and the listener.

examples.ja: そんな話、信じられない。
examples.romaji: Sonna hanashi, shinjirarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I can't believe that kind of story.

examples.ja: そんな映画、見たくない。
examples.romaji: Sonna eiga, mitakunai.
examples.meaningEn: I don't want to watch that kind of movie.

title: そんなに～ (sonna ni〜)
formation: そんなに + Verb-negation, そんなに + Adjective-negation
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'not so much', 'not that', or 'not so ~'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point そんなに is an adverb used to express that something is not as extreme as described or not to that extent. It is often translated as 'not so much', 'not that', or 'not so ~'. It is commonly followed by a negative form of a verb or adjective.

examples.ja: そんなに急がなくてもいいですよ。
examples.romaji: Sonna ni isoganakutemo ii desu yo.
examples.meaningEn: You don't have to hurry that much.

examples.ja: そんなに難しくない問題だと思います。
examples.romaji: Sonna ni muzukashikunai mondai da to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I don't think the problem is that difficult.

examples.ja: 彼女はそんなに背が高くありません。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa sonna ni se ga takaku arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: She is not that tall.

examples.ja: そんなにたくさん食べる必要はないと思う。
examples.romaji: Sonna ni takusan taberu hitsuyou wa nai to omou.
examples.meaningEn: I don't think there's a need to eat so much.

title: ～たらいい (〜tara ii)
formation: Verb-casual-past + たらいい
shortExplanationEn: Expressing a desire or suggestion for a particular outcome; 'it would be good if', 'should', 'I hope'.
longExplanationEn: The ～たらいい grammar point is used to express a desire, suggestion, or hope for a particular outcome. It can be translated as 'it would be good if', 'should', or 'I hope' in English. The formation is simple, and it is used with verbs in their casual past tense form.

examples.ja: もっと勉強したらいいですよ。
examples.romaji: Motto benkyou shitara ii desu yo.
examples.meaningEn: It would be good if you studied more.

examples.ja: 雨が止んだらいいな。
examples.romaji: Ame ga yandara ii na.
examples.meaningEn: I hope the rain stops.

examples.ja: 早く元気になったらいいですね。
examples.romaji: Hayaku genki ni nattara ii desu ne.
examples.meaningEn: It would be good if you got well soon.

examples.ja: 彼に連絡したらいいと思います。
examples.romaji: Kare ni renraku shitara ii to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think it would be good if you contacted him.

title: ～たら いかがですか (〜tara ikaga desu ka)
formation: Verb-casual, past + たら いかがですか, い-Adjective + かったら いかがですか, な-Adjective + だったら いかがですか, Noun + だったら いかがですか
shortExplanationEn: Making polite suggestions; 'how about if', 'what if', 'it would be good if'.
longExplanationEn: The ～たら いかがですか grammar point is used to make polite suggestions, ask for opinions, or imply that something would be good or desirable if the condition is met. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 図書館に行ったらいかがですか。
examples.romaji: Toshokan ni ittara ikaga desu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How about going to the library?

examples.ja: 寒かったらカーディガンを着るのはいかがですか。
examples.romaji: Samukattara kaadigan wo kiru no wa ikaga desu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How about wearing a cardigan if it's cold?

examples.ja: 部屋が暗かったら照明を付けるのはいかがですか。
examples.romaji: Heya ga kurakattara shoumei wo tsukeru no wa ikaga desu ka.
examples.meaningEn: What if you turn on the light if the room is dark?

examples.ja: 自分の意見だったら他人に聞くのはいかがですか。
examples.romaji: Jibun no iken dattara tanin ni kiku no wa ikaga desu ka.
examples.meaningEn: How about asking others if it's your own opinion?

title: ～たら どうですか (〜tara doudesuka)
formation: Verb-た form + ら どうですか
shortExplanationEn: Express a suggestion or advice; 'what if', 'how about', 'why don't you'.
longExplanationEn: The ～たら どうですか grammar point is used to make a suggestion or give advice to someone. It can be translated as 'what if', 'how about', or 'why don't you' in English. This structure is used with the た-form of verbs to make a gentle, polite suggestion.

examples.ja: スキーに行ったらどうですか？
examples.romaji: Sukii ni ittara doudesuka?
examples.meaningEn: How about going skiing?

examples.ja: 少し休んだらどうですか？
examples.romaji: Sukoshi yasundara doudesuka?
examples.meaningEn: What if you take a little break?

examples.ja: この本を読んだらどうですか？
examples.romaji: Kono hon wo yondara doudesuka?
examples.meaningEn: Why don't you read this book?

examples.ja: タクシーで帰ったらどうですか？
examples.romaji: Takushii de kaettara doudesuka?
examples.meaningEn: How about going home by taxi?

title: ～たり～たり (〜tari 〜tari)
formation: Verb-ta + り + next verb-ta + り, い-Adjective + かったり + next verb-ta + り, な-Adjective + だったり + next verb-ta + り, Noun + だったり + next verb-ta + り
shortExplanationEn: Used to list multiple actions or states; 'doing things like', 'and so on', 'etc.'
longExplanationEn: The ～たり～たり grammar point is used to list two or more actions or states, without specifying their order or importance. It implies that these actions or states are representative examples, and there may be others. This construction is useful for describing general or habitual actions.

examples.ja: 週末は映画を観たり、本を読んだりする。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu wa eiga wo mitari, hon wo yondari suru.
examples.meaningEn: On weekends, I do things like watching movies and reading books.

examples.ja: 彼女は料理が上手だったり、歌がうまかったりする。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ryouri ga jouzu dattari, uta ga umakattari suru.
examples.meaningEn: She is good at things like cooking and singing.

examples.ja: このリゾートではスキーをしたり、温泉に入ったりできる。
examples.romaji: Kono rizooto de wa sukii wo shitari, onsen ni haittari dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: At this resort, you can do things like skiing and going to hot springs.

examples.ja: この地域の天気は暑かったり、寒かったりする。
examples.romaji: Kono chiiki no tenki wa atsukattari, samukattari suru.
examples.meaningEn: The weather in this area can be hot or cold.

title: ～だろう (〜darou)
formation: Verb-casual + だろう, い-Adjective (drop い) + だろう, な-Adjective + だろう, Noun + だろう
shortExplanationEn: Express probability, likelihood or certainty; 'probably', 'maybe', 'I think'.
longExplanationEn: The ～だろう grammar point is used to express probability, likelihood, or certainty about a situation or event. It can be translated as 'probably', 'maybe', or 'I think' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 明日は晴れるだろう。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa hareru darou.
examples.meaningEn: It will probably be sunny tomorrow.

examples.ja: 彼は忙しいだろう。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isogashii darou.
examples.meaningEn: He is probably busy.

examples.ja: この本は面白いだろう。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa omoshiroi darou.
examples.meaningEn: This book is probably interesting.

examples.ja: 彼女は先生だろう。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa sensei darou.
examples.meaningEn: She is probably a teacher.

title: ～っていう (〜tte iu)
formation: Noun/Adjective/Verb + っていう + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used to quote, define, or specify a noun.
longExplanationEn: The ～っていう grammar point is used for quoting, defining, or specifying a noun. It can be translated as 'called', 'that is to say', or 'I mean' in English. This expression is similar to ～という, but it is more casual and commonly used in spoken conversations.

examples.ja: ピザっていう料理が大好きです。
examples.romaji: Piza tte iu ryouri ga daisuki desu.
examples.meaningEn: I love the dish called pizza.

examples.ja: 彼っていう男性はすごく面白いです。
examples.romaji: Kare tte iu dansei wa sugoku omoshiroi desu.
examples.meaningEn: That man (called ‘kare’) is really interesting.

examples.ja: この映画っていうのは本当に感動的だよ。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga tte iu no wa hontou ni kandouteki da yo.
examples.meaningEn: This movie, I mean, is really touching.

examples.ja: 彼女が言っているっていう店はここではない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga itte iru tte iu mise wa koko de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: The store she's talking about, I mean, is not here.

title: ～で (〜de)
formation: Noun + ～で
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the means, method, or place.
longExplanationEn: The ～で grammar point is used to indicate the means or method used to perform an action, or to specify the place where an action took place. It is typically used with verbs and comes after a noun.

examples.ja: 私は自転車で学校に行きます。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa jitensha de gakkou ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I go to school by bicycle.

examples.ja: 彼は日本語で手紙を書いた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nihongo de tegami wo kaita.
examples.meaningEn: He wrote a letter in Japanese.

examples.ja: 鉛筆で絵を描きました。
examples.romaji: Enpitsu de e wo kakimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I drew a picture with a pencil.

examples.ja: レストランで昼食を食べました。
examples.romaji: Resutoran de chuushoku wo tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I had lunch at a restaurant.

title: ～でしょう (〜deshou)
formation: Verb-ますstem + でしょう, い-Adjective + でしょう, な-Adjective + でしょう, Noun + でしょう
shortExplanationEn: A conjecture or probability; 'probably', 'I think', 'it seems'.
longExplanationEn: The ～でしょう grammar point is used to indicate a conjecture or probability. It can be translated as 'probably', 'I think', or 'it seems' in English. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 明日は晴れでしょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa hare deshou.
examples.meaningEn: It will probably be sunny tomorrow.

examples.ja: 彼は元気でしょう。
examples.romaji: Kare wa genki deshou.
examples.meaningEn: He is probably fine.

examples.ja: この本は面白いでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa omoshiroi deshou.
examples.meaningEn: This book is probably interesting.

examples.ja: 彼女は医者でしょう。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa isha deshou.
examples.meaningEn: She is probably a doctor.

title: ～てはいけない (〜te wa ikenai)
formation: Verb-て-form + はいけない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses prohibition; 'must not', 'cannot'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てはいけない grammar point is used to express strict prohibition or something that must not be done. It can be translated as 'must not', 'cannot', or 'should not' in English. It is formed by adding て-form of a verb followed by はいけない.

examples.ja: 公園でたばこを吸ってはいけない。
examples.romaji: Kouen de tabako o sutte wa ikenai.
examples.meaningEn: You must not smoke in the park.

examples.ja: ここで写真を撮ってはいけない。
examples.romaji: Koko de shashin o totte wa ikenai.
examples.meaningEn: You cannot take pictures here.

examples.ja: 図書館で大きな声を出してはいけない。
examples.romaji: Toshokan de ookina koe o dashite wa ikenai.
examples.meaningEn: You must not speak loudly in the library.

examples.ja: バスの中で立っている人に席を譲ってはいけない。(Note: Unusual in real life, but grammatically correct.)
examples.romaji: Basu no naka de tatteiru hito ni seki o yuzutte wa ikenai.
examples.meaningEn: You should not give up your seat to someone standing on the bus. (Note: Odd in actual social context, but valid grammar.)

title: ～てもいい (〜temo ii)
formation: Verb-て form + もいい
shortExplanationEn: To give or ask permission; 'it's okay to', 'may', 'can'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てもいい grammar point is used to give or ask for permission to do something. It can be translated as 'it's okay to', 'may', or 'can' in English. The formation is derived by adding てもいい after the て-form of a verb.

examples.ja: ここで写真を撮ってもいいですか。
examples.romaji: Koko de shashin wo tottemo ii desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Is it okay to take a photo here?

examples.ja: このリンゴを食べてもいいですか。
examples.romaji: Kono ringo wo tabetemo ii desu ka.
examples.meaningEn: May I eat this apple?

examples.ja: 試験が終わったら、帰ってもいいです。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga owattara, kaettemo ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: You can go home after the exam is over.

examples.ja: 今夜友達と遊んでもいい。
examples.romaji: Kon'ya tomodachi to asondemo ii.
examples.meaningEn: It's okay to play with friends tonight.

title: ～ても/でも (〜te mo/demo)
formation: Verb-て-form + も, い-Adjective (-い) + くても, な-Adjective + でも, Noun + でも
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'even if' or 'even though'; used to convey contradiction or contrast.
longExplanationEn: The ～ても/でも grammar point is used to express 'even if' or 'even though' in a sentence, showing the idea of contradiction or contrast. The formation of ても differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, or な-adjective. For nouns, でも is used.

examples.ja: 雨が降っても、学校に行きます。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte mo, gakkou ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if it rains, I will go to school.

examples.ja: この部屋が汚くても、掃除しないつもりです。
examples.romaji: Kono heya ga kitanakute mo, souji shinai tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if this room is dirty, I don't intend to clean it.

examples.ja: その映画が面白くなくても、見ます。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga ga omoshirokunakute mo, mimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if that movie is not interesting, I will watch it.

examples.ja: 彼女が病気でも、働きます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga byouki demo, hatarakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if she is sick, she will work.

title: ～といい (〜to ii)
formation: Verb-casual + といい, い-Adjective + といい, な-Adjective + だといい, Noun + だといい
shortExplanationEn: Express a wish or hope; 'I hope', 'it would be good if'.
longExplanationEn: The ～といい grammar point is used to express a wish or hope for a certain situation or outcome. It can be translated as 'I hope', 'it would be good if', or 'it would be nice if' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.
