examples.romaji: Kanojo wa Furansu shusshin desu. Tsumari, Furansugo ga hanasemasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is from France. In other words, she can speak French.

title: ～つもりでした (〜tsumori deshita)
formation: Verb-casual (dictionary form) + つもりでした
shortExplanationEn: Expressing a past intention or plan; 'was planning to', 'meant to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～つもりでした grammar point is used to express a past intention or plan that did not happen, or a past belief that turned out incorrect. It can be translated as 'was planning to', 'intended to', or 'meant to'.

examples.ja: 昨日は友達に会うつもりでしたが、風邪を引いてしまいました。
examples.romaji: Kinou wa tomodachi ni au tsumori deshita ga, kaze o hiite shimaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I was planning to meet my friend yesterday, but I caught a cold.

examples.ja: 映画を見るつもりでしたが、時間がなくなりました。
examples.romaji: Eiga o miru tsumori deshita ga, jikan ga nakunarimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I intended to watch a movie, but I ran out of time.

examples.ja: 彼らが来るつもりでしたが、電話でキャンセルされました。
examples.romaji: Karera ga kuru tsumori deshita ga, denwa de kyanseru saremashita.
examples.meaningEn: They were supposed to come, but they canceled by phone.

examples.ja: この本を読むつもりでしたが、もう読み終わっていました。
examples.romaji: Kono hon o yomu tsumori deshita ga, mou yomiowatte imashita.
examples.meaningEn: I meant to read this book, but I realized I'd already finished it.

title: ～てくれと (〜te kureto)
formation: Verb-て form + くれと (言う / 頼む / 伝える, etc.)
shortExplanationEn: Quote someone's request (telling someone to do something).
longExplanationEn: The ～てくれと grammar point is used when quoting a request or command made by someone else. It literally means 'someone said “please do …”'. Typically used as 「～てくれと頼む/言う」 to indicate being asked or told to do something.

examples.ja: 電話をかけてくれと言われました。
examples.romaji: Denwa o kakete kureto iwaremashita.
examples.meaningEn: I was told to make a phone call.

examples.ja: この荷物を運んでくれと頼まれました。
examples.romaji: Kono nimotsu o hakonde kureto tanomaremashita.
examples.meaningEn: I was asked to carry this luggage.

examples.ja: 最後まで待ってくれとお願いされました。
examples.romaji: Saigo made matte kureto onegai saremashita.
examples.meaningEn: I was asked to wait until the end.

examples.ja: 休んでくれと言われました。
examples.romaji: Yasunde kureto iwaremashita.
examples.meaningEn: I was told to please take a rest.

title: ～てごらん (〜te goran)
formation: Verb-て form + ごらん
shortExplanationEn: Suggest or invite someone to try doing something; 'Why don't you try...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てごらん grammar point is used to gently suggest or invite someone to try doing something. It is softer and more casual than ～てみて, and is used among friends or family.

examples.ja: そのゲームをやってごらん。
examples.romaji: Sono geemu o yatte goran.
examples.meaningEn: Why don't you try playing that game?

examples.ja: 彼に話してごらん。
examples.romaji: Kare ni hanashite goran.
examples.meaningEn: Try talking to him.

examples.ja: 新しいレシピを作ってごらん。
examples.romaji: Atarashii reshipi o tsukutte goran.
examples.meaningEn: Why don't you try making a new recipe?

examples.ja: そのドレスを着てごらん。
examples.romaji: Sono doresu o kite goran.
examples.meaningEn: Try putting on that dress.

title: ですから～ (desu kara)
formation: Sentence A + ですから + Sentence B
shortExplanationEn: Expresses reason or cause; 'because', 'since', 'so'.
longExplanationEn: The ですから grammar point is used to indicate the reason or cause for a statement, often in polite speech. It can be translated as 'because', 'since', or 'so'.

examples.ja: 今日は寒いですから、コートを着てください。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa samui desu kara, kooto o kite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: It's cold today, so please wear a coat.

examples.ja: この映画は面白いですから、ぜひ見てください。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa omoshiroi desu kara, zehi mite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: This movie is interesting, so please watch it.

examples.ja: 明日は休みですから、ゆっくり休んでください。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa yasumi desu kara, yukkuri yasunde kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Tomorrow is a day off, so please take your time to rest.

examples.ja: 彼は親切ですから、手伝ってくれるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shinsetsu desu kara, tetsudatte kureru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: He's kind, so he'll probably help you.

title: ～てはじめて (〜te hajimete)
formation: Verb-て form + はじめて
shortExplanationEn: Realization or action done for the first time; 'only after', 'not until'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てはじめて grammar point indicates that something is realized or happens for the first time only after another action is completed. It can be translated as 'only after' or 'not until'.

examples.ja: 海外に行ってはじめて、日本の良さが分かりました。
examples.romaji: Kaigai ni itte hajimete, Nihon no yosa ga wakarimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Only after going abroad did I realize how great Japan is.

examples.ja: 彼女と別れてはじめて、彼女の大切さに気づいた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo to wakarete hajimete, kanojo no taisetsusa ni kizuita.
examples.meaningEn: I realized her importance only after breaking up with her.

examples.ja: 大学を卒業してはじめて、学生時代の楽しさが分かった。
examples.romaji: Daigaku o sotsugyou shite hajimete, gakusei jidai no tanoshisa ga wakatta.
examples.meaningEn: I understood how fun my student days were only after graduating from university.

examples.ja: 子供ができてはじめて、親の気持ちが分かった。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ga dekite hajimete, oya no kimochi ga wakatta.
examples.meaningEn: I understood how parents feel only after I had a child.

title: ～てほしい (〜te hoshii)
formation: Verb-て form + ほしい
shortExplanationEn: Express a desire for someone else to do something; 'I want you to do...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てほしい grammar point is used to express a desire that someone else perform an action. It is formed by attaching ほしい to the て-form of a verb. It can be translated as 'I want you to...' in English.

examples.ja: 子供にもっと勉強してほしい。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ni motto benkyou shite hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want my child to study more.

examples.ja: 彼に私に電話してほしい。
examples.romaji: Kare ni watashi ni denwa shite hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want him to call me.

examples.ja: 友達に手伝ってほしい。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni tetsudatte hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want my friend to help me.

examples.ja: 彼女に明日ここに来てほしい。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni ashita koko ni kite hoshii.
examples.meaningEn: I want her to come here tomorrow.

title: ～ても (〜temo)
formation: Verb-て form + も, い-Adjective (～く) + ても, な-Adjective/Noun (～で) + も
shortExplanationEn: 'Even if', 'even though', or 'although'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ても grammar point expresses that even if a certain condition holds, the outcome or situation doesn't change. It can be translated as 'even if' or 'even though'.

examples.ja: 雨が降っても、運動会は開催されます。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte mo, undoukai wa kaisai saremasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if it rains, the sports festival will be held.

examples.ja: 忙しくても、毎日勉強する時間を作る。
examples.romaji: Isogashikute mo, mainichi benkyou suru jikan o tsukuru.
examples.meaningEn: Even though I'm busy, I make time to study every day.

examples.ja: 高くても、良い品質のものを買います。
examples.romaji: Takakute mo, yoi hinshitsu no mono o kaimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if it's expensive, I'll buy something of good quality.

examples.ja: 彼女が有名人でも、私は彼女のことを知らない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga yuumeijin demo, watashi wa kanojo no koto o shiranai.
examples.meaningEn: Even if she's famous, I don't know her.

title: ～といいなあ (〜to ii naa)
formation: Verb-casual + といいなあ, い-Adjective + といいなあ, な-Adjective + だといいなあ, Noun + だといいなあ
shortExplanationEn: Express a wish or hope; 'it would be nice if', 'I hope that'.
longExplanationEn: The ～といいなあ grammar point is used to express a wish or hope for something to happen or to be in a certain way. It can be translated as 'it would be nice if' or 'I hope that' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 明日晴れるといいなあ。
examples.romaji: Ashita hareru to ii naa.
examples.meaningEn: I hope it's sunny tomorrow.

examples.ja: この試験に合格するといいなあ。
examples.romaji: Kono shiken ni goukaku suru to ii naa.
examples.meaningEn: I hope I pass this exam.

examples.ja: 彼女が元気だといいなあ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga genki da to ii naa.
examples.meaningEn: I hope she is doing well.

examples.ja: 彼が新しい仕事が見つかるといいなあ。
examples.romaji: Kare ga atarashii shigoto ga mitsukaru to ii naa.
examples.meaningEn: I hope he finds a new job.

title: ～という (〜to iu)
formation: Noun + という + Noun, Verb-casual + という + Noun, い-Adjective + という + Noun, な-Adjective + という + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Used for quoting, defining, or giving an example; 'called', 'that is to say', 'like'.
longExplanationEn: The ～という grammar point is used to quote, define, or give an example of something. It can be translated as 'called', 'that is to say', or 'like' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 本屋で「ハリー・ポッター」という本を買いました。
examples.romaji: Hon'ya de 'Harry Potter' to iu hon o kaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I bought a book called 'Harry Potter' at the bookstore.

examples.ja: 昨日、美味しいというケーキを食べました。
examples.romaji: Kinou, oishii to iu keeki o tabemashita.
examples.meaningEn: Yesterday, I ate a cake that is said to be delicious.

examples.ja: 彼はとても親切だという評判です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa totemo shinsetsu da to iu hyouban desu.
examples.meaningEn: He has a reputation for being very kind.

examples.ja: 今年の夏、東京にある「ディズニーランド」というテーマパークに行きたいです。
examples.romaji: Kotoshi no natsu, Toukyou ni aru 'Disneyland' to iu teema paaku ni ikitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to go to a theme park called 'Disneyland' in Tokyo this summer.

title: ～ということだ (〜to iu koto da)
formation: Verb-casual + ということだ, い-Adjective + ということだ, な-Adjective + だということだ, Noun + だということだ
shortExplanationEn: Express a conclusion or hearsay; 'I heard', 'it means', 'it can be said'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ということだ grammar point is used to express a conclusion, summary, or hearsay about a certain situation or statement. It can be translated as 'I heard', 'it means', or 'it can be said' in English. This grammar point is often used with verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は来週結婚するということだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa raishuu kekkon suru to iu koto da.
examples.meaningEn: I heard that he is getting married next week.

examples.ja: この試験は難しいということだ。
examples.romaji: Kono shiken wa muzukashii to iu koto da.
examples.meaningEn: It means this exam is difficult.

examples.ja: 彼女が引っ越したということだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga hikkoshita to iu koto da.
examples.meaningEn: I heard that she has moved.

examples.ja: 彼は仕事がうまくいかないだということだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shigoto ga umaku ikanai da to iu koto da.
examples.meaningEn: It can be said that his work is not going well.

title: ～というと (〜to iu to)
formation: Noun + というと
shortExplanationEn: Used to provide an example or characteristic related to the topic; 'when it comes to', 'speaking of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～というと grammar point is used to indicate an example, characteristic, or association related to the topic being discussed. It can be translated as 'when it comes to', 'speaking of', or 'in terms of' in English.

examples.ja: 日本料理というと、寿司が思い浮かぶ。
examples.romaji: Nihon ryouri to iu to, sushi ga omoiukabu.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to Japanese cuisine, sushi comes to mind.

examples.ja: パリというと、エッフェル塔を思い出す。
examples.romaji: Pari to iu to, Efferu Tou wo omoidasu.
examples.meaningEn: Speaking of Paris, I recall the Eiffel Tower.

examples.ja: 夏休みというと、海に行くことが楽しみだ。
examples.romaji: Natsu yasumi to iu to, umi ni iku koto ga tanoshimi da.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to summer vacation, I look forward to going to the beach.

examples.ja: 彼というと、いつも元気な人だと思う。
examples.romaji: Kare to iu to, itsumo genki na hito da to omou.
examples.meaningEn: Speaking of him, I think he's always energetic.

title: ～というの～ (〜to iu no〜)
formation: Verb-casual + というの, い-Adjective + というの, な-Adjective + だというの, Noun + だというの
shortExplanationEn: Explain or emphasize the reason for a situation or action.
longExplanationEn: The ～というの〜 grammar point is used to provide an explanation or emphasize the reason for a certain situation or action. It can be translated as 'the reason is that...', or 'it's because...'.

examples.ja: 遅刻したのは、電車が遅れたというのだ。
examples.romaji: Chikoku shita no wa, densha ga okureta to iu no da.
examples.meaningEn: The reason I was late is because the train was delayed.

examples.ja: この部屋が汚いのは、掃除をしなかったというのだ。
examples.romaji: Kono heya ga kitanai no wa, souji o shinakatta to iu no da.
examples.meaningEn: The reason this room is dirty is because I didn't clean it.

examples.ja: 彼女が怒っているのは、彼が約束を破ったというのだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga okotte iru no wa, kare ga yakusoku o yabutta to iu no da.
examples.meaningEn: The reason she is angry is that he broke his promise.

examples.ja: 彼が成功したのは、努力して勉強したというのだ。
examples.romaji: Kare ga seikou shita no wa, doryoku shite benkyou shita to iu no da.
examples.meaningEn: The reason he succeeded is because he studied hard.

title: ～というのは (〜to iu no wa)
formation: Phrase/sentence + というのは + explanation/definition
shortExplanationEn: Used to provide an explanation or definition; 'that is to say...', 'by that, I mean...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～というのは grammar point is used to provide an explanation or definition of the preceding statement or word. It can be translated as 'that is to say...', 'by that, I mean...', or 'what I mean by...'.

examples.ja: 日本に行くというのは、日本に旅行することです。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni iku to iu no wa, Nihon ni ryokou suru koto desu.
examples.meaningEn: Going to Japan, that is to say, traveling to Japan.

examples.ja: この問題は難しいというのは、解決に時間がかかるということです。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa muzukashii to iu no wa, kaiketsu ni jikan ga kakaru to iu koto desu.
examples.meaningEn: When I say this problem is difficult, I mean it takes time to solve.

examples.ja: 彼が怒っているというのは、彼が不満を感じているということです。
examples.romaji: Kare ga okotteiru to iu no wa, kare ga fuman wo kanjiteiru to iu koto desu.
examples.meaningEn: By saying that he's angry, I mean he's feeling dissatisfied.

examples.ja: 太るというのは、体重が増えるということです。
examples.romaji: Futoru to iu no wa, taijuu ga fueru to iu koto desu.
examples.meaningEn: Gaining weight, that is to say, increasing one's body weight.

title: ～というより (〜to iu yori)
formation: Verb-casual + というより, い-Adjective + というより, な-Adjective + だというより, Noun + だというより
shortExplanationEn: Express a comparison with preference; 'rather than', 'more like', 'instead of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～というより grammar point is used to express a comparison where one description is more accurate than another. Translated as 'rather than', 'more like', or 'instead of'.

examples.ja: この部屋は狭いというより、居心地がいい。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa semai to iu yori, igokochi ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: Rather than saying this room is small, it's more like it's cozy.

examples.ja: 彼は怖いというより、頼りがいがある。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kowai to iu yori, tayori gai ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: Instead of saying he's scary, he's more like a reliable person.

examples.ja: 映画はつまらないというより、ちょっと長かった。
examples.romaji: Eiga wa tsumaranai to iu yori, chotto nagakatta.
examples.meaningEn: Rather than calling the movie boring, I'd say it was a bit long.

examples.ja: 彼女は友達だというより、家族のような存在だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa tomodachi da to iu yori, kazoku no you na sonzai da.
examples.meaningEn: Rather than a friend, she's more like family.

title: ～といっても (〜to ittemo)
formation: Verb-casual + といっても, い-Adjective + といっても, な-Adjective + だといっても, Noun + だといっても
shortExplanationEn: Express the meaning 'even though', 'although', 'but'.
longExplanationEn: The ～といっても grammar point is used to show a contrast or clarify that 'even though' something is the case, it does not necessarily lead to the expected result. It can be translated as 'even though', 'although', or 'but'.

examples.ja: 彼は有名だといっても、みんなに知られているわけではありません。
examples.romaji: Kare wa yuumei da to ittemo, minna ni shirarete iru wake de wa arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Even though he's famous, it doesn't mean everyone knows him.

examples.ja: このスープは辛いといっても、私にはちょうどいいです。
examples.romaji: Kono suupu wa karai to ittemo, watashi ni wa choudo ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: Although this soup is spicy, it's just right for me.

examples.ja: 彼女は若いですが、経験があるといっても、まだ新人です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa wakai desu ga, keiken ga aru to ittemo, mada shinjin desu.
examples.meaningEn: She's young, and although she has some experience, she's still a newcomer.

examples.ja: このレストランは高いだといっても、美味しい料理が食べられるので価値があります。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa takai da to ittemo, oishii ryouri ga taberareru node kachi ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even though this restaurant is expensive, it's worth it because you can enjoy delicious dishes.

title: ～とおり (〜toori)
formation: Verb-て form + とおり, Noun + のとおり
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is done 'as is', 'as stated', or 'as instructed'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とおり grammar point is used to express that something is done exactly as stated, instructed, or shown. It is commonly used with verbs and nouns, and can be translated as 'just like', 'as per', or 'according to'.

examples.ja: 先生が言ったとおり、宿題をやりました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga itta toori, shukudai wo yarimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I did my homework exactly as the teacher said.

examples.ja: 地図に書いてあるとおりに行けば、駅に着きます。
examples.romaji: Chizu ni kaite aru toori ni ikeba, eki ni tsukimasu.
examples.meaningEn: If you follow what's written on the map, you'll arrive at the station.

examples.ja: レシピのとおりにケーキを作った。
examples.romaji: Reshipi no toori ni keeki o tsukutta.
examples.meaningEn: I baked the cake according to the recipe.

examples.ja: 彼女は約束したとおりに時間通りに来ました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa yakusoku shita toori ni jikan doori ni kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: She arrived on time, just as she promised.

title: ～とく (〜toku)
formation: Verb-て form + とく (e.g., 食べておく→食べとく, 読んでおく→読んどく)
shortExplanationEn: Express doing something in advance or for future convenience (casual form of ～ておく).
longExplanationEn: The ～とく grammar point (from ～ておく) is used to express doing something in advance or preparing for something. It implies you're performing an action now to avoid inconvenience later. It is a casual, colloquial contraction of ～ておく.

examples.ja: 夜遅くなるから、今晩のご飯を作っとく。
examples.romaji: Yoru osoku naru kara, konban no gohan wo tsukutoku.
examples.meaningEn: Since it will be late tonight, I'll prepare dinner in advance.

examples.ja: 傘が必要かもしれないので、持っておく。
examples.romaji: Kasa ga hitsuyou kamoshirenai node, motte oku.
examples.meaningEn: I might need an umbrella, so I'll bring one along (just in case).

examples.ja: 試験が近いから、今から勉強しとこう。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga chikai kara, ima kara benkyou shitokou.
examples.meaningEn: The exam is coming up, so I'll start studying now.

examples.ja: 明日は忙しいので、洗濯をしとく。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa isogashii node, sentaku wo shitoku.
examples.meaningEn: Since I'll be busy tomorrow, I'll do the laundry in advance.

title: ～ところが (〜tokoro ga)
formation: Phrase 1 (expected situation) + ところが + Phrase 2 (unexpected result)
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a contrast or unexpected outcome; 'however', 'but', 'even though'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ところが grammar point is used to express a contrast or an unexpected outcome. It can be translated as 'however', 'but', or 'even though' in English. It emphasizes a situation that contradicts what was expected.

examples.ja: 彼はいつも遅刻するところが、今日は早く来ました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa itsumo chikoku suru tokoro ga, kyou wa hayaku kimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He's always late, but today he arrived early.

examples.ja: 昨日は暑かったところが、今日は寒くなりました。
examples.romaji: Kinou wa atsukatta tokoro ga, kyou wa samuku narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Yesterday was hot, but today it got cold.

examples.ja: 試合で負けると思ったところが、意外にも勝ちました。
examples.romaji: Shiai de makeru to omotta tokoro ga, igai ni mo kachimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I thought we would lose the match, but surprisingly we won.

examples.ja: 決勝戦で優勝するところが、最後のポイントで負けました。
examples.romaji: Kesshousen de yuushou suru tokoro ga, saigo no pointo de makemashita.
examples.meaningEn: We were about to win the final, but we lost at the last point.

title: ～ところだった (〜tokoro datta)
formation: Verb (dictionary form or ている form) + ところだった
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a near miss or a situation that almost happened; 'was just about to', 'almost', 'nearly'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ところだった grammar point is used to express a situation that almost occurred or something that the speaker was just about to do. It can be translated as 'was just about to', 'almost', or 'nearly' in English. Typically, it is formed with the dictionary form or ている form of a verb + ところだった.

examples.ja: もう少しで遅れるところだった。
examples.romaji: Mou sukoshi de okureru tokoro datta.
examples.meaningEn: I was just about to be late.

examples.ja: 彼が危うく事故にあうところだった。
examples.romaji: Kare ga ayauku jiko ni au tokoro datta.
examples.meaningEn: He almost had an accident.

examples.ja: 電車に乗るところだったが、ドアが閉まってしまった。
examples.romaji: Densha ni noru tokoro datta ga, doa ga shimatte shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: I was just about to get on the train, but the door closed.

examples.ja: 忘れ物をするところだった。
examples.romaji: Wasuremono wo suru tokoro datta.
examples.meaningEn: I almost forgot my belongings.

title: ところで (tokorode)
formation: ところで + new topic or question
shortExplanationEn: Used for changing the topic or bringing up a new topic; 'by the way', 'speaking of'.
longExplanationEn: The ところで (tokorode) grammar point is used to change the topic of conversation or introduce a new topic. It can be translated as 'by the way' or 'speaking of' in English. It is often used when the speaker wants to ask a question or share some information on a different subject. It can be placed at the beginning or in the middle of a sentence.

examples.ja: ところで、昨日の宿題はもう終わりましたか？
examples.romaji: Tokorode, kinou no shukudai wa mou owari mashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: By the way, did you finish yesterday's homework?

examples.ja: 彼はプールで泳いでいます。ところで、お昼ご飯は何にしますか？
examples.romaji: Kare wa puuru de oyoide imasu. Tokorode, ohiru gohan wa nani ni shimasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: He is swimming in the pool. By the way, what do you want for lunch?

examples.ja: 昨日は楽しかったですね。ところで、この写真を見ましたか？
examples.romaji: Kinou wa tanoshikatta desu ne. Tokorode, kono shashin wo mimashita ka?
examples.meaningEn: Yesterday was fun, wasn't it? By the way, did you see this photo?

examples.ja: ところで、明日の会議の時間を知っていますか？
examples.romaji: Tokorode, ashita no kaigi no jikan wo shitte imasu ka?
examples.meaningEn: By the way, do you know the time of tomorrow's meeting?

title: ～としたら (〜to shitara)
formation: Verb-casual + としたら, い-Adjective + としたら, な-Adjective + だとしたら, Noun + だとしたら
shortExplanationEn: Express a hypothetical situation; 'if', 'suppose', 'assuming'.
longExplanationEn: The ～としたら grammar point is used to express a hypothetical situation or assumption. It can be translated as 'if', 'suppose', or 'assuming' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 明日雨が降るとしたら、傘を持って行きましょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita ame ga furu to shitara, kasa wo motte ikimashou.
examples.meaningEn: If it rains tomorrow, let's bring an umbrella.

examples.ja: このケーキが美味しくないとしたら、誰も食べないでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki ga oishikunai to shitara, daremo tabenai deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If this cake is not delicious, nobody will eat it.

examples.ja: 彼が病気だとしたら、すぐに病院に行かせてあげてください。
examples.romaji: Kare ga byouki da to shitara, sugu ni byouin ni ikasete agete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: If he is sick, please take him to the hospital right away.

examples.ja: 彼女が学生だとしたら、このレストランは割引があるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga gakusei da to shitara, kono resutoran wa waribiki ga aru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If she is a student, there will be a discount at this restaurant.

title: ～として (〜to shite)
formation: Verb-casual + として, い-Adjective + として, な-Adjective + だとして, Noun + だとして
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a role, capacity, or identity; 'as', 'in the capacity of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～として grammar point is used to express a role, capacity, or identity in which someone or something functions. It can be translated as 'as' or 'in the capacity of' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 彼は弁護士として働いています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa bengoshi to shite hataraite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is working as a lawyer.

examples.ja: この料理はスナックとして食べられます。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa sunakku to shite taberaremasu.
examples.meaningEn: This dish can be eaten as a snack.

examples.ja: 彼女はリーダーとして尊敬されている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa riidaa to shite sonkei sarete iru.
examples.meaningEn: She is respected as a leader.

examples.ja: この本は参考書として使われます。
examples.romaji: Kono hon wa sankousho to shite tsukawaremasu.
examples.meaningEn: This book is used as a reference.

title: ～どんなに～ことか (〜donna ni〜koto ka)
formation: どんなに + Adjective + ことか, どんなに + Verb + ことか
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the extent of something or emphasizes the degree of a condition or situation.
longExplanationEn: The ～どんなに～ことか grammar point is used to emphasize the extent of a condition or circumstance, and to express how much or to what degree something is happening. It is often translated as 'how much', 'how greatly', or 'to what extent' in English. This pattern is usually used with an adjective or verb in a sentence.

examples.ja: どんなに疲れたことか、言葉では表せません。
examples.romaji: Donna ni tsukareta koto ka, kotoba de wa arawasemasen.
examples.meaningEn: I can't put into words how tired I am.

examples.ja: どんなに辛いことか、彼女は毎日頑張って働いています。
examples.romaji: Donna ni tsurai koto ka, kanojo wa mainichi ganbatte hataraiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: How hard it must be, she works hard every day.

examples.ja: 彼の成功はどんなに喜ばしいことか。
examples.romaji: Kare no seikou wa donna ni yorokobashii koto ka.
examples.meaningEn: How happy his success must be.

examples.ja: どんなに楽しいことか、みんなで旅行に行く。
examples.romaji: Donna ni tanoshii koto ka, minna de ryokou ni iku.
examples.meaningEn: How fun it is to go on a trip with everyone.

title: どんなに～ても (donna ni ~ temo)
formation: どんなに + Verb-てform + も, どんなに + い-Adjective-てform + も, どんなに + な-Adjective + でも
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'no matter how much' or 'even if'; emphasizes the degree of a situation.
longExplanationEn: The どんなに～ても grammar point is used to emphasize the degree of a situation or condition, often translated as 'no matter how much' or 'even if' in English. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, and な-adjectives.

examples.ja: どんなに勉強しても、まだわからないことがある。
examples.romaji: Donna ni benkyou shite mo, mada wakaranai koto ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how much I study, there are still things I don't understand.

examples.ja: どんなに速く走っても、彼には勝てません。
examples.romaji: Donna ni hayaku hashitte mo, kare ni wa katemasen.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how fast I run, I can't beat him.

examples.ja: どんなに疲れても、仕事を終わらせるまで帰れません。
examples.romaji: Donna ni tsukarete mo, shigoto o owaraseru made kaeremasen.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how tired I am, I can't go home until I finish my work.

examples.ja: どんなに美味しくても、これ以上食べられない。
examples.romaji: Donna ni oishikute mo, kore ijou taberarenai.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how delicious it is, I can't eat any more.

title: ～ないことはない (〜nai koto wa nai)
formation: Verb-ない form + ことはない
shortExplanationEn: Express the idea that 'it's not that it's impossible' or 'it's not that it cannot be done'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ないことはない grammar point is used to express that something is not impossible, or not that it cannot be done. It creates a softer, more indirect way of saying that something is possible or can be done, but not necessarily easy or preferred.

examples.ja: この問題を解けないことはないが、時間がかかりそうだ。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wo tokenai koto wa nai ga, jikan ga kakari sou da.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I can't solve this problem, but it seems like it will take time.

examples.ja: 彼に会わないことはないけど、あまり話したくない。
examples.romaji: Kare ni awanai koto wa nai kedo, amari hanashitakunai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I can't meet him, but I don't really want to talk.

examples.ja: この映画を見ないことはないけれど、他の映画がもっと面白いと思う。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wo minai koto wa nai keredo, hoka no eiga ga motto omoshiroi to omou.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I cannot watch this movie, but I think other movies are more interesting.

examples.ja: その本を読まないことはないが、長いので気をつけてください。
examples.romaji: Sono hon wo yomanai koto wa nai ga, nagai node ki wo tsukete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I can't read that book, but it's long, so be careful.

title: ～ないと (〜nai to)
formation: Verb-negative form + ないと
shortExplanationEn: Express a condition; 'if... not', 'unless', 'must'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ないと grammar point is used to express a condition, focusing on the negative consequences if the action is not performed. It can be translated as 'if... not', 'unless', or 'must' in English. The formation is simply attaching ないと to the negative form of a verb.

examples.ja: 朝食を食べないと、お腹が空く。
examples.romaji: Asa gohan wo tabenai to, onaka ga suku.
examples.meaningEn: If I don't eat breakfast, I will be hungry.

examples.ja: 宿題をしないと、先生に怒られる。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo shinai to, sensei ni okorareru.
examples.meaningEn: If I don't do my homework, the teacher will be angry.

examples.ja: 充電をしないと、電話が使えなくなる。
examples.romaji: Juden wo shinai to, denwa ga tsukaenaku naru.
examples.meaningEn: If I don't charge it, I won't be able to use the phone.

examples.ja: この事故を報告しないと、問題が大きくなる。
examples.romaji: Kono jiko wo houkoku shinai to, mondai ga ookiku naru.
examples.meaningEn: If I don't report this accident, the problem will become bigger.

title: ～なぜなら (〜nazenara)
formation: Sentence/Statement + なぜなら + Reason + から/だから
shortExplanationEn: Express a reason or explanation; 'because', 'the reason is'.
longExplanationEn: The ～なぜなら grammar point is used to provide a reason or explanation for a preceding statement or situation. It can be translated as 'because' or 'the reason is' in English. It is often followed by から or だから to emphasize the reason or cause.

examples.ja: このレストランが大好きだ。なぜなら料理が美味しいから。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran ga daisuki da. Nazenara ryouri ga oishii kara.
examples.meaningEn: I love this restaurant because the food is delicious.

examples.ja: 彼女は試験に合格しなかった。なぜなら勉強しなかったから。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa shiken ni goukaku shinakatta. Nazenara benkyou shinakatta kara.
examples.meaningEn: She didn't pass the exam because she didn't study.

examples.ja: 私は映画を見に行かない。なぜなら今日は忙しいから。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa eiga o mi ni ikanai. Nazenara kyou wa isogashii kara.
examples.meaningEn: I won't go to see a movie because I am busy today.

examples.ja: 彼はこのプロジェクトを任せられる。なぜなら経験が豊富だから。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kono purojekuto wo makaserareru. Nazenara keiken ga houfu dakara.
examples.meaningEn: He can be trusted with this project because he has a wealth of experience.

title: ～など (〜nado)
formation: Noun + など
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a non-exhaustive list of examples; 'such as', 'things like', 'among others'.
longExplanationEn: The ～など grammar point is used to give a non-exhaustive list of examples, meaning the mentioned items are only some of many possibilities. It can be translated as 'such as', 'things like', or 'among others' in English. This grammar point can be used with nouns.

examples.ja: 魚、果物などを市場で買いました。
examples.romaji: Sakana, kudamono nado wo ichiba de kaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I bought fish, fruits, and such at the market.

examples.ja: 彼は日本語、英語、中国語などを話せます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nihongo, eigo, chuugokugo nado wo hanasemasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can speak languages such as Japanese, English, and Chinese.

examples.ja: 美術館では絵画、彫刻などの作品を見ました。
examples.romaji: Bijutsukan de wa kaiga, choukoku nado no sakuhin wo mimashita.
examples.meaningEn: At the art museum, I saw works like paintings, sculptures, and others.

examples.ja: この店ではパン、ケーキなどのお菓子を売っています。
examples.romaji: Kono mise de wa pan, keeki nado no okashi wo utteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: This store sells sweets such as bread, cakes, and so on.

title: ～なんか (〜nanka)
formation: Noun + なんか, Verb-casual + なんか, い-Adjective + なんか, な-Adjective + なんか
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the idea of 'things like' or 'something like', used for listing examples or downplaying something.
longExplanationEn: The ～なんか grammar point is used for listing examples by indicating 'things like' or 'something like'. It can also be used to downplay or devalue something, making it seem less important or less significant than it really is.

examples.ja: お金なんかいらない。
examples.romaji: Okane nanka iranai.
examples.meaningEn: I don't need money or anything like that.

examples.ja: 彼はピアノなんかも弾けます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa piano nanka mo hikemasu.
examples.meaningEn: He can play things like the piano.

examples.ja: 忙しいなんかじゃないよ。
examples.romaji: Isogashii nanka janai yo.
examples.meaningEn: I'm not really busy or anything like that.

examples.ja: 彼女は親切なんかじゃない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa shinsetsu nanka janai.
examples.meaningEn: She's not kind or anything like that.

title: ～において (〜ni oite)
formation: Noun (Place/Time/Context) + において
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the place, time, or context where an action or situation takes place.
longExplanationEn: The ～において grammar point is used to indicate where an action or situation occurs. It can often be translated as 'in', 'at', or 'on' in English. In some cases, it can also express a specific context or aspect. This grammar point can be used with verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 日本において、お盆は家族が集まる大切な行事です。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni oite, obon wa kazoku ga atsumaru taisetsu na gyouji desu.
examples.meaningEn: In Japan, Obon is an important event where families gather.

examples.ja: 会議において、彼の意見が一番説得力がありました。
examples.romaji: Kaigi ni oite, kare no iken ga ichiban settokuryoku ga arimashita.
examples.meaningEn: In the meeting, his opinion was the most persuasive.

examples.ja: 科学の分野において、研究が日々進んでいます。
examples.romaji: Kagaku no bun'ya ni oite, kenkyuu ga hibi susunde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: In the field of science, research is advancing daily.

examples.ja: 幼少期において、子供たちは勢いよく物を覚えます。
examples.romaji: Youshouki ni oite, kodomo-tachi wa ikioi yoku mono wo oboemasu.
examples.meaningEn: During early childhood, children learn things rapidly.

title: ～にかわって (〜ni kawatte)
formation: Noun + にかわって
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'in place of', 'on behalf of', or 'instead of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にかわって grammar point is used to express that someone or something is acting in place of, on behalf of, or instead of someone or something else. Typically used with nouns (e.g., 人, 会社, etc.).

examples.ja: 彼が病気なので、私が会議に出席することになりました。私が彼にかわって会議に出席します。
examples.romaji: Kare ga byouki na no de, watashi ga kaigi ni shusseki suru koto ni narimashita. Watashi ga kare ni kawatte kaigi ni shusseki shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He is sick, so I will attend the meeting. I will attend the meeting on his behalf.

examples.ja: 母は忙しいので、私が母にかわって料理を作ります。
examples.romaji: Haha wa isogashii no de, watashi ga haha ni kawatte ryouri wo tsukurimasu.
examples.meaningEn: My mother is busy, so I will cook the meal instead of her.

examples.ja: 今度の旅行は妹にかわって、私が一緒に行きます。
examples.romaji: Kondo no ryokou wa imouto ni kawatte, watashi ga issho ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: For this trip, I will go together instead of my younger sister.

examples.ja: 先生が出張中でいないので、助手が先生にかわってクラスを担当します。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga shucchou chuu de inai no de, joshu ga sensei ni kawatte kurasu wo tantou shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The teacher is not here because of a business trip, so the assistant will take charge of the class instead of the teacher.

title: ～にしては (〜ni shite wa)
formation: Verb-casual + にしては, い-Adjective + にしては, な-Adjective + にしては, Noun + にしては
shortExplanationEn: Express 'for' or 'considering' in the sense of being unusual or unexpected.
longExplanationEn: The ～にしては grammar point is used to express that something is unusual or unexpected considering a specific condition or context. It can be translated as 'for' or 'considering' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 彼女は初心者にしては、上手に水泳ができる。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa shoshinsha ni shite wa, jouzu ni suiei ga dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: Considering she is a beginner, she can swim well.

examples.ja: このお寿司は安いにしては、とても美味しい。
examples.romaji: Kono o-sushi wa yasui ni shite wa, totemo oishii.
examples.meaningEn: Considering this sushi is cheap, it's very delicious.

examples.ja: 彼は若いにしては、とても落ち着いている。
examples.romaji: Kare wa wakai ni shite wa, totemo ochitsuite iru.
examples.meaningEn: Considering he is young, he is very calm.

examples.ja: あの人は外国人にしては、日本語がうまい。
examples.romaji: Ano hito wa gaikokujin ni shite wa, nihongo ga umai.
examples.meaningEn: For a foreigner, that person is good at Japanese.

title: ～にしても (〜ni shitemo)
formation: Verb-casual + にしても, い-Adjective + にしても, な-Adjective + だにしても, Noun + だにしても
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'even if', 'no matter', 'regardless of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にしても grammar point is used to express that even if a certain condition is true or a certain situation occurs, the outcome will not change. It can be translated as 'even if', 'no matter', or 'regardless of' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 雨が降るにしても、学校へ行かなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu ni shitemo, gakkou e ikanakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Even if it rains, I have to go to school.

examples.ja: 彼女が怒っているにしても、話し合いは避けられない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga okotte iru ni shitemo, hanashiai wa sakerarenai.
examples.meaningEn: Even if she is angry, we can't avoid talking.

examples.ja: このレストランが高いにしても、料理の質がよくておすすめです。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran ga takai ni shitemo, ryouri no shitsu ga yokute osusume desu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if this restaurant is expensive, I recommend it because the quality of the food is good.

examples.ja: 彼が有名だにしても、彼の私生活について話すのは失礼だ。
examples.romaji: Kare ga yuumei da ni shitemo, kare no shiseikatsu ni tsuite hanasu no wa shitsurei da.
examples.meaningEn: Even if he is famous, it's rude to talk about his private life.

title: ～について (〜ni tsuite)
formation: Noun + について
shortExplanationEn: Expressing 'about', 'concerning', or 'regarding'.
longExplanationEn: The ～について grammar point is used to express 'about', 'concerning', or 'regarding' a certain topic or subject. It is often used in both casual and formal contexts. The formation is simple – just add について after the noun that represents the topic you are discussing.

examples.ja: 日本の文化について学びたいです。
examples.romaji: Nihon no bunka ni tsuite manabitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I want to learn about Japanese culture.

examples.ja: 彼の意見について話し合いましょう。
examples.romaji: Kare no iken ni tsuite hanashiaimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's discuss his opinion.

examples.ja: この問題について詳しく教えてください。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai ni tsuite kuwashiku oshiete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please explain this issue in detail.

examples.ja: 子供たちの教育について考えなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi no kyouiku ni tsuite kangaenakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: We have to think about the children's education.

title: ～にとって (〜ni totte)
formation: Noun (person or thing) + にとって + Verb/Adjective/Noun
shortExplanationEn: Express the meaning 'for', 'to', 'from the perspective of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にとって grammar point is used to express the meaning 'for', 'to', or 'from the perspective of' when describing someone or something's point of view, feelings, benefits, or importance. It is used with all types of words, such as verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 私にとって、健康は一番大切なことです。
examples.romaji: Watashi ni totte, kenkou wa ichiban taisetsu na koto desu.
examples.meaningEn: For me, health is the most important thing.

examples.ja: あの会社にとって、このプロジェクトは重要な意味があります。
examples.romaji: Ano kaisha ni totte, kono purojekuto wa juuyou na imi ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: For that company, this project has significant meaning.

examples.ja: 子供たちにとって、おもちゃは楽しいものです。
examples.romaji: Kodomotachi ni totte, omocha wa tanoshii mono desu.
examples.meaningEn: For children, toys are fun things.

examples.ja: 外国人にとって、日本語は難しい言語の一つです。
examples.romaji: Gaikokujin ni totte, nihongo wa muzukashii gengo no hitotsu desu.
examples.meaningEn: For foreigners, Japanese is one of the difficult languages.

title: ～によって (〜ni yotte)
formation: Verb-casual + によって, い-Adjective + によって, な-Adjective + によって, Noun + によって
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the reason or cause, difference depending on a factor, or methods used.
longExplanationEn: The ～によって grammar point is used to express the reason or cause of something, to show a difference depending on a certain factor, or to indicate the method or means by which something is done. It can be translated as 'by', 'depending on', 'due to', or 'according to' in English. The formation is the same for verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: その事故は悪天候によって引き起こされた。
examples.romaji: Sono jiko wa akutenkou ni yotte hikiokosareta.
examples.meaningEn: The accident was caused by bad weather.

examples.ja: 生活習慣によって、健康状態が変わります。
examples.romaji: Seikatsu shuukan ni yotte, kenkou joutai ga kawarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Health conditions change depending on lifestyle habits.

examples.ja: 彼の成功は努力によって得られたものだ。
examples.romaji: Kare no seikou wa doryoku ni yotte erareta mono da.
examples.meaningEn: His success was achieved through hard work.

examples.ja: 結果は試験の難易度によって異なります。
examples.romaji: Kekka wa shiken no nan'ido ni yotte kotonarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The results vary depending on the difficulty of the exam.

title: ～によれば (〜ni yoreba)
formation: Noun + によれば
shortExplanationEn: Means 'according to', 'based on' or 'by'.
longExplanationEn: The ～によれば grammar point is used to express information, opinions or a judgement made based on a certain source. It can be translated as 'according to', 'based on', or 'by' in English. This grammar point is typically used with a noun that represents the source of information.

examples.ja: 天気予報によれば、明日は晴れるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Tenki yohou ni yoreba, ashita wa hareru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: According to the weather forecast, it will be sunny tomorrow.

examples.ja: 先生によれば、この問題は簡単だそうです。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni yoreba, kono mondai wa kantan da sou desu.
examples.meaningEn: According to the teacher, this problem seems to be easy.

examples.ja: 調査によれば、若者の喫煙率が減っているということです。
examples.romaji: Chousa ni yoreba, wakamono no kitsuenritsu ga hette iru to iu koto desu.
examples.meaningEn: According to the survey, the smoking rate among young people is decreasing.

examples.ja: 新聞記事によれば、その企業は倒産の危機にあるらしい。
examples.romaji: Shinbun kiji ni yoreba, sono kigyou wa tousan no kiki ni aru rashii.
examples.meaningEn: According to the newspaper article, the company appears to be on the verge of bankruptcy.

title: ～に対して (～ni taishite)
formation: Verb-casual + に対して, い-Adjective + に対して, な-Adjective + に対して, Noun + に対して
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a target or contrast; 'toward', 'against', 'in contrast to', 'in response to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に対して grammar point is used to indicate the target or direction of an action, or to show a contrast between two situations or objects. It can be translated as 'toward', 'against', 'in contrast to', or 'in response to' in English. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 子供たちに対して、親は常にやさしくあるべきです。
examples.romaji: Kodomotachi ni taishite, oya wa tsune ni yasashiku aru beki desu.
examples.meaningEn: Parents should always be kind towards their children.

examples.ja: 彼は質問に対して正直に答えました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shitsumon ni taishite shoujiki ni kotaemashita.
examples.meaningEn: He answered the questions honestly.

examples.ja: 田中さんは成功したに対して、佐藤さんは失敗しました。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san wa seikou shita ni taishite, Satou-san wa shippai shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: In contrast to Tanaka-san's success, Satou-san failed.

examples.ja: 彼の意見に対して、私は反対の立場を取りました。
examples.romaji: Kare no iken ni taishite, watashi wa hantai no tachiba wo torimashita.
examples.meaningEn: In response to his opinion, I took an opposing stance.

title: ～に比べて (〜ni kurabete)
formation: Verb-casual + に比べて, い-Adjective + に比べて, な-Adjective + だ/である + に比べて, Noun + だ/である + に比べて
shortExplanationEn: Comparing to; in comparison with; as opposed to.
longExplanationEn: The ～に比べて grammar point is used to compare two things or situations by highlighting their differences. It can be translated as 'comparing to', 'in comparison with', or 'as opposed to' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 夏に比べて、冬は寒いです。
examples.romaji: Natsu ni kurabete, fuyu wa samui desu.
examples.meaningEn: Winter is cold compared to summer.

examples.ja: このトマトは赤いトマトに比べて美味しくない。
examples.romaji: Kono tomato wa akai tomato ni kurabete oishikunai.
examples.meaningEn: This tomato is not as delicious compared to the red tomatoes.

examples.ja: 彼は私に比べて英語が上手です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa watashi ni kurabete eigo ga jouzu desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is better at English compared to me.

examples.ja: 東京に比べて、京都は静かな町です。
examples.romaji: Toukyou ni kurabete, Kyouto wa shizukana machi desu.
examples.meaningEn: Kyoto is a quieter city compared to Tokyo.

title: ～に関して (〜ni kanshite)
formation: Noun + に関して
shortExplanationEn: Regarding, concerning, about, with respect to.
longExplanationEn: The ～に関して grammar point is used to express the topic or subject matter one is talking about. It can be translated as 'regarding', 'concerning', 'about', or 'with respect to' in English. The formation is simple and remains the same regardless of it being used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 健康に関して気を付けることが大切です。
examples.romaji: Kenkou ni kanshite ki wo tsukeru koto ga taisetsu desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is important to be careful regarding health.

examples.ja: このプロジェクトに関しては、彼がすべての詳細を知っています。
examples.romaji: Kono purojekuto ni kanshite wa, kare ga subete no shousai wo shitteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Regarding this project, he knows all the details.

examples.ja: 環境問題に関して、私たちはもっと責任を持たなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Kankyou mondai ni kanshite, watashitachi wa motto sekinin wo motanakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Concerning environmental issues, we must take more responsibility.

examples.ja: この事件に関しての情報はまだ十分ではありません。
examples.romaji: Kono jiken ni kanshite no jouhou wa mada juubun de wa arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: The information regarding this incident is still insufficient.

title: ～の～ (〜no 〜)
formation: Noun1 + の + Noun2
shortExplanationEn: Indicates possession, attribution, or connection between two nouns.
longExplanationEn: The ～の～ grammar point is used to show the relationship between two nouns in Japanese. It can be translated as 'of', 'in', or 'the' in English. This grammar point is used to indicate possession, attribution, or connection between the two nouns.

examples.ja: これは私の傘です。
examples.romaji: Kore wa watashi no kasa desu.
examples.meaningEn: This is my umbrella.

examples.ja: あのビルは東京の有名な観光スポットです。
examples.romaji: Ano biru wa Toukyou no yuumei na kankou supotto desu.
examples.meaningEn: That building is a famous tourist spot in Tokyo.

examples.ja: 彼は部長の秘書です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa buchou no hisho desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is the chief's secretary.

examples.ja: 彼女は音楽の先生です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ongaku no sensei desu.
examples.meaningEn: She is a music teacher.

title: ～ばかり (〜bakari)
formation: Verb-ますstem + ばかり, い-Adjective + ばかり, な-Adjective + ばかり, Noun + ばかり
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'only', 'nothing but', 'always', 'just'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ばかり grammar point is used to express that something is only, nothing but, always, or just a certain way. It emphasizes the repeated or continuous nature of an action or state. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼はゲームばかりしている。
examples.romaji: Kare wa geemu bakari shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: He is always playing games.

examples.ja: この部屋は綺麗なものばかりだ。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa kirei na mono bakari da.
examples.meaningEn: This room has nothing but clean things.

examples.ja: 彼女は勉強ばかりしていて、遊んでいる暇がない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa benkyou bakari shite ite, asonde iru hima ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: She is always studying and has no time to play.

examples.ja: 彼の話は嘘ばかりで、信じることができない。
examples.romaji: Kare no hanashi wa uso bakari de, shinjiru koto ga dekinai.
examples.meaningEn: His words are nothing but lies, and I cannot believe them.

title: ～ばかりか (〜bakarika) ～も (mo)
formation: Verb-casual + ばかりか/も, い-Adjective + ばかりか/も, な-Adjective + だ + ばかりか/も, Noun + だ + ばかりか/も
shortExplanationEn: Not only... but also; more than just; not just
longExplanationEn: The grammar point ～ばかりか ～も is used to emphasize that something is not limited to only one thing, but also includes another thing. It can be translated as 'not only... but also', 'more than just', or 'not just' in English. This expression is typically used with verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼女は英語ばかりか、フランス語も話せます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa eigo bakarika, furansugo mo hanasemasu.
examples.meaningEn: Not only can she speak English, but she can also speak French.

examples.ja: このレストランは味が良いばかりか、サービスも素晴らしい。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa aji ga yoi bakarika, saabisu mo subarashii.
examples.meaningEn: Not only does this restaurant have great taste, but the service is also wonderful.

examples.ja: パーティは楽しかったばかりか、料理も美味しかった。
examples.romaji: Paatii wa tanoshikatta bakarika, ryouri mo oishikatta.
examples.meaningEn: Not only was the party fun, but the food was also delicious.

examples.ja: 彼は頭がいいばかりか、スポーツも得意だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa atama ga ii bakarika, supootsu mo tokui da.
examples.meaningEn: Not only is he smart, but he is also good at sports.

title: ～はずだ (〜hazu da)
formation: Verb-casual + はずだ, い-Adjective + はずだ, な-Adjective + なはずだ, Noun + のはずだ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an expectation, 'should' or 'it is supposed to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～はずだ grammar point is used to express an expectation or belief that something is true, even if it has not been confirmed. It can be translated as 'should', 'it is supposed to', or 'ought to' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 彼はもう着いたはずだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mou tsuita hazu da.
examples.meaningEn: He should have arrived by now.

examples.ja: この薬を飲むと、痛みがなくなるはずだ。
examples.romaji: Kono kusuri wo nomu to, itami ga nakunaru hazu da.
examples.meaningEn: If you take this medicine, the pain should go away.

examples.ja: 明日は晴れるはずだ。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa hareru hazu da.
examples.meaningEn: It is supposed to be sunny tomorrow.

examples.ja: 彼女は元気なはずだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa genki na hazu da.
examples.meaningEn: She is supposed to be well.

title: ～ば～のに (〜ba 〜noni)
formation: Verb-ば form + ～のに, い-Adjective-ば form + ～のに, な-Adjective + であれば + ～のに, Noun + であれば + ～のに
shortExplanationEn: Expresses regret or dissatisfaction; 'if only', 'wish'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ば～のに grammar point is used to express regret or dissatisfaction about a situation that didn't happen or to express a contrary view of the actual situation. It can be translated as 'if only' or 'wish' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: もっと早く来れば、電車に間に合ったのに。
examples.romaji: Motto hayaku kureba, densha ni maniatta noni.
examples.meaningEn: If only I had come earlier, I would have made it to the train.

examples.ja: もっと勉強すれば、試験に合格したのに。
examples.romaji: Motto benkyou sureba, shiken ni goukaku shita noni.
examples.meaningEn: I wish I had studied more, I would have passed the exam.

examples.ja: 彼女がもっと優しければ、彼と上手くいくのに。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga motto yasashikereba, kare to umaku iku noni.
examples.meaningEn: If only she were kinder, she would get along with him.

examples.ja: 日本語が上手であれば、この本が読めるのに。
examples.romaji: Nihongo ga jouzu de areba, kono hon ga yomeru noni.
examples.meaningEn: I wish I were good at Japanese, then I could read this book.

title: ～ば～ほど (〜ba 〜hodo)
formation: Verb-casual + ば + Verb-casual + ほど, い-Adjective + ば + い-Adjective + ほど, な-Adjective + であれば + な-Adjective + ほど
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'the more ~, the more ~'; a proportional relationship between two phrases.
longExplanationEn: The ～ば～ほど grammar point is used to express a proportional relationship between two phrases, meaning 'the more A, the more B'. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, or な-adjective.

examples.ja: 早く起きれば起きるほど、時間がたくさんある。
examples.romaji: Hayaku okireba okiru hodo, jikan ga takusan aru.
examples.meaningEn: The earlier you wake up, the more time you have.

examples.ja: 練習すればするほど、上達します。
examples.romaji: Renshuu sureba suru hodo, joutatsu shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The more you practice, the more you will improve.

examples.ja: 料理が美味しければ美味しいほど、皆が喜びます。
examples.romaji: Ryouri ga oishikereba oishii hodo, mina ga yorokobimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The more delicious the food is, the happier everyone will be.

examples.ja: 日本語が上手であれば上手なほど、日本の生活が楽になります。
examples.romaji: Nihongo ga jouzu de areba jouzu na hodo, Nihon no seikatsu ga raku ni narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: The better your Japanese is, the easier life in Japan will be.

title: ～はもちろん～も (〜wa mochiron 〜mo)
formation: Noun1 + はもちろん + Noun2 + も
shortExplanationEn: Used for emphasizing that not only one thing but also another thing is included. It means 'not only... but also' or 'of course... and...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～はもちろん～も grammar point is used to emphasize that not only the first thing mentioned is included, but also the second thing. It can be translated as 'not only... but also' or 'of course... and...' in English. This grammar phrase can be used with nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は数学はもちろん、英語も得意です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa suugaku wa mochiron, eigo mo tokui desu.
examples.meaningEn: Not only is he good at math, but he is also good at English.

examples.ja: このレストランは和食はもちろん、洋食も美味しいです。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa washoku wa mochiron, youshoku mo oishii desu.
examples.meaningEn: This restaurant not only has delicious Japanese food but also delicious Western food.

examples.ja: 私の趣味は音楽はもちろん、読書も大好きです。
examples.romaji: Watashi no shumi wa ongaku wa mochiron, dokusho mo daisuki desu.
examples.meaningEn: My hobbies are not only listening to music, but I also love reading.

examples.ja: 東京は観光はもちろん、ビジネスも盛んです。
examples.romaji: Toukyou wa kankou wa mochiron, bijinesu mo sakan desu.
examples.meaningEn: Tokyo is not only famous for sightseeing but also has a thriving business scene.

title: ～ばよかった (〜ba yokatta)
formation: Verb-ば-form + よかった, い-Adjective-く + ばよかった, な-Adjective + ならよかった
shortExplanationEn: Express a sense of regret; 'I wish', 'it would have been better if'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ばよかった grammar point is used to express a sense of regret or to convey that one wishes something had been done differently. It can be translated as 'I wish' or 'it would have been better if' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, or な-adjective.

examples.ja: もっと早く起きればよかった。
examples.romaji: Motto hayaku okireba yokatta.
examples.meaningEn: I wish I had woken up earlier.

examples.ja: 昨日のパーティーに行けばよかった。
examples.romaji: Kinou no paatii ni ikeba yokatta.
examples.meaningEn: I wish I had gone to yesterday's party.

examples.ja: もっと安いものを買えばよかった。
examples.romaji: Motto yasui mono wo kaeba yokatta.
examples.meaningEn: I wish I had bought something cheaper.

examples.ja: あの時、もっと勉強すればよかった。
examples.romaji: Ano toki, motto benkyou sureba yokatta.
examples.meaningEn: I wish I had studied more at that time.

title: ～ふりをする (〜furi wo suru)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + ふりをする, い-Adjective + ふりをする, な-Adjective + ふりをする, Noun + ふりをする
shortExplanationEn: Pretending or acting as if; 'pretend', 'act like'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ふりをする grammar point is used to express that someone is pretending or acting as if they are doing something, feeling a certain way, or possessing a certain quality. It can be translated as 'pretend' or 'act like' in English. The formation is the same for verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は知らないふりをしました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shiranai furi wo shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He pretended not to know.

examples.ja: 彼女は病気のふりをして、学校を休みました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa byouki no furi wo shite, gakkou wo yasumimashita.
examples.meaningEn: She pretended to be sick and skipped school.

examples.ja: 彼は嬉しいふりをしていますが、本当は悲しいです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa ureshii furi wo shiteimasu ga, hontou wa kanashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: He is pretending to be happy, but he is actually sad.

examples.ja: 彼女はリーダーのふりをして、チームを率いました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa riidaa no furi wo shite, chiimu wo hikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: She pretended to be a leader and led the team.

title: ～べきだ (〜beki da)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + べきだ
shortExplanationEn: Express an obligation or something that should be done (mainly with verbs).
longExplanationEn: The ～べきだ grammar point is used to express a sense of obligation or to indicate something that should be done. In modern Japanese, it is almost always used with verbs in the dictionary form. In English, it can be translated as 'should' or 'ought to'.

examples.ja: 勉強するべきだ。
examples.romaji: Benkyou suru beki da.
examples.meaningEn: You should study.

examples.ja: 早く寝るべきだ。
examples.romaji: Hayaku neru beki da.
examples.meaningEn: You should sleep early.

examples.ja: 静かにするべきだ。
examples.romaji: Shizuka ni suru beki da.
examples.meaningEn: You should be quiet.

examples.ja: 彼に謝るべきだ。
examples.romaji: Kare ni ayamaru beki da.
examples.meaningEn: You should apologize to him.

title: ～ほど～ (〜hodo〜)
formation: Verb-casual + ほど, い-Adjective + ほど, な-Adjective + ほど, Noun + ほど
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the degree or extent; 'about', 'to the extent', 'as much as'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ほど grammar point is used to express the degree or extent of something. It can be translated as 'about', 'to the extent', or 'as much as' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 彼女は私ほど速く走ることができません。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa watashi hodo hayaku hashiru koto ga dekimasen.
examples.meaningEn: She cannot run as fast as me.

examples.ja: このケーキはあのケーキほど美味しくない。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa ano keeki hodo oishikunai.
examples.meaningEn: This cake is not as delicious as that cake.

examples.ja: 日本ほど四季がはっきりしている国はない。
examples.romaji: Nihon hodo shiki ga hakkiri shite iru kuni wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: There is no country with as distinct seasons as Japan.

examples.ja: 東京は大阪ほど人が多くない。
examples.romaji: Toukyou wa Oosaka hodo hito ga ookunai.
examples.meaningEn: Tokyo does not have as many people as Osaka.

title: ～ますように (〜masu you ni)
formation: Verb-ます form, remove final る + ように
shortExplanationEn: Used to express a wish or hope for oneself or someone else.
longExplanationEn: The ～ますように grammar point is used to express a wish or hope. It is often used as a closing phrase in letters or messages, and can be applied to both oneself and others. The phrase is typically used with the ます form of the verb, with the final る replaced by ように.

examples.ja: 明日のテストで良い点をとりますように。
examples.romaji: Ashita no tesuto de ii ten wo torimasu you ni.
examples.meaningEn: I hope I get good grades on tomorrow's test.

examples.ja: 彼が無事に帰ってきますように。
examples.romaji: Kare ga buji ni kaette kimasu you ni.
examples.meaningEn: I hope he comes back safely.

examples.ja: みんなが健康でいられますように。
examples.romaji: Minna ga kenkou de iraremasu you ni.
examples.meaningEn: I hope everyone stays healthy.

examples.ja: 彼女に幸せになりますように。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni shiawase ni narimasu you ni.
examples.meaningEn: I hope she becomes happy.

title: まったく～ない (mattaku ~nai)
formation: まったく + Verb-negative, まったく + い-Adjective-negative, まったく + な-Adjective-negative
shortExplanationEn: Completely not; not at all.
longExplanationEn: The まったく～ない grammar point is used to express that something is not the case at all, or to emphasize the negativity of a situation or action. It is used with negative forms of verbs, い-adjectives, and な-adjectives. It can be translated as 'completely not' or 'not at all' in English.

examples.ja: 彼は、まったく覚えていない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa, mattaku oboete inai.
examples.meaningEn: He doesn't remember at all.

examples.ja: この部屋はまったく暖かくない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa mattaku atatakakunai.
examples.meaningEn: This room is not warm at all.

examples.ja: 彼女はまったく遅れていない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa mattaku okurete inai.
examples.meaningEn: She is not late at all.

examples.ja: これはまったく便利ではない。
examples.romaji: Kore wa mattaku benri de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: This is not convenient at all.

title: ～まで (〜made)
formation: Verb-casual + まで, い-Adjective + まで, な-Adjective + まで, Noun + まで
shortExplanationEn: Express 'until', 'up to', or 'as far as' to indicate the end point of an action or range.
longExplanationEn: The ～まで grammar point is used to express the end point, limit, or extent of an action or range in terms of time, space, or quantity. It can be translated as 'until', 'up to', or 'as far as' in English. The formation depends on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 10時まで働きます。
examples.romaji: Ju-ji made hatarakimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will work until 10 o'clock.

examples.ja: 東京まで電車で行きます。
examples.romaji: Toukyou made densha de ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will go by train as far as Tokyo.

examples.ja: 彼は疲れるまで走りました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tsukareru made hashirimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He ran until he got tired.

examples.ja: 5ページまで読んでください。
examples.romaji: Go-peeji made yonde kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please read up to page 5.

title: ～まま (〜mama)
formation: Verb-ます stem + まま, い-Adjective + まま, な-Adjective + なまま, Noun + のまま
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the state or condition remaining unchanged.
longExplanationEn: The 〜まま grammar point is used to indicate that a state or condition remains unchanged or continues as it is, without any change or intervention. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, and nouns to express the idea of something being left, kept, or maintained in its current state.

examples.ja: 部屋の窓を開けたまま寝ました。
examples.romaji: Heya no mado wo aketa mama nemashita.
examples.meaningEn: I slept with the window of the room open.

examples.ja: 熱いままのコーヒーを飲んで、舌を焦がしました。
examples.romaji: Atsui mama no koohii wo nonde, shita wo kogashimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I drank the coffee while it was still hot and burned my tongue.

examples.ja: 彼は忙しいなまま、休みの日も仕事をしています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isogashii na mama, yasumi no hi mo shigoto wo shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He continues to be busy, working even on his days off.

examples.ja: 彼女は故郷にいるまま、外国に行かないで仕事を見つけた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa furusato ni iru mama, gaikoku ni ikanaide shigoto wo mitsuketa.
examples.meaningEn: She found a job without going abroad, staying in her hometown.

title: まるで～よう (maru de ~ you)
formation: まるで + phrase/sentence + よう
shortExplanationEn: Used to express a strong resemblance or similarity; 'as if', 'just like', 'as though'.
longExplanationEn: The まるで～よう grammar point is used to emphasize a strong resemblance or similarity between two things or situations. It can be translated as 'as if', 'just like', or 'as though' in English. The formation is まるで + phrase/sentence + よう.

examples.ja: 彼女はまるで天使のように美しい。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa maru de tenshi no you ni utsukushii.
examples.meaningEn: She is as beautiful as an angel.

examples.ja: 彼はまるでロボットのように仕事をしている。
examples.romaji: Kare wa maru de robotto no you ni shigoto wo shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: He works as if he is a robot.

examples.ja: あの家はまるでお城のようだ。
examples.romaji: Ano ie wa maru de oshiro no you da.
examples.meaningEn: That house is just like a castle.

examples.ja: ここはまるで楽園のような場所です。
examples.romaji: Koko wa maru de rakuen no you na basho desu.
examples.meaningEn: This place is as though it's a paradise.

title: ～てみる (〜te miru)
formation: Verb-て form + みる
shortExplanationEn: A grammar structure used to express the action of trying or attempting something to see what happens.
longExplanationEn: The 〜てみる grammar point is used with a verb in the 〜て form followed by みる. It emphasizes doing something as a trial or to see the result. It often translates as 'to try doing something' or 'try it out'.

examples.ja: 彼は走ってみたが、まだ膝が痛いと感じた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa hashitte mita ga, mada hiza ga itai to kanjita.
examples.meaningEn: He tried running, but he still felt pain in his knee.

examples.ja: 彼女は料理を作ってみたが、失敗してしまった。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ryouri o tsukutte mita ga, shippai shite shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: She tried cooking but ended up failing.

examples.ja: 初めてのデートで、彼は彼女にサプライズをしようと考え、歌ってみた。
examples.romaji: Hajimete no deeto de, kare wa kanojo ni sapuraizu o shiyou to kangae, utatte mita.
examples.meaningEn: On their first date, he thought of surprising her and tried singing.

examples.ja: このケーキは美味しそうだから、味見に食べてみる。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki wa oishisou dakara, ajimi ni tabete miru.
examples.meaningEn: Since this cake looks delicious, I will try tasting it.

title: ～みたいだ (〜mitai da)
formation: Verb-casual + みたいだ, い-Adjective + みたいだ, な-Adjective + みたいだ, Noun + みたいだ
shortExplanationEn: Express similarity or appearance; 'like', 'seems', 'looks'.
longExplanationEn: The ～みたいだ grammar point is used to express similarity, appearance or resemblance to something. It can be translated as 'like', 'seems', or 'looks' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 彼は怒っているみたいだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa okotte iru mitai da.
examples.meaningEn: He seems to be angry.

examples.ja: そのビルは古いみたいだ。
examples.romaji: Sono biru wa furui mitai da.
examples.meaningEn: That building looks old.

examples.ja: 彼女は疲れているみたいだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa tsukarete iru mitai da.
examples.meaningEn: She seems to be tired.

examples.ja: この料理は辛いみたいだ。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa karai mitai da.
examples.meaningEn: This dish seems to be spicy.

title: ～めったに～ない (〜metta ni 〜nai)
formation: めったに + Verb[ない-form] / めったに + い-Adjective[ない-form] / めったに + な-Adjective[じゃない/ではない-form]
shortExplanationEn: Used to express that something rarely, hardly ever or seldom happens.
longExplanationEn: The ～めったに～ない grammar point is used to express that something rarely, hardly ever or seldom happens. It is a negative expression and always needs to be used with ～ない (negative form) at the end.

examples.ja: 彼はめったに外食しない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa metta ni gaishoku shinai.
examples.meaningEn: He hardly ever eats out.

examples.ja: 私はめったに寿司を食べない。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa metta ni sushi wo tabenai.
examples.meaningEn: I seldom eat sushi.

examples.ja: この地域はめったに雪が降らない。
examples.romaji: Kono chiiki wa metta ni yuki ga furanai.
examples.meaningEn: It hardly ever snows in this area.

examples.ja: 彼女はめったに怒らない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa metta ni okoranai.
examples.meaningEn: She hardly ever gets angry.

title: ～めったにない (〜metta ni nai)
formation: Verb-negative + めったにない, い-Adjective-negative + めったにない, な-Adjective-negative + めったにない, Noun-negative + めったにない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the rarity of an action, event or state; 'hardly', 'rarely', 'seldom'.
longExplanationEn: The ～めったにない grammar point is used to express that an action, event or state rarely, hardly, or seldom happens. It is usually combined with a negative verb form. Though it can be used with positive verbs, it is uncommon and sounds unnatural.

examples.ja: この町では雪が降ることはめったにない。
examples.romaji: Kono machi de wa yuki ga furu koto wa metta ni nai.
examples.meaningEn: It hardly ever snows in this town.

examples.ja: 彼はめったに外食しない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa metta ni gaishoku shinai.
examples.meaningEn: He rarely eats out.

examples.ja: 友達が遊びに来るのはめったにない。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga asobi ni kuru no wa metta ni nai.
examples.meaningEn: Friends seldom come to visit.

examples.ja: 彼女はめったに怒らない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa metta ni okoranai.
examples.meaningEn: She hardly ever gets angry.

title: もしかすると〜かもしれない (moshikasuru to 〜kamoshirenai)
formation: もしかすると + Sentence + かもしれない
shortExplanationEn: Express the possibility of something; 'maybe', 'perhaps', 'might'.
longExplanationEn: The もしかすると〜かもしれない grammar point is used to indicate that there might be a possibility of a certain situation happening or someone feeling a certain way. This structure is often used to describe uncertain situations and can be translated into English as 'maybe', 'perhaps', or 'might'.

examples.ja: もしかすると彼は遅刻するかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Moshikasuru to kare wa chikoku suru kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: Maybe he might be late.

examples.ja: もしかすると彼女は疲れているかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Moshikasuru to kanojo wa tsukarete iru kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: Perhaps she might be tired.

examples.ja: もしかすると雨が降るかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Moshikasuru to ame ga furu kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: It might rain, maybe.

examples.ja: もしかすると彼らはその映画が好きじゃないかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Moshikasuru to karera wa sono eiga ga suki janai kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: Maybe they might not like that movie.

title: もし～たなら (moshi ~ tanara)
formation: もし + Verb-casual past + なら, もし + い-Adjective-casual past + なら, もし + な-Adjective + だったなら, もし + Noun + だったなら
shortExplanationEn: Express a hypothetical condition, 'if' or 'in case'.
longExplanationEn: The もし～たなら grammar point is used to express a hypothetical condition or situation in a more polite manner. It emphasizes the conditional part of a sentence and can be translated as 'if' or 'in case' in English. It is mainly used with verbs in the past tense, casual form.

examples.ja: もし雨が降ったなら、傘を持って行きましょう。
examples.romaji: Moshi ame ga futta nara, kasa wo motte ikimashou.
examples.meaningEn: If it rains, let's bring an umbrella.

examples.ja: もしケーキが美味しくなかったなら、誰も食べないでしょう。
examples.romaji: Moshi keeki ga oishikunakatta nara, daremo tabenai deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If the cake was not delicious, nobody would eat it.

examples.ja: もし彼が病気だったなら、すぐに病院に行かせてあげてください。
examples.romaji: Moshi kare ga byouki datta nara, sugu ni byouin ni ikasete agete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: If he was sick, please take him to the hospital right away.

examples.ja: もし彼女が学生だったなら、このレストランは割引があるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Moshi kanojo ga gakusei datta nara, kono resutoran wa waribiki ga aru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If she were a student, there would be a discount at this restaurant.

title: もし～ても (moshi ~ temo)
formation: もし + Verb-て form + も / もし + い-Adjective(く) + ても / もし + な-Adjective/Noun + でも
shortExplanationEn: Express a hypothetical situation or assumption with the nuance of 'even if'.
longExplanationEn: The もし～ても grammar point is used to describe a situation in which even if the stated condition is true, the result or decision does not change. It can be translated as 'even if', 'no matter if', or 'suppose ~'.

examples.ja: もし雨が降っても、ピクニックに行きます。
examples.romaji: Moshi ame ga futtemo, pikunikku ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if it rains, we will go on a picnic.

examples.ja: もし彼が謝っても、まだ怒っているでしょう。
examples.romaji: Moshi kare ga ayamattemo, mada okotteiru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: Even if he apologizes, I will still be angry.

examples.ja: もし試験に合格しても、まだ勉強を続けます。
examples.romaji: Moshi shiken ni goukaku shitemo, mada benkyou wo tsuzukemasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if I pass the exam, I will still continue studying.

examples.ja: もし彼女が遅く来ても、待っています。
examples.romaji: Moshi kanojo ga osoku kite mo, matteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if she comes late, I will wait.

title: もしも～なら (moshimo ~ nara)
formation: Verb-casual + なら, い-Adjective + なら, な-Adjective + なら, Noun + なら
shortExplanationEn: Express a hypothetical condition or situation; 'if', 'in case', 'suppose'.
longExplanationEn: The もしも～なら grammar point is used to express a hypothetical condition or situation, often with a sense of uncertainty. It can be translated as 'if', 'in case', or 'suppose'. The formation varies slightly based on whether it is used with a verb, an い-adjective, a な-adjective, or a noun.

examples.ja: もしも明日雨が降ったなら、出かけません。
examples.romaji: Moshimo ashita ame ga futta nara, dekakemasen.
examples.meaningEn: If it rains tomorrow, I won't go out.

examples.ja: もしも彼が来なかったなら、私たちだけでパーティーをします。
examples.romaji: Moshimo kare ga konakatta nara, watashitachi dake de paatii wo shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: If he doesn't come, we'll have the party just by ourselves.

examples.ja: もしも彼女が疲れているなら、休ませてあげてください。
examples.romaji: Moshimo kanojo ga tsukarete iru nara, yasumasete agete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: If she is tired, please let her rest.

examples.ja: もしもそれが真実なら、認めなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Moshimo sore ga shinjitsu nara, mitomenakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: If that is true, we have to admit it.

title: ～ようとしない (〜you to shinai)
formation: Verb-volitional form + としない
shortExplanationEn: Not showing any sign or intention to do something.
longExplanationEn: The ～ようとしない grammar point indicates that someone is not showing any sign or intention of doing an action. It is used to express a refusal or unwillingness to do something. It is formed by using the volitional form of a verb followed by としない.

examples.ja: 彼は勉強しようとしない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa benkyou shiyou to shinai.
examples.meaningEn: He doesn't show any sign of trying to study.

examples.ja: 彼女は謝ろうとしない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ayamarou to shinai.
examples.meaningEn: She doesn't show any intention of apologizing.

examples.ja: 息子は部屋を片付けようとしない。
examples.romaji: Musuko wa heya wo katazuke you to shinai.
examples.meaningEn: My son doesn't show any intention of cleaning his room.

examples.ja: 犬は散歩に行こうとしない。
examples.romaji: Inu wa sanpo ni ikou to shinai.
examples.meaningEn: The dog doesn't show any sign of wanting to go for a walk.

title: ～ようと思う (〜you to omou)
formation: Verb-volitional + と思う
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the speaker's intention or plan to do something.
longExplanationEn: The ～ようと思う grammar point is used to express the speaker's intention or plan to do something in the future. It can be translated as 'I think I will...' or 'I am planning to...' in English. It is formed by taking the volitional form of a verb and adding と思う.

examples.ja: 今日は早く寝ようと思います。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa hayaku neyou to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think I will go to bed early today.

examples.ja: 明日美術館に行こうと思っています。
examples.romaji: Ashita bijutsukan ni ikou to omotteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am planning to go to the art museum tomorrow.

examples.ja: 新しいカメラを買おうと思います。
examples.romaji: Atarashii kamera wo kaou to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I think I will buy a new camera.

examples.ja: 来週友達と映画を見ようと思っています。
examples.romaji: Raishuu tomodachi to eiga wo miyou to omotteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am planning to watch a movie with my friend next week.

title: ～ように (〜you ni)
formation: Verb-casual + ように, い-Adjective + ように, な-Adjective + に, Noun + のように
shortExplanationEn: Expressing a purpose, a goal or a way of doing something; 'in order to', 'so that', 'like', 'as'
longExplanationEn: The ～ように grammar point is used to express a purpose, a goal, or a way of doing something. It can be translated as 'in order to', 'so that', 'like', or 'as' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 早く家に帰るように、仕事を急いで終わらせました。
examples.romaji: Hayaku ie ni kaeru you ni, shigoto wo isoide owarasemashita.
examples.meaningEn: In order to get home early, I hurried to finish my work.

examples.ja: 彼女は美しい花のように見えました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa utsukushii hana no you ni miemashita.
examples.meaningEn: She looked like a beautiful flower.

examples.ja: この部屋がきれいになるように、掃除をしましょう。
examples.romaji: Kono heya ga kirei ni naru you ni, souji wo shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's clean so that this room becomes clean.

examples.ja: 山田さんは元気なように振る舞っていましたが、実際は疲れているらしいです。
examples.romaji: Yamada-san wa genki na you ni furumatte imashita ga, jissai wa tsukarete iru rashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: Yamada-san was acting as if he was energetic, but he actually seems to be tired.

title: ～ように (〜you ni)
formation: Verb-casual + ように, い-Adjective + ように, な-Adjective + だように, Noun + のように
shortExplanationEn: Used to express a purpose, a way, or a natural tendency.
longExplanationEn: The ～ように grammar point is used to express a purpose, a way or a method, or a natural tendency. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, or nouns. It is frequently used with the verb する (to do) to express the purpose or method.

examples.ja: 子供が安全に過ごせるように、家の中を整理します。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ga anzen ni sugoseru you ni, ie no naka wo seiri shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I will organize the inside of the house so that the children can spend time safely.

examples.ja: 美しい花のように、彼女は笑顔が素敵です。
examples.romaji: Utsukushii hana no you ni, kanojo wa egao ga suteki desu.
examples.meaningEn: Like a beautiful flower, her smile is lovely.

examples.ja: 自然に英語を話せるように、毎日練習しています。
examples.romaji: Shizen ni eigo wo hanaseru you ni, mainichi renshuu shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I practice every day to be able to speak English naturally.

examples.ja: 成功するように、計画を立てましょう。
examples.romaji: Seikou suru you ni, keikaku wo tatemashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's make a plan to succeed.

title: ～ように (〜you ni)
formation: Verb-casual + ように, い-Adjective + ように, な-Adjective + に, Noun + のように
shortExplanationEn: Used to express purpose or how something is done; 'like', 'as if', 'in order to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ように grammar point is used to express the purpose of an action or the manner in which something is done. It can be translated as 'like', 'as if', or 'in order to' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 分かりやすいように説明してください。
examples.romaji: Wakariyasui you ni setsumei shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please explain it in a way that is easy to understand.

examples.ja: 彼は鳥のように歌います。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tori no you ni utaimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He sings like a bird.

examples.ja: 友達と仲良くするように努めています。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to nakayoku suru you ni tsutometeimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am trying to get along well with my friends.

examples.ja: 日本語が上手になるように勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Nihongo ga jouzu ni naru you ni benkyou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am studying to become better at Japanese.

title: ～ようにしましょう (〜you ni shimashou)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + ようにしましょう / Verb-negative form + ようにしましょう
shortExplanationEn: A suggestion to make an effort or try to do something; 'let's try to', 'let's make sure to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ようにしましょう grammar point is used to suggest making an effort or trying to do something. It can be translated as 'let's try to' or 'let's make sure to' in English. Typically, we use the dictionary or negative form of the verb with ようにする/ようにしましょう to show the effort we want to make.

examples.ja: 毎日運動するようにしましょう。
examples.romaji: Mainichi undou suru you ni shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's try to exercise every day.

examples.ja: 早く寝るようにしましょう。
examples.romaji: Hayaku neru you ni shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's make sure to go to bed early.

examples.ja: 親切にするようにしましょう。
examples.romaji: Shinsetsu ni suru you ni shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: Let's try to be kind.

examples.ja: 時間を無駄にしないようにしましょう。
examples.romaji: Jikan wo muda ni shinai you ni shimashou.
