formation: Verb-volitional + か + Verb-volitional + まいか, い-Adjective + であるか + い-Adjective + であるまいか, な-Adjective + であるか + な-Adjective + であるまいか, Noun + であるか + Noun + であるまいか
shortExplanationEn: Used to express a dilemma or indecision between two options or actions.
longExplanationEn: The ～か～まいか grammar point expresses uncertainty or indecision, often with a more formal or literary feel. Typically, it is seen with verbs in the volitional form plus まい (the negative volitional). In more academic or stiff writing, い／な-adjectives and nouns plus である can also appear before まいか.

examples.ja: このプロジェクトを引き受けようか引き受けまいか、まだ決めていない。
examples.romaji: Kono purojekuto o hikiukeyou ka hikiukemai ka, mada kimete inai.
examples.meaningEn: I haven't decided whether to take on this project or not.

examples.ja: 彼が成功しようか成功するまいか、今は予測できない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga seikou shiyou ka seikou suru maika, ima wa yosoku dekinai.
examples.meaningEn: Whether he will succeed or not is unpredictable at the moment.

examples.ja: この問題は簡単であるか難しいであるまいか、意見が分かれている。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa kantan de aru ka muzukashii de aru maika, iken ga wakarete iru.
examples.meaningEn: Opinions are divided on whether this problem is simple or difficult.

examples.ja: 彼はリーダーであるかフォロワーであるまいか、自分で選ぶべきだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa riidā de aru ka forowā de aru maika, jibun de erabu beki da.
examples.meaningEn: He should choose for himself whether he is a leader or a follower.

title: ～からこそ (〜kara koso)
formation: Verb-casual + からこそ, い-Adjective + からこそ, な-Adjective + だからこそ, Noun + だからこそ
shortExplanationEn: Express the emphasis on the reason; 'precisely because', 'it is precisely because'.
longExplanationEn: The ～からこそ grammar point is used to emphasize the reason or cause of a situation, often implying that the effect is especially notable or even surprising. It can be translated as 'precisely because' or 'it is precisely because.'

examples.ja: 失敗したからこそ、成功の意味がわかる。
examples.romaji: Shippai shita kara koso, seikou no imi ga wakaru.
examples.meaningEn: It is precisely because I failed that I understand the meaning of success.

examples.ja: 苦しかったからこそ、今日の私がある。
examples.romaji: Kurushikatta kara koso, kyou no watashi ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: It is precisely because I went through hardships that I am who I am today.

examples.ja: 彼は親切だからこそ、人気がある。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shinsetsu da kara koso, ninki ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: He is popular precisely because he is kind.

examples.ja: 昔の経験があるからこそ、その問題がよく理解できる。
examples.romaji: Mukashi no keiken ga aru kara koso, sono mondai ga yoku rikai dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: I can understand that problem well precisely because I have past experience.

title: 〜からして (〜kara shite)
formation: Noun + からして
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is exceptional, unusual, or negative based on a particular aspect or point.
longExplanationEn: The 〜からして grammar point is used when you want to make a conclusion or emphasize that something is exceptional (often negative or surprising) from a certain aspect or starting point. The part before からして serves as the base or clue for the judgment.

examples.ja: 彼の顔つきからして、何か悪いことがあったのだろう。
examples.romaji: Kare no kaotsuki kara shite, nanika warui koto ga atta no darou.
examples.meaningEn: From his facial expression, something bad must have happened.

examples.ja: この料理の見た目からして、おいしくなさそうだ。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri no mitame kara shite, oishikunasa sou da.
examples.meaningEn: Judging from the appearance of this dish, it doesn't look delicious.

examples.ja: 彼女の試験成績からして、合格するのは難しいでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no shiken seiseki kara shite, goukaku suru no wa muzukashii deshou.
examples.meaningEn: Considering her test scores, it will be difficult for her to pass.

examples.ja: その映画のタイトルからして、面白くないと思う。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga no taitoru kara shite, omoshirokunai to omou.
examples.meaningEn: Judging from the title of the movie, I think it's not interesting.

title: ～からすると (〜kara suru to)
formation: Noun + からすると
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a judgment or evaluation based on a certain standard; 'from this perspective', 'considering', 'judging from'.
longExplanationEn: The ～からすると grammar point is used to make a judgment or evaluation based on some criterion, perspective, or standard. It can be translated as 'from this perspective', 'considering', or 'judging from' in English.

examples.ja: 経験からすると、この仕事は難しいでしょう。
examples.romaji: Keiken kara suru to, kono shigoto wa muzukashii deshou.
examples.meaningEn: Considering their experience, this job might be difficult.

examples.ja: 評判からすると、あのレストランはとても美味しいです。
examples.romaji: Hyouban kara suru to, ano resutoran wa totemo oishii desu.
examples.meaningEn: Judging from the reputation, that restaurant is very delicious.

examples.ja: 価格からすると、この商品は高品質のものと思われます。
examples.romaji: Kakaku kara suru to, kono shouhin wa kouhinshitsu no mono to omowaremasu.
examples.meaningEn: Considering the price, this product seems to be of high quality.

examples.ja: このエリアですが、交通の便からすると、便利です。
examples.romaji: Kono eria desu ga, koutsuu no ben kara suru to, benri desu.
examples.meaningEn: As for this area, considering the transportation convenience, it's convenient.

title: ～からといって (〜kara to itte)
formation: Verb-casual + からといって, い-Adjective + からといって, な-Adjective + だからといって, Noun + だからといって
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'just because...doesn't mean' or 'even though...not necessarily'.
longExplanationEn: The ～からといって grammar point is used to convey that even though something is true, it does not necessarily mean a certain result will occur. It can be translated as 'just because...doesn't mean' or 'even though...not necessarily.'

examples.ja: 雨が降ったからといって、試合が中止になるわけではありません。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futta kara to itte, shiai ga chuushi ni naru wake de wa arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Just because it rained doesn't mean the game will be canceled.

examples.ja: 彼が有名だからといって、彼の意見がいつも正しいわけではない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga yuumei da kara to itte, kare no iken ga itsumo tadashii wake de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: Just because he is famous doesn't mean his opinions are always correct.

examples.ja: このスープが辛いからといって、全ての人が辛いと感じるわけではない。
examples.romaji: Kono suupu ga karai kara to itte, subete no hito ga karai to kanjiru wake de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: Just because this soup is spicy doesn't mean everyone will find it spicy.

examples.ja: 彼女が泣いているからといって、悲しいわけではないかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga naiteiru kara to itte, kanashii wake de wa nai kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: Just because she is crying doesn't necessarily mean she's sad.

title: ～から～にかけて (〜kara 〜ni kakete)
formation: Noun 1 + から + Noun 2 + にかけて
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a range of time, space, or quantities; 'from...to', 'between...and', 'over'.
longExplanationEn: The ～から～にかけて grammar pattern is used to indicate a range or scope—often in time or space. It can be translated as 'from...to', 'between...and', or 'over' in English.

examples.ja: 午前９時から午後５時にかけて、仕事をしています。
examples.romaji: Gozen ku-ji kara gogo go-ji ni kakete, shigoto wo shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: I work from 9 in the morning to 5 in the evening.

examples.ja: 東京から大阪にかけて、新幹線が走っています。
examples.romaji: Toukyou kara Oosaka ni kakete, shinkansen ga hashitte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: The bullet train runs from Tokyo to Osaka.

examples.ja: 春から夏にかけて、花がたくさん咲いています。
examples.romaji: Haru kara natsu ni kakete, hana ga takusan saite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: Many flowers bloom between spring and summer.

examples.ja: ２０歳から３０歳にかけて、多くの人が大学を卒業し、仕事に就くことが一般的です。
examples.romaji: Hatachi kara sanjuu-sai ni kakete, ooku no hito ga daigaku wo sotsugyou shi, shigoto ni tsuku koto ga ippan-teki desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is common for many people to graduate from university and start working between the ages of 20 and 30.

title: ～からには (〜kara niwa)
formation: Noun + だ + からには, な-Adjective + だ + からには, い-Adjective + からには, Verb-casual + からには
shortExplanationEn: Express a sense of responsibility or determination; 'since', 'now that', 'if'.
longExplanationEn: The ～からには grammar point is used to show a sense of responsibility or strong resolve once a certain condition is met. It can be translated as 'since', 'now that', or 'if'.

examples.ja: 試験に合格したからには、一所懸命働かなければならない。
examples.romaji: Shiken ni goukaku shita kara niwa, isshokenmei hatarakanakereba naranai.
examples.meaningEn: Since I passed the exam, I have to work hard.

examples.ja: 約束したからには、守らなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Yakusoku shita kara niwa, mamoranakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Since I made a promise, I must keep it.

examples.ja: この映画が面白いからには、友達にも見せたい。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga ga omoshiroi kara niwa, tomodachi ni mo misetai.
examples.meaningEn: Since this movie is interesting, I want to show it to my friends too.

examples.ja: 彼女が助けを求めたからには、手伝わなければならない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga tasuke wo motometa kara niwa, tetsudawanakereba naranai.
examples.meaningEn: Since she asked for help, I must help her.

title: ～から見ると (〜kara miru to)
formation: Noun + から見ると
shortExplanationEn: Express a perspective or point of view; 'from the standpoint of', 'in terms of', 'from the perspective of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～から見ると grammar point is used to express a particular perspective or point of view. In English, it translates to 'from the standpoint of', 'from the perspective of', or 'in terms of'. It is usually used with a noun that indicates the perspective.

examples.ja: 費用から見ると、このプランはお得です。
examples.romaji: Hiyou kara miru to, kono puran wa otoku desu.
examples.meaningEn: In terms of cost, this plan is a good deal.

examples.ja: 彼女の意見から見ると、社長の決定は理解できる。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no iken kara miru to, shachou no kettei wa rikai dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: From her perspective, the president's decision is understandable.

examples.ja: 実力から見ると、彼はチームで最も優れている。
examples.romaji: Jitsuryoku kara miru to, kare wa chiimu de mottomo sugurete iru.
examples.meaningEn: In terms of ability, he is the best in the team.

examples.ja: 健康面から見ると、この食品は良くない。
examples.romaji: Kenkoumen kara miru to, kono shokuhin wa yokunai.
examples.meaningEn: From the standpoint of health, this food is not good.

title: ～から言うと (〜kara iuto)
formation: Noun + から言うと (common), [Verb-casual / Adjective + から言って] (less common)
shortExplanationEn: Express a judgment or opinion based on a specific criterion; 'speaking from', 'in terms of', 'from the viewpoint of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～から言うと grammar point is used to express a judgment or opinion based on a specific criterion or aspect. It can be translated as 'speaking from', 'in terms of', or 'from the viewpoint of' in English. It is most commonly used with nouns, although you may occasionally see other forms (like adjectives).

examples.ja: 値段から言うと、この商品が最もお得です。
examples.romaji: Nedan kara iuto, kono shouhin ga mottomo otoku desu.
examples.meaningEn: Speaking from the viewpoint of price, this product is the best deal.

examples.ja: 味から言って、このレストランはおいしいと思います。
examples.romaji: Aji kara itte, kono resutoran wa oishii to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: In terms of taste, I think this restaurant is delicious.

examples.ja: 便利さから言って、このアパートは最適な場所にあります。
examples.romaji: Benrisa kara itte, kono apaato wa saiteki na basho ni arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Speaking from the perspective of convenience, this apartment is in the best location.

examples.ja: 健康から言うと、毎日運動することが大切です。
examples.romaji: Kenkou kara iuto, mainichi undou suru koto ga taisetsu desu.
examples.meaningEn: From the viewpoint of health, it is important to exercise every day.

title: ～くせに (〜kuse ni)
formation: Verb (casual) + くせに / い-Adjective + くせに / な-Adjective + な(or の) + くせに / Noun + の + くせに
shortExplanationEn: Used to criticize someone for doing something contrary to what one would expect from their nature or situation.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point ～くせに is used to express criticism or disapproval toward someone who does something contradictory to their expected nature, role, or prior statement. It's used in fairly casual or spoken contexts and can sound quite critical or accusing. Note that there's a colloquial variant ～くせして, but ～くせに is the standard form taught in most references.

examples.ja: 彼はいつも遅刻してくるくせに、自分が待たされるのが嫌いだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa itsumo chikoku shite kuru kuse ni, jibun ga matasareru no ga kirai da.
examples.meaningEn: He’s always late, yet he hates being kept waiting.

examples.ja: 悪いなと言ってるくせに、また同じことを繰り返している。
examples.romaji: Warui na to itteru kuse ni, mata onaji koto o kurikaeshite iru.
examples.meaningEn: He says he's sorry, yet he keeps doing the same thing.

examples.ja: 彼は背が高いくせに、バスケが下手だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa se ga takai kuse ni, basuke ga heta da.
examples.meaningEn: Despite being tall, he's bad at basketball.

examples.ja: この店は有名なのくせに、サービスが悪い。
examples.romaji: Kono mise wa yuumei na no kuse ni, saabisu ga warui.
examples.meaningEn: Despite being famous, this store has poor service.

title: ～ことから (〜koto kara)
formation: Verb-casual + ことから / い-Adjective + ことから / な-Adjective + なことから / Noun + のことから
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the reason or basis for something; 'because', 'since', 'due to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ことから grammar point is used to indicate the reason or basis for a conclusion, fact, or premise. It can be translated as 'because', 'since', or 'due to' in English. The same pattern is used whether it’s with verbs, adjectives, or nouns (with the appropriate こと or の).

examples.ja: 彼は毎日運動することから、体が丈夫です。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mainichi undou suru koto kara, karada ga joubu desu.
examples.meaningEn: Because he exercises every day, he is physically strong.

examples.ja: この店のサービスがいいことから、いつも混んでいます。
examples.romaji: Kono mise no saabisu ga ii koto kara, itsumo konde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: This store is always crowded because of its good service.

examples.ja: 彼女は親切なことから、みんなに好かれています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa shinsetsu na koto kara, minna ni sukarete imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She’s well-liked by everyone due to her kindness.

examples.ja: その会社の評判が良いことから、就職先として人気があります。
examples.romaji: Sono kaisha no hyouban ga yoi koto kara, shuushoku-saki toshite ninki ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Because that company’s reputation is good, it’s popular as a place to work.

title: ～ことに (〜koto ni)
formation: Verb-casual + ことに / い-Adjective + ことに / な-Adjective + なことに / Noun + のことに
shortExplanationEn: Used to express feelings or emotions about a situation, action, or outcome.
longExplanationEn: The ～ことに grammar point is used to highlight the speaker’s feelings or emotional reaction toward a situation, action, or outcome. It often appears in constructions like 驚いたことに, 残念なことに, 不思議なことに, etc.

examples.ja: 彼が急に帰国することに驚いた。
examples.romaji: Kare ga kyuu ni kikoku suru koto ni odoroita.
examples.meaningEn: I was surprised that he suddenly returned to his home country.

examples.ja: 彼女が優勝したことに感動した。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga yuushou shita koto ni kandou shita.
examples.meaningEn: I was moved by the fact that she won the championship.

examples.ja: 彼がいつも遅刻することに腹が立つ。
examples.romaji: Kare ga itsumo chikoku suru koto ni hara ga tatsu.
examples.meaningEn: I get angry that he is always late.

examples.ja: 彼女がこんなに美しいことに気がつかなかった。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga konna ni utsukushii koto ni ki ga tsukanakatta.
examples.meaningEn: I hadn’t realized how beautiful she was.

title: ～ことになっている (〜koto ni natte iru)
formation: Verb-plain form + ことになっている / い-Adjective + ことになっている / な-Adjective + だ + ことになっている / Noun + だ + ことになっている
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is supposed to be or has been decided (rule, plan, custom).
longExplanationEn: The ～ことになっている grammar point is used when something has been decided, planned, or there is a rule/custom that everyone follows. It can be translated as 'it is supposed to be,' 'it is decided,' or 'it is customary that.'

examples.ja: 日本では、左側を運転することになっています。
examples.romaji: Nihon de wa, hidarigawa o unten suru koto ni natte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: In Japan, it is decided that people drive on the left side.

examples.ja: 会社の規則では、制服を着ることになっている。
examples.romaji: Kaisha no kisoku de wa, seifuku o kiru koto ni natte iru.
examples.meaningEn: According to the company rules, we are supposed to wear uniforms.

examples.ja: 彼女は明日結婚することになっています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ashita kekkon suru koto ni natte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She is supposed to get married tomorrow.

examples.ja: この商品は高品質だということになっています。
examples.romaji: Kono shouhin wa kouhinshitsu da to iu koto ni natte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: It is said (or generally accepted) that this product is of high quality.

title: ～さえ～ば (〜sae ~ba)
formation: Noun + さえ + Verb-conditional / Verb-ますstem + さえすれば / etc. (varies by part of speech)
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that, as long as a certain condition is met, something else will follow; 'if only', 'provided that'.
longExplanationEn: The ～さえ～ば grammar point is used to indicate that if a certain single condition is fulfilled, everything else will be fine or a certain result will occur. It often translates as 'as long as...' or 'if only...'.

examples.ja: お金さえあれば、幸せです。
examples.romaji: Okane sae areba, shiawase desu.
examples.meaningEn: As long as I have money, I’m happy.

examples.ja: 試験に合格しさえすれば、卒業できます。
examples.romaji: Shiken ni goukaku shi sae sureba, sotsugyou dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: If only I pass the exam, I can graduate.

examples.ja: 野菜さえたくさん食べれば、母は喜びます。
examples.romaji: Yasai sae takusan tabereba, haha wa yorokobimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As long as I eat plenty of vegetables, my mother will be happy.

examples.ja: 交通費さえ支払ってもらえれば、どこへでも行きます。
examples.romaji: Koutsuuhi sae shiharatte moraereba, doko e demo ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As long as my transportation costs are covered, I’ll go anywhere.

title: ～ざるを得ない (〜zaru wo enai)
formation: Verb-ない form (drop ない) + ざるを得ない
shortExplanationEn: Have no choice but to; cannot help but.
longExplanationEn: The ～ざるを得ない grammar point is used to indicate that one has no choice but to do something or cannot avoid doing it. It is formed from the negative form (ない) but replaces ない with ざる, followed by を得ない. (Note: する → せざるを得ない.)

examples.ja: この仕事は締め切りが近いので、残業せざるを得ない。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto wa shimekiri ga chikai no de, zangyou sezaru wo enai.
examples.meaningEn: Since the deadline is near, I have no choice but to work overtime.

examples.ja: 電車が遅れたため、タクシーに乗るざるを得なかった。
examples.romaji: Densha ga okureta tame, takushii ni noru zaru wo enakatta.
examples.meaningEn: Because the train was delayed, I had no choice but to take a taxi.

examples.ja: 彼女の説明が納得できなかったが、とりあえず信じざるを得ない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no setsumei ga nattoku dekinakatta ga, toriaezu shinjizaru wo enai.
examples.meaningEn: I couldn’t really accept her explanation, but for now I have no choice but to believe her.

examples.ja: この状況では、新しい計画を立て直さざるを得ない。
examples.romaji: Kono joukyou de wa, atarashii keikaku wo tatenaosazaru wo enai.
examples.meaningEn: In this situation, we have no choice but to revise the new plan.

title: ～ずにはいられない (〜zu ni wa irarenai)
formation: Verb-ない form (drop ない) + ずにはいられない
shortExplanationEn: Cannot help but; cannot resist doing; an irresistible urge.
longExplanationEn: The ～ずにはいられない grammar point expresses that one cannot resist or help doing something. In other words, there is a strong urge that one cannot suppress. It's formed from the negative stem of a verb plus ずにはいられない. (Note: する → せずにはいられない.)

examples.ja: この曲を聞くと、歌わずにはいられない。
examples.romaji: Kono kyoku o kiku to, utawazu ni wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: When I hear this song, I can’t help but sing.

examples.ja: 彼女が泣いているのを見ると、慰めずにはいられない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga naite iru no o miru to, nagusamezu ni wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: When I see her crying, I can’t resist comforting her.

examples.ja: この小説は面白いので、読まずにはいられない。
examples.romaji: Kono shousetsu wa omoshiroi no de, yomazu ni wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: This novel is so interesting that I can’t help but read it.

examples.ja: 彼はおいしい料理を前にすると、食べずにはいられない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa oishii ryouri o mae ni suru to, tabezu ni wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: When he’s faced with delicious food, he can’t help but eat it.

title: ～そうにない (〜sou ni nai)
formation: Verb-stem + そうにない / い-Adjective (drop い) + そうにない / な-Adjective + そうにない
shortExplanationEn: Express the unlikelihood of something happening; 'not likely', 'doesn't seem'.
longExplanationEn: The ～そうにない grammar point is used to indicate that something does not appear likely to happen. Often translated as 'doesn't seem like' or 'not likely to...'. Attach そうにない to the verb stem or adjective stem.

examples.ja: この雨は止みそうにない。
examples.romaji: Kono ame wa yami sou ni nai.
examples.meaningEn: It doesn't seem like this rain will stop.

examples.ja: 彼が試験に合格しそうにない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga shiken ni goukaku shi sou ni nai.
examples.meaningEn: It doesn't look like he'll pass the exam.

examples.ja: この料理がおいしそうにない。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri ga oishisou ni nai.
examples.meaningEn: This dish doesn't look like it will be tasty.

examples.ja: この部屋はきれいそうにない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa kirei sou ni nai.
examples.meaningEn: This room doesn't seem likely to be clean.

title: ～たかと思ったら (〜ta ka to omottara)
formation: Verb-た form + かと思ったら
shortExplanationEn: Express a sequence of events happening in quick succession; 'as soon as', 'no sooner than'.
longExplanationEn: The ～たかと思ったら grammar point indicates that one action occurs almost immediately after another, often conveying surprise at how quickly it happens. It is frequently translated as 'as soon as...' or 'no sooner than...'.

examples.ja: 電話をかけたかと思ったら、すぐに彼女が出た。
examples.romaji: Denwa o kaketa ka to omottara, sugu ni kanojo ga deta.
examples.meaningEn: No sooner had I called than she picked up.

examples.ja: 離陸したかと思ったら、飛行機が揺れ始めた。
examples.romaji: Ririku shita ka to omottara, hikouki ga yure hajimeta.
examples.meaningEn: As soon as we took off, the plane started shaking.

examples.ja: 買い物に行ったかと思ったら、雨が降ってきた。
examples.romaji: Kaimono ni itta ka to omottara, ame ga futte kita.
examples.meaningEn: No sooner had I gone shopping than it began to rain.

examples.ja: 仕事を終わったかと思ったら、上司が追加の仕事を持ってきた。
examples.romaji: Shigoto o owatta ka to omottara, joushi ga tsuika no shigoto o motte kita.
examples.meaningEn: As soon as I finished work, my boss brought more tasks.

title: ～たきり (〜takiri)
formation: Verb-past + きり
shortExplanationEn: Express 'since then' or 'after'; used to indicate that an action occurred once and has not been repeated or that a state has continued since a certain event or time.
longExplanationEn: The ～たきり grammar point is used to express that an action occurred once and has not been repeated, or that a situation or state has continued since a certain event or time. It can be translated as 'since then', 'after', or 'one time only' in English.

examples.ja: 彼は会社を辞めたきり、連絡がない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kaisha o yameta kiri, renraku ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: Ever since he quit the company, there's been no contact.

examples.ja: 昨日の朝、メールを送ったきり、返事がない。
examples.romaji: Kinō no asa, mēru o okutta kiri, henji ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: I sent an email yesterday morning, and there's been no reply since then.

examples.ja: 子供のころ海に行ったきりで、もう20年になる。
examples.romaji: Kodomo no koro umi ni itta kiri de, mō nijūnen ni naru.
examples.meaningEn: It's been 20 years since I last went to the sea as a child.

examples.ja: 彼女と別れたきり、彼女のことを考えなくなった。
examples.romaji: Kanojo to wakareta kiri, kanojo no koto o kangaenaku natta.
examples.meaningEn: Ever since I broke up with her, I stopped thinking about her.

title: ～だけあって (〜dake atte)
formation: Verb-casual + だけあって, い-Adjective + だけあって, な-Adjective + なだけあって, Noun + だけあって
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is as expected or deserves its reputation.
longExplanationEn: The ～だけあって grammar point is used to indicate that something is as expected or deserves its reputation, based on certain facts or qualities. It can be translated as 'no wonder', 'as expected', or 'it is natural that' in English.

examples.ja: 彼は日本語が上手だけあって、日本人のように話すことができる。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nihongo ga jōzu dake atte, nihonjin no yō ni hanasu koto ga dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: As expected from his good Japanese skills, he can speak like a native.

examples.ja: このレストランは有名だけあって、料理がとても美味しい。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa yūmei dake atte, ryōri ga totemo oishii.
examples.meaningEn: No wonder this restaurant is famous; the food is very delicious.

examples.ja: 彼女は経験豊富なだけあって、その問題を簡単に解決できた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa keiken hōfu na dake atte, sono mondai o kantan ni kaiketsu dekita.
examples.meaningEn: It's natural that she's experienced; she solved the problem easily.

examples.ja: 彼は優秀な学生だけあって、いつもテストの点数が高い。
examples.romaji: Kare wa yūshū na gakusei dake atte, itsumo tesuto no tensū ga takai.
examples.meaningEn: As expected of an excellent student, he always scores high on tests.

title: ～だけましだ (〜dake mashi da)
formation: Verb-casual + だけましだ, い-Adjective + だけましだ, な-Adjective + だけましだ, Noun + だけましだ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that although the situation isn’t ideal, it could be worse; 'it’s not great, but at least...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～だけましだ grammar point is used to compare two undesirable or less-than-ideal situations, saying that one is not good, but still better than the worse alternative. It often translates as 'at least (it’s only...)' or 'it could be worse.'

examples.ja: 失敗するだけましだ。もっと大変なことにならなくてよかった。
examples.romaji: Shippai suru dake mashi da. Motto taihen na koto ni naranakute yokatta.
examples.meaningEn: At least we only failed—it could’ve been much worse.

examples.ja: 遅れるだけましだ。来られないよりはいい。
examples.romaji: Okureru dake mashi da. Korarenai yori wa ii.
examples.meaningEn: At least I’m only late. It’s better than not being able to come at all.

examples.ja: 小さな部屋だけましだ。全く部屋がないよりはいい。
examples.romaji: Chīsana heya dake mashi da. Mattaku heya ga nai yori wa ii.
examples.meaningEn: At least we have a small room; it’s better than having no room at all.

examples.ja: 彼が結婚しないだけましだ。大変なことになるところだったよ。
examples.romaji: Kare ga kekkon shinai dake mashi da. Taihen na koto ni naru tokoro datta yo.
examples.meaningEn: At least he’s not getting married—things could have gotten complicated.

title: ～たところ (〜ta tokoro)
formation: Verb-past + ところ
shortExplanationEn: Used to describe an action that has just been completed, often followed by a result or observation.
longExplanationEn: The ～たところ grammar point is used when an action has just been completed and the speaker wants to describe an observation or result that follows. It can be translated as 'just finished doing something' or 'as soon as (I did something)...'.

examples.ja: 掃除したところ、部屋がずっとキレイになりました。
examples.romaji: Sōji shita tokoro, heya ga zutto kirei ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I just finished cleaning, and the room is so much cleaner now.

examples.ja: 彼に聞いたところ、その質問の答えは分からないみたいです。
examples.romaji: Kare ni kiita tokoro, sono shitsumon no kotae wa wakaranai mitai desu.
examples.meaningEn: I just asked him, and it seems he doesn't know the answer.

examples.ja: 宿題を出したところ、先生が間違いを見つけました。
examples.romaji: Shukudai o dashita tokoro, sensei ga machigai o mitsukemashita.
examples.meaningEn: As soon as I turned in my homework, the teacher found a mistake.

examples.ja: 昼ごはんを食べたところ、気分が良くなりました。
examples.romaji: Hirugohan o tabeta tokoro, kibun ga yoku narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Right after eating lunch, I started feeling better.

title: ～たとたん (〜ta totan)
formation: Verb-past + たとたん
shortExplanationEn: Express the moment something happens; 'as soon as', 'just when'.
longExplanationEn: The ～たとたん grammar point is used to express that an action or event occurred at the same moment another action or event happened. It can be translated as 'as soon as' or 'just when.' It requires the past tense of a verb.

examples.ja: ドアを開けたとたん、雨が降り始めた。
examples.romaji: Doa o aketa totan, ame ga furi-hajimeta.
examples.meaningEn: As soon as I opened the door, it started raining.

examples.ja: 電話をかけたとたん、バッテリーが切れた。
examples.romaji: Denwa o kaketa totan, batterī ga kireta.
examples.meaningEn: Just when I made the phone call, the battery died.

examples.ja: 彼女に会ったとたん、思い出の歌が流れた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni atta totan, omoide no uta ga nagareta.
examples.meaningEn: No sooner had I met her than a nostalgic song started playing.

examples.ja: テストが終わったとたん、リラックスできた。
examples.romaji: Tesuto ga owatta totan, rirakkusu dekita.
examples.meaningEn: The moment the test ended, I was able to relax.

title: ～だらけ (〜darake)
formation: Noun + だらけ
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that something is full of or covered with a certain (usually negative) characteristic.
longExplanationEn: The ～だらけ grammar point is used to express that something is full of, covered with, or filled with a certain characteristic. It frequently has a negative or undesirable nuance.

examples.ja: 彼の部屋はゴミだらけだ。
examples.romaji: Kare no heya wa gomi darake da.
examples.meaningEn: His room is full of trash.

examples.ja: このビーチは岩だらけで泳ぎにくい。
examples.romaji: Kono bīchi wa iwa darake de oyogi nikui.
examples.meaningEn: This beach is full of rocks and is difficult to swim at.

examples.ja: 彼の顔はしわだらけだ。
examples.romaji: Kare no kao wa shiwa darake da.
examples.meaningEn: His face is covered with wrinkles.

examples.ja: このリンゴは虫食いだらけだ。
examples.romaji: Kono ringo wa mushikui darake da.
examples.meaningEn: This apple is riddled with wormholes.

title: ～っこない (〜kkonai)
formation: Verb-negative stem + っこない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the impossibility or very low probability of something happening; 'no way', 'impossible'.
longExplanationEn: The ～っこない grammar point is used to express that something is impossible or extremely unlikely. It’s formed by attaching ～っこない to the negative stem of a verb. It can be translated as 'no way', 'no chance', or 'impossible.'

examples.ja: こんなに雪が降っているんだから、学校に間に合いっこない。
examples.romaji: Konna ni yuki ga futteirun dakara, gakkō ni maniai kkonai.
examples.meaningEn: With snow falling this heavily, there's no way we'll make it to school on time.

examples.ja: 彼は泳げないので、プールの向こう側に泳いで行きっこない。
examples.romaji: Kare wa oyogenai node, pūru no mukōgawa ni oyoide ikkonai.
examples.meaningEn: Since he can’t swim, there’s no way he’ll make it to the other side of the pool.

examples.ja: 彼女はとても怖がりなので、一人で夜の公園に行きっこない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa totemo kowagari na node, hitori de yoru no kōen ni ikkonai.
examples.meaningEn: She’s so timid, there’s no chance she’d go to the park alone at night.

examples.ja: この短い時間で、その本を全部読むことはできっこない。
examples.romaji: Kono mijikai jikan de, sono hon o zenbu yomu koto wa dekikkonai.
examples.meaningEn: It's impossible to read the entire book in such a short time.

title: ～つつ (〜tsutsu)
formation: Verb-stem + つつ
shortExplanationEn: Shows two actions happening simultaneously or a contrast; 'while', 'although'.
longExplanationEn: The ～つつ grammar point is used to express two actions occurring simultaneously, or to show a contrast between an ongoing action and another factor. It can be translated as 'while' or 'although.' It is attached to a verb in its stem form.

examples.ja: 彼はテレビを見つつ、宿題をしていました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa terebi o mitsutsu, shukudai o shite imashita.
examples.meaningEn: He was doing his homework while watching TV.

examples.ja: 最近忙しいと思いつつ、毎日運動を続けています。
examples.romaji: Saikin isogashii to omoi tsutsu, mainichi undō o tsuzukete imasu.
examples.meaningEn: Although I feel busy these days, I still exercise every day.

examples.ja: 彼女は泣きつつ、笑顔で別れを告げた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa naki tsutsu, egao de wakare o tsugeta.
examples.meaningEn: She was crying, yet she said goodbye with a smile on her face.

examples.ja: 雨に濡れつつ、彼は歩き続けました。
examples.romaji: Ame ni nure tsutsu, kare wa aruki tsuzukemashita.
examples.meaningEn: He continued walking even though he was getting soaked by the rain.

title: ～つつある (〜tsutsu aru)
formation: Verb-stem + つつある
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an ongoing process or gradual change.
longExplanationEn: The ～つつある grammar point is used to describe something that is in the middle of a gradual change or development. It can be translated as '(something) is in the process of ~ing.' The most common usage is with the verb stem plus つつある.

examples.ja: 地球温暖化は進行しつつある。
examples.romaji: Chikyū ondanka wa shinkō shitsutsu aru.
examples.meaningEn: Global warming is in progress.

examples.ja: この町は急速に発展しつつある。
examples.romaji: Kono machi wa kyūsoku ni hatten shitsutsu aru.
examples.meaningEn: This town is rapidly developing.

examples.ja: 子供たちは成長しつつある。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi wa seichō shitsutsu aru.
examples.meaningEn: The children are growing up.

examples.ja: 景気が回復しつつある。
examples.romaji: Keiki ga kaifuku shitsutsu aru.
examples.meaningEn: The economy is recovering.

title: ～っぱなし (〜ppanashi)
formation: Verb-ます stem + っぱなし
shortExplanationEn: Express a continuous action or state; 'left as is', 'non-stop'.
longExplanationEn: The ～っぱなし grammar point is used to express that something is left in a certain condition for an extended time or that an action continues without stopping. It attaches to the ます-stem of verbs.

examples.ja: 窓を開けっぱなしにして、部屋が寒くなってしまった。
examples.romaji: Mado o akeppanashi ni shite, heya ga samuku natte shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: I left the window open, and the room got cold.

examples.ja: 彼は音楽を聴くのが好きで、いつもイヤホンをつけっぱなしです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa ongaku o kiku no ga suki de, itsumo iyahon o tsukeppanashi desu.
examples.meaningEn: He loves listening to music and always has his earphones on.

examples.ja: 子供はおもちゃを床に置きっぱなしで、片付けなかった。
examples.romaji: Kodomo wa omocha o yuka ni okippanashi de, katazukenakatta.
examples.meaningEn: The child left the toys on the floor and didn’t put them away.

examples.ja: 昨夜は忙しかったので、テレビをつけっぱなしにして寝てしまった。
examples.romaji: Sakuya wa isogashikatta node, terebi o tsukeppanashi ni shite nete shimatta.
examples.meaningEn: I was busy last night, so I left the TV on and fell asleep.

title: ～っぽい (〜ppoi)
formation: Noun + っぽい, Verb-casual + っぽい, い-Adjective + っぽい, な-Adjective + っぽい
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that something has the appearance, characteristics, or behavior of something else.
longExplanationEn: The ～っぽい grammar point is used when something or someone appears to have characteristics, traits, or a vibe that resemble something else. It can mean 'looks like,' 'seems like,' or 'has the quality of.'

examples.ja: 彼女は大人っぽい服を着ている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa otonappoi fuku o kite iru.
examples.meaningEn: She’s wearing clothes that look mature.

examples.ja: この説明は分かりにくっぽい。
examples.romaji: Kono setsumei wa wakari nikuppoi.
examples.meaningEn: This explanation seems hard to understand.

examples.ja: 彼は疲れたっぽい顔をしている。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tsukareta ppoi kao o shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: He has a face that looks tired.

examples.ja: この部屋は寒っぽい感じがする。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa samuppoi kanji ga suru.
examples.meaningEn: This room feels kind of chilly.

title: ～ていられない (〜te irarenai)
formation: Verb-て form + いられない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that one cannot afford to do something; 'can't keep on', 'unable to continue'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ていられない grammar point is used to express a feeling of not being able to continue doing something or remaining in a certain state. It can be translated as 'can't keep on', 'unable to continue', or 'can't afford to' in English. The formation is the same for all verb types.

examples.ja: こんなに忙しいのに、無理に遊んでいられない。
examples.romaji: Konna ni isogashii noni, muri ni asonde irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I'm so busy that I can't afford to just play around.

examples.ja: 寒すぎて、外で待っていられない。
examples.romaji: Samusugite, soto de matte irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: It's too cold; I can't keep waiting outside.

examples.ja: この問題は重要だから、放っておいていられない。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa jūyō dakara, houtte oite irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: This issue is too important to ignore.

examples.ja: 彼女の態度が我慢できず、黙っていられない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no taido ga gaman dekizu, damatte irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I can't stand her attitude, so I can't stay silent.

title: ～てかなわない (〜te kanawanai)
formation: Adjective (〜く / 〜で) + て + かなわない / Verb phrase (reworded to an adjective/negative) + て + かなわない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something cannot be endured, tolerated, or is unbearable.
longExplanationEn: The ～てかなわない grammar point is typically used with adjectives (or a rephrased verb) in 〜て form to show an unbearable situation or feeling. It means you 'can’t stand' or 'can’t endure' something. It often appears with expressions like 「暑くてかなわない」「うるさくてかなわない」 and so on.

examples.ja: こんなに暑くてかなわない。
examples.romaji: Konnani atsukute kanawanai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t stand this heat.

examples.ja: 彼の話は失礼すぎてかなわない。
examples.romaji: Kare no hanashi wa shitsurei sugite kanawanai.
examples.meaningEn: His remarks are so rude I can’t put up with them.

examples.ja: 映画があまりに感動的で、忘れられなくてかなわない。
examples.romaji: Eiga ga amari ni kandō-teki de, wasurerarenakute kanawanai.
examples.meaningEn: The movie was so moving that I can’t stand how it stays on my mind.

examples.ja: 彼女の笑顔がまぶしくてかなわない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no egao ga mabushikute kanawanai.
examples.meaningEn: Her smile is so dazzling that it’s overwhelming.

title: ～てからでないと (〜te kara denai to)
formation: Verb-て + からでないと
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that something cannot be done before a certain action is completed; 'not until', 'only after'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てからでないと grammar point is used to express that an action cannot be done before another action has been completed. It can be translated as 'not until' or 'only after' in English. It attaches to a verb in the て-form, plus からでないと.

examples.ja: 宿題をしてからでないと、遊びに行けません。
examples.romaji: Shukudai o shite kara denai to, asobi ni ikemasen.
examples.meaningEn: You cannot go play until you’ve finished your homework.

examples.ja: 食事をしてからでないと、デザートを食べられません。
examples.romaji: Shokuji o shite kara denai to, dezāto o taberaremasen.
examples.meaningEn: You cannot eat dessert until after having a meal.

examples.ja: チケットを買ってからでないと、映画を見られません。
examples.romaji: Chiketto o katte kara denai to, eiga o miraremasen.
examples.meaningEn: You can’t watch the movie until after you buy a ticket.

examples.ja: 試験が終わってからでないと、休みを取れません。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga owatte kara denai to, yasumi o toremasen.
examples.meaningEn: You can’t take time off until the exam is over.

title: ～てこそ (〜te koso)
formation: Verb-て form + こそ / い-Adjective + くてこそ / な-Adjective + でこそ / Noun + でこそ
shortExplanationEn: Emphasizes the condition; 'only when', 'precisely because'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てこそ grammar point is used to emphasize that 'only when' or 'precisely because' the stated condition is met, a certain result or meaning arises. For nouns and adjectives, similar forms appear (e.g., Noun + でこそ, い-Adjective くてこそ, な-Adjective でこそ).

examples.ja: 勉強してこそ、テストに合格できる。
examples.romaji: Benkyō shite koso, tesuto ni gōkaku dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: Only when you study can you pass the test.

examples.ja: 素直になってこそ、相手の気持ちが分かります。
examples.romaji: Sunao ni natte koso, aite no kimochi ga wakarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Only when you’re honest can you understand the other person’s feelings.

examples.ja: 静かでこそ、心が落ち着く。
examples.romaji: Shizuka de koso, kokoro ga ochitsuku.
examples.meaningEn: Only in silence can one’s mind be at peace.

examples.ja: 友達でこそ、お互いに助け合うのは当然だ。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi de koso, otagai ni tasukeau no wa tōzen da.
examples.meaningEn: Precisely because we’re friends, it’s natural to help each other.

title: ～でしょうがない (〜deshou ga nai)
formation: い-Adjective(く) + てしょうがない / な-Adjective + で(は)しょうがない / Verb-て form + しょうがない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an intense emotional or physical state; 'extremely', 'can’t help but'.
longExplanationEn: The pattern ～(て)しょうがない (or 〜でしょうがない) indicates a condition or feeling so intense that one is almost 'overcome' by it. It’s often used with emotions (e.g. 退屈で(しょうがない), 嬉しくてしょうがない, 寒くてしょうがない).

examples.ja: 今日は暇で(は)しょうがない。
examples.romaji: Kyō wa hima de shō ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: I’m extremely bored today.

examples.ja: 試験が近いから不安でしょうがない。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga chikai kara fuan deshō ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: I’m so anxious because the exam is coming up.

examples.ja: 頭が痛くてしょうがないから、早退したい。
examples.romaji: Atama ga itakute shō ga nai kara, sōtai shitai.
examples.meaningEn: My head hurts so much I can’t stand it—I want to leave early.

examples.ja: 新しいゲームが気になってしょうがない。
examples.romaji: Atarashii gēmu ga ki ni natte shō ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t help but keep thinking about the new game.

title: ～でたまらない (〜de tamaranai)
formation: い-Adjective (く) + てたまらない / な-Adjective + でたまらない / Verb-て form + たまらない
shortExplanationEn: Express extremely strong feelings or sensations; 'can't stand', 'unbearably'.
longExplanationEn: The ～でたまらない grammar point is used to convey very strong emotion or sensation—so strong it’s almost unbearable. It’s common with expressions like 暑くてたまらない, うれしくてたまらない, etc.

examples.ja: この暑さは本当に暑くてたまらない。
examples.romaji: Kono atsusa wa hontō ni atsukute tamaranai.
examples.meaningEn: This heat is absolutely unbearable.

examples.ja: 彼女のことが好きでたまらない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no koto ga suki de tamaranai.
examples.meaningEn: I like her so much I can hardly stand it.

examples.ja: この部屋は寒くてたまらない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa samukute tamaranai.
examples.meaningEn: This room is unbearably cold.

examples.ja: 彼の態度を見ていると、腹が立ってたまらない。
examples.romaji: Kare no taido o mite iru to, hara ga tatte tamaranai.
examples.meaningEn: Seeing his attitude makes me extremely angry.

title: ～でならない (〜de naranai)
formation: な-Adjective + でならない / い-Adjective (く) + てならない / Verb-て + ならない
shortExplanationEn: Indicates an uncontrollable emotion or feeling; 'can’t help but', 'overwhelmingly'.
longExplanationEn: The ～でならない grammar pattern expresses a very strong, often involuntary feeling—e.g., '不思議でならない' (it’s so mysterious I can’t help thinking about it), '残念でならない' (I’m so disappointed), '心配でならない' (I’m extremely worried).

examples.ja: 彼の演技が素晴らしくてならない。
examples.romaji: Kare no engi ga subarashikute naranai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t help but be amazed at his wonderful performance.

examples.ja: 合格発表が気になってならない。
examples.romaji: Gōkaku happyō ga ki ni natte naranai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t stop worrying about the exam results.

examples.ja: 友達が引っ越してしまって、寂しくてならない。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga hikkoshite shimatte, sabishikute naranai.
examples.meaningEn: My friend moved away, and I feel unbearably lonely.

examples.ja: 初めての海外旅行で、不安でならない。
examples.romaji: Hajimete no kaigai ryokō de, fuan de naranai.
examples.meaningEn: It’s my first trip abroad, and I’m extremely anxious.

title: ～でばかりはいられない (〜de bakari wa irarenai)
formation: Verb-て form + ばかりはいられない / Noun + ばかりはいられない (with appropriate particles)
shortExplanationEn: Cannot afford to only do something; cannot always rely on it.
longExplanationEn: The ～でばかりはいられない grammar point is used when one realizes they cannot keep doing only that one thing or cannot always rely on a certain situation. It implies the need to take other actions or responsibilities.

examples.ja: いつも助けてもらってばかりはいられない。
examples.romaji: Itsumo tasukete moratte bakari wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t just keep letting people help me all the time.

examples.ja: 好きな仕事ばかりしてはいられない。
examples.romaji: Suki na shigoto bakari shite wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t afford to only do the work I love.

examples.ja: 親に頼ってばかりはいられない。
examples.romaji: Oya ni tayotte bakari wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t keep depending on my parents forever.

examples.ja: 休んでばかりはいられない。仕事もしなくちゃ。
examples.romaji: Yasunde bakari wa irarenai. Shigoto mo shinakucha.
examples.meaningEn: I can’t just keep taking breaks; I have work to do as well.

title: ～ではないか (〜de wa nai ka)
formation: Noun + ではないか / い-Adjective + ではないか / な-Adjective + ではないか / Verb-casual + ではないか
shortExplanationEn: Used to show a strong assertion or emphasis on one’s opinion; 'isn't it?', 'aren't you?', 'right?'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ではないか grammar point is used at the end of a sentence to make a rhetorical question or a strong, emphatic statement. It often translates as 'isn't it…?' 'aren't you…?' or 'wouldn’t you say…?'.

examples.ja: 彼は優秀な学生ではないか。
examples.romaji: Kare wa yūshū na gakusei de wa nai ka.
examples.meaningEn: Isn’t he an excellent student?

examples.ja: この料理は美味しいではないか。
examples.romaji: Kono ryōri wa oishii de wa nai ka.
examples.meaningEn: Isn’t this dish delicious?

examples.ja: あなたは彼女の友達ではないか。
examples.romaji: Anata wa kanojo no tomodachi de wa nai ka.
examples.meaningEn: Aren’t you her friend?

examples.ja: 明日は休みではないか。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa yasumi de wa nai ka.
examples.meaningEn: Tomorrow’s a day off, right?

title: ～てはならない (〜te wa naranai)
formation: Verb-て form + はならない
shortExplanationEn: Express prohibition; 'must not', 'cannot', 'should not'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てはならない grammar point is used to express a strong prohibition. It is often more formal or strict and can be translated as 'must not', 'cannot', or 'should not.'

examples.ja: ここでタバコを吸ってはならない。
examples.romaji: Koko de tabako o sutte wa naranai.
examples.meaningEn: You must not smoke here.

examples.ja: 秘密を他人に話してはならない。
examples.romaji: Himitsu o tanin ni hanashite wa naranai.
examples.meaningEn: You cannot reveal this secret to others.

examples.ja: この部屋に入ってはならない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya ni haitte wa naranai.
examples.meaningEn: You must not enter this room.

examples.ja: 飲酒運転をしてはならない。
examples.romaji: Inshu unten o shite wa naranai.
examples.meaningEn: You must not drink and drive.

title: ～てまで (〜te made)
formation: Verb-て form + まで
shortExplanationEn: Express the extent someone goes in order to achieve something; 'to the point of', 'even', 'so far as to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～てまで grammar point is used to convey the extent someone goes to achieve something, highlighting the action one takes or the sacrifice one makes for a certain purpose. It can be translated as 'to the point of', 'even', or 'so far as to' in English. It is used with verbs, not adjectives or nouns.

examples.ja: 彼は試験に合格するために、徹夜してまで勉強しました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shiken ni gōkaku suru tame ni, tetsuya shite made benkyō shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: He studied to the point of staying up all night in order to pass the exam.

examples.ja: この商品がほしかったので、朝早く起きてまで並んで買いました。
examples.romaji: Kono shōhin ga hoshikatta node, asa hayaku okite made narande kaimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I wanted this product so much that I went so far as to get up early and wait in line to buy it.

examples.ja: 彼女は彼を助けるために、お金を借りてまで手術代を払いました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kare o tasukeru tame ni, okane o karite made shujutsudai o haraimashita.
examples.meaningEn: In order to help him, she even borrowed money to pay for the surgery.

examples.ja: 部長はそのプロジェクトを成功させるために、休日も働いてまで取り組んでいます。
examples.romaji: Buchō wa sono purojekuto o seikō saseru tame ni, kyūjitsu mo hataraite made torikunde imasu.
examples.meaningEn: The manager is even working on holidays to make the project a success.

title: ～て当然だ (〜te tōzen da)
formation: Verb-て form + 当然だ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is natural or expected.
longExplanationEn: The ～て当然だ grammar point is used to express that something is natural, obvious, or expected under the circumstances. It can be translated as 'it's natural that', 'as expected', or 'no wonder' in English.

examples.ja: 彼は毎日勉強しているから、試験に合格して当然だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mainichi benkyō shite iru kara, shiken ni gōkaku shite tōzen da.
examples.meaningEn: Since he studies every day, it's only natural that he passes the exam.

examples.ja: そのレストランは料理が美味しいので、予約がいっぱいになって当然だ。
examples.romaji: Sono resutoran wa ryōri ga oishii node, yoyaku ga ippai ni natte tōzen da.
examples.meaningEn: Because that restaurant’s food is delicious, no wonder its reservations are always full.

examples.ja: 彼女は毎晩練習しているので、上手になって当然だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa maiban renshū shite iru node, jōzu ni natte tōzen da.
examples.meaningEn: She practices every night, so it's natural that she becomes skilled.

examples.ja: 彼は仕事で成功していて、お金持ちになって当然だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shigoto de seikō shite ite, okanemochi ni natte tōzen da.
examples.meaningEn: He has succeeded at his job, so it's only natural that he's become wealthy.

title: ～というものだ (〜to iu mono da)
formation: Sentence + というものだ
shortExplanationEn: Expressing a general rule or common sense.
longExplanationEn: The ～というものだ grammar point is used to emphasize a general rule, a piece of common sense, or an indisputable fact. It often appears in comments or observations about something commonly accepted as true.

examples.ja: 若者の熱情は尊敬に値するというものだ。
examples.romaji: Wakamono no netsujō wa sonkei ni ataisuru to iu mono da.
examples.meaningEn: The passion of young people is something that deserves respect.

examples.ja: 勉強は一日でもサボれば遅れるというものだ。
examples.romaji: Benkyō wa ichinichi demo saboreba okureru to iu mono da.
examples.meaningEn: If you skip even a day of studying, you'll fall behind—that's just how it is.

examples.ja: 成功するためには困難に立ち向かうというものだ。
examples.romaji: Seikō suru tame ni wa konnan ni tachimukau to iu mono da.
examples.meaningEn: To succeed, one must face difficulties—that's just the way it is.

examples.ja: 友情はお金で買えないというものだ。
examples.romaji: Yūjō wa okane de kaenai to iu mono da.
examples.meaningEn: It is said that friendship cannot be bought with money.

title: どうにか～ないものか (dō ni ka ~ nai mono ka)
formation: どうにか + Verb-negative form + ないものか
shortExplanationEn: Express a strong desire or wish for something to be possible.
longExplanationEn: どうにか～ないものか is used to express a strong desire or wish for something to be resolved or made possible. It translates roughly as 'isn't there some way...?', 'can't we somehow...?', or 'is there no way...?'

examples.ja: どうにかこの仕事を終わらせないものか。
examples.romaji: Dō ni ka kono shigoto o owarasenai mono ka.
examples.meaningEn: Isn't there some way to finish this job?

examples.ja: どうにか次の試験で合格しないものか。
examples.romaji: Dō ni ka tsugi no shiken de gōkaku shinai mono ka.
examples.meaningEn: Can't we somehow pass the next exam?

examples.ja: どうにか込んでいる電車に乗れないものか。
examples.romaji: Dō ni ka konde iru densha ni norenai mono ka.
examples.meaningEn: Is there no way to get on the crowded train somehow?

examples.ja: どうにか彼女と仲直りしないものか。
examples.romaji: Dō ni ka kanojo to nakanaori shinai mono ka.
examples.meaningEn: Isn't there some way to make up with her?

title: ～とおり (〜toori)
formation: Verb-casual + とおり, い-Adjective + とおり, な-Adjective + だとおり, Noun + だとおり
shortExplanationEn: Express doing something as instructed or describing how something matches a rule or guideline; 'as', 'according to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とおり grammar point is used to express doing something exactly as instructed or describing how something matches a guideline or is exactly as one heard/expected. It can be translated as 'as', 'according to', or 'the same way...'.

examples.ja: 先生が言ったとおりに宿題をしました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ga itta tōri ni shukudai o shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I did my homework exactly as the teacher said.

examples.ja: この地図のとおりに歩いてください。
examples.romaji: Kono chizu no tōri ni aruite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please walk according to this map.

examples.ja: 彼はいつもルールどおりに遊びます。
examples.romaji: Kare wa itsumo rūru-dōri ni asobimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He always plays by the rules.

examples.ja: その道路は昼間と同じくらい夜も賑やかだというとおりでした。
examples.romaji: Sono dōro wa hiruma to onaji kurai yoru mo nigiyaka da to iu tōri deshita.
examples.meaningEn: As I heard, that street was just as lively at night as it is in the daytime.

title: ～とか (〜to ka)
formation: Verb-casual + とか, い-Adjective + とか, な-Adjective + だとか, Noun + だとか
shortExplanationEn: Used to list examples or express uncertainty; 'such as', 'like', 'or something like that'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とか grammar point can be used to list examples or express a somewhat vague mention of items/activities. It can be translated as 'such as', 'like', or 'or something like that'.

examples.ja: 休日は映画を見るとか、買い物に行くとかして過ごします。
examples.romaji: Kyūjitsu wa eiga o miru to ka, kaimono ni iku to ka shite sugoshimasu.
examples.meaningEn: On my days off, I do things like watch movies or go shopping.

examples.ja: 彼女はオーストラリアとかニュージーランドとかに行きたがっています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa Ōsutoraria to ka Nyūjīrando to ka ni ikitagatte imasu.
examples.meaningEn: She wants to go to places like Australia or New Zealand.

examples.ja: 今日の天気は晴れたり、曇ったりとかだそうです。
examples.romaji: Kyō no tenki wa hare tari, kumottari to ka da sō desu.
examples.meaningEn: I heard today’s weather is something like sunny at times, cloudy at times.

examples.ja: 彼は野球をしたり、サッカーをしたりとかしています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa yakyū o shitari, sakkā o shitari to ka shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: He spends his time playing baseball and soccer and such.

title: ～ところ (〜tokoro)
formation: Verb-casual + ところ, い-Adjective + ところ, な-Adjective + なところ, Noun + のところ
shortExplanationEn: Express a specific point in time or state; 'when', 'just about', or 'in the middle of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ところ grammar point is used to describe a specific point in time or stage of action. Depending on the verb tense, it can mean 'just about to do something', 'in the middle of doing something', or 'just did something'.

examples.ja: 出かけるところです。
examples.romaji: Dekakeru tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: I'm just about to go out.

examples.ja: 映画を見ているところです。
examples.romaji: Eiga o mite iru tokoro desu.
examples.meaningEn: I'm in the middle of watching a movie.

examples.ja: おいしいところで食事をするつもりです。
examples.romaji: Oishii tokoro de shokuji o suru tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to have a meal at a good (tasty) place.

examples.ja: 彼は忙しいところを手伝ってくれました。
examples.romaji: Kare wa isogashii tokoro o tetsudatte kuremashita.
examples.meaningEn: He helped me when he himself was busy.

title: ～どころか (〜dokoro ka)
formation: Verb-casual + どころか, い-Adjective + どころか, な-Adjective + どころか, Noun + どころか
shortExplanationEn: Express 'far from', 'not just', 'not only', or 'to say nothing of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～どころか grammar point is used to emphasize that reality is far from what one might expect or that something is not only one way, but actually goes beyond it. It can be translated as 'far from', 'not just', or 'to say nothing of'.

examples.ja: 彼は歌が上手どころか、プロの歌手みたいだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa uta ga jōzu dokoro ka, puro no kashu mitai da.
examples.meaningEn: Far from just being good at singing, he sounds like a professional singer.

examples.ja: この料理はおいしいどころか、最高の味だ。
examples.romaji: Kono ryōri wa oishii dokoro ka, saikō no aji da.
examples.meaningEn: This dish is not just delicious, it’s absolutely the best.

examples.ja: 彼女はただの友達どころか、私の親友だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa tada no tomodachi dokoro ka, watashi no shin’yū da.
examples.meaningEn: She’s far from just a friend; she’s my best friend.

examples.ja: 彼は英語ができるどころか、他の3つの言語も話せる。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eigo ga dekiru dokoro ka, hoka no mittsu no gengo mo hanaseru.
examples.meaningEn: Not only can he speak English, he also speaks three other languages.

title: ～どころではない (〜dokoro de wa nai)
formation: Verb-casual + どころではない, い-Adjective + どころではない, な-Adjective + どころではない, Noun + どころではない
shortExplanationEn: Not the time/place for; far from; can't possibly.
longExplanationEn: The ～どころではない grammar point is used to express that circumstances make it impossible or inappropriate to do something—there’s a much bigger issue at hand. It can also mean ‘far from’ in a figurative sense.

examples.ja: 彼の失敗を笑うどころではない。私たちも同じミスをしている。
examples.romaji: Kare no shippai o warau dokoro de wa nai. Watashitachi mo onaji misu o shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: This is not the time to laugh at his mistakes—we’re making the same ones.

examples.ja: 病気で寝ている時は、遊ぶどころではない。
examples.romaji: Byōki de nete iru toki wa, asobu dokoro de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: When you’re sick in bed, it's far from the time to play around.

examples.ja: 試験が近いので、映画を見るどころではない。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga chikai node, eiga o miru dokoro de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: Since the exam is coming up, I can’t possibly watch a movie.

examples.ja: 仕事が忙しすぎて、旅行なんて考えるどころではない。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga isogashisugite, ryokō nante kangaeru dokoro de wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: I'm way too busy with work to even think about going on a trip.

title: ～としたら (〜to shitara)
formation: Verb-casual + としたら, い-Adjective + としたら, な-Adjective + だとしたら, Noun + だとしたら
shortExplanationEn: Express a hypothetical situation; 'if', 'suppose', 'assuming'.
longExplanationEn: The ～としたら grammar point is used to express a hypothetical situation or assumption. It can be translated as 'if', 'suppose', or 'assuming' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it’s used with verbs, adjectives, or nouns.

examples.ja: 明日雨が降るとしたら、傘を持っていきましょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita ame ga furu to shitara, kasa o motte ikimashō.
examples.meaningEn: If it rains tomorrow, let’s bring umbrellas.

examples.ja: このケーキが美味しくないとしたら、誰も食べないだろう。
examples.romaji: Kono kēki ga oishikunai to shitara, dare mo tabenai darō.
examples.meaningEn: If this cake isn’t tasty, probably no one will eat it.

examples.ja: 彼が病気だとしたら、早めに病院へ行かないといけません。
examples.romaji: Kare ga byōki da to shitara, hayame ni byōin e ikanai to ikemasen.
examples.meaningEn: If he is sick, he needs to go to the hospital soon.

examples.ja: 彼女が学生だとしたら、このレストランは割引があるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga gakusei da to shitara, kono resutoran wa waribiki ga aru deshō.
examples.meaningEn: If she’s a student, there’s probably a discount at this restaurant.

title: ～としても (〜to shite mo)
formation: Verb-casual + としても, い-Adjective + としても, な-Adjective + だとしても, Noun + だとしても
shortExplanationEn: Even if, despite, although
longExplanationEn: The ～としても grammar point is used to say ‘even if...’ or ‘even assuming that...’, often followed by a conclusion or stance. It’s similar to ～ても, but puts more emphasis on the hypothetical assumption or viewpoint.

examples.ja: 雨が降るとしても、レースは中止しません。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu to shite mo, rēsu wa chūshi shimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Even if it rains, we won't cancel the race.

examples.ja: このケーキが美味しくないとしても、私は食べるつもりです。
examples.romaji: Kono kēki ga oishikunai to shite mo, watashi wa taberu tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if this cake isn't delicious, I plan on eating it.

examples.ja: 彼女が遅刻するとしても、私たちは待ち続けます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga chikoku suru to shite mo, watashitachi wa machitsuzukemasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even if she is late, we will keep waiting.

examples.ja: 忙しいだとしても、友達との約束は守りたい。
examples.romaji: Isogashii da to shite mo, tomodachi to no yakusoku wa mamoritai.
examples.meaningEn: Even if I'm busy, I want to keep my promise to my friend.

title: ～と～ともに (〜to 〜tomoni)
formation: Verb-casual + とともに, い-Adjective + とともに, な-Adjective + とともに, Noun + とともに
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'together with' or 'along with'; used to describe two events or actions happening simultaneously or in parallel.
longExplanationEn: The ～と～ともに grammar point is used to express that two events or actions are happening simultaneously or in parallel. It is translated as 'together with' or 'along with' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 彼が来るとともに、パーティーが始まりました。
examples.romaji: Kare ga kuru to tomoni, paatii ga hajimarimashita.
examples.meaningEn: The party started as soon as he arrived.

examples.ja: 季節が変わるとともに、服のファッションも変わります。
examples.romaji: Kisetsu ga kawaru to tomoni, fuku no fasshon mo kawarimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As the seasons change, clothing fashion also changes.

examples.ja: 成長とともに、いろいろな経験を積むことが大切です。
examples.romaji: Seichou to tomoni, iroirona keiken wo tsumu koto ga taisetsu desu.
examples.meaningEn: As you grow, it is important to gain various experiences.

examples.ja: 技術の進歩とともに、便利なアプリが増えています。
examples.romaji: Gijutsu no shinpo to tomoni, benrina apuri ga fueteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: With the advancement of technology, the number of convenient apps is increasing.

title: ～とは限らない (〜to wa kagiranai)
formation: Verb-casual + とは限らない, い-Adjective + とは限らない, な-Adjective + だとは限らない, Noun + だとは限らない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is not necessarily true; 'not always', 'not necessarily'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とは限らない grammar point is used to express that something is not necessarily true or does not always occur. It can be translated as 'not always' or 'not necessarily' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: いつも勉強しているとは限らない。
examples.romaji: Itsumo benkyou shite iru to wa kagiranai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not always true that I'm studying.

examples.ja: 高い商品がいいとは限らない。
examples.romaji: Takai shouhin ga ii to wa kagiranai.
examples.meaningEn: Expensive products are not necessarily good.

examples.ja: 彼が正しいとは限らない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga tadashii to wa kagiranai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not necessarily true that he is right.

examples.ja: 雨が降るとは限らない。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu to wa kagiranai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not necessarily going to rain.

title: ～ないことはない (〜nai koto wa nai)
formation: Verb-ないform + ことはない
shortExplanationEn: Express that something is not impossible, meaning it is possible or could be done.
longExplanationEn: The ～ないことはない grammar point is used to express a double negative, meaning that something is not impossible, implying that it is possible or could be done. Essentially, it downplays the possibility or ability to do something but still acknowledges that it's possible.

examples.ja: 彼が来ないことはない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga konai koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that he won't come (it's possible that he will come).

examples.ja: あの問題が解けないことはない。
examples.romaji: Ano mondai ga tokenai koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not impossible to solve that problem (it can be solved).

examples.ja: その仕事が難しくないことはない。
examples.romaji: Sono shigoto ga muzukashikunai koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: That job is not without difficulty (it has some difficulties).

examples.ja: 彼女が正しくないことはない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga tadashikunai koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not impossible that she is right (she could be right).

title: ～ないこともない (〜nai koto mo nai)
formation: Verb-ないForm + こともない, い-adjective + こともない
shortExplanationEn: Used to express an action or state is 'not impossible', but emphasizes reluctance or doubt.
longExplanationEn: The ～ないこともない grammar point is used to express that something is not impossible, but the speaker is reluctant, has doubts, or deems it as a less favorable option. It can be translated as 'not that one can't', 'not that it's impossible', or 'it's not out of the question'.

examples.ja: この問題は解けないこともないけど、難しいです。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa tokenai koto mo nai kedo, muzukashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I can't solve this problem, but it's difficult.

examples.ja: 彼に会わないこともないが、あまり話す時間がない。
examples.romaji: Kare ni awanai koto mo nai ga, amari hanasu jikan ga nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I can't meet him, but there's not much time to talk.

examples.ja: お金がないこともないけれど、節約した方がいい。
examples.romaji: O-kane ga nai koto mo nai keredo, setsuyaku shita hou ga ii.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I don't have money, but it's better to save.

examples.ja: このゲームが面白くないこともないが、他のゲームの方が楽しい。
examples.romaji: Kono geemu ga omoshirokunai koto mo nai ga, hoka no geemu no hou ga tanoshii.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that this game isn't interesting, but other games are more fun.

title: ～ないではいられない (〜nai de wa irarenai)
formation: Verb-ない form + ではいられない or じゃいられない
shortExplanationEn: Cannot help but; cannot avoid; unable to refrain from.
longExplanationEn: The ～ないではいられない grammar point is used to express a strong feeling, urge, or uncontrollable action that someone cannot resist or help doing, even if it may be undesirable. It can be translated as 'cannot help but', 'cannot avoid', or 'unable to refrain from' in English.

examples.ja: この映画はとても感動的で、泣かないではいられなかった。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa totemo kandouteki de, nakanai de wa irarenakatta.
examples.meaningEn: This movie was so touching, I couldn't help but cry.

examples.ja: 彼女の笑顔を見ると、笑わないではいられない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no egao wo miru to, warawanai de wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: When I see her smile, I can't help but laugh.

examples.ja: この部屋はとても寒いので、震えないではいられない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya wa totemo samui node, furuenai de wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: This room is so cold, I can't help but shiver.

examples.ja: 彼の話はつまらないが、聞かないではいられない。
examples.romaji: Kare no hanashi wa tsumaranai ga, kikanai de wa irarenai.
examples.meaningEn: His story is boring, but I can't help but listen.

title: ～ながら (〜nagara)
formation: Verb-stem + ながら
shortExplanationEn: Express doing two things simultaneously or at the same time.
longExplanationEn: The ～ながら grammar point is used to express the concept of doing two things simultaneously or at the same time. In English, it can be translated as 'while', 'as', or 'during'. ～ながら can be attached to the stem of a verb to convey this meaning.

examples.ja: テレビを見ながら宿題をしています。
examples.romaji: Terebi wo minagara shukudai wo shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am doing my homework while watching TV.

examples.ja: 彼女は歌いながら料理を作っていました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa utainagara ryouri wo tsukutte imashita.
examples.meaningEn: She was cooking while singing.

examples.ja: 電車の中で立ちながら本を読んでいる人が多いです。
examples.romaji: Densha no naka de tachinagara hon wo yonde iru hito ga ooi desu.
examples.meaningEn: There are many people who read books while standing on the train.

examples.ja: 僕は歩きながらスマホを使っています。
examples.romaji: Boku wa arukinagara sumaho wo tsukatteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I am using my smartphone while walking.

title: ～にあたり (〜ni atari)
formation: Noun + にあたり
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the meaning of 'at the time of', 'in', 'on', or 'upon'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にあたり grammar point is used to describe an important event or situation that occurs at a specific moment. It can be translated as 'at the time of', 'in', 'on', or 'upon' in English. The formation is quite simple, as it only requires a noun before it.

examples.ja: 入学にあたり、必要な書類を準備してください。
examples.romaji: Nyuugaku ni atari, hitsuyou na shorui wo junbi shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please prepare the necessary documents upon entering the school.

examples.ja: 新年にあたり、抱負を立てる人が多いです。
examples.romaji: Shinnen ni atari, houfu wo tateru hito ga ooi desu.
examples.meaningEn: Many people set goals at the time of the new year.

examples.ja: 結婚にあたり、両家は大切な儀式を行います。
examples.romaji: Kekkon ni atari, ryouke wa taisetsu na gishiki wo okonaimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Upon getting married, both families conduct an important ceremony.

examples.ja: 新しい仕事を始めるにあたり、準備が必要です。
examples.romaji: Atarashii shigoto wo hajimeru ni atari, junbi ga hitsuyou desu.
examples.meaningEn: In starting a new job, preparation is necessary.

title: ～において (〜ni oite)
formation: Noun + において
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the place, time, or context where an action or event occurs; 'in', 'at', 'on', 'regarding'.
longExplanationEn: The ～において grammar point is used to show the place, time, or context where an action or event takes place. It can be translated as 'in', 'at', 'on', or 'regarding' in English. It is often used in formal situations and can be replaced by more casual equivalents such as で, に, or は in informal contexts.

examples.ja: この大学において、英語のクラスが一番人気があります。
examples.romaji: Kono daigaku ni oite, eigo no kurasu ga ichiban ninki ga arimasu.
examples.meaningEn: In this university, English classes are the most popular.

examples.ja: 彼の研究において新しい発見がありました。
examples.romaji: Kare no kenkyuu ni oite atarashii hakken ga arimashita.
examples.meaningEn: In his research, there were new discoveries.

examples.ja: 運動会において、彼は大活躍しました。
examples.romaji: Undoukai ni oite, kare wa daikatsuyaku shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: At the sports festival, he performed exceptionally well.

examples.ja: 環境問題においては、みんなが責任を持って行動すべきです。
examples.romaji: Kankyou mondai ni oite wa, minna ga sekinin wo motte koudou subeki desu.
examples.meaningEn: Regarding environmental issues, everyone should take responsibility and act accordingly.

title: ～にかかわらず (〜ni kakawarazu)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + にかかわらず, い-Adjective + にかかわらず, な-Adjective + にかかわらず, Noun + にかかわらず
shortExplanationEn: Regardless of; irrespective of; (often used as) 'whether or not'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にかかわらず grammar point is used to express that something happens or is true regardless of a particular fact, condition, or circumstance. It can be translated as 'regardless of', 'irrespective of', or 'whether or not'. It can be used with verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives, or nouns.

examples.ja: 天気にかかわらず、今日の試合は行われます。
examples.romaji: Tenki ni kakawarazu, kyou no shiai wa okonawaremasu.
examples.meaningEn: The game today will be held, regardless of the weather.

examples.ja: 彼は年齢にかかわらず、新しいことを学び続けています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nenrei ni kakawarazu, atarashii koto wo manabi tsuzukete imasu.
examples.meaningEn: He continues to learn new things, regardless of his age.

examples.ja: その仕事が難しいかどうかにかかわらず、彼女は努力し続けた。
examples.romaji: Sono shigoto ga muzukashii ka dou ka ni kakawarazu, kanojo wa doryoku shi tsuzuketa.
examples.meaningEn: Regardless of whether the job is difficult or not, she continued to make an effort.

examples.ja: 経験の有無にかかわらず、このプログラムは誰でも利用できます。
examples.romaji: Keiken no umu ni kakawarazu, kono puroguramu wa daredemo riyou dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Regardless of experience, this program can be used by anyone.

title: ～にかけては (〜ni kakete wa)
formation: Noun + にかけては
shortExplanationEn: Indicates a specific area or aspect in which someone or something excels.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point ～にかけては is used to emphasize a particular area or aspect in which someone or something excels or is exceptional. This structure is often used with positive connotations regarding the subject's expertise or outstanding qualities.

examples.ja: 料理の腕前にかけては、彼女は誰にも負けません。
examples.romaji: Ryouri no udemae ni kakete wa, kanojo wa dare ni mo makemasen.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to cooking skills, she is second to none.

examples.ja: この町にかけては、彼の店が一番おいしいラーメンを売っています。
examples.romaji: Kono machi ni kakete wa, kare no mise ga ichiban oishii raamen wo utteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As far as this town is concerned, his store sells the most delicious ramen.

examples.ja: 音楽の知識にかけては、彼は私たちの中で一番詳しいです。
examples.romaji: Ongaku no chishiki ni kakete wa, kare wa watashitachi no naka de ichiban kuwashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to music knowledge, he is the most knowledgeable among us.

examples.ja: スポーツにかけては、彼はどんな競技も得意です。
examples.romaji: Supootsu ni kakete wa, kare wa donna kyogi mo tokui desu.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to sports, he excels in any competition.

title: ～にしたがって (〜ni shitagatte)
formation: Noun + にしたがって
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a natural consequence or a change that occurs according to something else; 'as', 'with', 'in accordance with'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にしたがって grammar point is used to express a natural consequence or a change that occurs according to something else. It can be translated as 'as', 'with', or 'in accordance with' in English. This grammar point is used with nouns and can be used to show a change in proportion or in accordance with a rule or principle.

examples.ja: 成長にしたがって、この植物の葉が大きくなります。
examples.romaji: Seichou ni shitagatte, kono shokubutsu no ha ga ookiku narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As it grows, the leaves of this plant become larger.

examples.ja: 説明書にしたがって、家具を組み立ててください。
examples.romaji: Setsumeisho ni shitagatte, kagu wo kumitatete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please assemble the furniture according to the instructions.

examples.ja: 年齢にしたがって、人の体力は低下します。
examples.romaji: Nenrei ni shitagatte, hito no tairyoku wa teika shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As people age, their physical strength declines.

examples.ja: 運動にしたがって、心拍数が増加することが一般的です。
examples.romaji: Undou ni shitagatte, shinpakusuu ga zouka suru koto ga ippan teki desu.
examples.meaningEn: It is common for the heart rate to increase with exercise.

title: ～にしたら (〜ni shitara)
formation: Noun + にしたら, Pronoun + にしたら
shortExplanationEn: Express a point of view; 'from the perspective of', 'if I were'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にしたら grammar point is used to express a point of view or opinion from someone else's perspective. It can be translated as 'from the perspective of' or 'if I were' in English. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a noun or a pronoun.

examples.ja: 彼にしたら、早く帰りたいだろう。
examples.romaji: Kare ni shitara, hayaku kaeritai darou.
examples.meaningEn: From his perspective, he probably wants to go home early.

examples.ja: 子供たちにしたら、この話はつまらないかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi ni shitara, kono hanashi wa tsumaranai kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: If I were the children, this story might be boring.

examples.ja: 私にしたら、そのプレゼントは素晴らしいと思います。
examples.romaji: Watashi ni shitara, sono purezento wa subarashii to omoimasu.
examples.meaningEn: From my perspective, I think that present is wonderful.

examples.ja: 彼女にしたら、この映画が好きではないでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ni shitara, kono eiga ga suki dewa nai deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If I were her, I would not like this movie.

title: ～にしろ～にしろ (〜ni shiro 〜ni shiro)
formation: Verb (dictionary form or negative form) + にしろ, い-Adjective + にしろ, な-Adjective + にしろ, Noun + にしろ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'either way', 'in any case', 'be it...', used when presenting two alternatives.
longExplanationEn: The ～にしろ～にしろ grammar point is used to present two alternatives and emphasize that regardless of which one is chosen or occurs, the result or conclusion remains the same. It can be translated as 'either way', 'in any case', or 'be it...'. The formation differs depending on whether it is used with a verb, い-adjective, な-adjective, or noun.

examples.ja: 行くにしろ行かないにしろ、早く決めてください。
examples.romaji: Iku ni shiro ikanai ni shiro, hayaku kimete kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Either way, whether you go or not, please decide quickly.

examples.ja: 彼が来るにしろ来ないにしろ、パーティーは始めましょう。
examples.romaji: Kare ga kuru ni shiro konai ni shiro, paatii wa hajimemashou.
examples.meaningEn: Be it if he comes or not, let's start the party either way.

examples.ja: 雨が降るにしろ降らないにしろ、傘を持って行ったほうがいいです。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu ni shiro furanai ni shiro, kasa wo motte itta hou ga ii desu.
examples.meaningEn: Either way, whether it rains or not, it's better to bring an umbrella.

examples.ja: このレストランが高いにしろ安いにしろ、料理は美味しいです。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran ga takai ni shiro yasui ni shiro, ryouri wa oishii desu.
examples.meaningEn: In any case, whether this restaurant is expensive or cheap, the food is delicious.

title: ～につけ～につけ (〜ni tsuke 〜ni tsuke)
formation: Verb-casual + につけ, い-Adjective + につけ, な-Adjective + につけ, Noun + につけ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'whenever', 'each time', 'whether...or'.
longExplanationEn: The ～につけ～につけ grammar point is used to express that something happens 'each time' or 'whenever' a certain event or situation occurs, and can also mean 'whether...or'. This grammar point indicates that the same event or feeling occurs no matter which of the following situations happen.

examples.ja: 外国へ行くにつけ、勉強するにつけ、言葉の壁にぶつかる。
examples.romaji: Gaikoku e iku ni tsuke, benkyou suru ni tsuke, kotoba no kabe ni butsukaru.
examples.meaningEn: Whether I go abroad or study, I always encounter language barriers.

examples.ja: 彼と話すにつけ、彼が考えるにつけ、彼の知識に驚かされる。
examples.romaji: Kare to hanasu ni tsuke, kare ga kangaeru ni tsuke, kare no chishiki ni odorokasareru.
examples.meaningEn: Whenever I talk to him or hear his thoughts, I'm always amazed by his knowledge.

examples.ja: この公園で遊ぶにつけ、散歩するにつけ、子供時代を思い出す。
examples.romaji: Kono kouen de asobu ni tsuke, sanpo suru ni tsuke, kodomo jidai wo omoidasu.
examples.meaningEn: Each time I play or take a walk in this park, it reminds me of my childhood.

examples.ja: 春につけ、秋につけ、彼は母校を訪れる。
examples.romaji: Haru ni tsuke, aki ni tsuke, kare wa bokou wo otozureru.
examples.meaningEn: Whether in spring or autumn, he visits his alma mater.

title: ～につれて (〜ni tsurete)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + につれて, Noun + につれて
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the idea that as one thing changes, so does another; 'as', 'along with', 'while'.
longExplanationEn: The ～につれて grammar point is used to express the idea that as one thing changes or progresses, another thing changes or progresses simultaneously. In English, it can be translated as 'as', 'along with', or 'while'.

examples.ja: 年を取るにつれて、健康に気をつけなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Toshi wo toru ni tsurete, kenkou ni ki wo tsukenakereba narimasen.
examples.meaningEn: As you get older, you have to pay more attention to your health.

examples.ja: 春が近づくにつれて、気温が上がる。
examples.romaji: Haru ga chikazuku ni tsurete, kion ga agaru.
examples.meaningEn: As spring approaches, the temperature rises.

examples.ja: 勉強するにつれて、だんだん分かるようになる。
examples.romaji: Benkyou suru ni tsurete, dandan wakaru you ni naru.
examples.meaningEn: As I study, I gradually come to understand.

examples.ja: 経験が増えるにつれて、自信もついてくる。
examples.romaji: Keiken ga fueru ni tsurete, jishin mo tsuite kuru.
examples.meaningEn: As your experience increases, so does your confidence.

title: ～にともなって (〜ni tomonatte)
formation: Noun (event) + にともなって, Verb-casual + にともなって
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'along with' or 'as a result of'; indicates that one event occurs along with another.
longExplanationEn: The ～にともなって grammar point is used to indicate that one event occurs along with another event, or as a result of another event. It can be translated as 'along with', 'as a result of', or 'in accordance with' in English.

examples.ja: 人口が増えるにともなって、交通渋滞も悪化しています。
examples.romaji: Jinkou ga fueru ni tomonatte, koutsuu juutai mo akka shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As the population increases, traffic congestion is also getting worse.

examples.ja: 季節の変わり目にともなって、体調を崩しやすくなります。
examples.romaji: Kisetsu no kawarime ni tomonatte, taichou wo kuzushi yasuku narimasu.
examples.meaningEn: As the seasons change, it's easier to get out of shape.

examples.ja: 経済の成長にともなって、雇用の機会も増えるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Keizai no seichou ni tomonatte, koyou no kikai mo fueru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: As the economy grows, employment opportunities will also increase.

examples.ja: 彼の昇進にともなって、引っ越しをすることになりました。
examples.romaji: Kare no shoushin ni tomonatte, hikkoshi wo suru koto ni narimashita.
examples.meaningEn: As a result of his promotion, we decided to move.

title: ～にほかならない (〜ni hoka naranai)
formation: Verb-plain + にほかならない, い-Adjective + にほかならない, な-Adjective + にほかならない, Noun + にほかならない
shortExplanationEn: Express that something is 'nothing but' or 'none other than'.
longExplanationEn: The ～にほかならない grammar point is used to emphasize that something is no more or no less than what is specified. It can be seen as a strong way to say that something is 'nothing but' or 'none other than'.

examples.ja: 彼の成功は努力にほかならない。
examples.romaji: Kare no seikou wa doryoku ni hoka naranai.
examples.meaningEn: His success is nothing but hard work.

examples.ja: この痛みは寒さにほかならない。
examples.romaji: Kono itami wa samusa ni hoka naranai.
examples.meaningEn: This pain is nothing but the cold.

examples.ja: 彼の失敗の原因は慢心にほかならない。
examples.romaji: Kare no shippai no gen'in wa manshin ni hoka naranai.
examples.meaningEn: The cause of his failure is nothing but overconfidence.

examples.ja: 彼女の笑顔は明るさにほかならない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no egao wa akarusa ni hoka naranai.
examples.meaningEn: Her smile is none other than brightness.

title: ～にもかかわらず (〜ni mo kakawarazu)
formation: Verb (any tense/form) + にもかかわらず, い-Adjective + にもかかわらず, な-Adjective (or Noun) + であるにもかかわらず
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'despite', 'in spite of' or 'even though'.
