examples.ja: 試験に落ちるとなったら、どうしよう。
examples.romaji: Shiken ni ochiru to nattara, dou shiyou.
examples.meaningEn: What should I do if I fail the exam?

examples.ja: 彼女と別れるとなったら、とても悲しいです。
examples.romaji: Kanojo to wakareru to nattara, totemo kanashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: If it comes to breaking up with her, I’ll be very sad.

examples.ja: 結婚するとなったら、人生が大きく変わるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kekkon suru to nattara, jinsei ga ookiku kawaru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to getting married, life will probably change drastically.

title: ～となると (〜to naru to)
formation: Verb-casual + となると / い-Adjective + となると / な-Adjective + だとなると / Noun + だとなると
shortExplanationEn: Used to highlight a certain circumstance; 'when it comes to...', 'if it becomes...', 'in the event that...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～となると grammar point highlights a specific situation or condition and indicates what normally or naturally happens under that circumstance. It can be translated as 'when it comes to...', 'if it becomes...', or 'in the event that...'.

examples.ja: 試験となると、多くの学生が緊張する。
examples.romaji: Shiken to naru to, ooku no gakusei ga kinchou suru.
examples.meaningEn: When it comes to exams, many students get nervous.

examples.ja: 東京へ引っ越すとなると、生活費が一気に上がる。
examples.romaji: Toukyou e hikkosu to naru to, seikatsuhi ga ikki ni agaru.
examples.meaningEn: If you move to Tokyo, the cost of living goes up drastically.

examples.ja: 締め切りが明日となると、今夜は徹夜になりそうだ。
examples.romaji: Shimekiri ga ashita to naru to, konya wa tetsuya ni narisou da.
examples.meaningEn: If the deadline is tomorrow, I’ll probably be up all night.

examples.ja: 彼がリーダーとなると、チームの活動は大きく改善されるかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga riidaa to naru to, chiimu no katsudou wa ookiku kaizen sareru kamoshirenai.
examples.meaningEn: If he becomes the leader, the team’s activities might improve significantly.

title: ～となれば (〜to nareba)
formation: Verb-casual + となれば, い-Adjective + となれば, な-Adjective + だとなれば, Noun + だとなれば
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the idea of 'if it comes to...; when we talk about...; in the case of...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～となれば grammar point is used to express a situation where a particular case or condition brings about a certain result or consequence. It translates as 'if it comes to...; when we talk about...; in the case of...' in English.

examples.ja: 彼がリーダーとなれば、プロジェクトは成功するでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kare ga riidaa to nareba, purojekuto wa seikou suru deshou.
examples.meaningEn: If he becomes the leader, the project will probably succeed.

examples.ja: 明日が雨だとなれば、ピクニックをキャンセルしましょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita ga ame da to nareba, pikunikku wo kyanseru shimashou.
examples.meaningEn: If it turns out to be raining tomorrow, let's cancel the picnic.

examples.ja: この仕事が終わったとなれば、休暇を取ることができます。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto ga owatta to nareba, kyuuka wo toru koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: If this job is over, you can take a vacation.

examples.ja: そのテストが難しいとなれば、より一層勉強しなければならない。
examples.romaji: Sono tesuto ga muzukashii to nareba, yori issou benkyou shinakereba naranai.
examples.meaningEn: If that test is difficult, we must study all the more.

title: ～とのことだ (〜to no koto da)
formation: Simplified phrase + とのことだ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses something that the speaker heard; 'I've heard that', 'I was told that'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とのことだ grammar point is used to express something that the speaker has heard from someone else. It can be roughly translated as 'I've heard that', 'I was told that' in English. It is a formal way to present hearsay or information the speaker did not directly witness but was informed about.

examples.ja: 週末にパーティーがあるとのことだ。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu ni paatii ga aru to no koto da.
examples.meaningEn: I've heard that there is a party this weekend.

examples.ja: 彼は来年ロンドンに引っ越すとのことだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa rainen Rondon ni hikkosu to no koto da.
examples.meaningEn: I was told that he is moving to London next year.

examples.ja: 彼女は新しい仕事についたとのことだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa atarashii shigoto ni tsuita to no koto da.
examples.meaningEn: I've heard that she has gotten a new job.

examples.ja: 明日は試験がないとのことだ。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa shiken ga nai to no koto da.
examples.meaningEn: I was told that there is no exam tomorrow.

title: ～とはいえ (～to wa ie)
formation: Noun + とはいえ, な-Adjective + だとはいえ, い-Adjective + とはいえ, Verb-casual + とはいえ
shortExplanationEn: Even though; although; but
longExplanationEn: The ～とはいえ grammar structure is used to express the contradictory nature of a situation or event. It means 'even though' or 'although', signifying a sense of contrast or contradiction. The usage of this structure is mainly seen in a formal or written context.

examples.ja: 彼は頭が良いとはいえ、少し怠け者だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa atama ga yoi to wa ie, sukoshi namakemono da.
examples.meaningEn: Although he is smart, he is a bit lazy.

examples.ja: このレストランは美味しいとはいえ、ちょっと高い。
examples.romaji: Kono resutoran wa oishii to wa ie, chotto takai.
examples.meaningEn: While this restaurant is delicious, it's a bit expensive.

examples.ja: 日本は小さい国だとはいえ、技術は世界一だ。
examples.romaji: Nihon wa chiisai kuni da to wa ie, gijutsu wa sekaiichi da.
examples.meaningEn: Even though Japan is a small country, its technology is among the best in the world.

examples.ja: 彼女は若いとはいえ、経験豊富だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa wakai to wa ie, keiken houfu da.
examples.meaningEn: Even though she is young, she is very experienced.

title: ～とみえて (〜to miete)
formation: Verb-casual + とみえて, い-Adjective + とみえて, な-Adjective + だとみえて, Noun + だとみえて
shortExplanationEn: It seems that; apparently
longExplanationEn: The ～とみえて grammar point is used to express the speaker's judgment about a situation based on visible or known information. It is often translated as 'apparently' or 'it seems that' in English.

examples.ja: 彼は運動が得意とみえて、毎日ジムに行っています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa undou ga tokui to miete, mainichi jimu ni itteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: He seems to be good at exercise, as he goes to the gym every day.

examples.ja: 彼女は忙しいとみえて、なかなか会えない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa isogashii to miete, nakanaka aenai.
examples.meaningEn: She seems to be busy, as it's hard to meet her.

examples.ja: 田中さんは疲れているとみえて、早く帰りました。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san wa tsukarete iru to miete, hayaku kaerimashita.
examples.meaningEn: It seems that Tanaka-san was tired, as he went home early.

examples.ja: その店は人気だとみえて、いつも行列ができている。
examples.romaji: Sono mise wa ninki da to miete, itsumo retsu ga dekite iru.
examples.meaningEn: That shop seems to be popular, as there is always a line.

title: ～とみられる (～to mirareru)
formation: Verb-casual + とみられる, Noun + とみられる
shortExplanationEn: It is considered that; It is thought that; It is believed that
longExplanationEn: The ～とみられる grammar point is used to express how an idea, fact, or circumstance is considered or understood. This phrase is often used in news or reports to show the speaker's or writer's estimation, judgment, or present belief about something.

examples.ja: 彼の行動は無責任とみられる。
examples.romaji: Kare no koudou wa musekinin to mirareru.
examples.meaningEn: His behavior is considered irresponsible.

examples.ja: この絵はピカソの作品とみられている。
examples.romaji: Kono e wa pikaso no sakuhin to mirareteiru.
examples.meaningEn: This painting is believed to be a work of Picasso.

examples.ja: 地震の後、被害はさらに増加するとみられる。
examples.romaji: Jishin no ato, higai wa sara ni zouka suru to mirareru.
examples.meaningEn: After the earthquake, damage is expected to increase further.

examples.ja: 週末は天気が良くなるとみられる。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu wa tenki ga yoku naru to mirareru.
examples.meaningEn: The weather is expected to improve on the weekend.

title: ～とみると (〜to miru to)
formation: Verb-casual + とみると, い-Adjective + とみると, な-Adjective + だとみると, Noun + だとみると
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the concept of 'when one sees it as', 'viewed as', or 'considering'.
longExplanationEn: The ～とみると grammar point is used to express the concept of seeing something from a specific perspective and responding accordingly. It often brings awareness toward something or a change in perception. It is translated as 'when one sees it as', 'viewed as', or 'considering'.

examples.ja: この問題を数学の視点でとみると、解決策が見えてきます。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai o suugaku no shiten de to miru to, kaiketsusaku ga miete kimasu.
examples.meaningEn: When one sees this problem from a mathematical perspective, a solution begins to emerge.

examples.ja: 彼女が優秀な学生だとみると、その賞をもらったのは当然です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga yuushuuna gakusei da to miru to, sono shou o moratta no wa touzen desu.
examples.meaningEn: Considering she is an excellent student, it's natural that she received that award.

examples.ja: 彼が新入社員だとみると、その成果は素晴らしいです。
examples.romaji: Kare ga shinnyuushain da to miru to, sono seika wa subarashii desu.
examples.meaningEn: Viewing him as a new employee, his achievements are exceptional.

examples.ja: このパンダが絶滅危惧種だとみると、保護が必要です。
examples.romaji: Kono panda ga zetsumetsu kigushu da to miru to, hogo ga hitsuyou desu.
examples.meaningEn: Considering this panda is an endangered species, it needs protection.

title: どんなに～うが (donna ni ～ u ga)
formation: どんなに + Verb-volitional + うが, どんなに + い-Adjective(～かろう) + が, どんなに + な-Adjective + だろうが
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'no matter how much...', 'even if...'.
longExplanationEn: The grammar point どんなに～うが is used to express a condition that is not affected by the degree of an action or state. It can be translated as 'no matter how much...' or 'even if...'. It is generally used with verbs, い-adjectives and な-adjectives in their volitional or かろう/だろう form.

examples.ja: どんなに頑張ろうが、彼の速さには追いつけない。
examples.romaji: Donna ni ganbarou ga, kare no hayasa ni wa oitsukenai.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how hard I try, I can't catch up with his speed.

examples.ja: どんなに食べようが、彼女は太らない。
examples.romaji: Donna ni tabeyou ga, kanojo wa futoranai.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how much she eats, she doesn't gain weight.

examples.ja: どんなに寒かろうが、彼は毎日ジョギングをします。
examples.romaji: Donna ni samukarou ga, kare wa mainichi jogging wo shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how cold it is, he goes jogging every day.

examples.ja: どんなに暑かろうが、エアコンは使いません。
examples.romaji: Donna ni atsukarou ga, eakon wa tsukaimasen.
examples.meaningEn: No matter how hot it is, I don't use air conditioning.

title: ～と言わんばかりに (〜to iwan bakari ni)
formation: Verb-volitional + と言わんばかりに, い-Adjective + と言わんばかりに, な-Adjective + だと言わんばかりに, Noun + だと言わんばかりに
shortExplanationEn: Indicates an action or behavior that strongly implies a certain unspoken message or feeling.
longExplanationEn: The ～と言わんばかりに grammar point is used to express that someone's action or behavior is so strong that it's as if they are saying something without actually saying it. It can be translated as 'as if to say' or 'almost as though stating'. This form is often used when the speaker wants to convey speculation about someone's unvoiced thoughts or feelings based on their behavior.

examples.ja: 彼女は優勝したと言わんばかりに笑った。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa yuushou shita to iwan bakari ni waratta.
examples.meaningEn: She laughed as if to say she won.

examples.ja: 彼は倒れると言わんばかりの疲労感があった。
examples.romaji: Kare wa taoreru to iwan bakari no hiroukan ga atta.
examples.meaningEn: He was so fatigued it was as if he might collapse.

examples.ja: 彼女は雨の日を恐れていて、晴れてほしいと言わんばかりに窓の外を見つめていた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ame no hi o osorete ite, harete hoshii to iwan bakari ni mado no soto o mitsumete ita.
examples.meaningEn: She was afraid of rainy days, almost as if to say she wanted it to be sunny, as she gazed out the window.

examples.ja: 彼の行動は、誰にも信じてもらえないと言わんばかりだった。
examples.romaji: Kare no koudou wa, dare ni mo shinjite moraenai to iwan bakari datta.
examples.meaningEn: His behavior was as if to say that no one would believe him.

title: ～と言わんばかりの Noun (～to iwan bakari no Noun)
formation: Verb-plain form + と言わんばかりの Noun
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the concept of 'as if to say'; 'almost says'; 'suggests'.
longExplanationEn: The ～と言わんばかりの Noun grammar point is used to express that someone's action or a situation suggests a certain message, even though it is not explicitly said. It can be translated as 'as if to say', 'almost says', or 'suggests'. It is typically used to convey an unspoken message through someone's demeanor or behavior.

examples.ja: 田中さんは嬉しくて飛び上がると言わんばかりの笑顔を見せた。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san wa ureshikute tobiagaru to iwan bakari no egao wo miseta.
examples.meaningEn: Tanaka showed a smile as if to say he was so happy he could jump for joy.

examples.ja: 彼女は僕に話しかけると言わんばかりの視線を送った。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa boku ni hanashikakeru to iwan bakari no shisen wo okutta.
examples.meaningEn: She sent me glances as if to say she wanted to speak with me.

examples.ja: その老人は寒さで震えていると言わんばかりの姿を見せていた。
examples.romaji: Sono roujin wa samusa de furueteiru to iwan bakari no sugata wo miseteita.
examples.meaningEn: The old man was shivering as if to say he was freezing from the cold.

examples.ja: 彼の体は緊張で固まっていると言わんばかりの風格を放っている。
examples.romaji: Kare no karada wa kinchou de katamatteiru to iwan bakari no fuukaku wo hanatteiru.
examples.meaningEn: He exudes an air as if his body were stiff from tension.

title: ～ながらに (～nagara ni)
formation: Common set phrases: 生まれながらに, 涙ながらに, 昔ながらに, 子供ながらに, etc.
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a state or condition continuing just as it is (e.g. 'from birth', 'while crying', 'in the old way').
longExplanationEn: The ～ながらに grammar point is used to indicate a condition that remains unchanged from some starting point or in a certain manner. It appears in set phrases such as 生まれながらに (from birth), 涙ながらに (while crying), 昔ながらに (in the old/original way), 子供ながらに (even though [still] a child), etc.

examples.ja: 彼女は生まれながらにして優れた音感を持っている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa umare nagara ni shite sugureta onkan o motte iru.
examples.meaningEn: She has had an excellent sense of pitch from birth.

examples.ja: 彼は子供ながらに大人の会話を理解していた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kodomo nagara ni otona no kaiwa o rikaishite ita.
examples.meaningEn: Even though he was just a child, he understood adult conversations.

examples.ja: 彼女は涙ながらに真実を訴えた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa namida nagara ni shinjitsu o uttaeta.
examples.meaningEn: She pleaded the truth while in tears.

examples.ja: 私たちは昔ながらに伝わる祭りを大切にしている。
examples.romaji: Watashi-tachi wa mukashi nagara ni tsutawaru matsuri o taisetsu ni shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: We cherish the traditional festival that has been passed down unchanged from old times.

title: ～ながらの Noun (〜nagara no Noun)
formation: Verb-ますstem (or plain minus る) + ながら + の + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Indicates one action or state happening at the same time as the noun in question (e.g. 'dinner while watching TV').
longExplanationEn: The ～ながらの Noun grammar point is used to modify a noun by describing an action happening simultaneously. For example, テレビを見ながらの夕食 means 'dinner while watching TV.' It can be translated as 'while ~ing' or 'during ~.'

examples.ja: テレビを見ながらの夕食は、子供たちにとって楽しいだろう。
examples.romaji: Terebi o mi-nagara no yuushoku wa, kodomotachi ni totte tanoshii darou.
examples.meaningEn: Eating dinner while watching TV might be fun for the children.

examples.ja: 地図を読みながらの運転は危険です。
examples.romaji: Chizu o yomi-nagara no unten wa kiken desu.
examples.meaningEn: Driving while reading a map is dangerous.

examples.ja: 散歩しながらの会話はリラックスできます。
examples.romaji: Sanpo shi-nagara no kaiwa wa rirakkusu dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Talking during a walk can be relaxing.

examples.ja: 音楽を聴きながらの勉強は集中力を高めるかもしれません。
examples.romaji: Ongaku o kiki-nagara no benkyou wa shuuchuuryoku o takameru kamoshiremasen.
examples.meaningEn: Studying while listening to music might improve concentration.

title: ～ながらも (〜nagara mo)
formation: Verb stem + ながらも, い-Adjective + ながらも, な-Adjective/Noun + ながらも
shortExplanationEn: Means 'even though' or 'despite'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ながらも grammar point is used to express contrast, highlighting the difference between what is usually expected from a situation and what actually happens. It can be translated as 'even though' or 'despite.'

examples.ja: 彼は貧しいながらも、幸せな生活を送っている。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mazushii nagara mo, shiawase na seikatsu o okutte iru.
examples.meaningEn: Despite being poor, he leads a happy life.

examples.ja: 僕は忙しいながらも、毎日運動しています。
examples.romaji: Boku wa isogashii nagara mo, mainichi undou shiteimasu.
examples.meaningEn: Even though I am busy, I exercise every day.

examples.ja: 病気ながらも、彼は仕事を続けました。
examples.romaji: Byouki nagara mo, kare wa shigoto o tsuzukemashita.
examples.meaningEn: Despite being sick, he continued working.

examples.ja: 雨が降っているながらも、彼は散歩に出かけた。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte iru nagara mo, kare wa sanpo ni dekaketa.
examples.meaningEn: Even though it was raining, he went for a walk.

title: ～なくはない (〜naku wa nai)
formation: Verb-ないform + なくはない, い-Adjective－い + くはない, な-Adjective + ではなくはない, Noun + ではなくはない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the idea 'it's not that it's impossible' or 'it's not that I can't'.
longExplanationEn: The ～なくはない grammar point implies that while something may not be your first choice or not guaranteed, it's not outright impossible. Translates to 'It's not that it's impossible,' 'It's not that I don't/can't ~.'

examples.ja: それを終えるのは難しくなくはない。
examples.romaji: Sore o oeru no wa muzukashiku naku wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that it's impossible to finish it.

examples.ja: 彼に話すのは怖くなくはない。
examples.romaji: Kare ni hanasu no wa kowaku naku wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I'm entirely unafraid to talk to him.

examples.ja: 映画を見に行く時間がなくはない。
examples.romaji: Eiga o mi ni iku jikan ga naku wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not that I don't have time to go see a movie.

examples.ja: 日本語で講演するのは難しくなくはない。
examples.romaji: Nihongo de kouen suru no wa muzukashiku naku wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not impossible to give a lecture in Japanese, but it won't be easy.

title: ～なくもない (〜naku mo nai)
formation: Verb-ないform + なくもない, い-Adjective－い + くもない, な-Adjective + でもない, Noun + でもない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'not impossible' or 'might as well'.
longExplanationEn: The ～なくもない grammar point is used to suggest that something is not necessarily preferred or likely, but it's also not out of the question. It can often translate to 'It’s not a bad idea' or 'It might be feasible.'

examples.ja: 彼がもうすぐ戻るなら、待つなくもない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga mou sugu modoru nara, matsu naku mo nai.
examples.meaningEn: If he’s coming back soon, waiting wouldn’t be a bad idea.

examples.ja: この本が役に立つなら、買うなくもない。
examples.romaji: Kono hon ga yaku ni tatsu nara, kau naku mo nai.
examples.meaningEn: If this book is useful, it might not be a bad idea to buy it.

examples.ja: 天気が良ければ、ピクニックに行くなくもない。
examples.romaji: Tenki ga yokereba, pikunikku ni iku naku mo nai.
examples.meaningEn: If the weather is nice, it wouldn't be impossible for us to go on a picnic.

examples.ja: そのプロジェクトが成功すれば、プロモーションもあり得なくもない。
examples.romaji: Sono purojekuto ga seikou sureba, puromooshon mo arienaku mo nai.
examples.meaningEn: If that project succeeds, a promotion might not be out of the question.

title: ～なら～なりに
formation: Noun/Adjective + なら + (same Noun/Adjective) + なりに
shortExplanationEn: Means 'if it is X, then it's X in its own way', emphasizing unique qualities.
longExplanationEn: The ～なら～なりに grammar point shows that something has its own particular way or style of being, based on that condition. It often conveys a positive acceptance or appreciation of the situation 'in its own way.'

examples.ja: 彼はまだ初心者なら初心者なりに頑張っている。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mada shoshinsha nara shoshinsha nari ni ganbatteiru.
examples.meaningEn: Since he’s still a beginner, he’s doing his best in his own beginner way.

examples.ja: この料理は辛いなら辛いなりに美味しい。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri wa karai nara karai nari ni oishii.
examples.meaningEn: If this dish is spicy, it’s delicious in its own spicy way.

examples.ja: あの人は変なら変なりに面白い。
examples.romaji: Ano hito wa hen nara hen nari ni omoshiroi.
examples.meaningEn: If that person is strange, they’re interesting in their own strange way.

examples.ja: この町は小さいなら小さいなりに楽しい。
examples.romaji: Kono machi wa chiisai nara chiisai nari ni tanoshii.
examples.meaningEn: If this town is small, it’s fun in its own small way.

title: ～には及ばない (〜ni wa oyobanai)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + には及ばない
shortExplanationEn: Means 'no need to go that far' or 'not necessary'.
longExplanationEn: The ～には及ばない grammar point is used to show that an action or degree of effort is unnecessary or excessive. It often translates to 'there’s no need to...' or 'it doesn’t warrant...' in English.

examples.ja: ファーストクラスに乗るには及ばない、エコノミーでも十分だ。
examples.romaji: Faasuto kurasu ni noru ni wa oyobanai, ekonomii demo juubun da.
examples.meaningEn: There’s no need to go as far as flying first class; economy is enough.

examples.ja: 毎回会うたびにギフトを持って行くには及ばない。
examples.romaji: Maikai au tabi ni gifuto o motte iku ni wa oyobanai.
examples.meaningEn: There’s no need to bring a gift every time you meet her.

examples.ja: その問題は先生に聞くには及ばない、自分で解決できる。
examples.romaji: Sono mondai wa sensei ni kiku ni wa oyobanai, jibun de kaiketsu dekiru.
examples.meaningEn: That issue doesn’t warrant asking the teacher; you can solve it yourself.

examples.ja: 小さな失敗のために、あんなに辛辣な言葉を使うには及ばないだろう。
examples.romaji: Chiisana shippai no tame ni, anna ni shinratsu na kotoba o tsukau ni wa oyobanai darou.
examples.meaningEn: For such a small mistake, there’s no need to use such harsh words.

title: ～に堪えない (～ni taenai)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + に堪えない, Noun + に堪えない
shortExplanationEn: Indicates something is unbearable or not worth doing (e.g., 'unbearable to watch', 'unbearable to listen to').
longExplanationEn: The ～に堪えない grammar pattern is used when something is so unpleasant, awful, or painful that one cannot tolerate it. Common verbs include 見るに堪えない, 聞くに堪えない, 読むに堪えない, etc., meaning 'unbearable to see/hear/read.'

examples.ja: 彼の冗談は聞くに堪えない。
examples.romaji: Kare no joudan wa kiku ni taenai.
examples.meaningEn: His jokes are unbearable to listen to.

examples.ja: その映画は見るに堪えないほどひどい出来だ。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga wa miru ni taenai hodo hidoi deki da.
examples.meaningEn: That movie is so bad that it’s unbearable to watch.

examples.ja: この小説は読むに堪えないほど退屈だ。
examples.romaji: Kono shousetsu wa yomu ni taenai hodo taikutsu da.
examples.meaningEn: This novel is so boring that it’s unbearable to read.

examples.ja: 彼の行動は見るに堪えない。
examples.romaji: Kare no koudou wa miru ni taenai.
examples.meaningEn: His behavior is unbearable to watch.

title: ～に堪える (～ni taeru)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + に堪える, Noun + に堪える
shortExplanationEn: Used to say that something is 'worth doing' or 'can stand up to' something (the positive counterpart of ～に堪えない).
longExplanationEn: The ～に堪える grammar point indicates that something is good enough or worthy enough to do (read, see, listen to, etc.). Common examples include 読むに堪える, 見るに堪える, 聞くに堪える, etc., meaning 'worth reading/watching/listening to.'

examples.ja: 彼の歌声は聞くに堪える。
examples.romaji: Kare no utagoe wa kiku ni taeru.
examples.meaningEn: His singing voice is worth listening to.

examples.ja: この小説は読むに堪える内容だ。
examples.romaji: Kono shousetsu wa yomu ni taeru naiyou da.
examples.meaningEn: This novel is worth reading.

examples.ja: あの映画は見るに堪える名作だ。
examples.romaji: Ano eiga wa miru ni taeru meisaku da.
examples.meaningEn: That movie is a masterpiece worth watching.

examples.ja: 彼の意見は十分に参考に堪える。
examples.romaji: Kare no iken wa juubun ni sankou ni taeru.
examples.meaningEn: His opinion is definitely worth considering.

title: ～に耐える (～ni taeru)
formation: Noun + に耐える
shortExplanationEn: To endure or withstand something; 'endure', 'withstand', 'tolerate'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に耐える grammar point is used to express the concept of enduring or withstanding something, often involving a degree of difficulty or hardship. It can be translated as 'endure', 'withstand', or 'tolerate' in English. Here we focus on usage with nouns (e.g., a force or a hardship to endure).

examples.ja: 彼は厳しい修行に耐えた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kibishii shugyou ni taeta.
examples.meaningEn: He endured the harsh training.

examples.ja: この橋は強風に耐えられる。
examples.romaji: Kono hashi wa kyoufuu ni taerareru.
examples.meaningEn: This bridge can withstand strong winds.

examples.ja: 彼女は苦しみに耐えた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa kurushimi ni taeta.
examples.meaningEn: She endured the pain.

examples.ja: この建物は地震に耐えることができます。
examples.romaji: Kono tatemono wa jishin ni taeru koto ga dekimasu.
examples.meaningEn: This building can withstand an earthquake.

title: ～に至った (〜ni itatta)
formation: Verb-てform + に至った or Noun / Verb-dictionary + に至る (more general).
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the outcome or final conclusion of a process; 'reached the point', 'led to'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に至った grammar point is used to express the final outcome or conclusion that has been reached as a result of a process or a series of events. It can be translated as 'reached the point', 'led to', or 'resulted in' in English. It often indicates a significant outcome.

examples.ja: 彼は練習を重ねて、ついにプロのギタリストに至った。
examples.romaji: Kare wa renshuu o kasanete, tsui ni puro no gitarisuto ni itatta.
examples.meaningEn: After repeated practice, he finally became a professional guitarist.

examples.ja: 調査を進めるうちに、驚くべき結論に至った。
examples.romaji: Chousa o susumeru uchi ni, odoroku beki ketsuron ni itatta.
examples.meaningEn: As the investigation progressed, it led to a surprising conclusion.

examples.ja: この問題はついに国際的な対立に至った。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa tsui ni kokusaiteki na tairitsu ni itatta.
examples.meaningEn: This issue finally led to an international conflict.

examples.ja: たくさんの失敗を経て、成功に至った。
examples.romaji: Takusan no shippai o hete, seikou ni itatta.
examples.meaningEn: After many failures, I finally achieved success.

title: ～に越したことはない (〜ni koshita koto wa nai)
formation: Verb (dictionary form) + に越したことはない, い-Adjective + に越したことはない, な-Adjective/Noun + である + に越したことはない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses that something is the best or most desirable course of action; 'nothing better than...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に越したことはない grammar point indicates that a certain state or action is the most favorable or ideal. It literally conveys the idea that nothing surpasses the stated condition.

examples.ja: 早く準備するに越したことはない。
examples.romaji: Hayaku junbi suru ni koshita koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: It's best to prepare early.

examples.ja: 新鮮な食材を使うに越したことはない。
examples.romaji: Shinsen na shokuzai o tsukau ni koshita koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: There's nothing better than using fresh ingredients.

examples.ja: 安全運転に越したことはない。
examples.romaji: Anzen unten ni koshita koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: Safe driving is always best.

examples.ja: 健康的な生活を送るに越したことはない。
examples.romaji: Kenkouteki na seikatsu o okuru ni koshita koto wa nai.
examples.meaningEn: There's nothing better than leading a healthy lifestyle.

title: ～に難くない (～ni katakunai)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + に難くない (commonly with 想像する, 理解する, 察する, etc.)
shortExplanationEn: Expresses 'not difficult to ~' in a formal/literary sense; e.g., 'not hard to imagine/understand'.
longExplanationEn: The ～に難くない (～にかたくない) grammar is typically used in formal or literary contexts to indicate that something is not difficult. Common expressions include 想像に難くない ('not hard to imagine'), 理解に難くない ('not difficult to understand'), 察するに難くない, and so on.

examples.ja: 彼の喜びは想像に難くない。
examples.romaji: Kare no yorokobi wa souzou ni katakunai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not hard to imagine his delight.

examples.ja: この結果が彼女を失望させたことは理解に難くない。
examples.romaji: Kono kekka ga kanojo o shitsubou saseta koto wa rikai ni katakunai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not difficult to understand that this outcome disappointed her.

examples.ja: 彼が怒る気持ちは察するに難くない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga okoru kimochi wa sassuru ni katakunai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not hard to guess why he is angry.

examples.ja: このような状況が起こった理由は想像に難くない。
examples.romaji: Kono youna joukyou ga okotta riyuu wa souzou ni katakunai.
examples.meaningEn: It's not hard to imagine why this situation occurred.

title: ～のは Noun ぐらいのものだ (〜no wa Noun gurai no mono da)
formation: Verb-casual + のは + Noun + ぐらいのものだ, い-Adjective + のは + Noun + ぐらいのものだ, etc.
shortExplanationEn: Conveys that 'only (Noun) is the case' or 'nothing but (Noun)'.
longExplanationEn: The ～のは Noun ぐらいのものだ pattern indicates that something is exclusively or solely that particular Noun, conveying the sense of 'That's the only thing...' or 'There's nothing but...' about a situation.

examples.ja: この小説を読むのは暇つぶしぐらいのものだ。
examples.romaji: Kono shousetsu o yomu no wa hima tsubushi gurai no mono da.
examples.meaningEn: Reading this novel is nothing but a way to kill time.

examples.ja: 今日の夕食で良かったのはピザぐらいのものだ。
examples.romaji: Kyou no yuushoku de yokatta no wa piza gurai no mono da.
examples.meaningEn: About the only good thing at tonight's dinner was the pizza.

examples.ja: この映画を見るのは時間の無駄ぐらいのものだ。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga o miru no wa jikan no muda gurai no mono da.
examples.meaningEn: Watching this movie is nothing but a waste of time.

examples.ja: 彼と話すのはストレスぐらいのものだ。
examples.romaji: Kare to hanasu no wa sutoresu gurai no mono da.
examples.meaningEn: Talking to him is nothing but stressful.

title: ～ば～ものを (～ba～mono o)
formation: Verb-ば + Verb-past + ものを, い-Adjective + ければ + ものを, な-Adjective/Noun + であれば + ものを, etc.
shortExplanationEn: Expresses regret or a missed opportunity: 'if only', 'had I but...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ば～ものを grammar point shows regret about what could have been, often translated as 'if only...' or 'it would have been better if...'. It can be used with verbs, adjectives, or nouns, usually in a past or conditional form.

examples.ja: 早く起きれば、遅刻しなかったものを。
examples.romaji: Hayaku okireba, chikoku shinakatta mono o.
examples.meaningEn: If only I'd woken up early, I wouldn't have been late.

examples.ja: もっと頑張れば、テストに合格したものを。
examples.romaji: Motto ganbareba, tesuto ni goukaku shita mono o.
examples.meaningEn: If only I'd tried harder, I would have passed the test.

examples.ja: お金があれば、新しい車を買ったものを。
examples.romaji: Okane ga areba, atarashii kuruma o katta mono o.
examples.meaningEn: If only I had the money, I would have bought a new car.

examples.ja: 友達がいれば、一緒に遊んだものを。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga ireba, issho ni asonda mono o.
examples.meaningEn: If only I had a friend, I would have hung out together.

title: ～びた (～bita)
formation: [Noun/Adjective stem] + びた (forms a 連体形 modifier, e.g., 大人びた人 “an adult-like person”)
shortExplanationEn: A suffix meaning “looking or seeming like ~” (often appears with nouns/adjectives).
longExplanationEn: The suffix ～びた attaches to certain words to mean “to have the look/feel of ~.” Common examples include 大人びた (adult-like), 古びた (old/worn), 田舎びた (countrified). It expresses that someone or something appears or has become like that noun/adjective.

examples.ja: 大人びた考え方をする子供
examples.romaji: Otonabita kangaekata o suru kodomo
examples.meaningEn: A child who thinks in a very grown-up way

examples.ja: 古びたカバンを捨てられない。
examples.romaji: Furubita kaban o suterarenai.
examples.meaningEn: I can't throw away that old, worn-looking bag.

examples.ja: 田舎びた雰囲気がこの町の魅力だ。
examples.romaji: Inakabita fun'iki ga kono machi no miryoku da.
examples.meaningEn: The countrified atmosphere is this town’s charm.

examples.ja: 彼女は少し大人びた服装をしている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa sukoshi otonabita fukusou o shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: She’s dressed in a slightly grown-up style.

title: ～びる (〜biru)
formation: Adjective/Noun + びる (to form a verb)
shortExplanationEn: A suffix meaning “to appear/to become ~,” often used with certain adjectives or nouns.
longExplanationEn: The suffix ～びる is used to form verbs meaning 'to become like' or 'to seem like.' Typical examples include 大人びる (to become or behave like an adult), 古びる (to become old or look old), 田舎びる (to become rustic/countrified).

examples.ja: 彼女は年齢より大人びている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa nenrei yori otona bite iru.
examples.meaningEn: She seems older than her actual age.

examples.ja: この建物はすっかり古びている。
examples.romaji: Kono tatemono wa sukkari furubite iru.
examples.meaningEn: This building has become completely old and worn.

examples.ja: 彼は都会育ちなのに、少し田舎びている話し方をする。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tokai sodachi na noni, sukoshi inakabite iru hanashikata o suru.
examples.meaningEn: Even though he grew up in the city, he speaks in a somewhat country-like way.

examples.ja: 子供が急に大人びる瞬間を見ると驚く。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ga kyuu ni otona biru shunkan o miru to odoroku.
examples.meaningEn: It’s surprising when you see a child suddenly act grown-up.

title: ～ぶった (～butta)
formation: Noun/Adjective + ぶる → ぶった (past tense), e.g., 偉そうぶった, 大人ぶった
shortExplanationEn: Often appears as a past form of ～ぶる, meaning 'acted/pretended like ~' (or literally 'hit/bashed', depending on context).
longExplanationEn: The verb ～ぶる means 'to act/behave as if one is ~' (e.g., 偉そうにぶる: 'to act all high and mighty'). In its past tense, ～ぶった can describe someone who acted or pretended in a certain way. Be aware that ぶった can also simply be 'hit' (past tense of 打つ/ぶつ) depending on the kanji/spelling, but as a grammar pattern, it’s about 'putting on airs.'

examples.ja: 彼は何でも知っているかのようにぶった態度を取る。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nandemo shitteiru ka no you ni butta taido o toru.
examples.meaningEn: He takes on an attitude as though he knows everything.

examples.ja: 偉そうぶった物言いをされると、少し腹が立つ。
examples.romaji: Erasou butta monoii o sareru to, sukoshi hara ga tatsu.
examples.meaningEn: It irritates me when he speaks in such a high-and-mighty way.

examples.ja: 彼女は大人ぶった口調で話しているが、実はまだ高校生だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa otona butta kuchou de hanashite iru ga, jitsu wa mada koukousei da.
examples.meaningEn: She’s speaking in a very grown-up manner, but she’s actually still in high school.

examples.ja: いい子ぶった態度を取らなくてもいいよ。
examples.romaji: Ii ko butta taido o toranakute mo ii yo.
examples.meaningEn: You don't have to pretend to be such a goody-goody.

title: ～ぶって (〜butte)
formation: Noun/Adjective + ぶる → ぶって (te-form)
shortExplanationEn: Te-form of ～ぶる, meaning 'acting/pretending to be ~'.
longExplanationEn: ～ぶる (in te-form: ～ぶって) indicates 'behaving as if one were ~.' This is often used negatively to criticize someone for putting on airs or pretending to know more than they do (e.g., 知ったかぶって 'to act like a know-it-all').

examples.ja: 彼は何でも知ったかぶって話をする。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nandemo shittaka butte hanashi o suru.
examples.meaningEn: He talks as if he knows everything.

examples.ja: 彼女は何も知らないふりをしてぶっていたが、本当は全部わかっている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa nanimo shiranai furi o shite butte ita ga, hontou wa zenbu wakatte iru.
examples.meaningEn: She was acting like she didn’t know anything, but in reality, she understands it all.

examples.ja: 彼はいつも偉そうぶって周りに指図している。
examples.romaji: Kare wa itsumo erasou butte mawari ni sashizu shite iru.
examples.meaningEn: He’s always bossing people around as if he’s so important.

examples.ja: 大人ぶっているけど、まだ子供っぽいところがある。
examples.romaji: Otona butte iru kedo, mada kodomoppoi tokoro ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: He acts grown-up, but he still has some childish traits.

title: ～ぶり (〜buri)
formation: Time expression + ぶり
shortExplanationEn: Expresses the amount of time elapsed since doing something last.
longExplanationEn: The ～ぶり grammar point is used to express that a certain amount of time has passed since you last did something. It often translates as 'for the first time in ~' or 'after (time period)'.

examples.ja: 三年ぶりに彼に会いました。
examples.romaji: San-nen buri ni kare ni aimashita.
examples.meaningEn: I met him for the first time in three years.

examples.ja: 一週間ぶりにジムに行った。
examples.romaji: Isshuukan buri ni jimu ni itta.
examples.meaningEn: I went to the gym for the first time in a week.

examples.ja: 一ヶ月ぶりに彼からの手紙が来た。
examples.romaji: Ikkagetsu buri ni kare kara no tegami ga kita.
examples.meaningEn: I received a letter from him for the first time in a month.

examples.ja: 五年ぶりに雪が降った。
examples.romaji: Go-nen buri ni yuki ga futta.
examples.meaningEn: It snowed for the first time in five years.

title: ～ぶる (〜buru)
formation: Noun / (Adjective stem) + ぶる
shortExplanationEn: Used when someone is acting or pretending to be something they are not (often a negative connotation).
longExplanationEn: The ～ぶる grammar point is used when someone pretends or acts like something they aren't. It often has a negative nuance, suggesting insincerity or putting on airs. Common usage includes nouns or descriptive words before ぶる (e.g., 学者ぶる, 偉そうぶる, etc.).

examples.ja: 彼はあたかも知っているかのように学者ぶる。
examples.romaji: Kare wa atakamo shitteiru ka no you ni gakusha buru.
examples.meaningEn: He acts like a scholar, as if he knows everything.

examples.ja: 女優ぶるのはやめて、自分自身でいろ。
examples.romaji: Joyu buru no wa yamete, jibun jishin de iro.
examples.meaningEn: Stop acting like you’re an actress and just be yourself.

examples.ja: 彼女は常にお嬢様ぶっている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa tsune ni ojou-sama butte iru.
examples.meaningEn: She’s always putting on the airs of a refined lady.

examples.ja: 私がいじめられていると無邪気ぶる彼女を見て、怒りがこみ上げた。
examples.romaji: Watashi ga ijimerarete iru to mujaki buru kanojo o mite, ikari ga komiageta.
examples.meaningEn: Seeing her act innocent while I was being bullied made my anger surge.

title: ～までだ (～made da)
formation: Verb (dictionary/ない form) + までだ, い-Adjective + までだ, Noun + までだ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses a limit or extent; 'it's only up to...', 'no more than...'.
longExplanationEn: The ～までだ grammar point is used to emphasize that something goes only up to a certain limit or extent. It can also imply 'it's only this and nothing more.'

examples.ja: 彼とは友達までだ。それ以上の関係ではありません。
examples.romaji: Kare to wa tomodachi made da. Sore ijou no kankei dewa arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: With him, it's only friendship—nothing more.

examples.ja: 飲み放題は9時までだ。それ以降は追加料金が必要だ。
examples.romaji: Nomihoudai wa kuji made da. Sore ikou wa tsuika ryoukin ga hitsuyou da.
examples.meaningEn: The all-you-can-drink deal is only until nine. After that, there's an extra charge.

examples.ja: 私たちの情報は昨日までだ。それ以降のアップデートはありません。
examples.romaji: Watashitachi no jouhou wa kinou made da. Sore ikou no appudeeto wa arimasen.
examples.meaningEn: Our information only goes up to yesterday; there are no further updates.

examples.ja: 彼の知識は中学生レベルまでだ。それ以上は理解できない。
examples.romaji: Kare no chishiki wa chuugakusei reberu made da. Sore ijou wa rikai dekinai.
examples.meaningEn: His knowledge is only at a junior-high level. He can't understand anything more advanced.

title: ～もなんでもない (〜mo nandemonai)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + もなんでもない, い-Adjective + もなんでもない, な-Adjective + なもなんでもない, Noun + もなんでもない
shortExplanationEn: Used to dismiss or negate something’s importance; 'it’s nothing at all', 'it doesn’t matter'.
longExplanationEn: The ～もなんでもない pattern is used to deny or downplay the significance of something. It can mean 'it doesn’t matter at all' or 'it’s no big deal.'

examples.ja: 100万円を負けるもなんでもない。
examples.romaji: Hyaku man-en o makeru mo nandemonai.
examples.meaningEn: Losing a million yen is no big deal at all.

examples.ja: 彼が社長だもなんでもない。
examples.romaji: Kare ga shachou da mo nandemonai.
examples.meaningEn: It doesn't matter one bit that he's the president.

examples.ja: あなたが来ないもなんでもない。
examples.romaji: Anata ga konai mo nandemonai.
examples.meaningEn: It doesn't matter at all if you don't come.

examples.ja: それが美しいもなんでもない。
examples.romaji: Sore ga utsukushii mo nandemonai.
examples.meaningEn: It doesn't matter at all whether it's beautiful.

title: ～ものとして (～mono to shite)
formation: Verb-casual + ものとして, い-Adjective + ものとして, な-Adjective + なものとして, Noun + のものとして
shortExplanationEn: Indicates an assumption or supposition; 'assuming', 'under the assumption that'.
longExplanationEn: The ～ものとして grammar pattern is used to express a supposition or assumption: 'assuming that ~', 'on the premise that ~'. It often sets up a condition or rule as the basis for some action or decision.

examples.ja: 雨が降るものとして、傘を持って行きました。
examples.romaji: Ame ga furu mono to shite, kasa o motte ikimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Assuming it would rain, I took an umbrella.

examples.ja: 彼が病気なものとして、代わりに私が出席した。
examples.romaji: Kare ga byouki na mono to shite, kawari ni watashi ga shusseki shita.
examples.meaningEn: On the assumption that he was sick, I attended in his place.

examples.ja: このケーキが美味しいものとして、みんなに分けよう。
examples.romaji: Kono keeki ga oishii mono to shite, minna ni wakeyou.
examples.meaningEn: Let’s share this cake, assuming it’s delicious.

examples.ja: 彼女が学生のものとして、学割を適用しました。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga gakusei no mono to shite, gakuwari o tekiyou shimashita.
examples.meaningEn: Assuming she is a student, I applied the student discount.

title: ～んがために (〜n ga tame ni)
formation: Verb-negative stem + んがために
shortExplanationEn: Emphasizes the reason or purpose for an action; 'in order to', 'for the sake of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～んがために form is a formal/literary expression meaning 'in order to' or 'for the purpose of.' It attaches to the negative stem (ない-form minus ない) of the verb: e.g., する→せん, 行く→いかん, 保つ→たもたん, etc.

examples.ja: 健康を保たんがために、毎日運動をしています。
examples.romaji: Kenkou o tamotan ga tame ni, mainichi undou o shite imasu.
examples.meaningEn: I exercise daily in order to maintain my health.

examples.ja: 合格せんがために、一生懸命勉強します。
examples.romaji: Goukaku sen ga tame ni, isshoukenmei benkyou shimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I study hard in order to pass.

examples.ja: 明日の試験に備えんがため、今夜は早く寝るつもりです。
examples.romaji: Ashita no shiken ni sonaen ga tame, kon'ya wa hayaku neru tsumori desu.
examples.meaningEn: I plan to go to bed early so I can prepare for tomorrow’s exam.

examples.ja: 彼女の笑顔を見んがために、毎日会いに行きます。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no egao o min ga tame ni, mainichi ai ni ikimasu.
examples.meaningEn: I go to see her every day in order to see her smile.

title: ～んばかりに (〜n bakari ni)
formation: Verb-negative stem + ん + ばかりに
shortExplanationEn: Indicates 'as though about to ~' or 'as if to say ~'.
longExplanationEn: The ～んばかりに form is typically built on the negative stem of a verb (ない-form minus ない: 行か→行かん, 言わ→言わん, 知ら→知らん, etc.) + ばかり. It describes someone’s action or expression as though it were about to happen, or as though conveying a certain meaning without explicitly stating it.

examples.ja: 彼は何も知らんばかりに、私の質問を無視した。
examples.romaji: Kare wa nanimo shir-an bakari ni, watashi no shitsumon o mushi shita.
examples.meaningEn: He ignored my question as if he didn’t know anything.

examples.ja: その子は泣かんばかりに顔をしかめた。
examples.romaji: Sono ko wa nakan bakari ni kao o shikameta.
examples.meaningEn: The child made a face as if he were about to cry.

examples.ja: 彼女は笑わんばかりに口元を押さえた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa warawan bakari ni kuchimoto o osaeta.
examples.meaningEn: She covered her mouth as though she were about to burst out laughing.

examples.ja: 彼は退屈だと言わんばかりに、ため息を何度もついた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa taikutsu da to iwan bakari ni, tameiki o nando mo tsuita.
examples.meaningEn: He sighed over and over as if to say he was bored.

title: ～差し支えない (〜sashitsukaenai)
formation: Verb-て form + も差し支えない, Noun + が差し支えない
shortExplanationEn: Indicates no problem or obstacle; 'it’s fine if...', 'there’s no issue'.
longExplanationEn: The ～差し支えない grammar point expresses that doing something (or something occurring) is not a problem and causes no inconvenience. It’s often used in polite or formal situations.

examples.ja: 多少遅くても差し支えないです。
examples.romaji: Tashou osokute mo sashitsukaenai desu.
examples.meaningEn: It’s no problem if you’re a bit late.

examples.ja: 部屋が少し汚くても差し支えない。
examples.romaji: Heya ga sukoshi kitanakute mo sashitsukaenai.
examples.meaningEn: It doesn’t matter if the room is slightly messy.

examples.ja: 雨が降っても差し支えない。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte mo sashitsukaenai.
examples.meaningEn: It’s not an issue even if it rains.

examples.ja: 彼がここにいることが差し支えないですか？
examples.romaji: Kare ga koko ni iru koto ga sashitsukaenai desu ka?
examples.meaningEn: Is it okay (no problem) for him to be here?

title: ～折に (〜ori ni)
formation: Verb-dictionary form + 折に, Verb-た形 + 折に, Noun + の折に
shortExplanationEn: Indicates the time or occasion when something happens; 'when', 'at the time of'.
longExplanationEn: The ～折に grammar point is used in a somewhat formal register to express that one event or action takes place on the occasion of another. It essentially means 'when' or 'at the time of'.

examples.ja: 父が病気の折に、私は留学していました。
examples.romaji: Chichi ga byouki no ori ni, watashi wa ryuugaku shite imashita.
examples.meaningEn: When my father was ill, I was studying abroad.

examples.ja: 日本へ行く折に、お土産を買ってきてください。
examples.romaji: Nihon e iku ori ni, omiyage o katte kite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: When you go to Japan, please buy souvenirs.

examples.ja: 子供が寝ている折に、静かにしてください。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ga nete iru ori ni, shizuka ni shite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please be quiet when the child is sleeping.

examples.ja: 彼に会う折に、この手紙を渡してください。
examples.romaji: Kare ni au ori ni, kono tegami o watashite kudasai.
examples.meaningEn: Please give him this letter when you see him.

title: ～極まりない (〜kiwamarinai)
formation: な-Adjective（語幹）+ 極まりない, い-Adjective（連用形）+ こと + 極まりない, Noun + の極まりない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an extreme state, often negative; 'utterly', 'extremely', 'boundlessly'.
longExplanationEn: The ～極まりない grammar point indicates an extreme or utmost degree, most commonly with a negative nuance (e.g., 失礼極まりない, 危険極まりない). It can sometimes be used for positive contexts, but that usage is less common.

examples.ja: 彼の失礼な態度は極まりない。
examples.romaji: Kare no shitsurei na taido wa kiwamarinai.
examples.meaningEn: His rude attitude is utterly beyond words.

examples.ja: その運転は危険極まりない。
examples.romaji: Sono unten wa kiken kiwamarinai.
examples.meaningEn: That driving is extremely dangerous.

examples.ja: 彼女のわがままぶりは非常識極まりない。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no wagamama-buri wa hijoushiki kiwamarinai.
examples.meaningEn: Her selfishness is utterly irrational.

examples.ja: この部屋の汚さは不衛生極まりない。
examples.romaji: Kono heya no kitasa wa fueisei kiwamarinai.
examples.meaningEn: The filthiness of this room is extremely unsanitary.

title: ～極まる (〜kiwamaru)
formation: な-Adjective（語幹）+ 極まる, い-Adjective（連用形）+ こと + 極まる, etc.
shortExplanationEn: Also expresses an extreme degree or state, typically negative.
longExplanationEn: The ～極まる form often appears with な-adjectives: e.g., 失礼極まる, 非常識極まる ('extremely rude/unreasonable'). It's largely synonymous with ～極まりない but tends to appear in set phrases. Both forms are quite formal and emphatic.

examples.ja: 彼の言動は失礼極まる。
examples.romaji: Kare no gendou wa shitsurei kiwamaru.
examples.meaningEn: His words and actions are extremely rude.

examples.ja: その態度は非常識極まる。
examples.romaji: Sono taido wa hijoushiki kiwamaru.
examples.meaningEn: That behavior is utterly irrational.

examples.ja: 彼女の発言は無責任極まる。
examples.romaji: Kanojo no hatsugen wa musekinin kiwamaru.
examples.meaningEn: Her remark is extremely irresponsible.

examples.ja: このような誤解は遺憾極まる。
examples.romaji: Kono youna gokai wa ikan kiwamaru.
examples.meaningEn: Such a misunderstanding is exceedingly regrettable.

title: ～足りない (～tarinai)
formation: Noun + (が) 足りない, Verb-casual + ほど/だけ + (が) 足りない
shortExplanationEn: Expresses insufficiency or lack; 'not enough', 'insufficient'.
longExplanationEn: The ～足りない pattern simply means that something is not sufficient in quantity, time, detail, ability, etc. It’s the negative form of 足りる ('to be enough').

examples.ja: 私の日本語の能力はまだまだ足りない。
examples.romaji: Watashi no nihongo no nouryoku wa madamada tarinai.
examples.meaningEn: My Japanese skills are still not enough.

examples.ja: 時間が足りないから、今すぐ行かなきゃ。
examples.romaji: Jikan ga tarinai kara, ima sugu ikanakya.
examples.meaningEn: We don't have enough time, so we must go right now.

examples.ja: 彼の説明には詳細が足りない。
examples.romaji: Kare no setsumei ni wa shousai ga tarinai.
examples.meaningEn: His explanation lacks detail.

examples.ja: 料理するための食材が足りない。
examples.romaji: Ryouri suru tame no shokuzai ga tarinai.
examples.meaningEn: There's not enough food to cook with.

title: ～足る Noun (〜taru Noun)
formation: Noun/Verb-dictionary form + に足る + Noun
shortExplanationEn: Indicates that something is worthy or sufficient to be called/used as ~; 'worthy of', 'enough to ~'.
longExplanationEn: The classical pattern 'Noun + に足る + Noun' or 'Verb + に足る + Noun' means 'something is worthy of or sufficient for ~.' For instance, '信頼に足る人物' means 'a person worthy of trust.' It’s different from the everyday verb 足りる ('to be enough').

examples.ja: 彼は信頼に足る人物だ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa shinrai ni taru jinbutsu da.
examples.meaningEn: He is a person worthy of trust.

examples.ja: この小説は読むに足る価値がある。
examples.romaji: Kono shousetsu wa yomu ni taru kachi ga aru.
examples.meaningEn: This novel is well worth reading.

examples.ja: 検討に足るデータが十分に集まった。
examples.romaji: Kentou ni taru deeta ga juubun ni atsumatta.
examples.meaningEn: We've gathered enough data to be worth examining.

examples.ja: 彼の説明は信用に足るものだと思う。
examples.romaji: Kare no setsumei wa shinyou ni taru mono da to omou.
examples.meaningEn: I think his explanation is worthy of trust.

title: ～限りだ (〜kagiri da)
formation: い-Adjective + 限りだ, な-Adjective + な/である + 限りだ, Noun + の + 限りだ
shortExplanationEn: Expresses an extreme degree of emotion or reaction; 'I feel so ~', 'as ~ as can be'.
longExplanationEn: The ～限りだ structure is often used with adjectives describing the speaker's emotion, highlighting how strongly the speaker feels. Translations often include 'I’m as ~ as can be,' 'I feel so ~.'

examples.ja: 彼女が急に辞めてしまった。驚きの限りだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo ga kyuu ni yamete shimatta. Odoroki no kagiri da.
examples.meaningEn: She suddenly quit. I'm as shocked as can be.

examples.ja: 息子が成功した。嬉しい限りだ。
examples.romaji: Musuko ga seikou shita. Ureshii kagiri da.
examples.meaningEn: My son succeeded. I'm so happy.

examples.ja: こんなに綺麗な花が見られるなんて、幸せの限りだ。
examples.romaji: Konna ni kirei na hana ga mirareru nante, shiawase no kagiri da.
examples.meaningEn: I'm as happy as can be to see such beautiful flowers.

examples.ja: 試験に合格できて、ほっとした限りだ。
examples.romaji: Shiken ni goukaku dekite, hotto shita kagiri da.
examples.meaningEn: I passed the exam—what a relief!
